Edible Plants (55) -

Consuming Wild Desert Gourd Flowers and Stem Tips for Water and Nutrition
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Identify wild desert gourd flowers, which are edible.
2. Harvest the flowers directly from the plant or collect them from the ground if fallen.
3. Eat the flowers raw or cook them briefly in water to make a nutritious meal.
4. For...
Procedure:

1. Identify wild desert gourd flowers, which are edible.
2. Harvest the flowers directly from the plant or collect them from the ground if fallen.
3. Eat the flowers raw or cook them briefly in water to make a nutritious meal.
4. For hydration, chew on the succulent stem tips of the wild desert gourd until moisture is released.

Notes:
• The stem tips are particularly useful for obtaining water during periods of dehydration.
• Flowers can be used as a food source when other resources are limited.
wild desert gourd, hydration, edible flowers, survival nutrition
Identify and Prepare Wild Apple for Consumption
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:
1. Identify wild apple (Malus species) by its small size (usually 5 to 7.5 centimeters in diameter), alternate leaves, thorns, white or pink flowers, and reddish or yellowish fruits.
2. Look for this plant in savanna regions of the...
Procedure:
1. Identify wild apple (Malus species) by its small size (usually 5 to 7.5 centimeters in diameter), alternate leaves, thorns, white or pink flowers, and reddish or yellowish fruits.
2. Look for this plant in savanna regions of the tropics, forested areas in temperate zones, and on the edge of woods or in fields throughout the Northern Hemisphere.
3. Prepare wild apples similarly to cultivated kinds:
- Eat them fresh when ripe.
- Cook them if needed.
4. For storage, cut the apples into thin slices and dry them.
5. Wild apples are a good source of vitamins.
CAUTION: Apple seeds contain cyanide compounds. Do not eat.
wild apple, identification, preparation, safety precautions
Prepare and Consume Edible Parts of Pokeweed Safely
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Young Leaves and Stems: Collect young leaves and stems and cook them by boiling twice, discarding the water from the first boiling.
2. Fruits: Cook the purple fruits before consuming them; never eat them raw.
3. **Avoid...
Procedure:

1. Young Leaves and Stems: Collect young leaves and stems and cook them by boiling twice, discarding the water from the first boiling.
2. Fruits: Cook the purple fruits before consuming them; never eat them raw.
3. Avoid Toxic Parts: Do not consume any part of the plant that is over 25 cm tall or shows red coloration.
4. Underground Parts: Never eat the underground portions, as they contain high concentrations of toxins.

Notes:
• All parts of this plant are poisonous if eaten raw; proper preparation is essential to avoid poisoning.
pokeweed preparation, edible parts, cooking instructions, safety precautions
Prepare and Consume Water Lily Parts
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:
1. Identify water lily (Nymphaea odorata) by its large triangular floating leaves, fragrant white or red flowers, and thick fleshy rhizomes.
2. Harvest the edible parts: flowers, seeds, and rhizomes.
3. To prepare rhizomes for...
Procedure:
1. Identify water lily (Nymphaea odorata) by its large triangular floating leaves, fragrant white or red flowers, and thick fleshy rhizomes.
2. Harvest the edible parts: flowers, seeds, and rhizomes.
3. To prepare rhizomes for eating:
- Peel off the corky rind.
- Eat raw or slice thinly, allow to dry, then grind into flour.
4. Dry, parch, and grind the seeds into flour.
5. Use the liquid from boiling the thickened root in water as a medicine for diarrhea or as a gargle for sore throats.
water lily, preparation, edible parts, medicinal uses
Boil Nettle to Remove Stinging Element
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:
1. Identify the nettle plant by its height (several feet), small inconspicuous flowers, and fine, hairlike bristles on the stems, leafstalks, and undersides of leaves.
2. Harvest young shoots and leaves for consumption.
3. **Boil the...
Procedure:
1. Identify the nettle plant by its height (several feet), small inconspicuous flowers, and fine, hairlike bristles on the stems, leafstalks, and undersides of leaves.
2. Harvest young shoots and leaves for consumption.
3. Boil the plant in water for 10 to 15 minutes to destroy the stinging element caused by the bristles.
4. After boiling, the plant is safe to eat and very nutritious.

Other Uses:
• The fibrous layer of mature stems can be divided into individual fibers and used to weave string or twine.
nettle,boil,remove stinging element,edible plants
Boil Water Plants for Safe Consumption
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:
1. Identify the plant as a water plant based on its habitat (bogs, lakes, slow-moving streams) and appearance (rounded, dark green leaves with bright yellow flowers).
2. Collect the plant parts that are to be consumed.
3. **Do not eat...
Procedure:
1. Identify the plant as a water plant based on its habitat (bogs, lakes, slow-moving streams) and appearance (rounded, dark green leaves with bright yellow flowers).
2. Collect the plant parts that are to be consumed.
3. Do not eat the plant raw. Raw water plants may carry dangerous organisms that are removed only by cooking.
4. Boil the plant in clean water for at least 10 minutes to ensure all harmful organisms are destroyed.
5. After boiling, the plant is safe to consume and can be eaten as part of a meal or used in recipes.

CAUTION: Always confirm that the plant is indeed a water plant before consuming it. Never eat any water plant raw.
boil,water plants,safe consumption,edible plants
Extract Coconut Milk from Young Coconuts
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure to Extract Coconut Milk:

1. Select a young, green coconut that is still firm and has not fully matured.
2. Use a sharp knife or machete to carefully cut off the top of the coconut shell, exposing the inner flesh and liquid.
3. Insert...
Procedure to Extract Coconut Milk:

1. Select a young, green coconut that is still firm and has not fully matured.
2. Use a sharp knife or machete to carefully cut off the top of the coconut shell, exposing the inner flesh and liquid.
3. Insert a long, narrow object (such as a stick or spoon) into the opening to break up the soft, white flesh inside.
4. Squeeze the broken-up flesh with your hands or use a manual press to extract the milk that flows out from the coconut.
5. Collect the extracted liquid in a container for immediate consumption or preservation.

Notes:
• Coconut milk is rich in sugar and vitamins, making it an excellent source of nutrition and hydration.
• This procedure can be performed by individuals with limited tools in survival situations.
coconut milk, extraction, young coconuts, survival food
Extract and Use Jujube Pulp for Nutrition and Hydration
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure to Extract and Use Jujube Pulp:

1. Select ripe jujubes from the tree, ensuring they are fully developed but not overripe.
2. Remove the fruit from the tree and gently crush it in water using your hands or a simple tool (such as a...
Procedure to Extract and Use Jujube Pulp:

1. Select ripe jujubes from the tree, ensuring they are fully developed but not overripe.
2. Remove the fruit from the tree and gently crush it in water using your hands or a simple tool (such as a stone or stick).
3. Collect the resulting liquid to make a refreshing beverage for immediate consumption.
4. If time permits, dry the ripe jujubes in the sun like dates for long-term storage.

Notes:
• Jujube pulp is high in vitamins A and C, making it an excellent source of nutrition.
• This procedure can be performed with minimal tools in survival or emergency situations.
jujube pulp, hydration, vitamin source, drying fruit
Harvest and Consume Nipa Palm for Food and Water
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:
1. Identify the Nipa palm by its short, mainly underground trunk, large erect leaves up to 6 meters tall, and a flowering head that forms on a short erect stern.
2. Harvest the young flower stalk of the Nipa palm.
3. Cut the flower...
Procedure:
1. Identify the Nipa palm by its short, mainly underground trunk, large erect leaves up to 6 meters tall, and a flowering head that forms on a short erect stern.
2. Harvest the young flower stalk of the Nipa palm.
3. Cut the flower stalk and collect the juice, which is rich in sugar and can be consumed directly or used for cooking.
4. Collect the hard seeds from the fruiting (seed) head, which are edible after preparation.

Other Uses:
• The large leaves of the Nipa palm are excellent as thatch and coarse weaving material.
nipa palm,harvest,consume food,water
Harvest and Consume Wild Caper Parts
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:
1. Identify wild caper (Capparis aphylla) by its thorny shrub structure, gray-green stems, pink flowers, and leaf loss during the dry season.
2. Look for this plant in scrub and thorn forests, desert scrub, and waste areas throughout...
Procedure:
1. Identify wild caper (Capparis aphylla) by its thorny shrub structure, gray-green stems, pink flowers, and leaf loss during the dry season.
2. Look for this plant in scrub and thorn forests, desert scrub, and waste areas throughout North Africa and the Middle East.
3. Harvest the edible parts: fruit and buds of young shoots.
4. Consume these parts raw.
wild caper, identification, harvesting, consumption
Harvest and Prepare Amaranth for Consumption
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Identify amaranth plants that are 90 centimeters to 150 centimeters tall with alternate simple leaves, possibly with red color on the stems.
2. Look for them along roadsides, in disturbed waste areas, or as weeds in crops...
Procedure:

1. Identify amaranth plants that are 90 centimeters to 150 centimeters tall with alternate simple leaves, possibly with red color on the stems.
2. Look for them along roadsides, in disturbed waste areas, or as weeds in crops worldwide.
3. Harvest young plants or the growing tips of older plants for consumption as a vegetable.
4. Remove any sharp spines from the plant before eating.
5. Boil the young plants or eat them raw.
6. For seeds:
- Shake the tops of older amaranth plants to collect the seeds.
- Eat the seeds raw, boiled, ground into flour, or popped like popcorn.

Notes:
• Seeds may be brown or black in weedy species and light-colored in domestic species.
• All parts are edible but require careful preparation.
amaranth, harvesting, preparing food, edible plants, survival food
Harvest and Prepare Arctic Willow for Consumption
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Identify arctic willow shrubs that grow in clumps up to 60 centimeters tall on tundras in North America, Europe, and Asia.
2. Look for them in mountainous areas of temperate regions as well.
3. In early spring, collect the...
Procedure:

1. Identify arctic willow shrubs that grow in clumps up to 60 centimeters tall on tundras in North America, Europe, and Asia.
2. Look for them in mountainous areas of temperate regions as well.
3. In early spring, collect the succulent, tender young shoots.
4. Strip off the outer bark of the new shoots and eat the inner portion raw.
5. Alternatively, peel and eat the young underground shoots raw.
6. Young willow leaves are an excellent source of vitamin C (7 to 10 times more than an orange).

Notes:
• Arctic willow is a shrub that forms dense mats on tundra.
arctic willow, vitamin C, survival food, edible plants
Harvest and Prepare Bamboo Shoots for Consumption
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Identify the bamboo plant: Look for bamboo in warm, moist regions in open or jungle country, in lowland, or on mountains.
2. Locate young shoots: Harvest the young shoots of almost all species.
3. **Remove protective...
Procedure:

1. Identify the bamboo plant: Look for bamboo in warm, moist regions in open or jungle country, in lowland, or on mountains.
2. Locate young shoots: Harvest the young shoots of almost all species.
3. Remove protective sheath: Remove the tough protective sheath that is coated with tawny or red hairs.
4. Boil raw shoots: Raw shoots have a slightly bitter taste that is removed by boiling.
5. Use mature bamboo for other purposes: Use mature bamboo to build structures, make containers, ladles, spoons, and various other cooking utensils.
6. Make tools and weapons: You can make a strong bow by splitting the bamboo and putting several pieces together.
7. CAUTION: Green bamboo may explode in a fire. Green bamboo has an internal membrane you must remove before using it as a food or water container.
bamboo,shoots,edible,preparation,fire,safety
Harvest and Prepare Banana and Plantain for Consumption
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Identify the plant: Look for bananas and plantains in open fields or margins of forests where they are grown as a crop.
2. Locate edible parts: The fruits are edible raw or cooked. They may be boiled or baked.
3. **Use...
Procedure:

1. Identify the plant: Look for bananas and plantains in open fields or margins of forests where they are grown as a crop.
2. Locate edible parts: The fruits are edible raw or cooked. They may be boiled or baked.
3. Use flowers as vegetables: Boil their flowers and eat them like a vegetable.
4. Cook rootstocks and leaf sheaths: You can cook and eat the rootstocks and leaf sheaths of many species.
5. Eat heart of plant year-round: The center or "heart" of the plant is edible year-round, cooked or raw.
6. Use leaves for cooking and storage: Use their leaves to wrap other foods for cooking or storage.
7. Cover coals with layers: You can use the layers of the lower third of the plants to cover coals to roast food.
8. Extract water from stumps: You can use their stumps to get water (see Chapter 6).
banana,plantain,edible,preparation,cooking,storage
Harvest and Prepare Baobab for Consumption and Other Uses
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Identify the tree: The baobab tree may grow as high as 18 meters and may have a trunk 9 meters in diameter.
2. Locate edible parts: Use the young leaves as a soup vegetable. The tender root of the young baobab tree is...
Procedure:

1. Identify the tree: The baobab tree may grow as high as 18 meters and may have a trunk 9 meters in diameter.
2. Locate edible parts: Use the young leaves as a soup vegetable. The tender root of the young baobab tree is edible. The pulp and seeds of the fruit are also edible.
3. Prepare drink from pulp: Use one handful of pulp to about one cup of water for a refreshing drink.
4. Make flour from seeds: To obtain flour, roast the seeds, then grind them.
5. Use hollow trunks as water sources: Often the hollow trunks are good sources of fresh water.
6. Extract fiber from bark: The bark can be cut into strips and pounded to obtain a strong fiber for making rope.
7. Cure diarrhea with pulp: Drinking a mixture of pulp and water will help cure diarrhea.
baobab,edible,pulp,seeds,fiber,water,cure_diarrhea
Harvest and Prepare Oak Acorns for Consumption
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:
1. Identify the oak tree by its alternate leaves and acorn fruits.
2. Harvest acorns from either red or white oak trees, but note that white oak acorns usually have a better flavor than red oak acorns.
3. For red oak acorns:
-...
Procedure:
1. Identify the oak tree by its alternate leaves and acorn fruits.
2. Harvest acorns from either red or white oak trees, but note that white oak acorns usually have a better flavor than red oak acorns.
3. For red oak acorns:
- Soak them in water for 1 to 2 days to remove the bitter substance.
- To speed up this process, add wood ashes to the soaking water.
4. After soaking, boil the acorns or grind them into flour and use the flour for baking.
5. Acorns that have been baked until very dark can be used as a coffee substitute.

CAUTION:
• Tannic acid gives the acorns their bitter taste. Eating an excessive amount of acorns high in tannic acid can lead to kidney failure.
• Before eating acorns, leach out this chemical by boiling or soaking them thoroughly.

Other Uses:
• Oak wood is excellent for building or burning.
• Small oaks can be split and cut into long thin strips (3 to 6 millimeters thick and 1.2 centimeters wide) used to weave mats, baskets, or frameworks for packs, sleds, furniture, etc.
• Oak bark soaked in water produces a tanning solution used to preserve leather.
oak acorns,harvest,prepare consumption,tannic acid
Harvest and Prepare Water Plantain Rootstocks
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:
1. Identify water plantain (Alisma plantago-aquatica) by its small white flowers, heart-shaped leaves with pointed tips, and clustered base leaves.
2. Look for this plant in fresh water and wet, full sun areas in temperate and...
Procedure:
1. Identify water plantain (Alisma plantago-aquatica) by its small white flowers, heart-shaped leaves with pointed tips, and clustered base leaves.
2. Look for this plant in fresh water and wet, full sun areas in temperate and tropical zones.
3. Harvest the rootstocks as a source of starch.
4. Boil or soak them in water to remove the bitter taste before consumption.
CAUTION: Always cook aquatic plants to avoid parasites.
water plantain, rootstocks, preparation, safety precautions
Harvesting and Consuming Wild Fig Fruits for Nutrition
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Identify ripe wild figs (Ficus species), which are usually yellow-brown in color.
2. Harvest the fruits directly from the tree or ground if fallen.
3. Eat the raw fruit as a quick source of nutrition, or cook it briefly for added...
Procedure:

1. Identify ripe wild figs (Ficus species), which are usually yellow-brown in color.
2. Harvest the fruits directly from the tree or ground if fallen.
3. Eat the raw fruit as a quick source of nutrition, or cook it briefly for added flavor.
4. Some fig varieties may have little flavor, so consider combining them with other ingredients to enhance taste.

Notes:
• Figs are commonly found in tropical and semitropical regions.
• They can be used as a food source when other fruits are unavailable.
wild fig, edible fruit, survival nutrition, tropical plants
Harvesting and Preparing Wild Desert Gourd Seeds for Consumption
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Identify ripe wild desert gourds, which are yellow when mature.
2. Carefully cut open the gourd to access the seeds inside.
3. Separate the seeds from the very bitter pulp using hands or a small tool.
4. Roast the seeds in an oven...
Procedure:

1. Identify ripe wild desert gourds, which are yellow when mature.
2. Carefully cut open the gourd to access the seeds inside.
3. Separate the seeds from the very bitter pulp using hands or a small tool.
4. Roast the seeds in an oven at 180°C (350°F) for approximately 20 minutes, or boil them in water until tender.
5. Consume the roasted or boiled kernels, which are rich in oil and edible.

Notes:
• Ensure that all bitter pulp is completely removed before consuming seeds to avoid bitterness.
• Seeds should be fully separated from the pulp for best results.
wild desert gourd, edible plants, seed preparation, survival food
Harvesting and Preparing Wild Dock and Wild Sorrel Leaves for Consumption
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Identify wild dock (Rumex crispus) or wild sorrel (Rumex acetosella), which have arrow-shaped leaves with a sour juice.
2. Harvest the tender, succulent leaves from the base of the plant.
3. Wash the leaves thoroughly to remove...
Procedure:

1. Identify wild dock (Rumex crispus) or wild sorrel (Rumex acetosella), which have arrow-shaped leaves with a sour juice.
2. Harvest the tender, succulent leaves from the base of the plant.
3. Wash the leaves thoroughly to remove any dirt or debris.
4. Eat the leaves fresh or slightly cook them in water for better digestion and flavor.
5. To reduce the strong taste during cooking, change the water once or twice during the process.

Notes:
• These plants are especially useful in desert areas where food sources may be scarce.
• The sour juice can be used as a natural flavor enhancer in meals.
wild dock, wild sorrel, edible leaves, survival food
Harvesting and Preparing Wild Luffa Sponge (Luffa cylindrica) for Consumption
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Identify young green luffa fruits, which are half-ripe and still edible.
2. Harvest the fruit before it fully matures to avoid the inedible spongelike texture that develops later.
3. Boil the young green fruit in water for 10–15...
Procedure:

1. Identify young green luffa fruits, which are half-ripe and still edible.
2. Harvest the fruit before it fully matures to avoid the inedible spongelike texture that develops later.
3. Boil the young green fruit in water for 10–15 minutes until tender.
4. Add coconut milk during or after boiling to improve flavor.
5. Consume the boiled luffa as a vegetable.
6. Harvest and cook the tender shoots, flowers, and young leaves of the plant for additional food sources.
7. Roast mature seeds in an oven at 180°C (350°F) for approximately 20 minutes until they are crunchy and edible, similar to peanuts.

Notes:
• The luffa sponge is a member of the squash family and can be found in tropical regions.
• Avoid consuming mature fruits as their interior becomes spongelike and inedible.
wild luffa, edible plants, survival food, tropical vegetables
Identify and Consume Bignay Fruit Safely
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Locate the bignay plant in tropical regions such as rain forests, semievergreen seasonal forests, or cultivated areas.
2. Identify the plant by its shiny, pointed leaves (about 15 cm long) and dark red or black fleshy fruit about...
Procedure:

1. Locate the bignay plant in tropical regions such as rain forests, semievergreen seasonal forests, or cultivated areas.
2. Identify the plant by its shiny, pointed leaves (about 15 cm long) and dark red or black fleshy fruit about 1 cm in diameter.
3. Harvest the fruit directly from the plant.
4. Consume the fruit raw as it is edible and safe for consumption.

Important Warnings:
• Do not eat any other parts of the tree, including roots (which are toxic in Africa) or other sections that may be poisonous.
• Ingesting large quantities of the fruit may cause a laxative effect.

Notes:
• Bignay is commonly found from the Himalayas to Ceylon and eastward through Indonesia to northern Australia.
bignay fruit, foraging, edible plants, tropical survival, laxative effect
Identify and Consume Blueberries and Huckleberries
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Locate blueberry or huckleberry shrubs in open, sunny areas.
2. Identify the plant by its alternate, simple leaves and fruits that may be dark blue, black, or red with many small seeds.
3. Harvest the ripe fruit directly from the...
Procedure:

1. Locate blueberry or huckleberry shrubs in open, sunny areas.
2. Identify the plant by its alternate, simple leaves and fruits that may be dark blue, black, or red with many small seeds.
3. Harvest the ripe fruit directly from the plant.
4. Consume the fruit raw as it is edible and safe for consumption.

Notes:
• These plants are found throughout much of the north temperate regions and at higher elevations in Central America.
blueberry, huckleberry, foraging, edible plants, temperate survival
Identify and Consume Sheep Sorrel Safely
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Locate sheep sorrel (Rumex acerosella), which is seldom more than 30 centimeters tall.
2. Confirm the presence of alternate leaves with arrowlike bases, very small flowers, and frequently reddish stems.
3. Find these plants in old...
Procedure:

1. Locate sheep sorrel (Rumex acerosella), which is seldom more than 30 centimeters tall.
2. Confirm the presence of alternate leaves with arrowlike bases, very small flowers, and frequently reddish stems.
3. Find these plants in old fields and other disturbed areas in North America and Europe.
4. Harvest the plant for consumption, either raw or cooked.

Important Warnings:
• Sheep sorrel contains oxalic acid that can be damaging if too many plants are eaten raw.
• Cooking seems to destroy the chemical, so it is recommended to cook the plant before consuming.
sheep sorrel, oxalic acid, edible plants
Identify and Consume Sorghum Grains for Nutrition
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Locate sorghum (Sorghum species), which is a plant that bears grains in heads at the top of the plants.
2. Confirm the presence of grains that are brown, white, red, or black.
3. Find these plants in warmer climates and open,...
Procedure:

1. Locate sorghum (Sorghum species), which is a plant that bears grains in heads at the top of the plants.
2. Confirm the presence of grains that are brown, white, red, or black.
3. Find these plants in warmer climates and open, sunny areas worldwide.
4. Harvest the grains for consumption:
- When young, the grains are milky and edible raw.
- For older grains, boil them before consuming.

Other Uses:
• Use the stems of tall sorghum as building materials.
sorghum, edible grains, survival food
Identify and Consume Spatterdock or Yellow Water Lily Parts for Nutrition
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Locate spatterdock or yellow water lily (Nuphar species), which has leaves up to 60 centimeters long with a triangular notch at the base.
2. Confirm that the plant's yellow flowers are 2.5 centimeters across and develop into...
Procedure:

1. Locate spatterdock or yellow water lily (Nuphar species), which has leaves up to 60 centimeters long with a triangular notch at the base.
2. Confirm that the plant's yellow flowers are 2.5 centimeters across and develop into bottle-shaped fruits that are green when ripe.
3. Find these plants in quiet, fresh, shallow water (never deeper than 1.8 meters) throughout most of North America.
4. Harvest and consume all parts of the plant:
- The fruits contain several dark brown seeds that can be parched or roasted and then ground into flour.
- The large rootstock contains starch. Dig it out of the mud, peel off the outside, and boil the flesh.

Important Notes:
• Sometimes the rootstock contains a very bitter compound. Boiling in several changes of water may remove the bitterness.
spatterdock, yellow water lily, edible plants
Identify and Consume Sterculia Seeds for Nutrition
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Locate sterculia (Sterculia foetida), which is a tall tree that can rise to 30 meters.
2. Confirm the presence of leaves that are either undivided or palmately lobed and red or purple flowers.
3. Find these trees in warm or...
Procedure:

1. Locate sterculia (Sterculia foetida), which is a tall tree that can rise to 30 meters.
2. Confirm the presence of leaves that are either undivided or palmately lobed and red or purple flowers.
3. Find these trees in warm or tropical climates, where they are mainly forest trees.
4. Harvest the large, red pods for consumption:
- The seeds inside are edible and have a pleasant taste similar to cocoa.
- Eat them like nuts, either raw or roasted.

Important Warnings:
• Avoid eating large quantities of the seeds, as they may have a laxative effect.
sterculia, edible seeds, survival food
Identify and Consume Strawberry Fruits and Leaves for Nutrition
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Locate strawberry (Fragaria species), which is a small plant with a three-leaved growth pattern.
2. Confirm the presence of small, white flowers usually produced during the spring and red, fleshy fruit.
3. Find these plants in the...
Procedure:

1. Locate strawberry (Fragaria species), which is a small plant with a three-leaved growth pattern.
2. Confirm the presence of small, white flowers usually produced during the spring and red, fleshy fruit.
3. Find these plants in the North Temperate Zone and also in the high mountains of the southern Western Hemisphere.
4. Harvest and consume:
- The fruit is edible fresh, cooked, or dried.
- Strawberries are a good source of vitamin C.
- Eat the plant's leaves or dry them and make a tea with them.

Important Warnings:
• Eat only whiteflowering true strawberries. Other similar plants without white flowers can be poisonous.
strawberry, edible fruits, survival food
Identify and Consume Sugarcane for Nutrition and Use in Survival Situations
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Locate sugarcane (Saccharum officinarum), which is a tall, grass-like plant with thick stalks.
2. Confirm the presence of fibrous, juicy stalks that are typically harvested for their sweet sap.
3. Find these plants in tropical and...
Procedure:

1. Locate sugarcane (Saccharum officinarum), which is a tall, grass-like plant with thick stalks.
2. Confirm the presence of fibrous, juicy stalks that are typically harvested for their sweet sap.
3. Find these plants in tropical and subtropical regions where they are commonly grown.
4. Harvest and consume:
- The stalks can be eaten raw or chewed to extract the juice.
- Boil or cook the stalks if necessary for consumption.

Other Uses:
• Sugarcane is a primary source of sugar in many parts of the world.
• It can also be used as a building material or for fuel in survival situations.
sugarcane, edible plants, survival food
Identify and Harvest Beechnuts for Consumption
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Locate beech trees in temperate zones, typically found in moist forest areas.
2. Identify the tree by its smooth, light-gray bark and clusters of prickly seedpods.
3. Collect mature beechnuts that have fallen out of the husklike...
Procedure:

1. Locate beech trees in temperate zones, typically found in moist forest areas.
2. Identify the tree by its smooth, light-gray bark and clusters of prickly seedpods.
3. Collect mature beechnuts that have fallen out of the husklike seedpods.
4. Break the thin shell of each nut with your fingernail to access the white, sweet kernel inside.
5. Consume the kernel directly or use it as a survival food due to its high oil content.

Additional Uses:
• Roast the kernels until they turn golden brown and become hard.
• Pulverize the roasted kernels and use them as a coffee substitute by boiling or steeping in hot water.

Notes:
• Beechnuts are safe to eat and provide valuable nutrition during survival situations.
beechnuts, foraging, edible plants, survival food, coffee substitute
Identify and Harvest Sea Orach Leaves for Consumption
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Locate the sea orach plant, which is a sparingly branched herbaceous plant with small, gray-colored leaves up to 2.5 centimeters long.
2. Confirm that it resembles Iamb's quarter, a common weed in most gardens in the United...
Procedure:

1. Locate the sea orach plant, which is a sparingly branched herbaceous plant with small, gray-colored leaves up to 2.5 centimeters long.
2. Confirm that it resembles Iamb's quarter, a common weed in most gardens in the United States.
3. Look for narrow, densely compacted spikes of flowers at the tips of its branches.
4. Harvest the leaves, which are edible and can be used to sustain man in times of want.

Notes:
• Sea orach is found in highly alkaline and salty areas along seashores from the Mediterranean countries to inland areas in North Africa and eastward to Turkey and central Siberia.
• It can also be found in tropical scrub and thorn forests, steppes in temperate regions, and most desert scrub and waste areas.
sea orach, edible plants, survival food
Identify and Harvest Water Lettuce Leaves Safely
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:
1. Look for water lettuce in still lakes, ponds, and the backwaters of rivers.
2. Identify the plant by its tender, succulent leaves and the small rosette-shaped plantlets growing from the leaf margins.
3. Only harvest leaves that are...
Procedure:
1. Look for water lettuce in still lakes, ponds, and the backwaters of rivers.
2. Identify the plant by its tender, succulent leaves and the small rosette-shaped plantlets growing from the leaf margins.
3. Only harvest leaves that are well out of the water to avoid contamination.
4. Eat the fresh leaves like lettuce.
CAUTION: This plant has carcinogenic properties and should only be used as a last resort.
water lettuce, identification, harvesting, edible plants, safety precautions
Identify and Prepare Yam Bean for Consumption
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Yam bean Pachyrhizus erosus is a climbing plant of the bean family, with alternate, three-parted leaves and a turniplike root. The bluish or purplish flowers are pealike in shape. The plants are often so rampant that they cover the vegetation...
Yam bean Pachyrhizus erosus is a climbing plant of the bean family, with alternate, three-parted leaves and a turniplike root. The bluish or purplish flowers are pealike in shape. The plants are often so rampant that they cover the vegetation upon which they are growing.

Habitat: Native to the American tropics but now commonly cultivated in Asia and the Pacific islands, as well as found growing wild in forested areas of tropical regions. Grows in wet areas of tropical regions.

Edible Parts:
1. The tubers are about the size of a turnip and they are crisp, sweet, and juicy with a nutty flavor. They are nourishing and quench thirst.
2. Eat them raw or boiled.
3. To make flour:
- Slice the raw tubers.
- Let them dry in the sun.
- Grind into a flour that is high in starch and may be used to thicken soup.

CAUTION: The raw seeds are poisonous.
yam bean, identification, preparation, edible plants, flour making
Identify and Use Blackberry, Raspberry, and Dewberry for Food and Medicine
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Locate blackberry, raspberry, or dewberry plants in open, sunny areas at the margin of woods, lakes, streams, and roads in temperate regions.
2. Identify the plant by its prickly stems (canes) that grow upward and arch back toward...
Procedure:

1. Locate blackberry, raspberry, or dewberry plants in open, sunny areas at the margin of woods, lakes, streams, and roads in temperate regions.
2. Identify the plant by its prickly stems (canes) that grow upward and arch back toward the ground, along with alternate, usually compound leaves.
3. Harvest the fruits when they are ripe (may be red, black, yellow, or orange).
4. Consume the fruits raw or use the peeled young shoots as food.

Additional Uses:
• Use dried root bark of the blackberry bush to make a tea for treating diarrhea.
• Brew the dried root bark by steeping it in hot water and drink the resulting tea.

Notes:
• Flavor of fruits varies greatly depending on the species and ripeness.
blackberry, raspberry, dewberry, foraging, diarrhea treatment, edible plants
Identify and Use Breadfruit as a Food Source
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Locate breadfruit trees in the humid tropics at the margins of forests or homesites.
2. Identify the tree by its dark green, deeply divided leaves (75 cm long and 30 cm wide) and large, green, ball-like fruits up to 30 cm across...
Procedure:

1. Locate breadfruit trees in the humid tropics at the margins of forests or homesites.
2. Identify the tree by its dark green, deeply divided leaves (75 cm long and 30 cm wide) and large, green, ball-like fruits up to 30 cm across when mature.
3. Harvest the fruit when it is fully mature.
4. Eat the fruit pulp raw or slice, dry, and grind it into flour for later use.
5. Cook and eat the seeds as a food source.

Additional Uses:
• Use the thick sap of the tree as glue, caulking material, or birdlime to trap small birds by smearing it on twigs where they perch.

Notes:
• Breadfruit is native to the South Pacific region but has been widely planted in the West Indies and parts of Polynesia.
breadfruit, foraging, edible plants, tropical survival, birdlime
Use Crowberries for Nutrition and Survival Purposes
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure to Use Crowberries in Survival Situations:

1. Locate crowberry plants in tundra regions of the Arctic, North America, or Eurasia.
2. Collect the small, shiny black berries that remain on the shrub throughout the winter.
3. **For...
Procedure to Use Crowberries in Survival Situations:

1. Locate crowberry plants in tundra regions of the Arctic, North America, or Eurasia.
2. Collect the small, shiny black berries that remain on the shrub throughout the winter.
3. For Immediate Consumption: Eat the berries fresh if they are available and not frozen solid.
4. For Storage: Dry the berries in the sun or over a fire to preserve them for later use.

Notes:
• Crowberries can be used as a food source during long winters or survival situations in cold climates.
• This procedure assumes access to crowberry plants and basic tools for collection and drying.
crowberry, winter food, tundra plant, survival nutrition
Use Pine Resin for Waterproofing and Glue
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Collect Resin: Gather pine resin from the tree by cutting a notch in the bark if there is not enough naturally exuding sap.
2. Heat Resin: Place the collected resin into a container and heat it until it becomes liquid.
3....
Procedure:

1. Collect Resin: Gather pine resin from the tree by cutting a notch in the bark if there is not enough naturally exuding sap.
2. Heat Resin: Place the collected resin into a container and heat it until it becomes liquid.
3. Apply as Glue: Use the hot resin immediately as glue or mix with a small amount of ash dust to strengthen it before applying.
4. Emergency Dental Filling: Hardened pine resin can be used as an emergency dental filling.

Notes:
• This method is useful in survival situations where traditional materials are unavailable.
pine resin, waterproofing, glue, dental filling, survival use
Use Plantain for Wound and Diarrhea Relief
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Wound Treatment: Wash and soak the entire plant in water briefly, then apply it directly to wounds or sores.
2. Diarrhea Tea: Boil 28 grams (1 ounce) of fresh plant leaves in 0.5 liters of water for tea.
3. **Laxative...
Procedure:

1. Wound Treatment: Wash and soak the entire plant in water briefly, then apply it directly to wounds or sores.
2. Diarrhea Tea: Boil 28 grams (1 ounce) of fresh plant leaves in 0.5 liters of water for tea.
3. Laxative Use: Consume seeds and seed husks as a natural laxative, though caution is advised due to potential effects on the digestive system.

Notes:
• This method can be used in survival or wilderness situations where medical supplies are limited.
plantain wound treatment, plantain tea for diarrhea, laxative use
Use Pokeweed Juice as a Dye
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Collect Fresh Berries: Gather fresh berries from the plant.
2. Extract Juice: Squeeze the juice from the berries to create a natural dye.
3. Apply Dye: Use the extracted juice for coloring fabrics or other...
Procedure:

1. Collect Fresh Berries: Gather fresh berries from the plant.
2. Extract Juice: Squeeze the juice from the berries to create a natural dye.
3. Apply Dye: Use the extracted juice for coloring fabrics or other materials.

Notes:
• This method is useful in survival situations where commercial dyes are unavailable.
pokeweed juice dye, natural dye, survival use
Collect and Prepare Edible Parts of Pine Trees
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Collect Seeds: Gather the seeds from all species of pine trees.
2. Young Male Cones (Spring Only): In spring, collect young male cones and boil or bake them for consumption.
3. Inner Bark of Young Twigs: Peel off the...
Procedure:

1. Collect Seeds: Gather the seeds from all species of pine trees.
2. Young Male Cones (Spring Only): In spring, collect young male cones and boil or bake them for consumption.
3. Inner Bark of Young Twigs: Peel off the bark from thin twigs and chew the juicy inner layer, which is rich in sugar and vitamins.
4. Green Pine Needle Tea: Use fresh green pine needles to make tea by steeping them in hot water; this tea is high in vitamin C.

Notes:
• Seeds can be eaten raw or cooked.
• Avoid consuming any part of the tree that shows signs of being over 25 cm tall or showing red coloration, as these may indicate toxicity.
pine seeds, young cones, inner bark, pine needle tea, survival food
Harvest and Prepare Mulberry Fruit for Consumption
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:
1. Identify the mulberry tree by its alternate, simple, often lobed leaves with rough surfaces and blue or black fruits that are many seeded.
2. Harvest only ripe mulberries (not green, unripe fruit). Ripe fruit is safe to eat raw or...
Procedure:
1. Identify the mulberry tree by its alternate, simple, often lobed leaves with rough surfaces and blue or black fruits that are many seeded.
2. Harvest only ripe mulberries (not green, unripe fruit). Ripe fruit is safe to eat raw or cooked.
3. If consuming in large quantities, be aware that mulberry fruit acts as a laxative.
4. Do not consume green, unripe fruit. Green, unripe fruit can be hallucinogenic and cause extreme nausea and cramps.
5. After harvesting, the fruit can be eaten immediately or dried for later consumption.

Other Uses:
• The inner bark of the tree can be shredded to make twine or cord.
mulberry,harvest,prepare fruit,consumption
Harvest and Use Date Palm Fruits and Other Parts
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
To use date palm:

1. Eat ripe fruit: Consume fresh when ripe (yellow). Avoid eating unripe fruit as it is very bitter.
2. Dry the fruits: Sun-dry them for long-term preservation.
3. Use trunks for building material: The trunks are...
To use date palm:

1. Eat ripe fruit: Consume fresh when ripe (yellow). Avoid eating unripe fruit as it is very bitter.
2. Dry the fruits: Sun-dry them for long-term preservation.
3. Use trunks for building material: The trunks are valuable in desert regions where few other trees exist.
4. Use leaves for thatching and weaving: Leaves are durable and can be used as weaving material or for scrubbing and cleaning (base of the leaves resembles coarse cloth).

Note: Date palms grow in arid semitropical regions, native to North Africa and the Middle East.
date palm, edible parts, desert survival, building material
Harvest and Use Elderberry Parts for Food and Drink
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
To use elderberry:

1. Eat the flowers and fruits: Both are edible.
2. Make a drink from the flowers:
- Soak flower heads in water for 8 hours.
- Discard the flowers.
- Drink the liquid.

CAUTION: All other parts of the plant...
To use elderberry:

1. Eat the flowers and fruits: Both are edible.
2. Make a drink from the flowers:
- Soak flower heads in water for 8 hours.
- Discard the flowers.
- Drink the liquid.

CAUTION: All other parts of the plant (leaves, stems, bark) are poisonous and dangerous if eaten.

Note: Elderberry grows in open, usually wet areas at the margins of marshes, rivers, ditches, and lakes. It is found throughout much of eastern North America and Canada.
elderberry, edible parts, elderberry drink, survival food
Identify and Harvest Burdock for Edible Use
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Identify burdock by its wavy-edged, arrow-shaped leaves and flower heads in burrlike clusters.
2. Look for it in open waste areas during the spring and summer.
3. Peel the tender leaf stalks and eat them raw or cook them like...
Procedure:

1. Identify burdock by its wavy-edged, arrow-shaped leaves and flower heads in burrlike clusters.
2. Look for it in open waste areas during the spring and summer.
3. Peel the tender leaf stalks and eat them raw or cook them like greens.
4. Harvest the large, fleshy root and boil or bake it for consumption.

CAUTION: Do not confuse burdock with rhubarb that has poisonous leaves.

Other Uses:
• A liquid made from the roots will help to produce sweating and increase urination.
- Dry the root.
- Simmer it in water.
- Strain the liquid.
- Drink the strained liquid.
• Use the fiber from the dried stalk to weave cordage.
burdock,identify,burdock root,edible plants,harvesting
Identify and Harvest Canna Lily for Edible Use
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Identify the canna lily by its large leaves resembling those of the banana plant but not as large.
2. Look for it in all tropical areas, especially in moist places along streams, springs, ditches, and the margins of woods.
3....
Procedure:

1. Identify the canna lily by its large leaves resembling those of the banana plant but not as large.
2. Look for it in all tropical areas, especially in moist places along streams, springs, ditches, and the margins of woods.
3. Harvest the large and much branched rootstocks which are full of edible starch.
4. Finely chop the younger parts and boil or pulverize into a meal.
5. Mix in the young shoots of palm cabbage for flavoring.
canna lily,identify,canna rootstock,harvesting
Identify and Harvest Cashew Nut for Edible Use
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Identify the cashew by its spreading evergreen tree with leaves up to 20 centimeters long and 10 centimeters wide, yellowish-pink flowers, and a peculiar fruit structure.
2. Look for it in tropical climates, originally native to...
Procedure:

1. Identify the cashew by its spreading evergreen tree with leaves up to 20 centimeters long and 10 centimeters wide, yellowish-pink flowers, and a peculiar fruit structure.
2. Look for it in tropical climates, originally native to the West Indies and northern South America but now found in Africa and India.
3. Harvest the nut encloses one seed and roast it for consumption.
4. Eat the pear-shaped fruit which is juicy, sweet-acid, and astringent.

CAUTION: The green hull surrounding the nut contains a resinous irritant poison that will blister the lips and tongue like poison ivy. Heat destroys this poison when roasting the nuts.
cashew,identify,cashew nut,harvesting
Use Edible Parts of Broad and Narrow Leaf Plantain
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Young Leaves: Eat the young, tender leaves raw.
2. Older Leaves: Cook older leaves before consuming them to reduce bitterness.
3. Seeds: Consume seeds raw or roast them for a different flavor.

Notes:
• These...
Procedure:

1. Young Leaves: Eat the young, tender leaves raw.
2. Older Leaves: Cook older leaves before consuming them to reduce bitterness.
3. Seeds: Consume seeds raw or roast them for a different flavor.

Notes:
• These plants are valuable in survival situations due to their widespread availability and nutritional value.
plantain leaves, plantain seeds, edible parts, survival food
Use Edible Parts of Prickly Pear Cactus Safely
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Fruits: Peel the fruits and eat them fresh or crush them to make a refreshing drink.
2. Seeds: Roast the seeds and grind them into flour for use in cooking.
3. Avoid Toxic Variants: Avoid any prickly pear cactus-like...
Procedure:

1. Fruits: Peel the fruits and eat them fresh or crush them to make a refreshing drink.
2. Seeds: Roast the seeds and grind them into flour for use in cooking.
3. Avoid Toxic Variants: Avoid any prickly pear cactus-like plant that exudes milky sap, as these may be toxic.

Notes:
• Be cautious of the tiny, pointed hairs on the stems; avoid contact with skin or mouth to prevent injury.
• The pads can also be used for hydration and wound healing when prepared properly.
prickly pear cactus edible parts, fruit preparation, seed flour, safety precautions
Use Purslane as Edible Food and for Health Benefits
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Edible Parts: Consume the entire plant, including leaves, stems, and flowers.
2. Raw or Cooked Consumption: Eat purslane raw in salads or cook it by steaming, boiling, or sautéing.
3. Nutritional Benefits: Purslane is...
Procedure:

1. Edible Parts: Consume the entire plant, including leaves, stems, and flowers.
2. Raw or Cooked Consumption: Eat purslane raw in salads or cook it by steaming, boiling, or sautéing.
3. Nutritional Benefits: Purslane is rich in omega-3 fatty acids, vitamins A and C, and minerals like magnesium and iron.

Notes:
• This plant is a valuable source of nutrition in survival situations due to its widespread availability.
purslane edible uses, omega-3 content, survival food
Harvest and Use Daylily Parts for Food and Other Purposes
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
To use daylilies:

1. Eat young green leaves: Consume raw or cooked.
2. Eat tubers: Consume raw or cooked.
3. Eat flowers: Eat raw, but they taste better when cooked. Fry the flowers for storage.

CAUTION: Eating excessive amounts of...
To use daylilies:

1. Eat young green leaves: Consume raw or cooked.
2. Eat tubers: Consume raw or cooked.
3. Eat flowers: Eat raw, but they taste better when cooked. Fry the flowers for storage.

CAUTION: Eating excessive amounts of raw flowers may cause diarrhea.

Note: Daylilies are found worldwide in tropical and temperate zones.
daylily, edible parts, survival food, daylily uses
Harvest and Use Duchesnea (Indian Strawberry) Fruits for Food
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
To use duchesnea:

1. Eat the fruit: Consume fresh.

Note: The plant is native to southern Asia but is a common weed in warmer temperate regions, found in lawns, gardens, and along roads.
duchesnea, indian strawberry, edible fruit, survival food
Identify Broad and Narrow Leaf Plantain (Plantago species)
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Look for plants with a spike of flowers rising from the middle of a cluster of leaves.
2. Narrow Leaf Plantain: Identify by its long, narrow leaves (up to 12 cm in length and 2.5 cm wide), covered with hairs, forming a...
Procedure:

1. Look for plants with a spike of flowers rising from the middle of a cluster of leaves.
2. Narrow Leaf Plantain: Identify by its long, narrow leaves (up to 12 cm in length and 2.5 cm wide), covered with hairs, forming a rosette.
3. Observe small, inconspicuous flowers on the spike.

Notes:
• This plant is commonly found in lawns and along roads in the North Temperate Zone.
• It is a widespread weed throughout much of the world.
plantain, identification, narrow leaf, broad leaf, survival plant
Identify Pine Trees by Needle Bundles and Sap
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Look for trees with needlelike leaves grouped in bundles.
2. Count the number of needles per bundle, which can range from 1 to 5 depending on the species.
3. Smell the tree; pines have a distinct odor that helps distinguish them...
Procedure:

1. Look for trees with needlelike leaves grouped in bundles.
2. Count the number of needles per bundle, which can range from 1 to 5 depending on the species.
3. Smell the tree; pines have a distinct odor that helps distinguish them from other conifers.
4. Check for sticky sap on the bark and branches, another characteristic feature of pine trees.

Notes:
• This method works well in open, sunny areas where pines are commonly found.
• Use this identification technique to locate edible parts such as seeds, young cones, and inner bark.
pine tree, needle bundles, identification, sap, survival food
Identify Pokeweed (Phytolacca americana)
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Look for a plant that can grow up to 3 meters tall with elliptic leaves up to 1 meter in length.
2. Observe large clusters of purple fruits appearing in late spring.

Notes:
• This plant is commonly found in open, sunny areas...
Procedure:

1. Look for a plant that can grow up to 3 meters tall with elliptic leaves up to 1 meter in length.
2. Observe large clusters of purple fruits appearing in late spring.

Notes:
• This plant is commonly found in open, sunny areas such as forest clearings, fields, and roadsides in eastern North America, Central America, and the Caribbean.
pokeweed identification, purple fruits, survival plant
Identify Purslane (Portulaca oleracea)
Reference Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Look for a plant with small, fleshy leaves and yellow flowers.
2. Observe the succulent nature of the stems and leaves, which are typically green and slightly rounded.
3. The plant often grows in dry or disturbed areas such as...
Procedure:

1. Look for a plant with small, fleshy leaves and yellow flowers.
2. Observe the succulent nature of the stems and leaves, which are typically green and slightly rounded.
3. The plant often grows in dry or disturbed areas such as fields, roadsides, and gardens.

Notes:
• Purslane is a common edible plant found in many regions around the world.
purslane identification, succulent leaves, survival plant

Herbal Preparations (41) -

Prepare Herbal Tinctures for Medicinal Use
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Purpose: Create a potent alcohol-based extract of dried herbs for long-term medicinal use.

Materials Needed:
• Quart canning jars with lids
• Dried herbs/botanicals (chopped root or crushed leaf)
• At least 90 proof Vodka or Everclear
-...
Purpose: Create a potent alcohol-based extract of dried herbs for long-term medicinal use.

Materials Needed:
• Quart canning jars with lids
• Dried herbs/botanicals (chopped root or crushed leaf)
• At least 90 proof Vodka or Everclear
• Dark bottles or jars for storage
• Distilled water (for nasal spray option)
• Nasal spray bottles (optional)

Procedure:
1. Fill a quart jar about one-third full with dried herbs, chopped root, or crushed leaf.
2. Pour vodka or Everclear into the jar until it reaches the "shoulder" of the jar.
3. Secure the lid tightly and shake the jar to mix the contents.
4. Store the jar in a dark, cool place.
5. Shake the jar every 2nd or 3rd day for 10–14 days.
6. After 10–14 days, strain the liquid into dark bottles or jars using a fine mesh strainer or cheesecloth.
7. Cap the bottles tightly and label them with the herb name and date of preparation.

Dosage:
• Commonly accepted dosage is 1–2 eye droppers full (approximately 1/2 teaspoon) per use.
• Place half to one teaspoon of tincture in a glass of water and drink.
• Alternatively, fill gelatine capsules with the tincture for easy consumption.

Optional Nasal Spray Preparation:
1. Purchase empty 1 oz (30 ml) nasal spray bottles from a drug store.
2. Add 8–10 drops of tincture to each bottle.
3. Fill the remaining space in the bottle with distilled water.
4. Use as needed, applying 1–2 sprays per nostril.

Notes:
• Avoid using other alcohol liquors or whiskey for tincturing due to potential interference with medicinal potency.
• Store tinctures in dark bottles to preserve potency and avoid light degradation.
herbal tincture preparation, alcohol extraction, medicinal potency, herbal storage
General Rules for the Distillation of Spirituous Waters
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Rules:
1. The plants and their parts should be moderately and newly dried, except those that are specifically ordered to be fresh.
2. After steeping ingredients in spirit for the prescribed time, add enough water to prevent a burnt flavor or...
Rules:
1. The plants and their parts should be moderately and newly dried, except those that are specifically ordered to be fresh.
2. After steeping ingredients in spirit for the prescribed time, add enough water to prevent a burnt flavor or more if needed.
3. The first liquor obtained during distillation is sometimes kept separately as 'spirit,' while the subsequent runnings may be fined down by art. However, it is preferable to mix all runnings together without fining them, ensuring that the waters retain the full virtues of the plant.
4. For spirituous water preparation, genuine brandy from wine is preferred. If unavailable, use half the quantity of well-rectified spirit made from other fermented liquors. Steep ingredients in this spirit and add spring-water to make up the required volume and prevent burning.
distillation rules,spirituous water,herbal preparation
Prepare Bergamot Water
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 2 gallons of fine old French brandy or 1 gallon of highly rectified spirit of wine
• 1 gallon of spring-water
• 3 oz. (or more) true Roman oil of bergamot
• Lump-sugar for trituration

Procedure:
1. Take the brandy or diluted...
Ingredients:
• 2 gallons of fine old French brandy or 1 gallon of highly rectified spirit of wine
• 1 gallon of spring-water
• 3 oz. (or more) true Roman oil of bergamot
• Lump-sugar for trituration

Procedure:
1. Take the brandy or diluted spirits and add 3 oz. (or more) of true Roman oil of bergamot.
2. Triturate the oil with lump-sugar in a glass mortar until well divided.
3. Add this mixture to the brandy or spirit.
4. Distill by water heat, using an alembic.
5. Draw off only 6 quarts of liquid.
6. This produces excellent bergamot water that can remain good for up to 20 years.
bergamot water,spirituous preparation,distillation
Prepare Common Distilled Water for Medicine Preparations
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 10 gallons of water

Procedure:
1. Take 10 gallons of water and distill it.
2. Discard the first 3 gallons of distillate, as they may contain impurities.
3. Draw off 4 gallons of distilled water.
4. Store the distilled water...
Ingredients:
• 10 gallons of water

Procedure:
1. Take 10 gallons of water and distill it.
2. Discard the first 3 gallons of distillate, as they may contain impurities.
3. Draw off 4 gallons of distilled water.
4. Store the distilled water in glass or stoneware containers.
5. This type of water is used when preparing medicines to avoid decomposition caused by salts in common water.
distilled water,medicine preparation,purification
Prepare French Hungary Water from Rosemary Flowers
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Wine spirit
• Rosemary flowers (from Montpellier)

Procedure:
1. Use wine spirit and rosemary flowers, which are abundant in Montpellier.
2. The fragrancy of these flowers is so great that the resulting waters are more...
Ingredients:
• Wine spirit
• Rosemary flowers (from Montpellier)

Procedure:
1. Use wine spirit and rosemary flowers, which are abundant in Montpellier.
2. The fragrancy of these flowers is so great that the resulting waters are more excellent and valuable than similar preparations made elsewhere.
3. Prepare the water using only rosemary flowers and wine spirit.
french hungary water,rosemary,wine spirit
Prepare Herbal Capsules for Easy Consumption
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Purpose: Create easy-to-consume herbal capsules using dried herbs.

Materials Needed:
• Empty gelatine capsules (available in health food stores)
• Finely crushed or powdered dried herb

Procedure:
1. Purchase empty gelatine capsules...
Purpose: Create easy-to-consume herbal capsules using dried herbs.

Materials Needed:
• Empty gelatine capsules (available in health food stores)
• Finely crushed or powdered dried herb

Procedure:
1. Purchase empty gelatine capsules from a health food store, either individually or by the bag of 1000.
2. Carefully pop open each capsule to access the larger section.
3. Fill the larger portion with finely crushed (powdered) dried herb.
4. Reassemble the capsule and close it securely.
5. Take the capsule with water as needed for consumption.

Notes:
• Ensure that the herbs are fully powdered for even distribution in each capsule.
• Store filled capsules in a cool, dry place to maintain potency.
herbal capsule preparation, easy consumption, herbal storage
Prepare Herbal Salve Using Oil Infusion and Beeswax
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Purpose: Create a solid salve from an oil infusion for topical application.

Materials Needed:
• Oil infusion (prepared as above)
• Glass or stainless steel cooking pot
• Chopped or shaved beeswax
• Saucer
• Appropriate containers for...
Purpose: Create a solid salve from an oil infusion for topical application.

Materials Needed:
• Oil infusion (prepared as above)
• Glass or stainless steel cooking pot
• Chopped or shaved beeswax
• Saucer
• Appropriate containers for storage

Procedure:
1. Place the oil infusion in a glass or stainless steel cooking pot.
2. Heat the oil gently, ensuring it does not overheat.
3. Add 2 oz of chopped or shaved beeswax per cup of oil.
4. Stir until the beeswax is fully melted.
5. Test the consistency by placing a few drops on a saucer and letting it cool:
- If too soft, add more beeswax to the pot.
- If too hard, add a bit more oil to the pot.
6. The ideal salve should remain slightly firm when pressed with a finger but soften quickly from body heat.
7. Pour the mixture into appropriate containers for storage and use as needed.

Notes:
• This is an effective method for creating long-lasting, portable salves.
• Ensure all materials are clean to avoid contamination.
herbal salve preparation, beeswax ratio, oil infusion hardening, salve consistency test, topical application
Prepare Herbal Steams for Inhalation Therapy
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Purpose: Use herbal steams to relieve respiratory issues through inhalation.

Materials Needed:
• Dried, crushed herb (not powdered)
• Fresh whole leaf herb (if available)
• Large pot (stew or spaghetti pot; avoid aluminium)
• Towel
-...
Purpose: Use herbal steams to relieve respiratory issues through inhalation.

Materials Needed:
• Dried, crushed herb (not powdered)
• Fresh whole leaf herb (if available)
• Large pot (stew or spaghetti pot; avoid aluminium)
• Towel
• Water

Procedure:
1. Place 1–2 handfuls of dried, crushed herb or fresh whole leaf into a large pot filled with water.
2. Bring the water to a boil with the herbs in the water.
3. Cover your head and the pot with a towel.
4. Hang your head over the pot and breathe deeply to inhale the steam.
5. Take sensible precautions to avoid burns from the pot, water, or steam.
6. When no steam is rising, reheat the water to boiling and repeat the treatment.
7. Continue adding herbs when they no longer have a scent in the steam until desired relief is achieved.

Warnings:
• Avoid burns by ensuring safe distance from the pot.
• Do not use aluminium pots as they may react with the herbs or release harmful substances.
herbal steam, inhalation therapy, respiratory relief, herbal preparation
Prepare Hungary Water (Original Receipt)
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 3 parts of aqua vitae (four times distilled)
• 2 parts of tops and flowers of rosemary

Procedure:
1. Combine the ingredients in a close-stopped vessel.
2. Allow the mixture to stand in a warm place for 50 hours.
3. Distill...
Ingredients:
• 3 parts of aqua vitae (four times distilled)
• 2 parts of tops and flowers of rosemary

Procedure:
1. Combine the ingredients in a close-stopped vessel.
2. Allow the mixture to stand in a warm place for 50 hours.
3. Distill the mixture in an alembic.
4. Take 1 dr. (approximately 1/8 of a fluid ounce) once every week in the morning, either with food or drink.
5. Wash the face and diseased limb with this preparation each morning.
hungary water,aqua vitae,rosemary,distillation

Prepare a Decoction for Compresses and Syrups
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Purpose: Create a strong decoction from dried herbs for use in compresses or syrups.

Materials Needed:
• Dried, crushed (not powdered) herb
• Water
• Cooking pot
• Straining cloth
• Honey (optional)

Procedure:
1. Use approximately a...
Purpose: Create a strong decoction from dried herbs for use in compresses or syrups.

Materials Needed:
• Dried, crushed (not powdered) herb
• Water
• Cooking pot
• Straining cloth
• Honey (optional)

Procedure:
1. Use approximately a heaping palmful of dried, crushed (not powdered) herb per pint of water.
2. Boil the herb and water together for about 15 minutes.
3. Let the mixture cool to room temperature.
4. Strain the liquid through a straining cloth to remove any solid particles.
5. Add sufficient water to bring the volume back to one pint if needed.
6. For compresses: Soak a sterile dressing in the decoction and apply it to the affected area of the body.
7. For syrup: Add enough honey to thicken the liquid and improve palatability.

Notes:
• This method is suitable for individuals with no taste buds, as the decoction can be consumed directly.
• The resulting syrup is an effective treatment for sore throats.
decoction preparation, herbal compress, syrup making, honey addition, sore throat treatment
Prepare an Oil Infusion for Topical Use
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Purpose: Create a topical oil infusion using dried herbs for skin infections, burns, and dry skin.

Materials Needed:
• Dried herbs (crushed, not powdered)
• Olive oil
• Glass baking pan
• Lightweight fabric or cover
• Straining cloth with...
Purpose: Create a topical oil infusion using dried herbs for skin infections, burns, and dry skin.

Materials Needed:
• Dried herbs (crushed, not powdered)
• Olive oil
• Glass baking pan
• Lightweight fabric or cover
• Straining cloth with tight weave
• Storage bottle

Procedure:
1. Crush the dried herbs (not powder) and place them in a glass baking pan.
2. Cover the bottom of the pan thinly with herbs, then pour olive oil over them until they are fully submerged, leaving at least 1/2 inch of oil above the herbs.
3. Stir to ensure all herbs are coated with oil.
4. Option A: Leave the pan in a gas stove oven overnight with the pilot light on.
5. Option B: Place the pan in direct sunlight for about 2 weeks, covering it with lightweight fabric to protect from bugs.
6. After infusion is complete, strain the oil through a tightly woven cloth into a storage bottle.
7. Use as needed topically for skin infections, burns, or dry skin.

Notes:
• Olive oil does not go rancid quickly and can be prepared in advance.
• This method is ideal for long-term storage without refrigeration.
oil infusion, herbal oil preparation, skin treatment, olive oil storage, sun infusion method, oven infusion method

2. Allow the mixture to digest thoroughly.
3. Draw off the liquid using a gentle fire.

Prepare Medicinal Tea Using a Tea Ball
Reference Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Purpose: Create a potent medicinal tea using dried herbs.

Materials Needed:
• Silvery tea ball
• Crushed (not powdered) dried herb
• Boiling water
• Cup
• Honey (optional)

Procedure:
1. Stuff the silvery tea ball full with crushed, not...
Purpose: Create a potent medicinal tea using dried herbs.

Materials Needed:
• Silvery tea ball
• Crushed (not powdered) dried herb
• Boiling water
• Cup
• Honey (optional)

Procedure:
1. Stuff the silvery tea ball full with crushed, not powdered, dried herb.
2. Place the tea ball in a cup.
3. Pour boiling water into the cup until it is filled.
4. Cover the cup if possible to retain heat and steeping effectiveness.
5. Let the tea steep for about 10 minutes.
6. If desired, add honey as a sweetener.
7. Sip the tea slowly.

Dosage:
• A standard dosage is one cup of tea 3–4 times per day.
• Adjust based on the herb used and individual needs.

Notes:
• Medicinal teas must be strong to be effective, but they often taste very bad.
• For infants or children, use a milder herbal tea with less strength for better palatability.
tea preparation, herbal tea, medicinal infusion, steeping, honey addition, dosage for adults, dosage for children

Prepare a Quart Jar of Medicinal Tea for Daily Use
Reference Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Purpose: Prepare a large batch of medicinal tea for repeated use throughout the day.

Materials Needed:
• Quart jar
• Silvery tea balls or loose dried herbs
• Boiling water
• Straining cloth (if using loose herbs)

Procedure:
1. Use one...
Purpose: Prepare a large batch of medicinal tea for repeated use throughout the day.

Materials Needed:
• Quart jar
• Silvery tea balls or loose dried herbs
• Boiling water
• Straining cloth (if using loose herbs)

Procedure:
1. Use one tea ball per cup of water, placing them in a quart jar.
2. Pour boiling water into the jar until it is filled to the top.
3. If using loose herbs instead of tea balls, strain the herbs after steeping to remove any solid particles.
4. The tea can be used as needed throughout the day.

Storage:
• Without refrigeration: Keep safely for up to 24 hours.
• With refrigeration: Store for 2–3 days for extended freshness.

Notes:
• This method is ideal for individuals who need frequent access to medicinal tea.
• Ensure the jar is sealed tightly after preparation.
batch tea preparation, quart jar storage, daily use, medicinal infusion, loose herbs straining
Extract Liquorice Root into Paste
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Extracting Liquorice:

1. Boiling the root: Take liquorice root and boil it in eight times its weight of water, reducing the volume to one half.
2. Expressing the liquid: After boiling, express (press) the liquid from the...
Procedure for Extracting Liquorice:

1. Boiling the root: Take liquorice root and boil it in eight times its weight of water, reducing the volume to one half.
2. Expressing the liquid: After boiling, express (press) the liquid from the root.
3. Filtering: Allow the feces (sediment) to settle, then filter the liquid.
4. Evaporation: Evaporate the filtrate with a heat between 200° and 212° until it becomes thick-ish.
5. Further evaporation: Continue evaporating at a heat less than 200°, frequently stirring, until it reaches a consistence suitable for forming pills.
6. Forming into pastils or cakes: Shape the extract into small pastils or flat cakes, often bearing the impression of the places where they are made.
7. Usage: Take a bit now and then and put it into the mouth to relieve coughs; dissolve slowly in the mouth for best results.
liquorice, extract, cough remedy, herbal preparation
Make Cinnamon Water via Simmering and Distillation
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required: Bruised cinnamon (1 lb.), water (2 gallons)

Procedure:
1. Place bruised cinnamon in a still with 2 gallons of water.
2. Simmer the mixture for half an hour.
3. Collect the liquid that condenses and pour it back into the...
Materials Required: Bruised cinnamon (1 lb.), water (2 gallons)

Procedure:
1. Place bruised cinnamon in a still with 2 gallons of water.
2. Simmer the mixture for half an hour.
3. Collect the liquid that condenses and pour it back into the still.
4. Once cooled, strain the mixture through flannel to obtain the final product.
cinnamon,water,simmering,distillation
Make Jessamine Water via Infusion and Filtration
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required: White sweet almond cakes (6 lbs.), fresh oil of jessamine, spring water (6 quarts), cotton, glass bottle

Procedure:
1. Beat and sift 6 lbs. of white sweet almond cakes into a fine powder.
2. Mix the powder with enough...
Materials Required: White sweet almond cakes (6 lbs.), fresh oil of jessamine, spring water (6 quarts), cotton, glass bottle

Procedure:
1. Beat and sift 6 lbs. of white sweet almond cakes into a fine powder.
2. Mix the powder with enough fresh oil of jessamine to create a stiff paste.
3. Add 6 quarts of previously boiled spring water that has been cooled to about half its original temperature.
4. Stir and mix well until the oil and water are fully combined.
5. Allow the mixture to stand until the powder settles at the bottom of the vessel.
6. Carefully pour off the liquid, filtering it through cotton in a large tin funnel into a glass bottle for storage.
7. The remaining powder can be dried under sunlight and used to make almond paste for the hands.
jessamine,water,almond,cotton,filtration
Make Myrtle Water via Infusion and Distillation
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required: Green myrtle cuttings (8-10 lbs.), rain or river water (20 gallons), fresh yeast (1 pint), bay-salt (1 lb.)

Procedure:
1. Infuse 8-10 lbs. of green myrtle cuttings in nearly 20 gallons of rain or river water.
2. Add 1...
Materials Required: Green myrtle cuttings (8-10 lbs.), rain or river water (20 gallons), fresh yeast (1 pint), bay-salt (1 lb.)

Procedure:
1. Infuse 8-10 lbs. of green myrtle cuttings in nearly 20 gallons of rain or river water.
2. Add 1 pint of fresh yeast after the mixture has stood for 24 hours.
3. After another day and night, place the mixture into a still with 1 lb. of bay-salt.
4. Draw off all the liquid from the still.
5. The next day, add more myrtle leaves as before and distill again.
6. Repeat this process a third time to obtain the final product.
myrtle,water,infusion,distillation,bay-salt
Make Orange-Peel Water via Distillation
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required: Outward yellow rind of Seville oranges (4 oz.), water (3 1/2 gallons)

Procedure:
1. Place the outward yellow rind of 4 oz. of Seville oranges in a distillation apparatus.
2. Add 3 1/2 gallons of water to the still.
3....
Materials Required: Outward yellow rind of Seville oranges (4 oz.), water (3 1/2 gallons)

Procedure:
1. Place the outward yellow rind of 4 oz. of Seville oranges in a distillation apparatus.
2. Add 3 1/2 gallons of water to the still.
3. Distill using an alembic with a brisk fire until 1 gallon of liquid is collected.
orange-peel,water,distillation,alembic
Make Rosemary Water Using Cold Distillation
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required: Fresh rosemary with morning dew

Procedure:
1. Place fresh rosemary on the plate or bottom of the still, ensuring it is unbruised.
2. Cover the plate with its conical head and attach a glass receiver to the nose of the...
Materials Required: Fresh rosemary with morning dew

Procedure:
1. Place fresh rosemary on the plate or bottom of the still, ensuring it is unbruised.
2. Cover the plate with its conical head and attach a glass receiver to the nose of the still.
3. Create a small fire using charcoal under the plate.
4. Continue heating until liquid begins to condense into the receiver.
5. When no more liquid comes over, remove the still head and replace the rosemary with fresh plant material.
6. Repeat this process successively until you have obtained a sufficient quantity of water.
7. Store the distilled water in clean bottles that are tightly sealed.
8. Keep the bottles in a cold place for several days to allow the water to become clear and fully infused with the taste and smell of rosemary.
rosemary,water,distillation,cold still

Materials Required: Dry pennyroyal leaves (1 1/2 lbs.), water

Procedure:
