- Keep a record of herbal usage to identify patterns or issues that may arise over time.

4. Interactions with Conventional Medications:
- Be cautious about combining herbs with prescription medications, as interactions can reduce the effectiveness of either treatment or cause harmful side effects.
- If on medication, consult a healthcare provider before using any herbal remedy.

Notes:
• Always use herbs in moderation and follow recommended dosages from reliable sources.
• In survival situations, avoid experimenting with unfamiliar plants unless you are certain of their safety and properties.
botanical misuse, herbal safety, contraindications, interactions
Harvest and Use Echinacea for Immune Support and Infections
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Echinacea (Echinacea angustifolia & Echinacea purpurea)

Steps:
1. Harvest Echinacea angustifolia roots in early winter after the plant has died back.
2. Harvest Echinacea purpurea flower heads with seeds before petals drop in late...
Echinacea (Echinacea angustifolia & Echinacea purpurea)

Steps:
1. Harvest Echinacea angustifolia roots in early winter after the plant has died back.
2. Harvest Echinacea purpurea flower heads with seeds before petals drop in late summer.
3. Prepare tincture, water infusion, oil infusion, decoction, poultice, compress, bolus, syrup, or capsule from both varieties.
4. Use internally for immune support: take a dropper full (1/2 tsp) daily for 10 days, then rest for 7 days, and repeat as needed.
5. Apply tincture to treat gingivitis by soaking a cotton ball in the decoction and applying it directly to the gums twice daily until resolved.
6. Use internally or externally for athlete’s foot, bladder infections, bursitis/tendinitis, Lyme disease, pneumonia, sinusitis, tonsillitis, viral infections, and yeast infections.

Benefits:
• Stimulates immune response system.
• Anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties.
• Effective against influenza, herpes, and common cold viruses.
echinacea, immune support, infections, gingivitis, flu, cold, tincture, poultice
Use Cardon Cactus to Clean and Stop Bleeding from Wounds
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Objective: Use the juice of the Cardon cactus to clean wounds and stop bleeding in emergency situations.

Steps:
1. Cut a piece of Cardon cactus (Pachycerius pectin-aboriginum) with a clean knife.
2. Squeeze the juice from the cut...
Objective: Use the juice of the Cardon cactus to clean wounds and stop bleeding in emergency situations.

Steps:
1. Cut a piece of Cardon cactus (Pachycerius pectin-aboriginum) with a clean knife.
2. Squeeze the juice from the cut section and apply it directly to the wound using your fingers or a clean cloth.
3. Press the cactus firmly against the wound until bleeding is under control.
4. Once bleeding has stopped, tie a piece of the cactus to the wound with a strip of cloth.
5. Leave the cactus in place for 2–3 hours, then remove it and clean the wound with boiled water and soap.

Additional Instructions:
• For more detailed instructions on wound care, refer to pages 82 to 87 in the book.

Warnings:
• Ensure that the cactus is not contaminated or damaged before use.
• If the wound shows signs of infection (e.g., redness, swelling, pus), seek medical attention.
cardon cactus, wound care, natural remedy, bleeding control, first aid
Utilize Herbal Remedies for Medical Emergencies
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Procedure:

1. Research and identify medicinal plants that are commonly used in survival situations (e.g., echinacea, yarrow, plantain).
2. Acquire knowledge on how to prepare herbal remedies such as tinctures, poultices, or teas.
3....
Procedure:

1. Research and identify medicinal plants that are commonly used in survival situations (e.g., echinacea, yarrow, plantain).
2. Acquire knowledge on how to prepare herbal remedies such as tinctures, poultices, or teas.
3. Store dried herbs in airtight containers away from moisture and light.
4. Include herbal remedy guides like *American Medicinal Plants* or *The Practical Encyclopedia of Natural Healing* in your survival library.
5. Use herbal remedies only under the guidance of a physician, especially if you have pre-existing medical conditions.
6. Test any new herb on a small area of skin before applying it more broadly to avoid allergic reactions.
7. Document all herbs used and their effects, including dosages and preparation methods, for future reference.
8. Combine herbal remedies with conventional medicines when appropriate, ensuring they do not interact negatively.

Notes:
• Herbal remedies can be useful in situations where professional medical care is unavailable but should never replace necessary medical treatment.
• Some herbs may have contraindications or interactions with other medications, so caution and research are essential.
herbal remedies, medicinal plants, natural healing, survival medicine, first aid
Harvest and Use Dandelion for Liver Health and Diuretic Effects
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Dandelion (Taraxacum officinale)

Steps:
1. Harvest 2nd year roots in the spring or fall; young leaves in the spring for fresh eating.
2. Prepare a tincture, water infusion, or syrup from the root or leaves.
3. Internally, use as a...
Dandelion (Taraxacum officinale)

Steps:
1. Harvest 2nd year roots in the spring or fall; young leaves in the spring for fresh eating.
2. Prepare a tincture, water infusion, or syrup from the root or leaves.
3. Internally, use as a potent diuretic (tincture or water infusion).
4. Use dandelion root to treat jaundice due to its alterative effect on the liver.
5. Apply dandelion syrup for tonsillitis discomfort.

Benefits:
• Treats jaundice and supports liver function.
• Acts as a powerful diuretic.
dandelion, liver, jaundice, diuretic, tonsillitis, syrup
Harvest and Use Mullein for Respiratory and Allergy Relief
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Mullein (Verbascum thapsus)

Steps:
1. Harvest leaves before flowering; take no more than 1/3 of the total.
2. Prepare tincture, decoction, water infusion, or syrup from the leaves.
3. Use internally for respiratory complaints: expectorant...
Mullein (Verbascum thapsus)

Steps:
1. Harvest leaves before flowering; take no more than 1/3 of the total.
2. Prepare tincture, decoction, water infusion, or syrup from the leaves.
3. Use internally for respiratory complaints: expectorant action, soothes throat, and helps stop muscle spasms that trigger coughs.
4. Apply as a protective coating on mucous membranes to reduce airborne allergen effects.
5. Take 1 tsp dose to protect against allergy symptoms for 4-5 hours with no negative side effects.

Benefits:
• Helps relieve respiratory issues (cough, bronchitis).
• May act as a painkiller and sleep inducer without being habit-forming or poisonous.
mullein, respiratory, allergy relief, expectorant, cough, bronchitis
Harvest and Use Plantain for Skin Healing and Hemorrhoid Relief
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Plantain (Plantago major)

Steps:
1. Harvest leaves before seeds form in early summer.
2. Prepare fresh leaves, poultice, or salve from the plant.
3. Apply a salve or compress to reduce hemorrhoid swelling and pain.
4. Crush fresh leaf and...
Plantain (Plantago major)

Steps:
1. Harvest leaves before seeds form in early summer.
2. Prepare fresh leaves, poultice, or salve from the plant.
3. Apply a salve or compress to reduce hemorrhoid swelling and pain.
4. Crush fresh leaf and apply as a poultice on insect bites to relieve pain and swelling.
5. Use poultices made from plantain leaves to control itching from poison ivy, oak, or sumac exposure.

Benefits:
• Proven healer of injured skin cells.
• Effective for treating hemorrhoids, insect bites, and poison ivy/oak/sumac rashes.
plantain, skin healing, hemorrhoid relief, poison ivy, poultice, salve
Harvest and Use Red Clover for Menopause and Respiratory Support
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Red Clover (Trifolium pratense)

Steps:
1. Harvest flower heads in early summer.
2. Prepare tincture, water infusion, salve, or syrup from the flowers.
3. Take red clover tea or tincture daily to relieve symptoms of menopause due to its...
Red Clover (Trifolium pratense)

Steps:
1. Harvest flower heads in early summer.
2. Prepare tincture, water infusion, salve, or syrup from the flowers.
3. Take red clover tea or tincture daily to relieve symptoms of menopause due to its estrogenic properties.
4. Use during menstrual periods to relieve cramps by taking it daily.
5. Apply red clover salve for burns and use as a mild sedative for children with coughs, wheezing, and bronchitis.

Benefits:
• Helps manage menopause symptoms.
• Alleviates menstrual cramps.
• Treats burns and respiratory issues in children.
red clover, menopause, menstrual cramps, burns, respiratory support, children
Harvesting and Using St Johns Wort for Medicinal Purposes
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Harvest St John's Wort leaves before flowering.
2. Dry the leaves for storage or use in tinctures or water infusions.
3. Since St John's Wort is often classified as a noxious weed, surreptitiously dig up a few sprouts and...
Procedure:

1. Harvest St John's Wort leaves before flowering.
2. Dry the leaves for storage or use in tinctures or water infusions.
3. Since St John's Wort is often classified as a noxious weed, surreptitiously dig up a few sprouts and transplant them onto your property to avoid exposure to herbicides.

Medicinal Use:
• St. John's Wort has many medicinal properties but is currently best known as a proven antidepressant with absolutely no side effects.
• It may be extremely important to have an antidepressant available during long-term survival situations, one that can be safely taken and still allow for mental and physical functionality.

Important Note:
• St. John's Wort is a monoamine oxidase inhibitor (MAOI), so the same food restrictions apply as with pharmaceutical MAOIs: avoid wine, cheese, and other foods that may interact negatively with it.
• Exercise caution when using this herb to ensure safety.
st johns wort, antidepressant, herbal medicine, survival remedy, monoamine oxidase inhibitor
Identify and Use Burdock Root as a Medicinal Plant
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Locate burdock plants in temperate regions where they grow in open areas or along roadsides.
2. Identify the plant by its large, heart-shaped leaves with rough edges and clusters of small purple flowers.
3. Harvest the root during...
Procedure:

1. Locate burdock plants in temperate regions where they grow in open areas or along roadsides.
2. Identify the plant by its large, heart-shaped leaves with rough edges and clusters of small purple flowers.
3. Harvest the root during the fall or early spring when it is most potent.
4. Clean the root thoroughly to remove dirt and debris.
5. Use the cleaned root in various ways:
- For skin health: Apply a poultice made from crushed burdock root to treat boils, rashes, or insect bites.
- As food: Boil or roast the root for consumption as it is edible and has a nutty flavor.

Additional Uses:
• Use burdock root in herbal teas to support liver health and detoxification.
• Apply an oil made from burdock seeds to treat skin conditions like eczema or psoriasis.

Notes:
• Burdock is commonly used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory, diuretic, and detoxifying properties.
burdock root, herbal medicine, skin treatment, liver health, foraging
Identify and Use Herbal Remedies When Medicines Are Unavailable
Standard Source: Life After Doomsday
Overview:
When your supply of pharmaceutical medicines runs out, herbal remedies can serve as a partial alternative. However, it is important to understand that many medicinal plants contain mild toxins, which means precise dosing is...
Overview:
When your supply of pharmaceutical medicines runs out, herbal remedies can serve as a partial alternative. However, it is important to understand that many medicinal plants contain mild toxins, which means precise dosing is critical.

Steps:
1. Study Reliable Sources: Consult reputable books such as *The Practical Encyclopedia of Natural Healing* by Mark Bricklin or *A Guide to Medicinal Plants of Appalachia* published by the United States Department of Agriculture Forest Service for accurate information on medicinal plants and their uses.
2. Learn Plant Identification: Become familiar with local flora that have known medicinal properties. This includes understanding which parts of the plant (leaves, berries, bark) are used and how to prepare them.
3. Understand Dosing Risks: Recognize that many herbal remedies contain mild poisons. Incorrect dosing can be extremely dangerous. Always start with small amounts and monitor for adverse effects.
4. Prepare Medicines Carefully: If you decide to extract medicinal ingredients yourself, follow detailed instructions from reliable sources. This may involve making teas, tinctures, or other forms of preparation.
5. Use Caution: Be aware that some herbal remedies are outdated or based on unverified claims. For example, *American Medicinal Plants* by Charles F. Millspaugh contains historical information that may not align with modern medical practices.
6. Consider Commercial Kits: If available, use pre-packaged home health care kits like the Marshall Family Black Bag Home Health Care Kit for emergency situations.

Warnings and Contraindications:
• Do not rely solely on herbal remedies for serious or life-threatening conditions without professional medical advice.
• Always verify the safety and efficacy of any plant before using it medicinally.
• Avoid consuming large quantities of any plant unless you are certain about its effects.
herbal remedies, medicinal plants, natural healing, dosing risks, plant identification
Prepare a Medicinal Herb Inventory for Survival Situations
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Select Appropriate Herbs:
- Choose herbs that are commonly available in your local area and have known medicinal properties (e.g., Echinacea, Yarrow, Plantain, Chamomile, Garlic, Ginger).
- Prioritize herbs with broad...
Procedure:

1. Select Appropriate Herbs:
- Choose herbs that are commonly available in your local area and have known medicinal properties (e.g., Echinacea, Yarrow, Plantain, Chamomile, Garlic, Ginger).
- Prioritize herbs with broad applications such as anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, or immune-boosting properties.

2. Create a List of Herbs:
- Based on your research and observations from nature walks, compile a list of herbs that can be used for common ailments (e.g., wounds, infections, digestive issues, colds).
- Include both dried and fresh herb options if possible.

3. Plan for Long-Term Use:
- For long-term survival scenarios, consider growing medicinal plants in your garden or cultivating them in containers.
- If relying on wild harvesting, ensure you have a map of reliable locations where these herbs can be found throughout the year.

4. Store Herbs Properly:
- Store dried herbs in airtight containers away from light and moisture to preserve potency.
- For tinctures or liquid extracts, use dark glass bottles and store them in cool, dry places.

5. Include Preparation Methods:
- Learn how to prepare herbal remedies such as teas, poultices, salves, and tinctures for immediate use in survival situations.
- Keep a small kit with basic tools (e.g., mortar and pestle, containers) to process herbs on the go if needed.

Notes:
• Always verify the safety of any herb before using it. Some plants may have toxic properties or interact negatively with other medications.
• In survival situations, prioritize herbs that are easy to prepare and do not require complex equipment.
herb inventory, survival, medicinal plants, preparation, herbal medicine
Prepare and Use Angel’s Trumpet for Cramps and Pain Relief
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Objective: Prepare a remedy using Angel’s Trumpet to relieve intestinal cramps, stomach-aches, or gallbladder pain.

Steps:
1. Gather 1 or 2 fresh leaves of Angel’s Trumpet (Datura arborea).
2. Grind the leaves into a fine powder.
3....
Objective: Prepare a remedy using Angel’s Trumpet to relieve intestinal cramps, stomach-aches, or gallbladder pain.

Steps:
1. Gather 1 or 2 fresh leaves of Angel’s Trumpet (Datura arborea).
2. Grind the leaves into a fine powder.
3. Soak the ground leaves in 7 tablespoons (100 ml.) of water for one full day.
4. After soaking, strain the mixture to obtain the liquid.
5. Use a dropper or small spoon to administer 10–15 drops of the solution every 4 hours (only for adults).

Warnings:
• Angel’s Trumpet is very poisonous if taken in more than the recommended dose.
• Do not use this remedy for children, pregnant women, or individuals with known allergies to nightshade family plants.
• If symptoms persist or worsen, seek professional medical help.
angel's trumpet, datura, cramps, pain relief, herbal remedy
Harvest and Prepare Hawthorn Berries for Medicinal Use
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Harvest hawthorn berries in the fall.
2. Dry or prepare a tincture of the harvested berries for medicinal use.
3. Use hawthorn as a supportive therapy for:
- Congestive heart failure
- Arrhythmias
- Enlarged...
Procedure:

1. Harvest hawthorn berries in the fall.
2. Dry or prepare a tincture of the harvested berries for medicinal use.
3. Use hawthorn as a supportive therapy for:
- Congestive heart failure
- Arrhythmias
- Enlarged heart
- Symptomatic relief from cardiac symptoms
4. Long-term regular use (daily for years) is considered safe.
5. Plant multiple hawthorn trees if heart disease or related problems run in your family.
6. Ensure the correct species of hawthorn is being grown to achieve the desired medicinal properties.
hawthorn, heart health, herbal medicine, cardiac support, congestive heart failure, arrhythmias
Harvest and Use Burdock Root for Arthritis and Swelling
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Burdock (Arctium lappa)

Steps:
1. Harvest 1st year roots in the fall.
2. Prepare a tincture or water infusion from the root.
3. Internally, use Burdock to help with arthritis.
4. Externally, apply as a poultice or compress to reduce...
Burdock (Arctium lappa)

Steps:
1. Harvest 1st year roots in the fall.
2. Prepare a tincture or water infusion from the root.
3. Internally, use Burdock to help with arthritis.
4. Externally, apply as a poultice or compress to reduce swelling around joints.

Benefits:
• Acts as a blood cleanser (detoxifier).
• Nutritive to the liver.
• Mildly diuretic.
burdock, arthritis, swelling, detoxifier, liver, poultice, compress
Harvesting Poppy Resin for Medicinal Use
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Identify fully formed, green, and juicy-looking seed pods on the poppy plant.
2. Using a small sharp knife tip, make 3-4 shallow slits 2/3rds the way down the seed pod from the top to bottom direction, spacing the cuts evenly...
Procedure:

1. Identify fully formed, green, and juicy-looking seed pods on the poppy plant.
2. Using a small sharp knife tip, make 3-4 shallow slits 2/3rds the way down the seed pod from the top to bottom direction, spacing the cuts evenly around the pod.
3. The resin will slowly ooze out and begin to air harden.
4. Daily scrape off the semi-hardened resin from the cuts and (wearing surgical gloves) shape the resin into a ball shape.
5. Store the shaped resin in a glass container, kept cool and dark.
6. Each day, form the newly collected resin onto the existing ball.
7. When the resin no longer oozes, make 3-4 new cuts spaced between the old ones evenly and repeat the process.
8. Perform this process for three series of cuts per pod.
9. When the seed pod fully dries, turns brown, and hardens (you can hear the seeds rattle when you gently shake the pod), pick the whole pod and break it open over wax paper or a paper towel to harvest the seeds.
10. Let a few pods remain on the stems so the plant will self-seed for the next year.

Notes:
• Be aware of local legal status regarding poppies and opiates, as illegal possession has harsh penalties.
• Most poppies sold in plant shops are not *Papavar somniferum*; ensure you have the correct species.
• Poppy seeds can be used for a severe pain-relieving tea if the patient is conscious. A dropperful of tincture may be used by inserting under the tongue of an unconscious patient.
poppy resin, harvesting poppies, medicinal use, pain relief, herbal medicine, survival remedy
Harvesting and Using Red Raspberry Leaves for Medicinal Purposes
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Harvest red raspberry leaves throughout the growing season, taking no more than 1/3 of the total until frost.
2. After harvesting, strip the canes.
3. Dry the leaves for storage or use.
4. Use as a water infusion for medicinal...
Procedure:

1. Harvest red raspberry leaves throughout the growing season, taking no more than 1/3 of the total until frost.
2. After harvesting, strip the canes.
3. Dry the leaves for storage or use.
4. Use as a water infusion for medicinal purposes.

Medicinal Uses:
• Safe and useful herb in pregnancy.
• Strengthens uterine muscle.
• Eases or prevents nausea.
• Helps prevent haemorrhage.
• Reduces labour pain.
• Helps reduce or prevent false labour.
• Decreases uterine swelling after delivery.
• Reduces postpartum bleeding.
• Provides good relief of vomiting in sick children.
• Aids in treating diarrhoea in infants.

Growing Instructions:
• Red raspberry grows as 6-8 foot canes from spreading roots.
• Prefers partial sun.
• Plant 1-year-old roots for best results.
• Will produce for years if properly maintained.
red raspberry leaves, medicinal use, pregnancy support, herbal medicine, survival remedy
Identify Medicinal Plants in Your Local Area
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Start with Research:
- Acquire a reputable field guide for medicinal plants specific to your region. Recommended guides include the Peterson Field Guides, *A Field Guide to Medicinal Plants and Herbs of Eastern and Central...
Procedure:

1. Start with Research:
- Acquire a reputable field guide for medicinal plants specific to your region. Recommended guides include the Peterson Field Guides, *A Field Guide to Medicinal Plants and Herbs of Eastern and Central North America* by S. Foster and James Duke, or *A Field Guide to Western Medicinal Plants and Herbs* by S. Foster.
- Consult scientific texts such as *Medical Botany: Plants Affecting Human Health* by Lewis and Lewis for evidence-based information on botanical medicine.

2. Explore Your Environment:
- Begin with nature walks in mid to late spring, focusing on areas where medicinal plants are likely to grow (e.g., forests, meadows, along trails).
- Repeat the walk in early summer and again in late summer or early fall to observe seasonal changes in plant appearance.

3. Document Findings:
- Create a mental or physical map of areas where medicinal plants are found. Note specific locations for each type of herb or botanical.
- Record details such as the time of year when the plant is best harvested (e.g., before flowering, after flowering, or during dormancy).

4. Verify Identification:
- Cross-reference any plants you identify with multiple sources to ensure accuracy and avoid misidentification, which can lead to harmful effects.

Notes:
• Some plants may look similar but have very different properties (e.g., poison hemlock vs. wild carrot). Always be cautious and double-check identification.
• Avoid consuming any plant unless you are 100% certain of its identity and safety for consumption.
identify, medicinal plants, field guide, herbal medicine, survival, local area
Identify and Learn About Local Medicinal Herbs
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Objective: Identify and learn about medicinal herbs in your area to use safely.

Steps:
1. Research the plants growing in your local environment, especially those known for curative properties.
2. Consult reliable sources or experts to...
Objective: Identify and learn about medicinal herbs in your area to use safely.

Steps:
1. Research the plants growing in your local environment, especially those known for curative properties.
2. Consult reliable sources or experts to determine which plants are safe and effective for medical use.
3. Learn proper usage methods, dosages, and potential risks associated with each herb.
4. Avoid using herbs without understanding their effects and contraindications.

Warnings:
• Not all plants labeled as 'curative' have real medicinal value.
• Some herbs can be harmful if used incorrectly or in excess.
• Always verify the safety and efficacy of a plant before use.
herbs, medicinal plants, local remedies, herbal medicine, natural healing
Prepare Garlic Remedy for Pinworms and Vaginal Infections
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Objective: Prepare a garlic-based drink to help eliminate pinworms or treat vaginal infections.

Steps:
1. Chop 4 cloves of garlic finely or crush them using a mortar and pestle.
2. Mix the crushed garlic with **1 glass (approximately...
Objective: Prepare a garlic-based drink to help eliminate pinworms or treat vaginal infections.

Steps:
1. Chop 4 cloves of garlic finely or crush them using a mortar and pestle.
2. Mix the crushed garlic with 1 glass (approximately 250 ml) of liquid, such as water, juice, or milk.
3. Drink this mixture once daily for 3 weeks to treat pinworms.

For Vaginal Infections:
• Refer to pages 241 and 242 in the book for specific instructions on using garlic for vaginal infections.

Warnings:
• Garlic can cause gastrointestinal discomfort if consumed in large amounts.
• If symptoms persist or worsen, consult a healthcare professional.
garlic, pinworms, vaginal infections, herbal remedy, natural treatment
Use Willow for Fever Reduction
Standard Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Procedure:

1. Harvest the inner bark of a Willow tree.
2. Boil water in a container.
3. Place the inner bark into the boiling water and let it steep (covered or uncovered) for 20 minutes to make tea.
4. Drink the resulting tea to reduce...
Procedure:

1. Harvest the inner bark of a Willow tree.
2. Boil water in a container.
3. Place the inner bark into the boiling water and let it steep (covered or uncovered) for 20 minutes to make tea.
4. Drink the resulting tea to reduce fever.

Notes:
• Willow is commonly found near wet soils, riverbanks, sandbars, and silt flats.
• This method is suitable for reducing mild to moderate fevers.
willow, fever reduction, herbal medicine, natural remedies
Harvest and Prepare Parsley for Medicinal or Culinary Use
Reference Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Harvest parsley leaves throughout the growing season.
2. Take no more than half of the total leaves each time during harvesting.
3. In the fall, cut the whole plant down to about 1-2" from the ground.
4. **Dry or use...
Procedure:

1. Harvest parsley leaves throughout the growing season.
2. Take no more than half of the total leaves each time during harvesting.
3. In the fall, cut the whole plant down to about 1-2" from the ground.
4. Dry or use fresh parsley for medicinal or culinary purposes.
5. Prepare tinctures, water infusions, or use as a raw ingredient.
6. Use parsley in the following ways:
- As a supportive therapy for liver disease with jaundice
- As a mild eye wash treatment for conjunctivitis and blepharitis
- For urinary tract infections and as a diuretic
- To dry up breast milk (useful for weaning)
- As a traditional breath freshener
7. Avoid use during pregnancy as it may precipitate labour.
8. Plant parsley seeds barely 1/2" deep in compost-rich soil.
9. Cover the plants after cutting back in fall, and remove the cover in spring, watering them to encourage regrowth.
parsley, medicinal use, liver disease, urinary tract infection, diuretic, pregnancy warning
Prepare and Use Corn Silk for Urinary Health and Swelling Reduction
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Objective: Prepare a tea from corn silk to help reduce swelling in the feet (especially during pregnancy) by increasing urine output.

Steps:
1. Gather a large handful of corn silk (the tassels or 'silk' from an ear of maize).
2. Boil...
Objective: Prepare a tea from corn silk to help reduce swelling in the feet (especially during pregnancy) by increasing urine output.

Steps:
1. Gather a large handful of corn silk (the tassels or 'silk' from an ear of maize).
2. Boil the corn silk in water for a few minutes until the liquid turns slightly yellowish.
3. Allow the tea to cool slightly, then drink 1 or 2 glasses of it.

Warnings:
• Corn silk is generally safe and not dangerous when used as instructed.
• If you have kidney disease or are on medication that affects urine output, consult a healthcare professional before use.
corn silk, swelling reduction, urinary health, herbal tea, pregnancy
Grow and Harvest Garlic for Medicinal or Culinary Use
Reference Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Plant garlic cloves in the fall or spring.
2. Bury cloves 1-2" deep, spacing them about 6" apart.
3. Harvest garlic when bulbs are fully formed (typically in late summer or early fall).
4. **Use fresh garlic solid or...
Procedure:

1. Plant garlic cloves in the fall or spring.
2. Bury cloves 1-2" deep, spacing them about 6" apart.
3. Harvest garlic when bulbs are fully formed (typically in late summer or early fall).
4. Use fresh garlic solid or as juice for medicinal or culinary purposes.
5. Be aware of potential side effects: Garlic taken internally may cause nausea and vomiting.
garlic, medicinal use, grow garlic, nausea, vomiting
Understand the Use of Herbs in Traditional Medicine
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
A herb is a plant valued for its medicinal or healing properties.

Herbs can be used to treat various ailments, such as colds, digestive issues, and skin conditions. However, it is important to use them correctly and consult with a health worker...
A herb is a plant valued for its medicinal or healing properties.

Herbs can be used to treat various ailments, such as colds, digestive issues, and skin conditions. However, it is important to use them correctly and consult with a health worker if unsure about their safety or effectiveness.
herbs, traditional medicine, plant remedies

Essential Oils (23) -

Distill Birch Oil
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Birch Oil:

• To distill birch oil:
1. Use 20 parts of birch bark and 1 part of ledum palustre, layered in an earthen pot.
2. Place a handful of tripoli between each layer.
3. Close the pot with a perforated oak plug.
4....
Birch Oil:

• To distill birch oil:
1. Use 20 parts of birch bark and 1 part of ledum palustre, layered in an earthen pot.
2. Place a handful of tripoli between each layer.
3. Close the pot with a perforated oak plug.
4. Invert the pot and attach it to another pot sunk in the ground, sealed with luted clay.
5. Surround the setup with fire to distill the oil per descensum (downward) into the lower jar.
• An 8-gallon pot yields about 2 lbs. or 2 1/2 lbs. of oil when properly filled.
• In Siberia, birch oil is prepared without ledum palustre.
• The oil is liquid when fresh, but becomes thick over time.
• Used in Russia for currying leather, giving it a smell that insects dislike.
birch oil, distillation, ledum palustre, tripoli, leather currying
Extract Butter of Roses
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Obtained from the flowers of damask roses; white, solid, separating slowly from the rosewater. It has little scent of its own, and is used to dilute the scent of musk, civet and ambergris. One cwt. of roses will yield from 1/2 an oz. to an oz.
butter of roses,damask roses,rosewater,dilution
Extract Cajeput Oil
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Cajeput Oil:

• Obtained from leaves imported from the East Indies, usually in large copper flasks.
• The oil is cooler than peppermint oil, but has a turpentine-like smell.
• Used externally for rheumatism.
cajeput oil, essential oils, rheumatism treatment
Extract Camphor from Tree Roots and Shoots
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Camphor:

• Obtained by distilling the roots and shoots of laurus camphora, laurus cinnamomum, and capura curundu with water.
• The crude camphor is refined by sublimation using one-sixteenth of its weight in lime, under a **very...
Camphor:

• Obtained by distilling the roots and shoots of laurus camphora, laurus cinnamomum, and capura curundu with water.
• The crude camphor is refined by sublimation using one-sixteenth of its weight in lime, under a very gentle heat.
camphor, essential oils, distillation, refinement
Extract Essence of Cedrat (Citron)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Essence of Cedrat:

• Obtained from the flowers of the citron tree, yielding an amber-colored, slightly fragrant oil.
• 60 lbs. of flowers yield 1 oz. of essence.
• Also obtained from the yellow part of citron peel:
- A...
Essence of Cedrat:

• Obtained from the flowers of the citron tree, yielding an amber-colored, slightly fragrant oil.
• 60 lbs. of flowers yield 1 oz. of essence.
• Also obtained from the yellow part of citron peel:
- A colorless, very thin and fragrant oil can be extracted by expression between two glass plates or by distilling the yellow part with water.
- Another method involves distilling the cake left after squeezing citron peel, yielding a greenish oil.
• 100 citrons yield:
- 1 oz. of white essence
- 1/2 oz. of greenish essence.
• The extracted oil is thick in consistency.
cedrat essence, citron oil, essential oils
Extract Oil of Aniseed
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Oil of Aniseed:

• Use 1 lb. of aniseed seeds to yield 2 drs. (drops) of oil.
• This oil is congealed, except in warm weather.
• It has a carmative effect (relieves gas or flatulence).
• Warning: This oil is **poisonous to...
Oil of Aniseed:

• Use 1 lb. of aniseed seeds to yield 2 drs. (drops) of oil.
• This oil is congealed, except in warm weather.
• It has a carmative effect (relieves gas or flatulence).
• Warning: This oil is poisonous to pigeons if rubbed on their bills or head.
oil of aniseed, essential oils, carmative, pigeon poison
Extract Oil of Caraway
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Oil of Caraway:

• Obtained from caraway seeds.
• It is carminative (relieves gas or flatulence).
• 2 lbs. of caraway seeds yield more than 1 oz. of oil.
• 4 cwt. 83 oz. of caraway seeds yields a specific amount, though the...
Oil of Caraway:

• Obtained from caraway seeds.
• It is carminative (relieves gas or flatulence).
• 2 lbs. of caraway seeds yield more than 1 oz. of oil.
• 4 cwt. 83 oz. of caraway seeds yields a specific amount, though the exact quantity is not specified in this text.
caraway oil, essential oils, carminative
Extract Oil of Cassia
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Oil of Cassia:

• A common oil of cinnamon, obtained from the bark of inferior cinnamon, imported under the name of cassia.
• 1 lb. of cassia bark yields between 1 to 1 1/2 drs. (drops) of oil.
• It is a **stimulant and...
Oil of Cassia:

• A common oil of cinnamon, obtained from the bark of inferior cinnamon, imported under the name of cassia.
• 1 lb. of cassia bark yields between 1 to 1 1/2 drs. (drops) of oil.
• It is a stimulant and stomachic.
• Another oil can be obtained from cassia buds.
oil of cassia, cinnamon oil, stimulant, stomachic
Extract Oil of Chamomile
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Oil of Chamomile:

• Obtained from chamomile flowers.
• It is a stomachic (aids digestion).
• 1 lb. of chamomile flowers yields about 1 dr. (drop) of oil.
• 82 lbs. of flowers yield between 13 to 18 drs. of oil.
• The oil...
Oil of Chamomile:

• Obtained from chamomile flowers.
• It is a stomachic (aids digestion).
• 1 lb. of chamomile flowers yields about 1 dr. (drop) of oil.
• 82 lbs. of flowers yield between 13 to 18 drs. of oil.
• The oil is a fine blue color, even if distilled in glass vessels.
chamomile oil, essential oils, stomachic
Extract Oil of Cinnamon
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Oil of Cinnamon:

• Obtained from the fresh bark, imported from Ceylon.
• De Guignes notes that cinnamon from Cochin China contains so much essential oil that it can be pressed out by fingers.
oil of cinnamon, essential oils, ceylon cinnamon
Extract Oil of Cloves
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Oil of Cloves:

• Obtained from cloves, a spice imported from the spice islands.
• The oil is very heavy, acrimonious, and contains some part of the resin of the clove.
• 1 lb. of cloves yields between **1 1/2 to 2 1/2 oz. of...
Oil of Cloves:

• Obtained from cloves, a spice imported from the spice islands.
• The oil is very heavy, acrimonious, and contains some part of the resin of the clove.
• 1 lb. of cloves yields between 1 1/2 to 2 1/2 oz. of oil.
• 7 1/2 lbs. of cloves yield 1 lb. of oil.
• Can also be expressed from ripe cloves.
• Muller’s method: Digest 1/2 oz. of cloves in ether, then mix with water to obtain 7 scruples (approximately 0.45 grams) of greenish yellow oil, which floats on water.
• Used as a stimulant, added to purgative pills to prevent griping, and applied externally to aching teeth.
oil of cloves, essential oils, stimulant, tooth pain
Extract Oil of Gum-Benzoin
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Oil of Gum-Benzoin:

• Obtained by distilling the residuum left after making flowers of benjamin, using a strong fire.
• Used as a substitute for birch oil in making an imitation of Russia leather.
oil of gum-benzoin, flowers of benjamin, imitation leather
Extract Oil of Pennyroyal
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Obtained from the herb when in flower. Three lbs. will yield 6 drs. Emmenagogue.
oil,pennyroyal,distillation,emmenagogue
Extract Oil of Peppermint
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Obtained from the dried plant. Four lbs. of the fresh herb will yield 3 drs. In general it requires rectification to render it bright and fine. It is stimulant and carminative.
oil,peppermint,distillation,rectification
Extract Oil of Rhodium
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Obtained from the true lignum rhodium. Eighty lbs. will yield 9 drs., and in very resinous old wood 80 lbs. will yield 2 oz. It is light yellowish but grows red by keeping. Another oil is obtained from the root of rose-wort, *rhodiola rosea*; it...
Obtained from the true lignum rhodium. Eighty lbs. will yield 9 drs., and in very resinous old wood 80 lbs. will yield 2 oz. It is light yellowish but grows red by keeping. Another oil is obtained from the root of rose-wort, *rhodiola rosea*; it is yellowish, and has the smell and taste of that from the true lignum rhodium. One lb. will yield a drachm.
oil,rhodium,distillation,lignum rhodium
Extract Oil of Rosemary
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Obtained from the flowering tops; it is sweet scented. One cwt. will yield 8 oz.; 1 lb. of dry leaves will yield from 1 to 3 drs.; 70 lbs. of fresh leaves will yield 5 oz.
oil,rosemary,distillation,sweet scent
Extract Oil of Sandal Wood
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
There are three kinds of sandal or santal wood, the white, yellow and red. The yellow is most used in perfumery. One cwt. of the wood will yield nearly 30 oz. of otto.
oil,sandalwood,distillation,perfumery
Extract Oil of Sassafras
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Obtained from the sassafras root. Twenty-four lbs. will yield 9 oz.; 30 lbs. will yield 7 oz. and 1 dr.; and 3 lbs. will yield 2 oz.
oil,sassafras,distillation,root
Extract Oil of Tar
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Obtained by distilling tar. It is highly valued by painters, varnishers, etc., on account of its drying qualities; it soon thickens of itself, almost to a balsam. The pyroligneous acid that comes over with it is useful for many purposes.
oil,tar,distillation,varnish
Extract Oil of Thyme
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Obtained from the plant; 2 cwt. fresh will yield 5 1/2 oz.; 3 1/2 lbs., dried, will yield 1/2 a dr. It is stimulant and caustic; and used in toothache, applied to the tooth.
oil,thyme,distillation,toothache
Extract Oil of Turpentine
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Distilled in Europe, from common turpentine, with the addition of about 6 times as much water; but in America, where the operation is carried on upon a very large scale, no water is added, and its accidental presence is even dreaded, lest it...
Distilled in Europe, from common turpentine, with the addition of about 6 times as much water; but in America, where the operation is carried on upon a very large scale, no water is added, and its accidental presence is even dreaded, lest it should produce a disruption of the stilling apparatus.
oil,turpentine,distillation,stilling apparatus
Make Otto of Roses
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Gather the leaves of the hundred-leaved rose (rosa centifolia).
2. Put them in a large jar or cask with just sufficient water to cover them.
3. Place the vessel in the sun.
4. After about a week, a butyraceous oil (otto) will form a scum on the...
1. Gather the leaves of the hundred-leaved rose (rosa centifolia).
2. Put them in a large jar or cask with just sufficient water to cover them.
3. Place the vessel in the sun.
4. After about a week, a butyraceous oil (otto) will form a scum on the surface.
5. Remove the scum using a piece of cotton.
otto of roses, essential oils, rose leaves, extraction, butyraceous oil
Rectify Oil of Turpentine
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Pour 3 parts of turpentine into a glass retort, capable of containing double the quantity of matter subjected to the experiment. Place this retort on a sand-bath, and having adapted to it a receiver 5 or 6 times as large, cement with paste made...
Pour 3 parts of turpentine into a glass retort, capable of containing double the quantity of matter subjected to the experiment. Place this retort on a sand-bath, and having adapted to it a receiver 5 or 6 times as large, cement with paste made of flour and water, some bands of paper over the place where the 2 vessels are joined. If the receiver is not tabulated, make a small hole with a pin in the bands of connected paper, to leave a free communication between the exterior and interior of the receiver; then place over the retort a dome of baked earth, and maintain the fire in such a manner as to make the essence and the water boil.

The receiver will become filled with abundance of vapors, composed of water and ethereous essence, which will condense the more readily if all the radiating heat of the furnace be intercepted by a plate of copper, or piece of board placed between the furnace and the receiver. When the mass of oil subjected to experiment has decreased nearly two-thirds, the distillation must be stopped. Then leave the product at rest to facilitate the separation of the ethereous oil, which is afterwards separated from the water, on which it floats, by means of a glass funnel, the beak of which is stopped by the finger.

This ethereous oil is often milky, or merely nebulous, by the interposition of some aqueous parts, from which it may be separated by a few days' rest. The essence, thus prepared, possesses a great degree of mobility, and is exceedingly limpid.
rectify,turpentine,distillation,copper plate

Dyeing Staining (22) -

Alternate Method for Dyeing Hats
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Another Method:

1. Boil 100 lbs of logwood, 12 lbs of gum, and 6 lbs of galls in a sufficient quantity of water for several hours.
2. Add about 6 lbs of verdigris and 10 lbs of green vitriol to the mixture.
3. Keep the mixture at a simmer or...
Another Method:

1. Boil 100 lbs of logwood, 12 lbs of gum, and 6 lbs of galls in a sufficient quantity of water for several hours.
2. Add about 6 lbs of verdigris and 10 lbs of green vitriol to the mixture.
3. Keep the mixture at a simmer or just below boiling point.
4. Immediately add ten or twelve dozens of hats, each on its block, and keep them down with crossbars for about an hour and a half.
5. Remove the hats and air them out.
6. Replace them with another set of hats and repeat the process.
7. Alternate dipping and airing the two sets of hats eight times each.
8. Refresh the liquor each time by adding more ingredients, but in smaller quantities than initially used.
hats,dye,logwood,gum,galls
Dye Bristles or Feathers Green
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Dye Bristles or Feathers Green:

1. Take 1 oz of verdigris and 1 oz of verditer.
2. Mix them with 1 pint of gum water.
3. Soak the bristles or feathers in hot water first.
4. Dip the materials into the mixture.

Blue Dye:
1. Take 1 oz of...
To Dye Bristles or Feathers Green:

1. Take 1 oz of verdigris and 1 oz of verditer.
2. Mix them with 1 pint of gum water.
3. Soak the bristles or feathers in hot water first.
4. Dip the materials into the mixture.

Blue Dye:
1. Take 1 oz of indigo and 1 oz of risse.
2. Add a piece of alum the size of a hazelnut to gumwater.
3. Dip the bristles or feathers (after soaking in hot water) into this mixture while it is hot.
4. Hang them up to dry, and clap them well so they may open.
5. By changing the colors, materials can be dyed any color.

Purple:
Use lake and indigo.

Carnation:
Use vermilion and smalt.

Red Dye:
1. Take 1 oz of Brazil-wood in powder, ½ oz of alum, ¼ oz of vermilion, and 1 pint of vinegar.
2. Boil the mixture to a moderate thickness.
3. Soak bristles or feathers in hot water first.
4. Dip them into the mixture.
bristles,feathers,dye,green,blue,purple,carnation,red
Dye Gloves
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Dye Gloves:

1. Choose the appropriate color for the occasion:
- For dark colors, use Spanish brown and black earth.
- For lighter colors, use yellow and whiting.
2. Mix the chosen materials with a moderate fire.
3. Daub the gloves wet...
To Dye Gloves:

1. Choose the appropriate color for the occasion:
- For dark colors, use Spanish brown and black earth.
- For lighter colors, use yellow and whiting.
2. Mix the chosen materials with a moderate fire.
3. Daub the gloves wet with the mixture.
4. Let them hang until dry.
5. Beat out any excess color from the gloves.
6. Smooth them over using a stretching or sleeking stick, reducing them to their proper shape.
gloves,dye,color,mixing,stretching
Dye Hats Using Galls and Logwood
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Dye Hats:

1. Strongly galle the hats by boiling them for a long time in a decoction of galls with a little logwood, to allow the dye to penetrate better.
2. Add a proper quantity of vitriol and decoction of logwood, along with a little...
To Dye Hats:

1. Strongly galle the hats by boiling them for a long time in a decoction of galls with a little logwood, to allow the dye to penetrate better.
2. Add a proper quantity of vitriol and decoction of logwood, along with a little verdigris, to the mixture.
3. Continue soaking the hats in this mixture for a considerable time.
4. Afterward, place the hats into fresh liquor containing logwood, galls, vitriol, and verdigris.
5. For high-value or difficult-to-dye hats, repeat the process a third time.
6. To achieve the most perfect color, dye the hair or wool blue before forming it into hats.
hats,dye,galls,logwood,vitriol
Dye Horse-Hair
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Dye or Color Horse-Hair:

1. Steep the hair in water that has had a small quantity of turpentine boiled in it for two hours.
2. Prepare the desired color very hot.
3. Boil the hair in the colored solution.
4. Any color, except black, will...
To Dye or Color Horse-Hair:

1. Steep the hair in water that has had a small quantity of turpentine boiled in it for two hours.
2. Prepare the desired color very hot.
3. Boil the hair in the colored solution.
4. Any color, except black, will take well; black hair will only take a dark-red or dark-blue shade.
horse-hair,dye,turpentine,color
Dye Wood Bright Green with Vitriolated Indigo
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Proceed as described for producing a yellow stain.
2. Instead of adding aquafortis, add an amount of vitriolated indigo sufficient to achieve the desired green color.
vitriolated indigo, bright green dye, wood staining
Dye Wood Fine Blue with Oil of Vitriol and Indigo
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. In a glass bottle, mix 1 lb. of oil of vitriol with 4 oz. of indigo.
2. Proceed as directed in previous methods to complete the dyeing process.
oil of vitriol, indigo, fine blue dye, wood staining
Dye Wood Purple with Logwood and Brazil-Dust
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Combine 2 lbs. of chip logwood with 1/2 lb. of Brazil-dust.
2. Add 4 galls. of water to the mixture.
3. Submerge as many veneers as the liquid can cover.
4. Boil the mixture thoroughly.
5. After boiling, add 6 oz. of pearlash and 2...
Procedure:
1. Combine 2 lbs. of chip logwood with 1/2 lb. of Brazil-dust.
2. Add 4 galls. of water to the mixture.
3. Submerge as many veneers as the liquid can cover.
4. Boil the mixture thoroughly.
5. After boiling, add 6 oz. of pearlash and 2 oz. of alum to the solution.
6. Continue boiling for 2 or 3 hours every day until the color has fully penetrated the wood.
logwood, Brazil-dust, purple dye, pearlash
Dye Wood a Silver Gray with Iron Liquor and Logwood
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Ensure the veneers are not overly dry before beginning.
2. Place the veneers in a copper container.
3. Pour hot iron liquor (acetate of iron) over them.
4. Add 1 lb. of chip logwood and 2 oz. of bruised nut-galls to the mixture.
5....
Procedure:
1. Ensure the veneers are not overly dry before beginning.
2. Place the veneers in a copper container.
3. Pour hot iron liquor (acetate of iron) over them.
4. Add 1 lb. of chip logwood and 2 oz. of bruised nut-galls to the mixture.
5. Boil another pot of iron liquor to supply the copper as needed during the process.
6. Keep the veneers covered and boiling for 2 hours a day until they are thoroughly penetrated by the dye.
iron liquor, logwood, silver gray dye, wood staining
Dye Wood with Barberry Roots and Turmeric
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Reduce 4 lbs. of barberry roots into fine dust by sawing.
2. Place the dust in a copper or brass pan.
3. Add 4 oz. of turmeric to the pan.
4. Pour 4 galls. of water into the mixture.
5. Submerge as many holly veneers as the liquid...
Procedure:
1. Reduce 4 lbs. of barberry roots into fine dust by sawing.
2. Place the dust in a copper or brass pan.
3. Add 4 oz. of turmeric to the pan.
4. Pour 4 galls. of water into the mixture.
5. Submerge as many holly veneers as the liquid can cover.
6. Boil the mixture for 3 hours, turning the veneers occasionally.
7. After cooling, add 2 oz. of aquafortis to the solution.
8. The dye will penetrate more quickly after this step.
barberry roots, turmeric, aquafortis, wood dye
Marble the Edges of Books or Paper with Gum Arabic and Color Mixtures
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Dissolve 4 oz. of gum arabic in 2 qts. of clear water.
2. Prepare several colors mixed with water in pots or shells.
3. Use pencils specific to each color to sprinkle the colored solutions into the gum-water, which should be placed...
Procedure:
1. Dissolve 4 oz. of gum arabic in 2 qts. of clear water.
2. Prepare several colors mixed with water in pots or shells.
3. Use pencils specific to each color to sprinkle the colored solutions into the gum-water, which should be placed in a trough or broad vessel.
4. With a stick, curl or draw out the colors in streaks to create varied patterns as desired.
5. Hold the book or books close together and dip their edges lightly into the mixture of water and colors on the top surface.
6. Remove the books and allow the colored impression to set on the leaves.
7. Repeat the process for both ends and fronts of the books.
gum arabic, marbling, book edges, paper
Prepare Logwood Stain with Walnut Peeling
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Boil 1 lb. of logwood in 4 qts. of water.
2. Add a double handful of walnut-peeling to the boiling mixture.
3. Bring the mixture back to a boil.
4. Remove the chips from the solution.
5. Add a pint of the best vinegar to the...
Procedure:
1. Boil 1 lb. of logwood in 4 qts. of water.
2. Add a double handful of walnut-peeling to the boiling mixture.
3. Bring the mixture back to a boil.
4. Remove the chips from the solution.
5. Add a pint of the best vinegar to the mixture.
6. The solution is now ready for use.
logwood, walnut peeling, stain, dye, vinegar
Prepare Woad Dye
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Prepare Woad:

1. Collect the leaves of the woad plant.
2. Grind the leaves into a paste.
3. Form the paste into balls and place them in heaps.
4. Occasionally sprinkle water over the heaps to promote fermentation.
5. Once fermentation is...
To Prepare Woad:

1. Collect the leaves of the woad plant.
2. Grind the leaves into a paste.
3. Form the paste into balls and place them in heaps.
4. Occasionally sprinkle water over the heaps to promote fermentation.
5. Once fermentation is complete, allow the material to fall into a coarse powder.
6. Use this powder as a blue dyestuff.
woad,dye,fermentation,blue,powder
Prepare a Bright Red Stain for Wood
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Combine 2 lbs. of genuine Brazil-dust with 4 galls. of water.
2. Submerge as many veneers as the liquid can cover.
3. Boil the mixture for 3 hours, turning the veneers occasionally.
4. Allow the solution to cool after boiling.
5....
Procedure:
1. Combine 2 lbs. of genuine Brazil-dust with 4 galls. of water.
2. Submerge as many veneers as the liquid can cover.
3. Boil the mixture for 3 hours, turning the veneers occasionally.
4. Allow the solution to cool after boiling.
5. Add 2 oz. each of alum and aquafortis to the mixture.
6. Keep the solution lukewarm until it has fully penetrated the wood.
Brazil-dust, bright red dye, alum, aquafortis
Stain Box-wood Brown with Aquafortis
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Hold the box-wood close to a fire to give it a gentle warmth.
2. Use aquafortis and a feather to apply it evenly over the surface of the wood until it turns a fine brown.
3. Once the desired color is achieved, oil and polish the...
Procedure:
1. Hold the box-wood close to a fire to give it a gentle warmth.
2. Use aquafortis and a feather to apply it evenly over the surface of the wood until it turns a fine brown.
3. Once the desired color is achieved, oil and polish the wood to complete the process.
box-wood, aquafortis, staining, brown dye
Stain Musical Instruments with Crimson Dye
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Grind 1 lb. of Brazil-wood.
2. Boil the ground Brazil-wood in 3 qts. of water for an hour.
3. Strain the mixture.
4. Add 1/2 oz. of cochineal to the strained solution.
5. Gently boil the mixture for another 1/2 hour.
6. The...
Procedure:
1. Grind 1 lb. of Brazil-wood.
2. Boil the ground Brazil-wood in 3 qts. of water for an hour.
3. Strain the mixture.
4. Add 1/2 oz. of cochineal to the strained solution.
5. Gently boil the mixture for another 1/2 hour.
6. The resulting solution is ready for use on musical instruments.
Brazil-wood, cochineal, crimson dye, musical instruments
Stain Paper or Parchment Green with Verdigris Solution
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Prepare a solution of verdigris in vinegar.
2. Alternatively, dissolve crystals of verdigris in water to create a green dye.
3. Apply the solution evenly on paper or parchment using a broad brush for staining.
verdigris, green stain, paper, parchment
Stain Paper or Parchment Purple with Archil or Logwood Tincture
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Use archil to stain paper or parchment purple.
2. Alternatively, use the tincture of logwood for a similar effect.
3. The juice of ripe privet-berries may also be used as a natural purple dye.
4. Apply the chosen solution evenly on...
Procedure:
1. Use archil to stain paper or parchment purple.
2. Alternatively, use the tincture of logwood for a similar effect.
3. The juice of ripe privet-berries may also be used as a natural purple dye.
4. Apply the chosen solution evenly on the surface using a broad brush.
archil, logwood tincture, privet berries, purple stain
Stain Paper or Parchment with Indian Lake for Crimson Color
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Infuse Indian lake in spirit of wine for several days.
2. Pour off the tincture from the dregs to create a fine crimson stain.
3. Apply the tincture evenly on paper or parchment using a broad brush, similar to applying varnish.
4....
Procedure:
1. Infuse Indian lake in spirit of wine for several days.
2. Pour off the tincture from the dregs to create a fine crimson stain.
3. Apply the tincture evenly on paper or parchment using a broad brush, similar to applying varnish.
4. Alternatively, use red ink or the tincture of dragon's blood in spirit of wine for a scarlet hue.
Indian lake, crimson stain, paper, parchment
Stain Paper or Parchment with Turmeric Tincture for Yellow Color
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Powder an oz. or more of turmeric root.
2. Infuse the powdered root in a pint of spirit of wine to create a tincture.
3. Apply the tincture evenly on paper or parchment using a broad brush, similar to applying varnish.
4. The...
Procedure:
1. Powder an oz. or more of turmeric root.
2. Infuse the powdered root in a pint of spirit of wine to create a tincture.
3. Apply the tincture evenly on paper or parchment using a broad brush, similar to applying varnish.
4. The solution can produce various shades of yellow, from light straw to French yellow.
5. For warmer or redder tones, add anatto or dragon's blood to the mixture.
turmeric tincture, yellow stain, paper, parchment
Stain Wood Bright Yellow with Aloes and Varnish
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Take a very small bit of aloes.
2. Add it to varnish.
3. Apply the mixture to the wood to achieve a bright yellow color.
aloes, varnish, yellow dye, wood staining
Test Colors of Dyed Stuffs
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Prove the Colors of Dyed Stuffs:

For crimson, scarlet flesh-color, violet, peachblossom, and other similar shades:
1. Dissolve half an ounce of alum in a pint of water in an earthen vessel.
2. Add one-eighth of an ounce of the fabric or...
To Prove the Colors of Dyed Stuffs:

For crimson, scarlet flesh-color, violet, peachblossom, and other similar shades:
1. Dissolve half an ounce of alum in a pint of water in an earthen vessel.
2. Add one-eighth of an ounce of the fabric or thread to be tested.
3. Boil the mixture for five minutes.
4. Wash it out thoroughly in clean water.

For yellow, green madder, red cinnamon, and similar colors:
1. Boil a quarter of an ounce of soap in a pint of water.
2. Add one-eighth of an ounce of the fabric or thread to be tested.
3. Boil for five minutes.

For hair-brown:
1. Powder one ounce of tartar and boil it in a pint of water.
2. Add one-quarter of an ounce of the fabric or thread to the solution.
3. Boil for five minutes.
test,color,dye,alum,soap,tartar

Foraging (19) -

Identify Amanita Mushrooms
Critical Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Recognize physical characteristics:
- Large group of mushrooms that can be difficult to tell apart.
- Memorable names include destroying angel, fly agaric, yellow patches, blusher, grisette, ringless panther, death cap,...
Procedure:

1. Recognize physical characteristics:
- Large group of mushrooms that can be difficult to tell apart.
- Memorable names include destroying angel, fly agaric, yellow patches, blusher, grisette, ringless panther, death cap, and fool’s mushroom.
2. Observe features:
- Some members contain amanitin, one of the deadliest poisons found in nature.
3. Avoid consumption:
- Amanitas are responsible for 90% of mushroom-related deaths in the United States.
4. Use identification resources:
- Refer to pictures and descriptions in guides like the Missouri Department of Conservation’s Field Guide (mdc.mo.gov/feld-guide) for accurate identification.

Cautions:
• Amanitas are extremely dangerous and should never be consumed.
• Even small amounts can lead to severe poisoning or death.
amanita mushrooms, identification, foraging safety, amanitin, destroying angel, death cap
Identify and Avoid Poisonous Mushrooms
High Priority Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Recognize the three most dangerous groups of fungi:
- Amanitas (e.g., destroying angel, death cap)
- False morels
- Little brown mushrooms (LBMs)
2. Avoid eating any mushroom resembling these categories: These are...
Procedure:

1. Recognize the three most dangerous groups of fungi:
- Amanitas (e.g., destroying angel, death cap)
- False morels
- Little brown mushrooms (LBMs)
2. Avoid eating any mushroom resembling these categories: These are responsible for virtually all fatal mushroom poisonings in the United States.
3. Identify specific poisonous mushrooms:
- Jack-o’-lantern: Causes symptoms such as vomiting, diarrhea, cramps, and loss of coordination.
- Green-spored lepiota: Also causes similar symptoms.
4. Use resources for identification: Refer to pictures and descriptions in guides like the Missouri Department of Conservation’s Field Guide (mdc.mo.gov/feld-guide) for accurate identification.

Cautions:
• Symptoms from poisoning by these mushrooms may be alarming but usually pass within 24 hours or less with no lasting effects.
• Notify your doctor immediately if you suspect mushroom poisoning of any kind.
poisonous mushrooms, identification, foraging safety, amanitas, false morels, little brown mushrooms
Avoid Poisonous False Morels
High Priority Source: MushroomGuide
Cautions:
• Morels must be thoroughly cooked and not eaten raw.
• Beware of the poisonous false morels (see Page 34).

Identification Tips for False Morels:
• Look for mushrooms that resemble morels but have a different cap...
Cautions:
• Morels must be thoroughly cooked and not eaten raw.
• Beware of the poisonous false morels (see Page 34).

Identification Tips for False Morels:
• Look for mushrooms that resemble morels but have a different cap structure.
• Avoid any mushroom with a completely black head or shrunken appearance, as these may be toxic.
avoid false morels, poisonous mushrooms, foraging safety, Morchella
Identify and Prepare Puffball Mushrooms for Consumption
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Identify the mushroom: Look for puffballs that are young and solid white inside. They have a smooth, rounded exterior with no special features except for their roots.
2. Check size and variety: Some puffballs can be as...
Procedure:

1. Identify the mushroom: Look for puffballs that are young and solid white inside. They have a smooth, rounded exterior with no special features except for their roots.
2. Check size and variety: Some puffballs can be as large as a foot in diameter, such as the brain puffball or giant puffball.
3. Clean the mushrooms: Wash them thoroughly under running water to remove dirt and debris.
4. Prepare for cooking: Peel the outer layer of the puffball and slice it into pieces suitable for frying.
5. Cook the mushrooms: Fry the sliced puffballs until golden brown and fully cooked.

Notes:
• Only consume puffballs when they are young and solid white inside; once they begin to puff, they become inedible and potentially toxic.
• Puffballs can be a good source of food for foragers if properly identified and prepared.
puffball mushroom, identification, foraging, cooking mushrooms, edible fungi
Identify Grass Species Based on Drought and Grazing Tolerance
Reference Source: forage guide
To identify grass species based on drought and grazing tolerance:

1. Assess the plant's drought tolerance:
- Excellent (exc.), Good (g), Fair (f), Poor (p).
2. Evaluate grazing tolerance:
- Excellent, Good, Fair, Poor.
3....
To identify grass species based on drought and grazing tolerance:

1. Assess the plant's drought tolerance:
- Excellent (exc.), Good (g), Fair (f), Poor (p).
2. Evaluate grazing tolerance:
- Excellent, Good, Fair, Poor.
3. Cross-reference with a table of characteristics to match the plant species.

Example: If you find a grass that is highly drought-tolerant and has good grazing tolerance, it may be Bluestem or Switchgrass.
drought tolerance,grazing tolerance,plant identification
Identify Grass Species Based on Growth Habit and Seasonality
Reference Source: forage guide
To identify grass species based on growth habit and seasonality:

1. Examine the growth habit of the plant:
- Sod: Forms a dense mat (e.g., Bluegrass, Grama).
- Bunch: Grows in clumps (e.g., Brome, Needlegrass).
2. **Determine the growth...
To identify grass species based on growth habit and seasonality:

1. Examine the growth habit of the plant:
- Sod: Forms a dense mat (e.g., Bluegrass, Grama).
- Bunch: Grows in clumps (e.g., Brome, Needlegrass).
2. Determine the growth season:
- Cool-season grasses grow best in cooler temperatures (e.g., Bluegrass, Fescue).
- Warm-season grasses thrive in warmer climates (e.g., Bluestem, Switchgrass).
3. Cross-reference with a table of characteristics to match the plant species.

Example: If you find a sod-forming grass that grows best in cool seasons, it may be Bluegrass or Fescue.
grass identification,growth habit,seasonality,plant species
Identify Grass Species Based on Soil and Moisture Requirements
Reference Source: forage guide
To identify grass species based on soil and moisture requirements:

1. Assess the soil type where the plant is growing:
- Sandy (s), Silt (si), or Clay (c) soils.
2. Determine the minimum moisture requirement of the plant:
- Irrigated,...
To identify grass species based on soil and moisture requirements:

1. Assess the soil type where the plant is growing:
- Sandy (s), Silt (si), or Clay (c) soils.
2. Determine the minimum moisture requirement of the plant:
- Irrigated, 14”, 10”-16”, etc.
3. Cross-reference with a table of characteristics to match the plant species.

Example: If you find a grass that grows in sandy soils and requires at least 14” of moisture, it may be Bluestem or Wheatgrass.
soil type,mass requirement,plant identification
Identify Legume Species Based on Drought and Bloat Tolerance
Reference Source: forage guide
To identify legume species based on drought and bloat tolerance:

1. Assess the plant's drought tolerance:
- Excellent (exc.), Good (g), Fair (f), Poor (p).
2. Evaluate bloat potential:
- Yes or No.
3. Cross-reference with a table of...
To identify legume species based on drought and bloat tolerance:

1. Assess the plant's drought tolerance:
- Excellent (exc.), Good (g), Fair (f), Poor (p).
2. Evaluate bloat potential:
- Yes or No.
3. Cross-reference with a table of characteristics to match the plant species.

Example: If you find a legume that is drought-tolerant and does not cause bloat, it may be Sainfoin or Milkvetch.
drought tolerance,bloat potential,legume identification
Identify Legume Species Based on Moisture and Yield Potential
Reference Source: forage guide
To identify legume species based on moisture and yield potential:

1. Assess the plant's moisture requirement:
- High, Low, or Depends on variety (a).
2. Evaluate yield potential:
- High, Medium, or Poor.
3. Cross-reference with a...
To identify legume species based on moisture and yield potential:

1. Assess the plant's moisture requirement:
- High, Low, or Depends on variety (a).
2. Evaluate yield potential:
- High, Medium, or Poor.
3. Cross-reference with a table of characteristics to match the plant species.

Example: If you find a legume that requires high moisture and has high yield potential, it may be Alfalfa or Clover.
legume identification,mass requirement,yield potential
Forage and Harvest Morels Safely
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Steps:
1. Look for morels in moist woodlands and river bottoms from March to early May.
2. Check for association with ash trees, dying elms, or apple trees.
3. Inspect the cap for honeycombed ridges and yellowish-brown pits.
4....
Steps:
1. Look for morels in moist woodlands and river bottoms from March to early May.
2. Check for association with ash trees, dying elms, or apple trees.
3. Inspect the cap for honeycombed ridges and yellowish-brown pits.
4. Ensure the stem is hollow at the base.
5. Avoid picking morels that are shrunken or have completely black heads (especially for _Morchella angusticeps_).
6. Only pick young morels, as older ones may be less edible.
7. Do not confuse with the poisonous false morels (see Page 34 of source text).
forage morels safely, harvesting, foraging, Morchella
Identify Black Morel (_Morchella angusticeps_)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Appearance:
• Young: Gray or tan ridges; brown pits.
• Aged: Ridges darken to nearly black.
• Pits are elongated and brown.

Identification Tips:
• Look for a conical cap with vertically elongated ridges and pits.
• Avoid any that...
Appearance:
• Young: Gray or tan ridges; brown pits.
• Aged: Ridges darken to nearly black.
• Pits are elongated and brown.

Identification Tips:
• Look for a conical cap with vertically elongated ridges and pits.
• Avoid any that are shrunken or have completely black heads.
• Best when picked young.
black morel, identification, foraging, Morchella angusticeps
Identify Chicken of the Woods Mushrooms
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Recognize species differences:
- _Laetiporus cincinnatus_ has a white pore surface.
- _L. sulphureus_ has a sulfur-yellow pore surface.
2. Observe growth patterns: Chicken of the woods grows in clusters on the sides...
Procedure:

1. Recognize species differences:
- _Laetiporus cincinnatus_ has a white pore surface.
- _L. sulphureus_ has a sulfur-yellow pore surface.
2. Observe growth patterns: Chicken of the woods grows in clusters on the sides of trees, often oaks or other hardwoods.
3. Check for distinctive features: The mushroom has a bright color and a fan-like shape with a porous underside.
4. Avoid confusion with similar species: Due to its unique appearance, it is rarely mistaken for other mushrooms.

Cautions:
• This mushroom may cause a mild allergic reaction (swollen lips) in some people.
• Always cook and try a small amount if eating it for the first time to determine if an allergic reaction occurs.
chicken of the woods, identification, mushroom foraging, Laetiporus cincinnatus, Laetiporus sulphureus
Identify Common Chanterelle (_Cantharellus cibarius_)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Appearance:
• Bright orange or yellow color.
• Funnel-shaped with wavy cap edges.

Identification Tips:
• Look for a funnel or trumpet-like shape with wavy cap edges.
• Cut the mushroom in half to reveal a completely white center.
• Check...
Appearance:
• Bright orange or yellow color.
• Funnel-shaped with wavy cap edges.

Identification Tips:
• Look for a funnel or trumpet-like shape with wavy cap edges.
• Cut the mushroom in half to reveal a completely white center.
• Check for blunt-edged ridges on the stem, not true gills.
common chanterelle, identification, foraging, Cantharellus cibarius
Identify Common Morel (_Morchella americana_)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Appearance:
• Young: White ridges and gray to brown pits (called the 'white morel' or 'gray morel').
• Aged: Ridges and pits turn yellow-brown ('yellow morel').
• Can grow up to 12 inches tall if conditions are right.

**Identification...
Appearance:
• Young: White ridges and gray to brown pits (called the 'white morel' or 'gray morel').
• Aged: Ridges and pits turn yellow-brown ('yellow morel').
• Can grow up to 12 inches tall if conditions are right.

Identification Tips:
• Look for a conical cap with vertically elongated ridges and pits.
• Check the color change from white to yellow as it ages.
common morel, identification, foraging, Morchella americana
Identify Half-Free Morel (_Morchella punctipes_)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Appearance:
• Cap is attached to the stem at about the middle (not fused at the base).
• Small caps and long, bulbous stems.

Identification Tips:
• Look for a cap that is not fully fused to the stem.
• May be confused with the wrinkled...
Appearance:
• Cap is attached to the stem at about the middle (not fused at the base).
• Small caps and long, bulbous stems.

Identification Tips:
• Look for a cap that is not fully fused to the stem.
• May be confused with the wrinkled thimble-cap (_Verpa bohemica_), which has a free cap except at the top.
• The wrinkled thimble-cap is edible in moderation.
half-free morel, identification, foraging, Morchella punctipes
Identify Hen of the Woods Mushrooms
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Recognize physical characteristics:
- Caps are spoon-shaped and grayish brown on top and white beneath.
- Form large clusters with a short, off-center stalk.
- Spore print is white.
2. Observe size: A single clump...
Procedure:

1. Recognize physical characteristics:
- Caps are spoon-shaped and grayish brown on top and white beneath.
- Form large clusters with a short, off-center stalk.
- Spore print is white.
2. Observe size: A single clump can grow up to 3 feet wide and weigh up to 100 pounds.
3. Check growth location: Found growing on the ground at the base of trees and stumps, especially oak trees during fall (September–November) in hardwood forests with mature and aging trees.
4. Note habit: Grows as a single large, circular cluster.

Cautions:
• Hen of the woods has no poisonous look-alikes but may be confused with similar species of pore fungi that are tough and inedible.
• If what you have tastes leathery or otherwise unpleasant, it is likely not hen of the woods.
hen of the woods, identification, mushroom foraging, Grifola frondosa
Identify Morel Mushrooms (_Morchella_)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Description:
• Cap: Honeycombed with black to brownish-black ridges and yellowish-brown pits.
• Stem: Sometimes enlarged at the base, completely hollow.
• Spores: White to cream-colored, located inside the pits.

Size: 2–12...
Description:
• Cap: Honeycombed with black to brownish-black ridges and yellowish-brown pits.
• Stem: Sometimes enlarged at the base, completely hollow.
• Spores: White to cream-colored, located inside the pits.

Size: 2–12 inches tall.

When and Where:
• Found on the ground in moist woodlands and river bottoms.
• Associated with ash trees, dying elms, and apple trees.
• Appears from March to early May.

Identification Tips:
• Look for vertically elongated ridges and pits on an elongate, conical cap.
• Check if the stem is hollow at the base.
morel mushrooms, identification, foraging, Morchella
Prepare Chicken of the Woods Mushrooms for Consumption
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Select only young mushrooms: Gather only the fresh, young mushrooms with vibrant color.
2. Avoid tough inner portions: Cook only the tender outer edges of the caps because the inner portions are tough and woody.
3....
Procedure:

1. Select only young mushrooms: Gather only the fresh, young mushrooms with vibrant color.
2. Avoid tough inner portions: Cook only the tender outer edges of the caps because the inner portions are tough and woody.
3. Cooking method:
- Slice the mushroom.
- Simmer in stock for 45 minutes.
- Serve creamed on toast or use as a chicken substitute in casseroles, enchiladas, and other dishes.

Cautions:
• As the mushroom ages and the color fades, it will turn tough and woody.
• If you experience any allergic reaction (e.g., swollen lips), discontinue consumption immediately.
chicken of the woods, cooking, mushroom preparation, Laetiporus cincinnatus, Laetiporus sulphureus
Prepare Hen of the Woods Mushrooms for Consumption
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Select only fresh mushrooms: Use only the fresh, tender portions.
2. Cooking method:
- Simmer in salted water until tender (requires long, slow cooking).
- Serve as a vegetable with cream sauce, add to soup, or chill...
Procedure:

1. Select only fresh mushrooms: Use only the fresh, tender portions.
2. Cooking method:
- Simmer in salted water until tender (requires long, slow cooking).
- Serve as a vegetable with cream sauce, add to soup, or chill after cooking and use on salads.

Cautions:
• Avoid using tough or leathery portions, which may indicate the mushroom is not hen of the woods.
• If you experience any discomfort after consumption, notify your doctor immediately.
hen of the woods, cooking, mushroom preparation, Grifola frondosa

Food Preparation (18) -

Prepare Portable Lemonade
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Tartaric acid: 1/2 oz.
• Loaf sugar: 3 oz.
• Essence of lemon: 1/2 dr.

Procedure:
1. Powder the tartaric acid and loaf sugar very fine in a marble or wedgewood mortar (never use a metal one).
2. Mix them together...
Ingredients:
• Tartaric acid: 1/2 oz.
• Loaf sugar: 3 oz.
• Essence of lemon: 1/2 dr.

Procedure:
1. Powder the tartaric acid and loaf sugar very fine in a marble or wedgewood mortar (never use a metal one).
2. Mix them together thoroughly.
3. Add essence of lemon by a few drops at a time, stirring the mixture after each addition until all is added.
4. Mix the ingredients thoroughly and divide into 12 equal parts.
5. Wrap each portion in a piece of white paper.
6. When ready to use, dissolve one portion in a tumbler of cold water to make fine lemonade with the flavor of lemon juice and peel, and ready sweetened.
Portable Lemonade, preparation, food remedy, tartaric acid, essence of lemon
Prepare Capillaire Syrup
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Capillaire

1. Mix 6 eggs well beaten with 14 pounds of loaf sugar and 3 pounds of coarse sugar.
2. Put the mixture into 3 quarts of water.
3. Boil it twice, skimming it well each time.
4. Add a quarter of a pint of orange-flower water.
5....
Capillaire

1. Mix 6 eggs well beaten with 14 pounds of loaf sugar and 3 pounds of coarse sugar.
2. Put the mixture into 3 quarts of water.
3. Boil it twice, skimming it well each time.
4. Add a quarter of a pint of orange-flower water.
5. Strain the mixture through a jelly-bag.
6. Pour the syrup into bottles for use.
7. To make a drink, add a spoonful or two of this syrup to either cold or warm water.
capillaire, eggs, sugar, orange-flower water, syrup, drink
Prepare Chicken Salad
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:

• Cooked chicken pieces
• Seasoned shredded lettuces
• White Mayonnaise sauce
• Hard-boiled eggs, cut in quarters
• Hearts of cabbage-lettuce
• Lettuce hearts for garnish
• Optional: plover's eggs, truffles, anchovies, capers,...
Ingredients:

• Cooked chicken pieces
• Seasoned shredded lettuces
• White Mayonnaise sauce
• Hard-boiled eggs, cut in quarters
• Hearts of cabbage-lettuce
• Lettuce hearts for garnish
• Optional: plover's eggs, truffles, anchovies, capers, colored butter (lobster coral or green Ravigotte), tarragon, chervil-leaves

Procedure:
1. Prepare the chickens as directed for a Mayonnaise.
2. Pile the pieces of chicken in the dish upon a bed of seasoned shredded lettuces, forming a conical shape.
3. Pour some white Mayonnaise sauce over the chicken pieces.
4. Place a border of hard-boiled eggs cut in quarters around the base of the dish.
5. Surround the base with hearts of cabbage-lettuce.
