6. Top the salad with a white heart of lettuce.

Garnishing Options:
• Use plover's eggs, decorated with truffles or anchovies and capers.
• Garnish with colored butter (lobster coral or green Ravigotte), tarragon, or chervil-leaves laid flat on the eggs or stuck into their pointed ends.
chicken salad, mayonnaise, lettuce, eggs, garnish, truffles, anchovies, capers, tarragon, chervil
Prepare Chocolate Drops
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Chocolate Drops

1. Scrape the chocolate to powder and add an ounce of it to each pound of sugar.
2. Moisture the paste with clear water and work it as described above for confectionary drops.
3. Ensure that all the prepared paste is used, as...
Chocolate Drops

1. Scrape the chocolate to powder and add an ounce of it to each pound of sugar.
2. Moisture the paste with clear water and work it as described above for confectionary drops.
3. Ensure that all the prepared paste is used, as reusing it on the fire will cause greasing and result in improper thickness.
chocolate drops, chocolate powder, sugar, clear water, paste
Prepare Clove Drops
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Clove Drops

1. Prepare the drops as described for peppermint drops.
2. Use either pounded cloves or the essence of cloves in the mixture.
3. Ensure that the gloves (likely a typo and should be "drops") are black, heavy, pungent, hot to the...
Clove Drops

1. Prepare the drops as described for peppermint drops.
2. Use either pounded cloves or the essence of cloves in the mixture.
3. Ensure that the gloves (likely a typo and should be "drops") are black, heavy, pungent, hot to the taste, and full of oil.
clove drops, cloves, essence of cloves, sugar
Prepare Confectionary Drops
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Confectionary Drops

1. Take double-refined sugar and pound it.
2. Sift the sugar through a hair sieve (not too fine), then through a silk sieve to remove all the fine dust that would destroy the beauty of the drop.
3. Put the sugar into a clean...
Confectionary Drops

1. Take double-refined sugar and pound it.
2. Sift the sugar through a hair sieve (not too fine), then through a silk sieve to remove all the fine dust that would destroy the beauty of the drop.
3. Put the sugar into a clean pan and moisten it with any favorite aromatic, such as rose-water, poured in slowly while stirring with a paddle.
4. Color the mixture with a small quantity of liquid carmine or another color ground fine.
5. Take a small pan with a lip, fill it three parts with paste, and place it on a small stove where the half hole is the size of the pan.
6. Stir the sugar with a little ivory or bone handle until it becomes liquid.
7. When it almost boils, remove it from the fire and continue to stir.
8. If the mixture is too moist, add a spoonful of powdered sugar to the paste and stir until it reaches the right consistency (it should run without too much extension).
9. Use a clean and smooth tin plate.
10. Hold the small pan in the left hand and use a piece of iron, copper, or silver wire (four inches long) in the right hand to take off the drop from the lip of the pan.
11. Let the drops fall regularly onto the tin plate.
12. After two hours, remove the drops with the blade of a knife.
confectionary drops, sugar, rose-water, carmine, tin plate, wire, pan
Prepare Ginger Drops
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ginger Drops

1. Pound and sift the required quantity of ginger through a silk sieve according to the desired strength.
2. Add the sager (a type of paste or mixture) with clear water.
3. Use China ginger, which is aromatic as well as hot and...
Ginger Drops

1. Pound and sift the required quantity of ginger through a silk sieve according to the desired strength.
2. Add the sager (a type of paste or mixture) with clear water.
3. Use China ginger, which is aromatic as well as hot and sharp-tasted.
ginger drops, ginger, sager, clear water
Prepare Liquorice Lozenges
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Liquorice Lozenges

1. Take extract of liquorice and double-refined sugar, each 10 ounces.
2. Add 3 ounces of powdered tragacanth.
3. Powder all ingredients thoroughly.
4. Make them into lozenges with rose-water.
5. These are agreeable pectorals...
Liquorice Lozenges

1. Take extract of liquorice and double-refined sugar, each 10 ounces.
2. Add 3 ounces of powdered tragacanth.
3. Powder all ingredients thoroughly.
4. Make them into lozenges with rose-water.
5. These are agreeable pectorals and may be used at pleasure for tickling coughs.
6. Use refined extract of liquorice, which should be easily powdered in the cold after being laid for some days in a dry and rather warm place.
liquorice lozenges, extract of liquorice, sugar, tragacanth, rose-water
Prepare Lobster Butter
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:

• Lobster spawn or oval
• Twice as much butter
• 1 anchovy
• A little Cayenne pepper

Procedure:
1. Procure some lobster spawn or oval.
2. Pound the lobster spawn with twice as much butter, one anchovy, and a little Cayenne...
Ingredients:

• Lobster spawn or oval
• Twice as much butter
• 1 anchovy
• A little Cayenne pepper

Procedure:
1. Procure some lobster spawn or oval.
2. Pound the lobster spawn with twice as much butter, one anchovy, and a little Cayenne pepper until well combined.
3. Rub the mixture through a hair sieve to achieve a smooth consistency.
4. Collect the strained mixture into a small basin.
5. Keep it in a cool place until ready for use.
lobster butter, lobster spawn, butter, anchovy, cayenne pepper
Prepare Lobster Salad
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:

• Lobster meat (from tails and claws)
• Oil
• Vinegar
• Pepper
• Salt
• Shred lettuce or salad of any kind (seasoned with oil, vinegar, pepper, and salt)
• Scarlet Mayonnaise sauce
• Hearts of lettuces (divided in halves)
-...
Ingredients:

• Lobster meat (from tails and claws)
• Oil
• Vinegar
• Pepper
• Salt
• Shred lettuce or salad of any kind (seasoned with oil, vinegar, pepper, and salt)
• Scarlet Mayonnaise sauce
• Hearts of lettuces (divided in halves)
• Plover's eggs
• Green tarragon
• White heart of lettuce

Procedure:
1. Break the lobster shells and remove the meat whole from the tails and claws.
2. Place the lobster meat into a basin with a little oil, vinegar, pepper, and salt.
3. Reserve the pith and coral to make lobster butter (see recipe above).
4. Spread a circular foundation of lobster butter on the bottom of the dish, about seven inches in diameter and one-fourth of an inch thick.
5. Scoop out the center of the butter layer, leaving a circular band around the edge.
6. Drain the lobster meat on a cloth and arrange it in oval scallops.
7. Use some of the lobster butter to stick the pieces firmly together and pile them up in three successive rows, leaving the center hollow.
8. Fill the hollow center with shredded lettuce or salad seasoned with oil, vinegar, pepper, and salt.
9. Pour some scarlet Mayonnaise sauce over the salad without mashing the lobster pieces.
10. Garnish the base with a border of hearts of lettuces divided in halves.
11. Surround these with a border of plover's eggs, each with a small sprig of green tarragon stuck into the pointed end.
12. Place a white heart of lettuce on top and serve.
lobster salad, lobster meat, mayonnaise sauce, lettuce, plover's eggs, tarragon
Prepare Mince-Meat
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:

• 4 pounds of beef and tongue (mixed)
• 3 pounds of suet
• 8 pounds of chopped apples
• 3 pounds of currants (washed, dried, and picked)
• 3 pounds of seeded raisins
• 6 pounds of light brown sugar
• 2 pounds of citron cut into...
Ingredients:

• 4 pounds of beef and tongue (mixed)
• 3 pounds of suet
• 8 pounds of chopped apples
• 3 pounds of currants (washed, dried, and picked)
• 3 pounds of seeded raisins
• 6 pounds of light brown sugar
• 2 pounds of citron cut into small thin pieces
• Rind of 1 orange grated
• 1 ounce of cinnamon
• 1/4 of an ounce of cloves
• 1/4 of an ounce of mace
• 1/4 of an ounce of allspice
• 3 nutmegs (grated)
• 1 quart of Madeira wine
• 1 pint of Brandy

Procedure:
1. Boil the beef and tongue in salted water until tender.
2. Once cooled, chop the meat very fine.
3. Free the suet from every particle of skin and chop it fine.
4. Mix the chopped suet through the meat with just enough salt to remove the fresh taste.
5. Add 8 pounds of chopped apples to the mixture.
6. Add the sugar, fruit (currants, seeded raisins, citron), spice (cinnamon, cloves, mace, allspice, grated nutmeg), and other ingredients (Madeira wine, Brandy) to the meat mixture.
7. Mix all components thoroughly together until well combined.
8. Cover the mixture tightly in a container for storage.
9. If the mince-meat is too dry before use, moisten it with a little sweet cider as needed.

Note:
• Mince-meat can be made much richer by using uncooked instead of cooked meat.
mince meat, beef, suet, apples, currants, raisins, sugar, citron, spices, Madeira wine, brandy
Prepare Orange-Flower Drops
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Orange-Flower Drops

1. Prepare the drops as described for sugar drops.
2. Use orange-flower water instead of other aromatics.
3. Alternatively, use the essence of neroli, which is the essential oil of that flower.
orange-flower drops, orange-flower water, essence of neroli
Prepare Peppermint Drops
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Peppermint Drops

1. Ensure extreme cleanliness in the preparation process.
2. Use the finest sugar available.
3. Add a few drops of the essence of peppermint to the mixture.
peppermint drops, sugar, essence of peppermint
Prepare Potted Lobster
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:

• Fresh lobsters
• Clarified fresh butter (one-third part per pound of lobster)
• Anchovies (4 whole anchovies per pound of lobster)
• Mace, peppercorns, and a little salt

Procedure:
1. Select very fresh lobsters for...
Ingredients:

• Fresh lobsters
• Clarified fresh butter (one-third part per pound of lobster)
• Anchovies (4 whole anchovies per pound of lobster)
• Mace, peppercorns, and a little salt

Procedure:
1. Select very fresh lobsters for potting.
2. Remove the meat, pith, and coral from the shells and cut them into slices.
3. Place all ingredients in a stewpan with one-third part of clarified fresh butter per pound of lobster.
4. Add 4 whole anchovies (washed and wiped dry) per pound of lobster to the stewpan.
5. Season with mace, peppercorns, and a little salt.
6. Cover the stewpan with a lid and simmer the mixture very gently over a slow fire for about a quarter of an hour.
7. After simmering, thoroughly pound the mixture in a mortar until smooth.
8. Rub the pounded mixture through a sieve to achieve a fine texture.
9. Transfer the mixture into small pots.
10. Steam the lobster mixture until it is ready to be used.
11. Once cooled, press down the mixture with the bowl of a spoon to ensure even distribution.
12. Cover the surface with a little clarified butter before serving.
potted lobster, fresh lobster, clarified butter, anchovies, mace, peppercorns, salt
Prepare Sidney Smith's Salad
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:

• 2 large potatoes, passed through a kitchen sieve
• A single spoon of mordant mustard (avoid overly sharp condiments)
• Salt (double quantity if desired)
• Three times the spoon of oil of Lucca
• Once the spoon of vinegar from...
Ingredients:

• 2 large potatoes, passed through a kitchen sieve
• A single spoon of mordant mustard (avoid overly sharp condiments)
• Salt (double quantity if desired)
• Three times the spoon of oil of Lucca
• Once the spoon of vinegar from town
• Two well-boiled eggs, pounded yellow
• Onion atoms (finely chopped onion)
• A magic teaspoon of anchovy sauce

Procedure:
1. Pass 2 large potatoes through a kitchen sieve.
2. Add a single spoon of mordant mustard to the mixture. Avoid overly sharp condiments.
3. Add double quantity of salt if desired (optional).
4. Add three times the spoon of oil of Lucca.
5. Add once the spoon of vinegar from town.
6. Add the pounded yellow of two well-boiled eggs.
7. Mix in finely chopped onion atoms to the bowl, allowing them to subtly animate the whole mixture.
8. Toss a magic teaspoon of anchovy sauce over the salad.

Notes:
• This recipe is designed for an epicurean experience and can be served with confidence even if other meats are unavailable or undercooked.
salad, recipe, potatoes, mustard, oil of Lucca, vinegar, eggs, anchovy sauce, onion, Sidney Smith
Prepare Snow Balls
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Snow Balls

1. Pare and remove the cores of 5 large baking apples.
2. Fill the holes with orange or quince marmalade.
3. Take some good hot paste, roll the apples in it, and make the crust an equal thickness.
4. Place them in a tin dripping-pan...
Snow Balls

1. Pare and remove the cores of 5 large baking apples.
2. Fill the holes with orange or quince marmalade.
3. Take some good hot paste, roll the apples in it, and make the crust an equal thickness.
4. Place them in a tin dripping-pan and bake in a moderate oven.
5. When taken out of the oven, prepare icing for them.
6. Let the icing be a quarter of an inch thick.
7. Set the snow balls a good distance from the fire until the icing hardens.
8. Be cautious that they are not browned.
snow balls, apples, marmalade, hot paste, icing, baking
Prepare Solid Syllabub
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Solid Syllabub

1. To a quart of rich cream, add a quart of white wine.
2. Add the juice of 2 lemons and the rind of 1 grated lemon.
3. Sweeten the mixture to taste.
4. Whip it up well until frothy.
5. Take off the froth as it rises and place it...
Solid Syllabub

1. To a quart of rich cream, add a quart of white wine.
2. Add the juice of 2 lemons and the rind of 1 grated lemon.
3. Sweeten the mixture to taste.
4. Whip it up well until frothy.
5. Take off the froth as it rises and place it in a hair sieve.
6. Let the mixture stand in a cool place overnight.
7. The next day, half-fill glasses with the scum (froth).
8. Heap up the froth as high as possible on top of the scum.
9. The bottom will look clear and it will keep for several days.
solid syllabub, cream, white wine, lemon juice, grated lemon rind, froth, hair sieve
Prepare Whipped Syllabub
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Whipped Syllabub

1. Rub a lump of loaf sugar on the outside of a lemon, then put it into a pint of thick cream.
2. Sweeten the mixture to taste.
3. Squeeze in the juice of a lemon.
4. Add a glass of Madeira wine or French brandy.
5. Mill the...
Whipped Syllabub

1. Rub a lump of loaf sugar on the outside of a lemon, then put it into a pint of thick cream.
2. Sweeten the mixture to taste.
3. Squeeze in the juice of a lemon.
4. Add a glass of Madeira wine or French brandy.
5. Mill the mixture to a froth using a chocolate mill.
6. As the froth rises, take it off and place it in a hair sieve.
7. Fill one-half of the glass with red wine.
8. Lay the froth as high as possible on top of the wine.
9. Ensure that the froth is well drained in the sieve to prevent mixing with the wine, which would spoil the syllabub.
whipped syllabub, cream, lemon, Madeira wine, French brandy, froth, hair sieve

Fabric Care (18) -

Removing Stains from Silk Using Lemon Essence and Turpentine
Critical Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Mix together in a phial 2 oz. of essence of lemon, 1 oz. of oil of turpentine.
2. For grease and other spots on silk, gently rub the affected area with a linen rag dipped in the above composition.
3. Benzine may be used instead of...
Procedure:
1. Mix together in a phial 2 oz. of essence of lemon, 1 oz. of oil of turpentine.
2. For grease and other spots on silk, gently rub the affected area with a linen rag dipped in the above composition.
3. Benzine may be used instead of the above mixture.

Notes:
• This method is effective for removing stains from silk.
stain removal,silk,lemon essence,turpentine,benzine
Removing Grease Spots from Silks and Colored Muslins Using French Chalk
High Priority Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Scrape French chalk onto the grease spot.
2. Hold it near the fire, or over a warm iron, or water-plate filled with boiling water.
3. The grease will melt and be absorbed by the French chalk.
4. Brush or rub it off.
5. Repeat if...
Procedure:
1. Scrape French chalk onto the grease spot.
2. Hold it near the fire, or over a warm iron, or water-plate filled with boiling water.
3. The grease will melt and be absorbed by the French chalk.
4. Brush or rub it off.
5. Repeat if necessary.

Notes:
• This method is effective for removing grease spots from silks and colored muslins.
grease removal,french chalk,boiling water,silks,muslins
Cleaning Silk Stockings with Soap and Dyes
High Priority Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Wash the silk stockings with soap and water.
2. Simmer them in the same for 10 minutes, rinsing in cold water.
3. For a blue cast, put 1 drop of liquid blue into a pan of cold spring-water, run the stockings through this for a...
Procedure:
1. Wash the silk stockings with soap and water.
2. Simmer them in the same for 10 minutes, rinsing in cold water.
3. For a blue cast, put 1 drop of liquid blue into a pan of cold spring-water, run the stockings through this for a minute or two, and dry them.
4. For a pink cast, put 1 or 2 drops of saturated pink dye into cold water and rinse them through this.
5. For a flesh color, add a little rose pink in a thin soap liquor, rub them with clean flannel, and calender or mangle them.

Notes:
• This method is specifically for silk stockings.
cleaning,silk stockings,soap,dye,blue,pink,flesh color
Dipping Rusty Black Silks for Red Dye
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. If the silk requires to be red dyed, boil logwood and in half an hour put in the silk and let it simmer for half an hour.
2. Take it out and dissolve a little blue vitriol and green copperas.
3. Cool the copper and let it simmer...
Procedure:
1. If the silk requires to be red dyed, boil logwood and in half an hour put in the silk and let it simmer for half an hour.
2. Take it out and dissolve a little blue vitriol and green copperas.
3. Cool the copper and let it simmer for 1/2 hour.
4. Dry it over a stick in the air.

Notes:
• If not red dyed, pin it out and rinse it in spring water with 1/2 teaspoonful of oil of vitriol added.
• Work it about 5 minutes, rinse in cold water, and finish by pinning and rubbing it with gumwater.
dyeing,rusty black silks,logwood,blue vitriol,copperas,oil of vitriol,gumwater
Cleaning Black Silks Using Bullock's Gall
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Add boiling water to bullock's gall sufficient to make it warm.
2. With a clean sponge, rub the silk well on both sides.
3. Squeeze it well out and proceed again in like manner.
4. Rinse it in springwater and change the water until...
Procedure:
1. Add boiling water to bullock's gall sufficient to make it warm.
2. With a clean sponge, rub the silk well on both sides.
3. Squeeze it well out and proceed again in like manner.
4. Rinse it in springwater and change the water until perfectly clean.
5. Dry it in the air and pin it out on a table.
6. First dip the sponge in glue-water and rub it on the wrong side.
7. Dry it before a fire.

Notes:
• This method is specifically for black silks.
cleaning,black silks,bullock's gall,springwater,glue-water
Cleaning Colored Silks Using Soft Soap and Water
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Put some soft soap into boiling water and beat it until dissolved in a strong lather.
2. At hand heat, put the silk article into the solution.
3. If the silk is strong, rub it as in washing.
4. Rinse it quickly in warm water.
5....
Procedure:
1. Put some soft soap into boiling water and beat it until dissolved in a strong lather.
2. At hand heat, put the silk article into the solution.
3. If the silk is strong, rub it as in washing.
4. Rinse it quickly in warm water.
5. Add oil of vitriol sufficient to give another water a sourish taste if for bright yellow, crimsons, maroons, and scarlets; but for oranges, fawns, browns, or their shades, use no acid.
6. For bright scarlet, use a solution of tin.
7. Gently squeeze the silk and then roll it in a coarse sheet and wring it.
8. Hang it in a warm room to dry.
9. Finish by calendering or mangling.

Notes:
• For pinks, rose colors, and thin shades, use lemon juice, white tartar, or vinegar instead of oil of vitriol or solution of tin.
• For blues, purples, and their shades, add a small quantity of pearlash to restore the color. Wash like linen but gently squeeze and sheet them when dry. Finish with fine gum-water or dissolved isinglass to which some pearlash has been added, rubbed on the wrong side; then pin them out.
• Blues of all shades are dyed with archil and afterwards dipped in a vat. Twice cleaning with pearlash restores the color.
• For olive greens, use a small quantity of verdigris dissolved in water or a solution of copper mixed with the water to revive the color again.
• Grease spots may be removed by benzine.
cleaning,colored silks,soft soap,warm water,oil of vitriol,pearlash,verdigris,benzine
Bleach Wool, Silks, and Straw Bonnets Using Brimstone
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Place a chafing-dish with lighted charcoal into a close room or large box.
2. Sprinkle 1 or 2 oz. of powdered brimstone on the hot coals in the dish.
3. Hang the articles (wool, silks, straw bonnets) in the room or box.
4. Make...
Procedure:
1. Place a chafing-dish with lighted charcoal into a close room or large box.
2. Sprinkle 1 or 2 oz. of powdered brimstone on the hot coals in the dish.
3. Hang the articles (wool, silks, straw bonnets) in the room or box.
4. Make sure the door is fastened securely to contain the fumes.
5. Let the articles hang for several hours until they are sufficiently bleached or sulphured.
bleach, wool, silks, straw bonnets, brimstone
Clean Scarlet Cloth Using Soap and Tin Solution
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Dissolve the best white soap in warm water.
2. If black-looking spots appear on the cloth, rub dry soap directly onto them.
3. While the soap is dissolving, brush off the soap with hot water.
4. For very dirty cloth, immerse it...
Procedure:
1. Dissolve the best white soap in warm water.
2. If black-looking spots appear on the cloth, rub dry soap directly onto them.
3. While the soap is dissolving, brush off the soap with hot water.
4. For very dirty cloth, immerse it into the warm soap solution and rub the stained parts.
5. Dispatch the cloth quickly (do not let it sit in the solution too long).
6. As soon as the color begins to give, wring the cloth out and immerse it in a pan or pail of warm water.
7. Wring it again and immerse it in cool spring-water mixed with a tablespoonful of solution of tin.
8. Stir the mixture and let it soak for 10 minutes.
9. Hang the cloth to dry in the shade.
10. Cold press the cloth after drying.
clean, scarlet cloth, soap, tin solution, fabric care
Clean Scarlet Cloth Using Wheaten Bran and Tartar
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Pour 1/4 of a peck of wheaten bran into a hair sieve.
2. Pour boiling water over the bran in the sieve to create a bran-water solution.
3. At hand-heat (warm but not hot), immerse the cloth in the bran-water and rub it, looking...
Procedure:
1. Pour 1/4 of a peck of wheaten bran into a hair sieve.
2. Pour boiling water over the bran in the sieve to create a bran-water solution.
3. At hand-heat (warm but not hot), immerse the cloth in the bran-water and rub it, looking through it to see the spots.
4. Prepare a second liquor by adding nearly 1/4 oz. of white or crude tartar to the bran-water.
5. If the cloth is darkened, make a clean liquor of cold spring-water with a drop or two of solution of tin.
6. Soak the cloth in this new solution for 10 minutes, stirring it occasionally.
7. Hang the cloth up to dry.
clean, scarlet cloth, wheaten bran, tartar, fabric care
Cleaning Fabrics with French Chalk
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Strew French chalk over the fabric.
2. Brush it off with a hard brush once or twice.

Notes:
• This method is effective for removing stains and dirt from fabrics.
cleaning,fabric,chalk,brush,stain removal
Dip Scarlet Cloth for Color Restoration
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. After thoroughly cleaning the cloth with soap and rinsing it in warm water, prepare a boiling spring-water solution.
2. Add 1/4 lb. of young fustic or zant, 1 dr. of pounded and sifted cochineal, and an equal quantity of cream of...
Procedure:
1. After thoroughly cleaning the cloth with soap and rinsing it in warm water, prepare a boiling spring-water solution.
2. Add 1/4 lb. of young fustic or zant, 1 dr. of pounded and sifted cochineal, and an equal quantity of cream of tartar to the boiling water.
3. Boil for 5-6 minutes, then cool by adding 1 or 2 pts. of cold springwater and 1 tablespoonful of solution of tin.
4. Stir the mixture thoroughly.
5. Immerse the cloth in the solution and boil it for 10 minutes.
6. Once dry, cold press the cloth to complete the process.
dip, scarlet cloth, color restoration, fabric care
Dry Clean Cloth Using Gall and Sand
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Dip a brush in warm gall and apply it to greasy areas of the cloth.
2. Rinse off the gall with cold water.
3. Dry the cloth by the fire.
4. Lay the coat flat and strew damp sand over it.
5. Use a brush to beat the sand into the...
Procedure:
1. Dip a brush in warm gall and apply it to greasy areas of the cloth.
2. Rinse off the gall with cold water.
3. Dry the cloth by the fire.
4. Lay the coat flat and strew damp sand over it.
5. Use a brush to beat the sand into the cloth until the dirt is removed.
6. Brush out the sand with a hard brush.
7. Rub a drop of oil of olives over the cloth with a soft brush to brighten the colors.
dry clean, fabric care, gall, sand, oil of olives
Make Breeches-Ball for Dyeing or Coloring Fabric
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Mix the following ingredients:
- 1 lb. of Bath brick
- 2 lbs. of pipe-clay
- 4 oz. of pumice-stone powder
- 6 oz. of ox galls
2. Color the mixture with pigments such as rose-pink, yellow ochre, umber, or Irish slate to...
Procedure:
1. Mix the following ingredients:
- 1 lb. of Bath brick
- 2 lbs. of pipe-clay
- 4 oz. of pumice-stone powder
- 6 oz. of ox galls
2. Color the mixture with pigments such as rose-pink, yellow ochre, umber, or Irish slate to achieve the desired shade.
3. Use this colored mixture for dyeing or coloring fabric, particularly breeches.
make, breeches-ball, dyeing, fabric care, pigments
Prevent Prints from Fading Using Salt and Soap Lather
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Wash the dress in lather rather than applying soap directly to muslin.
2. Make a lather by boiling soap and water together until it is sufficiently cool for use.
3. Before putting the dress into the lather, throw in a handful of...
Procedure:
1. Wash the dress in lather rather than applying soap directly to muslin.
2. Make a lather by boiling soap and water together until it is sufficiently cool for use.
3. Before putting the dress into the lather, throw in a handful of salt.
4. Rinse the dress in clear, cold water with a little salt added, without wringing it.
5. Remove the dress and rinse it in a fresh supply of clear water and salt.
6. Wring the dress in a cloth and hang it to dry immediately, spreading it open as much as possible to avoid one part lying over another.
7. If there are any white areas in the pattern, mix a little blue into the water used for rinsing.
prevent fading, fabric care, salt, soap lather, blue rinse
Raise the Nap on Cloth
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Soak the cloth in cold water for 1/2 an hour.
2. Place it on a board and rub the thread-bare parts with a half-worn hatter's card filled with flocks or with a prickly thistle until a nap is raised.
3. Hang the cloth up to dry.
4....
Procedure:
1. Soak the cloth in cold water for 1/2 an hour.
2. Place it on a board and rub the thread-bare parts with a half-worn hatter's card filled with flocks or with a prickly thistle until a nap is raised.
3. Hang the cloth up to dry.
4. Use a hard brush to lay the nap in the correct direction.
raise, nap, fabric care, brushing
Remove Iron-Moulds from Linen Using Oxalic Acid or Lemon Juice
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure 1 (Oxalic Acid Method):
1. Hold the iron-mould on the cover of a tankard of boiling water.
2. Rub a little oxalic acid or salt of sorrel onto the spot.
3. Allow the cloth to thoroughly absorb the acid.
4. Wash the cloth in lye to...
Procedure 1 (Oxalic Acid Method):
1. Hold the iron-mould on the cover of a tankard of boiling water.
2. Rub a little oxalic acid or salt of sorrel onto the spot.
3. Allow the cloth to thoroughly absorb the acid.
4. Wash the cloth in lye to remove the stain.

Procedure 2 (Lemon Juice Method):
1. Wet the stained area with lemon juice.
2. Sprinkle salt over the spot and lay the cloth in the sun until it dries.
3. Repeat this process multiple times until the stain is completely removed.
remove, iron-moulds, linen, oxalic acid, lemon juice
Revive Faded Black Cloth Using Logwood and Copperas
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Clean the cloth thoroughly.
2. Boil 2 or 3 oz. of logwood for 1/2 an hour.
3. Dip the cloth in warm water, squeeze it dry, and put it into a copper vessel.
4. Boil the cloth in the copper vessel for another 1/2 an hour.
5. Remove...
Procedure:
1. Clean the cloth thoroughly.
2. Boil 2 or 3 oz. of logwood for 1/2 an hour.
3. Dip the cloth in warm water, squeeze it dry, and put it into a copper vessel.
4. Boil the cloth in the copper vessel for another 1/2 an hour.
5. Remove the cloth from the vessel and add a small piece of green copperas (ferrous sulfate).
6. Boil the cloth again for another 1/2 an hour.
7. Hang it in the air for an hour or two to allow it to cool.
8. Rinse the cloth in 2 or 3 cold waters.
9. Dry the cloth and regularly brush it with a soft brush, over which 1 or 2 drops of oil of olives have been rubbed.
revive, faded black cloth, logwood, copperas, fabric care
Cleaning Fabrics Using Soap Solution
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Pass the fabric through a solution of fine hard soap at hand heat, drawing them through the hand.
2. Rinse in lukewarm water.
3. Dry and finish by pinning out.
4. Brush the flossy or bright side with a clean clothes-brush in the...
Procedure:
1. Pass the fabric through a solution of fine hard soap at hand heat, drawing them through the hand.
2. Rinse in lukewarm water.
3. Dry and finish by pinning out.
4. Brush the flossy or bright side with a clean clothes-brush in the direction of the nap.
5. Finish by dipping a sponge into a size made by boiling isinglass in water, and rub the wrong side.
6. Rinse out a second time.
7. Brush and dry near a fire or in a warm room.

Notes:
• Silks may be treated in the same way but not brushed.
• If silks are for dyeing, instead of passing them through a solution of soap and water they must be boiled off.
• For very stout silks, use only enough heat to extract the dirt. After rinsing in warm water, they are ready for dye.
cleaning,fabric,care,soap,solution,boiling,isinglass

Chemical Preparation (16) -

Make Butyric Acid
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Make Butyric Acid:

1. Dissolve 6 lbs. of sugar and 1/2 oz. of tartaric acid in 26 lbs. of water.
2. Allow the solution to stand for several days.
3. Add 8 oz. of putrid cheese, 3 lbs. of skimmed and curdled sour milk, and 3 lbs. of levigated...
To Make Butyric Acid:

1. Dissolve 6 lbs. of sugar and 1/2 oz. of tartaric acid in 26 lbs. of water.
2. Allow the solution to stand for several days.
3. Add 8 oz. of putrid cheese, 3 lbs. of skimmed and curdled sour milk, and 3 lbs. of levigated chalk.
4. Keep the mixture in a warm place at approximately 92° Fahr., stirring occasionally.
5. After about 6 weeks, butyric acid will form and combine with lime from the chalk.
6. To separate the butyric acid:
- Add hydrochloric (muriatic) acid and distill.
- Neutralize the distillate with carbonate of soda and re-distill.
- Saturate the distillate with fused chloride of calcium and re-distill.
butyric acid,fermentation,acid preparation
Make Carbonic Acid
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make carbonic acid:

1. Pour muriatic acid onto fragments of chalk or marble.
2. Collect the gas by allowing the delivery tube to pass to the bottom of a receiving vessel, as the gas is heavy and may be collected without water.
carbonic acid, muriatic acid, preparation
Make Chlorine
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make chlorine:

1. Mix muriatic acid with black oxide of manganese.
2. Heat the mixture gently to produce chlorine gas.
3. Collect the gas like carbonic acid, by allowing the delivery tube to pass to the bottom of a receiving vessel.
4....
To make chlorine:

1. Mix muriatic acid with black oxide of manganese.
2. Heat the mixture gently to produce chlorine gas.
3. Collect the gas like carbonic acid, by allowing the delivery tube to pass to the bottom of a receiving vessel.
4. Warning: Chlorine is toxic; take precautions to avoid inhalation.
chlorine, muriatic acid, black oxide of manganese, preparation
Make Laughing Gas
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make laughing gas:

1. Prepare nitrate of ammonia by adding carbonate of ammonia to nitric acid until no more gas is released.
2. Allow the prepared nitrate of ammonia to stand over water for some time.
3. Heat gently in a flask or retort to...
To make laughing gas:

1. Prepare nitrate of ammonia by adding carbonate of ammonia to nitric acid until no more gas is released.
2. Allow the prepared nitrate of ammonia to stand over water for some time.
3. Heat gently in a flask or retort to produce laughing gas.
4. Warning: Ensure proper ventilation and avoid direct inhalation.
laughing gas, nitrate of ammonia, preparation, chemical process
Make Sulphuretted Hydrogen
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make sulphuretted hydrogen:

1. Pour dilute sulphuric acid onto sulphuret of iron.
2. Alternative method to prepare sulphuret of iron:
- Apply a roll of sulphur to a bar of iron heated white hot.
- Or heat in a crucible a mixture of...
To make sulphuretted hydrogen:

1. Pour dilute sulphuric acid onto sulphuret of iron.
2. Alternative method to prepare sulphuret of iron:
- Apply a roll of sulphur to a bar of iron heated white hot.
- Or heat in a crucible a mixture of 2 parts (by weight) of iron filings and 1 part of flowers of sulphur.
sulphuretted hydrogen, sulphuric acid, preparation
Prepare Ginger-Beer Powders
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare ginger-beer powders:

1. Mix the following ingredients in a Wedgwood's ware mortar:
- 2 drams of fine loaf sugar.
- 8 grains of ginger.
- 26 grains of carbonate of potassa (all in fine powder).
2. Keep 27 grains of citric or...
To prepare ginger-beer powders:

1. Mix the following ingredients in a Wedgwood's ware mortar:
- 2 drams of fine loaf sugar.
- 8 grains of ginger.
- 26 grains of carbonate of potassa (all in fine powder).
2. Keep 27 grains of citric or tartaric acid separate from the mixture.
3. To prepare the beer:
- Fill two tumbler-glasses with water, each about half full.
- Stir the compound powder into one glass and the acid powder into another.
- Mix the contents of both glasses; an effervescence will occur due to carbon dioxide release from the carbonate of potassa.
4. The beer is ready for consumption but may become flat if left to stand, as it loses its carbonic acid.
ginger-beer powders, preparation, chemical process
Prepare Soda Water from Powders
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare soda water:

1. Use the following powders for one glass:
- 30 grains of carbonate of soda.
- 25 grains of tartaric or citric acid.
2. Mix the two powders in separate glasses, each half-filled with water.
3. Combine the two...
To prepare soda water:

1. Use the following powders for one glass:
- 30 grains of carbonate of soda.
- 25 grains of tartaric or citric acid.
2. Mix the two powders in separate glasses, each half-filled with water.
3. Combine the two liquids; an effervescence will occur due to the release of carbon dioxide from the carbonate of soda.
4. The soda water is ready for consumption and may be drunk immediately.
soda water, preparation, chemical process
Use Gun-Cotton as a Filter
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To use gun-cotton as a filter:

1. Ensure the gun-cotton is carefully prepared and not acted upon by chemical agents at ordinary temperatures.
2. Use it to filter solutions containing strong acids, alkalies, etc., due to its resistance to...
To use gun-cotton as a filter:

1. Ensure the gun-cotton is carefully prepared and not acted upon by chemical agents at ordinary temperatures.
2. Use it to filter solutions containing strong acids, alkalies, etc., due to its resistance to chemical action.
gun-cotton, filtration, preparation
Make Artificial Saxon Blue
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Mix a divided earth prussiate of iron at the moment of its formation and precipitation.
2. Into a solution of 144 grs. of sulphate of iron, pour a solution of yellow prussiate of potash.
3. At the time of forming the iron, add...
Procedure:

1. Mix a divided earth prussiate of iron at the moment of its formation and precipitation.
2. Into a solution of 144 grs. of sulphate of iron, pour a solution of yellow prussiate of potash.
3. At the time of forming the iron, add a solution of 2 oz. of alum to the same vessel, then pour in the solution of potash just enough to decompose the sulphate of alumina.
4. Ensure that the amount of alkali is not excessive; leave some alum to be removed later by washing.
5. After adding the alkaline liquor, the precipitated alumina becomes mixed with prussiate of iron, reducing its intensity to match common Saxon blue.
6. Filter the mixture, wash it in clean water, and dry it.

Important Notes:
• The intensity of the final product may vary depending on how much sulphate of alumina is decomposed.
artificial saxon blue,prussiate of iron,sulphate of alumina,alkali,washing
Make Blue Verditer
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Dissolve copper in cold nitric acid (aquafortis).
2. Precipitate the solution using quicklime in sufficient quantity to absorb all the acid, ensuring a pure oxide of copper precipitate.
3. Decant the liquor, wash the...
Procedure:

1. Dissolve copper in cold nitric acid (aquafortis).
2. Precipitate the solution using quicklime in sufficient quantity to absorb all the acid, ensuring a pure oxide of copper precipitate.
3. Decant the liquor, wash the precipitate, and spread it on a linen cloth to drain.
4. Take a portion of the green precipitate and place it on a grinding-stone; add quicklime powder in amounts from 7 to 10 parts per 100 parts of precipitate.
5. The addition of lime will change the green color into a beautiful blue.
6. Once the mixture reaches a paste-like consistency, allow it to desiccate.

Important Notes:
• Blue verditer is suitable for distemper and varnish but not for oil painting, as oil darkens it. If used in oil painting, brighten with a large amount of white.
blue verditer,nitric acid,quicklime,copper oxide,paste consistency
Make Cadmium Yellow
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Cadmium yellow is a compound of cadmium and sulphur.
2. It can be obtained by precipitating from a salt of cadmium using either:
- A current of sulphuretted hydrogen gas, or
- An alkaline sulphide.

**Important...
Procedure:

1. Cadmium yellow is a compound of cadmium and sulphur.
2. It can be obtained by precipitating from a salt of cadmium using either:
- A current of sulphuretted hydrogen gas, or
- An alkaline sulphide.

Important Notes:
• This method produces a stable yellow pigment suitable for various applications.
cadmium yellow,cadmium sulphide,sulphuretted hydrogen,alkaline sulphide
Make Chrome Yellow
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. To a solution of bichromate of potassa, add a solution of nitrate of lead until a precipitate forms.
2. Wash and dry the resulting precipitate.

Important Notes:
• This process produces chrome yellow, which is commonly...
Procedure:

1. To a solution of bichromate of potassa, add a solution of nitrate of lead until a precipitate forms.
2. Wash and dry the resulting precipitate.

Important Notes:
• This process produces chrome yellow, which is commonly used in painting.
chrome yellow,bichromate of potassa,nitrate of lead,washing,drying
Make Lemon Yellow (Steinbuhl Yellow)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Mix hot saturated solutions of bichromate of potassa and nitrate of baryta.
2. The mixture will produce a precipitate, which should be washed and dried.

Important Notes:
• This method yields lemon yellow (Steinbuhl...
Procedure:

1. Mix hot saturated solutions of bichromate of potassa and nitrate of baryta.
2. The mixture will produce a precipitate, which should be washed and dried.

Important Notes:
• This method yields lemon yellow (Steinbuhl yellow), considered superior to chrome yellow in quality.
lemon yellow,steinbuhl yellow,bichromate of potassa,nitrate of baryta,washing,drying
Prepare Bichloride of Tin
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Take 4 lbs. of commercial nitric acid.
2. Add 1/2 lb. of sal ammoniac to a stone vessel.
3. Put in 1/2 lb. of pure granulated tin, or dissolve granulated tin in a mixture of:
- 2 parts muriatic acid
- 1 part nitric acid
4....
Procedure:

1. Take 4 lbs. of commercial nitric acid.
2. Add 1/2 lb. of sal ammoniac to a stone vessel.
3. Put in 1/2 lb. of pure granulated tin, or dissolve granulated tin in a mixture of:
- 2 parts muriatic acid
- 1 part nitric acid
4. Continue dissolving until any tin is taken up.
bichloride of tin, salt of tin, nitromuriate of tin, chemical preparation
Prepare Cobalt Blue (Bleu de Thenard)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Reduce ore to powder and calcine it in a reverberatory furnace, stirring frequently.
2. Ensure the chimney of the furnace has a strong draught to carry off arsenical and sulphurous acid vapors.
3. Continue calcination until...
Procedure:

1. Reduce ore to powder and calcine it in a reverberatory furnace, stirring frequently.
2. Ensure the chimney of the furnace has a strong draught to carry off arsenical and sulphurous acid vapors.
3. Continue calcination until these vapors cease; this can be determined by collecting gas in a ladle and checking for the presence or absence of a garlic odor.
4. Boil the result slightly in an excess of weak nitric acid in a glass matrass.
5. Decant the supernatant liquor, then evaporate the solution nearly to dryness in a platina or porcelain capsule.
6. Throw the residuum into boiling water, filter it, and add a solution of subphosphate of soda to precipitate an insoluble phosphate of cobalt.
7. Wash the precipitate well on a filter and collect it while still in a gelatinous form.
8. Mix with eight times its weight of alumina (also in gelatinous form), ensuring uniform tint throughout the mixture.
9. Spread the mixture on smooth plates, place them in a stove, and allow to dry until brittle.
10. Pound the dried mixture in a mortar inside a covered earthen crucible and heat it to a cherry-red for half an hour.
11. After cooling, open the crucible; if done correctly, the desired product will be obtained.

Important Notes:
• Ensure alumina is precipitated from alum using sufficient ammonia and thoroughly purified by washing with charcoal-filtered water.
cobalt blue,Bleu de Thenard,calcination,nitric acid,alumina,precipitation
Prepare Nitrate of Iron
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Take 10 1/4 lbs. each of nitric and muriatic acids.
2. Add little by little 72 1/2 lbs. of copperas dissolved in water.
nitrate of iron, chemical preparation

Chemical Preparations (16) -

Prepare Chlorine Water
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare chlorine water:

1. Generate chlorine gas by reacting commercial muriatic acid with black oxide of manganese.
2. Pass the chlorine gas into a bottle that is half-filled with distilled water.
3. Shake the bottle occasionally to ensure...
To prepare chlorine water:

1. Generate chlorine gas by reacting commercial muriatic acid with black oxide of manganese.
2. Pass the chlorine gas into a bottle that is half-filled with distilled water.
3. Shake the bottle occasionally to ensure proper absorption of the gas.
4. The water will absorb twice its volume of chlorine gas, turning yellowish in color and acquiring the characteristic smell of chlorine.
5. Store the solution in the dark or in a bottle covered with blue paper to prevent degradation.

Uses: This solution is used in medicine and for bleaching linen and removing fruit marks.
chlorine water, preparation, chemical process, bleaching agent
Prepare Sulphurous Acid Water
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare sulphurous acid water:

1. Generate sulphurous gas by reacting sulphuric acid with copper, charcoal, or sulphur.
2. Pass the sulphurous gas into a bottle that is half-filled with distilled water.
3. Shake the bottle occasionally to ensure...
To prepare sulphurous acid water:

1. Generate sulphurous gas by reacting sulphuric acid with copper, charcoal, or sulphur.
2. Pass the sulphurous gas into a bottle that is half-filled with distilled water.
3. Shake the bottle occasionally to ensure proper absorption of the gas.
4. The water will absorb 50 volumes of sulphurous gas.

Uses: This solution is used for bleaching purposes, in medicine, and to check fermentation.
sulphurous acid water, preparation, chemical process, bleaching agent
Prepare Blue Litmus Paper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare blue litmus paper:

1. Bruise 1 oz. of litmus in a mortar.
2. Add boiling water and triturate together.
3. Transfer the mixture to a flask and add more boiling water to make up to half a pint.
4. When cool, strain the liquid.
5. Dip paper...
To prepare blue litmus paper:

1. Bruise 1 oz. of litmus in a mortar.
2. Add boiling water and triturate together.
3. Transfer the mixture to a flask and add more boiling water to make up to half a pint.
4. When cool, strain the liquid.
5. Dip paper into the solution and allow it to absorb evenly.
6. Dry the paper in the air.

Reaction:
• Acids change the color to red.
• It does not become green with alkalies.
blue litmus paper, preparation, chemical test paper
Prepare Brazil Paper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare Brazil paper:

1. Make a strong decoction of Brazil wood.
2. Dip the paper into the decoction and allow it to absorb the liquid thoroughly.
3. Dry the paper completely.

Reaction:
• Alkalies render the paper purple or violet.
• Acids...
To prepare Brazil paper:

1. Make a strong decoction of Brazil wood.
2. Dip the paper into the decoction and allow it to absorb the liquid thoroughly.
3. Dry the paper completely.

Reaction:
• Alkalies render the paper purple or violet.
• Acids generally turn it yellow.
brazil paper, preparation, chemical test paper
Prepare Cabbage Paper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare cabbage paper:

1. Make a strong infusion of red cabbage leaves.
2. Strain the liquid and evaporate it gently until considerably reduced in volume.
3. Dip the paper into the concentrated solution and allow it to absorb evenly.
4. Dry the...
To prepare cabbage paper:

1. Make a strong infusion of red cabbage leaves.
2. Strain the liquid and evaporate it gently until considerably reduced in volume.
3. Dip the paper into the concentrated solution and allow it to absorb evenly.
4. Dry the paper in the air.

Reaction:
• Alkalies change the color to green.
• Acids turn it red.
• This is a very delicate test; if slightly green from an alkali, carbonic acid will restore the original color.
cabbage paper, preparation, chemical test paper
Prepare Carlsbad Water
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare Carlsbad water:

1. Dissolve the following ingredients in a pint of carbonated water:
- Chloride of calcium: 8 grains.
- Tincture of chloride of iron: 1 drop.
- Sulphate of soda: 50 grains.
- Carbonate of soda: 8 grains.
carlsbad water, preparation, saline carbonated waters
Prepare Elderberry Paper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare elderberry paper:

1. Stain the paper with the juice of elderberries.
2. Allow it to dry completely.

Reaction:
• Acids change the blue color to red.
• Alkalies turn it green.
elderberry paper, preparation, chemical test paper
Prepare Indigo Paper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare indigo paper:

1. Immerse the paper in a solution of sulphate of indigo.
2. Wash the paper with water that has been made slightly alkaline.
3. Rinse it again with pure water.
4. Dry the paper in the air.
indigo paper, preparation, chemical test paper
Prepare Iodide of Potassium and Starch Paper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare iodide of potassium and starch paper:

1. Mix a starch paste with a solution of iodide of potassium.
2. Moisten bibulous paper with the mixture.

Reaction:
• Exposure to ozone or chlorine will turn the paper blue.
iodide of potassium and starch paper, preparation, chemical test paper
Prepare Lead Paper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare lead paper:

1. Dip the paper in a solution of acetate of lead.
2. Allow it to dry completely.

Reaction:
• When moistened, this paper detects sulphuretted hydrogen, which turns it black.
lead paper, preparation, chemical test paper
Prepare Manganese Paper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare manganese paper:

1. Dip the paper in a solution of sulphate of manganese.
2. Allow it to dry completely.

Reaction:
• In an ozonized atmosphere, the paper becomes black.
manganese paper, preparation, chemical test paper
Prepare Red Litmus Paper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare red litmus paper:

1. Follow the same procedure as for blue litmus paper.
2. After straining the infusion, add a few drops of nitric acid or pure acetic acid to the solution before dipping the paper.
red litmus paper, preparation, chemical test paper
Prepare Rhubarb Paper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare rhubarb paper:

1. Make a strong infusion of rhubarb.
2. Dip the paper into the solution and allow it to absorb evenly.
3. Dry the paper completely.

Reaction:
• Alkalies render it brown.
• Unlike turmeric paper, it is not affected by...
To prepare rhubarb paper:

1. Make a strong infusion of rhubarb.
2. Dip the paper into the solution and allow it to absorb evenly.
3. Dry the paper completely.

Reaction:
• Alkalies render it brown.
• Unlike turmeric paper, it is not affected by boracic acid.
rhubarb paper, preparation, chemical test paper
Prepare Rose Paper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare rose paper:

1. Make a strong infusion of the petals of red roses.
2. Dip unsized paper into the solution and allow it to absorb evenly.
3. Dry the paper in the air.

Reaction:
• When dipped in an alkaline solution that is weak enough...
To prepare rose paper:

1. Make a strong infusion of the petals of red roses.
2. Dip unsized paper into the solution and allow it to absorb evenly.
3. Dry the paper in the air.

Reaction:
• When dipped in an alkaline solution that is weak enough not to affect turmeric paper, it assumes a bright green color.
rose paper, preparation, chemical test paper
Prepare Test Papers Using Distilled Water
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare test papers:

1. Use only distilled water in these preparations.
2. Place the liquid in an earthenware plate or dish.
3. Carefully immerse the paper in the liquid to ensure it is uniformly wetted.
4. Dry the paper out of the reach of...
To prepare test papers:

1. Use only distilled water in these preparations.
2. Place the liquid in an earthenware plate or dish.
3. Carefully immerse the paper in the liquid to ensure it is uniformly wetted.
4. Dry the paper out of the reach of acid, ammoniacal, or other vapors that may affect it.
5. Store the dried papers in bottles, jars, or cases for future use.
test paper preparation, distilled water, chemical testing
Prepare Turmeric Paper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare turmeric paper:

1. Boil 1 oz. of coarsely powdered turmeric root in 1/2 a pint of water for 30 minutes.
2. Strain the liquid and dip the paper into it.
3. Dry the paper completely.

Reaction:
• Alkalies render it brown.
• Boracic...
To prepare turmeric paper:

1. Boil 1 oz. of coarsely powdered turmeric root in 1/2 a pint of water for 30 minutes.
2. Strain the liquid and dip the paper into it.
3. Dry the paper completely.

Reaction:
• Alkalies render it brown.
• Boracic acid and borates also turn it brown.
turmeric paper, preparation, chemical test paper

Painting Colors (16) -

Creating Bright Red for Painting
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Bright red is made by mixing lake with vermilion, used for sanguine parts in painting:

• For varnishing small appendages of the toilette: Use a bright red mixture.

Steps:
1. Grind the mixture with **mastic gallipot...
Bright red is made by mixing lake with vermilion, used for sanguine parts in painting:

• For varnishing small appendages of the toilette: Use a bright red mixture.

Steps:
1. Grind the mixture with mastic gallipot varnish.
2. Mix up with the same varnish after grinding.
3. Glaze and polish using camphorated mastic varnish.

Varnish types used: Mastic gallipot for grinding; common gallipot for mixing up; camphorated mastic for glazing.
bright red,painting,varnish,lake,vermilion
Creating Crimson or Rose-color for Painting
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Crimson or rose-color is made using carminated lake, which contains alum charged with the coloring part of cochineal, ceruse, and carmine:

• Ingredients: Requires a particle of vermilion and **white...
Crimson or rose-color is made using carminated lake, which contains alum charged with the coloring part of cochineal, ceruse, and carmine:

• Ingredients: Requires a particle of vermilion and white lead.

Application: This varnish is used for valuable articles only. It is prepared by mixing carminated lake, vermilion, and white lead in appropriate proportions.
crimson,rose-color,painting,varnish,carmine
Creating Green Color for Painting
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Green color can be made by mixing various green pigments with a white ground:

• Simple or compound greens: When mixed with a white ground (such as Bougival white, white of Troyes, or Spanish white), they produce sea-green in varying...
Green color can be made by mixing various green pigments with a white ground:

• Simple or compound greens: When mixed with a white ground (such as Bougival white, white of Troyes, or Spanish white), they produce sea-green in varying intensities.
• For varnish or oil painting: Use metallic oxide such as ceruse or pure white oxide of lead as the white base.

Common green pigments include:
• Green oxides of copper (e.g., chrome green, verdigris)
• Dry crystallized acetate of copper
• Blue verditer
• Dutch pink of Troyes or other yellows
green color,painting,varnish,oil,ceruse
Making Sea-Green for Distemper Painting
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Sea-green for distemper is made by:

1. Grind mountain-green and ceruse separately with water.
2. Mix the ground pigments with parchment size and water, adding ceruse until the desired intensity of color is achieved.
3. Watin...
Sea-green for distemper is made by:

1. Grind mountain-green and ceruse separately with water.
2. Mix the ground pigments with parchment size and water, adding ceruse until the desired intensity of color is achieved.
3. Watin recommends using Dutch pink of Troyes and white oxide of lead in proportions determined by experience for a more durable result.

For a triple composition:
• Mix Dutch pink with blue verditer, then lower the color to sea-green by adding ceruse ground with water.
sea green,distemper,painting,ceruse,blue verditer
Making Sea-Green for Varnish and Oil Painting
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Sea-green for varnish and oil painting requires a more robust formulation:

• Varnish: Requires the color to have more body than in distemper. This is achieved by mixing with oil.
• For metallic surfaces: Use crystallized verdigris,...
Sea-green for varnish and oil painting requires a more robust formulation:

• Varnish: Requires the color to have more body than in distemper. This is achieved by mixing with oil.
• For metallic surfaces: Use crystallized verdigris, dried and pulverized, substituted for common verdigris.

Steps:
1. Grind a quantity of verdigris (pounded and sifted through a silk sieve) separately with nut-oil (half drying and half fat).
2. If painting on metallic surfaces, dilute the mixture with camphorated mastic or gallipot varnish.
3. Grind ceruse with essence or oils to which 1/2 of essence has been added.
4. Mix both colors in proportions relative to the desired intensity (the principal part is ceruse).
5. For valuable articles, cover the painting with a stratum of transparent or turpentine copal varnish for durability.

Note: Sea-greens containing metallic coloring parts are durable and do not change over time.
sea green,varnish,oil,painting,verdigris
Prepare Blue Colors for Painting
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Indigo
• Prussian blue
• Ultramarine
• Saxon blue
• Ceruse
• White (for softening)

Procedure:
1. Choose from vegetable substances like indigo, metallic substances like Prussian blue, stony mineral substances like...
Materials Required:
• Indigo
• Prussian blue
• Ultramarine
• Saxon blue
• Ceruse
• White (for softening)

Procedure:
1. Choose from vegetable substances like indigo, metallic substances like Prussian blue, stony mineral substances like ultramarine, or vitreous substances colored by a metallic oxide like Saxon blue.
2. Ultramarine is best suited for pictures, while Saxon blue can also be used in some cases.
3. When using prussiate of iron or indigo alone, the color may appear too dark and lack splendor; to improve this, mix with white to soften the tone.
Blue color,paint preparation,Ultramarine,Saxon blue
Prepare Blue Distemper Paint
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Ceruse
• Indigo or Prussian blue
• Water

Procedure:
1. Grind ceruse with water to create a base for the distemper.
2. Mix in indigo or Prussian blue to achieve the desired color tone.
3. This mixture produces little...
Materials Required:
• Ceruse
• Indigo or Prussian blue
• Water

Procedure:
1. Grind ceruse with water to create a base for the distemper.
2. Mix in indigo or Prussian blue to achieve the desired color tone.
3. This mixture produces little effect initially but gains brilliance and splendor when covered with a vitreous varnish layer.
Blue distemper,paint preparation,varnish
Prepare Chamois and Buff Color for Painting
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Yellow
• Minium
• Cinnabar
• Ceruse
• Common oil of pinks
• Mastic gallipot varnish
• Common gallipot varnish
• Drying oil

Procedure:
1. Use yellow as the base for chamois color.
2. Add a small amount of minium, or...
Materials Required:
• Yellow
• Minium
• Cinnabar
• Ceruse
• Common oil of pinks
• Mastic gallipot varnish
• Common gallipot varnish
• Drying oil

Procedure:
1. Use yellow as the base for chamois color.
2. Add a small amount of minium, or better yet, cinnabar and ceruse to modify the color.
3. For varnish: Mix 1/2 common oil of pinks with 1/2 mastic gallipot varnish; then combine with common gallipot varnish.
4. For oil painting: Grind the mixture with drying oil.
Chamois color,Buff color,paint preparation,varnish,oil painting
Prepare Golden Yellow Color for Painting
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Naples or Montpellier yellow
• Spanish white
• White of Morat
• Ochre de Berri
• Realgar
• Drying oil
• Pure nut-oil
• Essence

Procedure:
1. Combine Naples or Montpellier yellow with Spanish white or white of Morat to...
Materials Required:
• Naples or Montpellier yellow
• Spanish white
• White of Morat
• Ochre de Berri
• Realgar
• Drying oil
• Pure nut-oil
• Essence

Procedure:
1. Combine Naples or Montpellier yellow with Spanish white or white of Morat to create a base.
2. Add ochre de Berri and realgar in small quantities to achieve the illusion of gold.
3. This mixture can be used for distemper, varnish, or oil painting.
4. For oil painting: Grind the mixture with drying or pure nut-oil added to essence, or mix with drying oil.
Golden yellow,paint preparation,oil painting,varnish,distemper
Prepare Jonquil Color for Distemper and Varnish
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Dutch pink
• Ceruse
• Mastic gallipot varnish
• Gallipot varnish

Procedure:
1. Use a vegetable color as the base.
2. Mix Dutch pink with ceruse to create the foundation of Jonquil color.
3. Grind the mixture with...
Materials Required:
• Dutch pink
• Ceruse
• Mastic gallipot varnish
• Gallipot varnish

Procedure:
1. Use a vegetable color as the base.
2. Mix Dutch pink with ceruse to create the foundation of Jonquil color.
3. Grind the mixture with mastic gallipot varnish for use in distemper or varnish.
4. For varnish, mix the prepared color with gallipot varnish.
Jonquil color,distemper,varnish,painting materials
Prepare Naples and Montpellier Yellow Paint
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Yellow ochre
• Ceruse
• Water (for distemper)
• Drying nut-oil
• Essence
• Camphorated mastic varnish
• Gallipot varnish

Procedure:
1. For Distemper: Mix yellow ochre with ceruse and grind with water.
2. **For...
Materials Required:
• Yellow ochre
• Ceruse
• Water (for distemper)
• Drying nut-oil
• Essence
• Camphorated mastic varnish
• Gallipot varnish

Procedure:
1. For Distemper: Mix yellow ochre with ceruse and grind with water.
2. For Varnish: Combine drying nut-oil and essence in equal parts, then mix with camphorated mastic varnish for delicate objects or gallipot varnish for other uses.
3. The splendor of the color depends on the amount of ceruse used; adjust accordingly based on the coloring matter employed.
4. If intended for oil painting, omit the grinding with oil added to essence and use only essence.
5. Oil provides more pliability and body to the paint.
Naples yellow,Montpellier yellow,paint preparation,distemper,varnish,oil painting
Prepare Olive Color for Distemper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Yellow (as previously described)
• Indigo
• Ceruse
• Spanish white

Procedure:
1. Use the yellow base as previously prepared.
2. Add indigo, ceruse, or Spanish white to create olive color for distemper.
3. Ensure the...
Materials Required:
• Yellow (as previously described)
• Indigo
• Ceruse
• Spanish white

Procedure:
1. Use the yellow base as previously prepared.
2. Add indigo, ceruse, or Spanish white to create olive color for distemper.
3. Ensure the mixture is thoroughly blended and suitable for use in distemper.
Olive color,distemper,paint preparation
Prepare Olive Color for Oil and Varnish
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Yellow de Berri or d'Auvergne
• Verdigris
• Charcoal
• Mastic gallipot varnish
• Common gallipot varnish
• Drying oil
• Essence

Procedure:
1. Combine yellow de Berri or d'Auvergne with a small amount of verdigris and...
Materials Required:
• Yellow de Berri or d'Auvergne
• Verdigris
• Charcoal
• Mastic gallipot varnish
• Common gallipot varnish
• Drying oil
• Essence

Procedure:
1. Combine yellow de Berri or d'Auvergne with a small amount of verdigris and charcoal to create olive color.
2. Grind the mixture with mastic gallipot varnish for use in varnish, or mix with common gallipot varnish.
3. For oil painting: Grind the mixture with oil added to essence, then combine with drying oil.
Olive color,paint preparation,varnish,oil painting
Prepare Prussian Blue Paint for Varnish and Oil Painting
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Ceruse
• Drying oil
• Essence
• Camphorated mastic varnish
• Gallipot varnish
• Turpentine copal varnish (for expensive articles)

Procedure:
1. For varnish: Grind ceruse with drying oil or essence, then mix with...
Materials Required:
• Ceruse
• Drying oil
• Essence
• Camphorated mastic varnish
• Gallipot varnish
• Turpentine copal varnish (for expensive articles)

Procedure:
1. For varnish: Grind ceruse with drying oil or essence, then mix with camphorated mastic varnish for delicate objects or gallipot varnish for wainscoting.
2. If intended for oil painting, grind ceruse with oil added to essence or use only essence.
3. Add a sufficient quantity of Prussian blue to achieve the desired tone.
4. For expensive articles: Glaze the paint with turpentine copal varnish after applying it.
Prussian blue,paint preparation,varnish,oil painting
Preparing Blue Verditer for Painting
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Blue verditer is a preparation made from oxide of copper and lime. It can be used in various ways depending on the application:

• For delicate articles: Grind with pure alcoholic varnish added to a little essence, or mix with compound...
Blue verditer is a preparation made from oxide of copper and lime. It can be used in various ways depending on the application:

• For delicate articles: Grind with pure alcoholic varnish added to a little essence, or mix with compound mastic varnish.
• For ceilings, wainscoting, etc.: Use mastic gallipot varnish mixed with a little drying oil for grinding; common gallipot varnish for mixing up.

Note: Blue verditer is soft and dull, requiring a varnish (such as turpentine copal varnish) to heighten the tone and give it play.
blue verditer,painting,varnish,mastic,gallipot
Preparing Ultramarine for Painting
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ultramarine is a vitreous matter colored by oxide of cobalt, used for sky-blues. It differs in tone from prussiate of iron and indigo. For use in distemper, varnish, or oil painting:

• For distemper, varnish, or oil: Use ultramarine as...
Ultramarine is a vitreous matter colored by oxide of cobalt, used for sky-blues. It differs in tone from prussiate of iron and indigo. For use in distemper, varnish, or oil painting:

• For distemper, varnish, or oil: Use ultramarine as is.
• For oil painting: Mix with resinous drying oil to give body to the vitreous matter.
ultramarine,painting,varnish,oil,distemper

Dyeing And Color Preparation (16) -

Make Another Yellow Lake Using Broom-Flowers
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make another Yellow Lake:

1. Make a lye of potash and lime sufficiently strong.
2. Gently boil fresh broom-flowers in the lye until they are white.
3. Remove the flowers and continue boiling the lye in earthen vessels over the fire.
4. Add...
To make another Yellow Lake:

1. Make a lye of potash and lime sufficiently strong.
2. Gently boil fresh broom-flowers in the lye until they are white.
3. Remove the flowers and continue boiling the lye in earthen vessels over the fire.
4. Add as much alum as the liquor will dissolve.
5. Pour this lye into a vessel of clean water, resulting in a yellow color at the bottom.
6. Settle and decant off the clear liquor.
7. Wash the powder found at the bottom with more water until all salts from the lye are washed off.
8. Separate the yellow matter and dry it in the shade.
yellow, lake, broom-flowers, alum, dye
Make Chinese Yellow Using Acacia Flowers
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make Chinese Yellow:

1. Use the acacia, an Egyptian thorn (a species of mimosa), from which the Chinese extract yellow for use in silks, stuffs, and paper painting.
2. Gather flowers before they are fully opened and put them into an...
To make Chinese Yellow:

1. Use the acacia, an Egyptian thorn (a species of mimosa), from which the Chinese extract yellow for use in silks, stuffs, and paper painting.
2. Gather flowers before they are fully opened and put them into an earthen vessel over a gentle heat, stirring continually until nearly dry and yellow in color.
3. To 1/2 lb. of the flowers, add enough rain-water to incorporate the flowers together.
4. Boil until thick, then strain.
5. Add 1/2 oz. of common alum and 1 oz. of calcined oystershells reduced to a fine powder.
6. Mix all into a mass.
7. Adding ripe seeds deepens the color; for the deepest yellow, add a small quantity of Brazil-wood.
chinese yellow, acacia, alum, oystershells, brazil-wood, dye
Make Chrome Green Using Bichromate and Ammonia
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Chrome Green:

1. Mix bichromate of potash with half its weight of muriate of ammonia.
2. Heat the mixture to redness.
3. Wash the mass thoroughly with plenty of boiling water.
4. Dry the residue thoroughly.
5. The result is a...
Chrome Green:

1. Mix bichromate of potash with half its weight of muriate of ammonia.
2. Heat the mixture to redness.
3. Wash the mass thoroughly with plenty of boiling water.
4. Dry the residue thoroughly.
5. The result is a sesquioxide of chromium, which forms the basis of green ink used in bank-note printing.

> Alternative method: Mix chrome yellow and Prussian blue to make chrome green.
chrome green, bichromate of potash, muriate of ammonia, sesquioxide of chromium, ink
Make Orange Lake
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make Orange Lake:

1. Boil 4 oz. of the best anatto and 1 lb. of pearlash in 1 gallon of water for 1/2 hour.
2. Strain the solution through paper.
3. Mix gradually with this 1 1/2 lbs. of alum in another **gallon of...
To make Orange Lake:

1. Boil 4 oz. of the best anatto and 1 lb. of pearlash in 1 gallon of water for 1/2 hour.
2. Strain the solution through paper.
3. Mix gradually with this 1 1/2 lbs. of alum in another gallon of water, stopping when no ebullition occurs during the mixture.
4. Treat the sediment as directed for other kinds of lake.
5. Dry it in square bits or lozenges.
orange, lake, anatto, pearlash, alum, dye
Make Pearl White Using Nitrate of Bismuth
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make a Pearl White:

1. Pour some distilled water into a solution of nitrate of bismuth until precipitation occurs.
2. Filter the solution.
3. Wash the precipitate with distilled water as it lies on the filter.
4. Dry the powder gently by...
To make a Pearl White:

1. Pour some distilled water into a solution of nitrate of bismuth until precipitation occurs.
2. Filter the solution.
3. Wash the precipitate with distilled water as it lies on the filter.
4. Dry the powder gently by heat.
5. The resulting powder is known as pearl white.
pearl white, nitrate of bismuth, precipitate, dye
Make Tunic White as a Substitute for White Lead
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Tunic White:

• Used as a substitute for white lead.
• Can be made by burning zinc or precipitating from a solution using caustic alkali.
• It is the oxide of zinc, and it is not blackened by sulphuretted hydrogen.
tunic white, substitute for white lead, zinc oxide, sulphuretted hydrogen
Make Yellow Lake Using Turmeric-Root
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make a Yellow Lake:

1. Take 1 lb. of turmeric-root in fine powder, 3 pt. of water, and 1 oz. of salt of tartar.
2. Put all into a glazed earthen vessel and boil together over a clear gentle fire until the water is highly...
To make a Yellow Lake:

1. Take 1 lb. of turmeric-root in fine powder, 3 pt. of water, and 1 oz. of salt of tartar.
2. Put all into a glazed earthen vessel and boil together over a clear gentle fire until the water is highly impregnated and stains paper to a beautiful yellow.
3. Filter this liquor.
4. Gradually add a strong solution of alum in water until the yellow matter is all curdled and precipitated.
5. Pour the whole into a filter of paper, allowing the water to run off and leaving the yellow matter behind.
6. Wash it with fresh water until the water runs insipid.
7. The result is the beautiful yellow called lacque of turmeric.

> Note: This method works well for substances of strong texture like madder or logwood, but not for tender species such as rose or violet flowers.
yellow, lake, turmeric-root, alum, dye
Make Yellow Using Natural Materials
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Make a Yellow:

• Gum guttae and terra merita give very beautiful yellows and readily communicate their color to copal varnish made with turpentine.
• Aloes provide a varied and orange tint.
• Chloride of lead tinges vitreous...
To Make a Yellow:

• Gum guttae and terra merita give very beautiful yellows and readily communicate their color to copal varnish made with turpentine.
• Aloes provide a varied and orange tint.
• Chloride of lead tinges vitreous matters of a yellow color, used in glazing Queen's ware. The composition is:
- 80 lbs. of chloride of lead
- 20 lbs. of flints ground very fine
- Mixed with water until the mixture becomes as thick as cream.
• Vessels to be glazed are dipped into this glaze and allowed to dry.
yellow, gum guttae, terra merita, aloes, chloride of lead, glazing
Make Chrome Red
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Melt saltpetre in a crucible heated to dull redness.
2. Throw in gradually chrome yellow until no more red fumes arise.
3. Allow the mixture to settle, pour off the liquid portion, and wash rapidly the sediment.
4. **The liquid...
1. Melt saltpetre in a crucible heated to dull redness.
2. Throw in gradually chrome yellow until no more red fumes arise.
3. Allow the mixture to settle, pour off the liquid portion, and wash rapidly the sediment.
4. The liquid portion contains chromate of potash, and may be used to make chrome yellow.
chrome red, saltpetre, chrome yellow, crucible, chromate of potash
Make Pink Using Cochineal and Brazil-wood
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Boil cochineal with bitartrate of potash and sulphate alumina.
2. Alternatively, prepare a decoction of Brazil-wood with sulphate alumina.
3. The color may be varied by the addition of carbonate potash.
pink, cochineal, bitartrate of potash, sulphate alumina, brazil-wood, carbonate potash
Make Prussian Blue
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Dissolve sulphate of iron (copperas, green vitriol) in water.
2. Boil the solution.
3. Add nitric acid until red fumes cease to come off.
4. Add enough sulphuric acid to render the liquor clear; this is the persulphate of iron.
5....
1. Dissolve sulphate of iron (copperas, green vitriol) in water.
2. Boil the solution.
3. Add nitric acid until red fumes cease to come off.
4. Add enough sulphuric acid to render the liquor clear; this is the persulphate of iron.
5. To this, add a solution of ferrocyanide of potassium (yellow prussiate of potash) as long as any precipitate is produced.
6. Wash this precipitate thoroughly with water acidulated with sulphuric acid.
7. Dry in a warm place.
prussian blue, sulphate of iron, nitric acid, ferrocyanide of potassium, washing precipitate
Make Purple Using Brazil-wood and Logwood
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Prepare a decoction of Brazil-wood and logwood.
2. Combine with carbonate of potash to produce a permanent purple.
purple, brazil-wood, logwood, carbonate of potash, decoction preparation
Make Red Lake Using Brazil Wood and Alum
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Boil 3 lbs. of Brazil wood for an hour in a solution of 3 lbs. of common salt in 3 galls. of water.
2. Filter the hot fluid through paper.
3. Add to this a solution of 5 lbs. of alum in 3 galls. of water.
4. **Dissolve 3 lbs. of the...
1. Boil 3 lbs. of Brazil wood for an hour in a solution of 3 lbs. of common salt in 3 galls. of water.
2. Filter the hot fluid through paper.
3. Add to this a solution of 5 lbs. of alum in 3 galls. of water.
4. Dissolve 3 lbs. of the best pearlash in 1 1/2 galls. of water, and purify it by filtering.
5. Put this gradually to the other until the whole of the color appears to be precipitated, and the fluid is left clear and colorless.
6. If any appearance of purple is seen, add a fresh quantity of the solution of alum by degrees until a scarlet hue is produced.
7. Proceed with the directions given in the first process regarding the sediment.
8. If 1/2 lb. of seed-lac is added to the solution of pearlash, and dissolved in it before its purification by the filter, and 2 lbs. of the wood and a proportional quantity of common salt and water be used in the colored solution, a lake will be produced that stands well in oil or water.
9. However, this lake is not as transparent in oil as without the seed-lac.
brazil wood, alum, pearlash, seed-lac, solution preparation
Make Red Lake Using Cochineal and Pearlash
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Boil 2 oz. of cochineal in 1 pt. of water.
2. Filter the solution through paper.
3. Add 2 oz. of pearlash dissolved in 1/2 pint of warm water, filtered through paper.
4. Make a solution of cuttlebone as in the former process.
5....
1. Boil 2 oz. of cochineal in 1 pt. of water.
2. Filter the solution through paper.
3. Add 2 oz. of pearlash dissolved in 1/2 pint of warm water, filtered through paper.
4. Make a solution of cuttlebone as in the former process.
5. To 1 pt. of it, add 2 oz. of alum dissolved in 1/2 pt. of water.
6. Put this mixture gradually to the cochineal and pearlash as long as any ebullition arises.
7. Proceed as above.
cochineal, pearlash, cuttlebone, alum, solution preparation
Make Red Lake Using Pearlash and Scarlet Shreds
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Dissolve 1 lb. of the best pearlash in 2 qts. of water.
2. Filter the liquor through paper.
3. Add 2 more qts. of water and 1 lb. of clean scarlet shreds.
4. Boil them in a pewter boiler until the shreds have lost their scarlet...
1. Dissolve 1 lb. of the best pearlash in 2 qts. of water.
2. Filter the liquor through paper.
3. Add 2 more qts. of water and 1 lb. of clean scarlet shreds.
4. Boil them in a pewter boiler until the shreds have lost their scarlet color.
5. Take out the shreds and press them, then put the colored water yielded by them to the other solution.
6. Repeat this process with another lb. of the shreds, proceeding in the same manner, and likewise a third and fourth pound.
7. Dissolve 1 1/2 lbs. of cuttle-fish bone in 1 lb. of strong aquafortis in a glass receiver.
8. Add more of the bone if it appears to produce any ebullition in the aquafortis.
9. Pour this strained solution gradually into the other; if any ebullition is occasioned, dissolve and add more cuttle-fish bone until no ebullition occurs.
10. The crimson sediment deposited by this liquor is the lake: pour off the water, and stir the lake in 2 galls. of hard spring-water.
11. Mix the sediment in 2 galls. of fresh water; repeat this method 4 or 5 times.
12. If no hard water can be procured, or if the lake appears too purple, add 1/2 an oz. of alum to each quantity of water before it is used.
13. Drain off the water through a filter covered with a worn linen cloth.
