14. When drained to a proper dryness, let it be dropped through a proper funnel on clean boards, and the drops will become small cones or pyramids in which form the lake must be dried and the preparation is completed.
red lake, pearlash, scarlet shreds, cuttle-fish bone, aquafortis, alum, hard spring water, filter, drying
Make Soluble Prussian Blue
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Add ferrocyanide of potassium to a solution freshly made of green vitriol in water.
2. The white precipitate which falls becomes blue on exposure to the air, and is soluble in water.
soluble prussian blue, ferrocyanide of potassium, green vitriol solution

• Chloride of calcium: 3 grs.
• Sulphate of soda: 8 grs.
• Simple sulphuretted water: 2 1/2 oz.
• Slightly carbonated water: 17 1/2 oz.

Procedure:
1. Combine the bicarbonate...
Ingredients:
• Bicarbonate of soda: 12 grs.
• Common salt: 25 grs.
• Chloride of calcium: 3 grs.
• Sulphate of soda: 8 grs.
• Simple sulphuretted water: 2 1/2 oz.
• Slightly carbonated water: 17 1/2 oz.

Procedure:
1. Combine the bicarbonate of soda, common salt, chloride of calcium, and sulphate of soda in a container.
2. Add the simple sulphuretted water to the mixture.
3. Fill the remaining volume with slightly carbonated water.

1. Combine all the ingredients listed...

Prepare Seltzer Water
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Chloride of calcium: 4 grs.
• Chloride of magnesium: 4 grs.
• Bicarbonate of soda: 8 grs.
• Common salt: 20 grs.
• Phosphate of soda: 2 grs.
• Sulphate of iron: 1/4 of a gr.
• Aerated water: Fill to 20 oz....
Ingredients:
• Chloride of calcium: 4 grs.
• Chloride of magnesium: 4 grs.
• Bicarbonate of soda: 8 grs.
• Common salt: 20 grs.
• Phosphate of soda: 2 grs.
• Sulphate of iron: 1/4 of a gr.
• Aerated water: Fill to 20 oz. bottle

Procedure:
1. Dissolve the chloride of calcium and chloride of magnesium in a small quantity of water.
2. Add this solution to a similar solution containing bicarbonate of soda, common salt, and phosphate of soda.
3. Mix thoroughly and add a solution of sulphate of iron.
4. Put the mixed solution into a 20-oz. bottle and fill up with aerated water.

Alternative Method:
1. Fill a stone Seltzer bottle with water.
2. Add 2 drs. bicarbonate of soda and 2 drs. citrate acid in crystals to the bottle.
3. Cork the bottle immediately.
Seltzer Water, preparation, aerated water, alternative method

• Sulphuretted hydrogen
• Cold water (previously deprived of air by boiling and cooled in a closed vessel)

Procedure:
1. Pass sulphuretted hydrogen into the cold water that has been previously deprived of air by boiling and...
Ingredients:
• Sulphuretted hydrogen
• Cold water (previously deprived of air by boiling and cooled in a closed vessel)

Procedure:
1. Pass sulphuretted hydrogen into the cold water that has been previously deprived of air by boiling and cooled in a closed vessel.
2. Continue passing the gas until it ceases to be absorbed.
Simple Sulphuretted Water, preparation, sulphuretted hydrogen, air removal
Prepare Vichy Salts
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia

• Effloresced sulphate of magnesia: 1 scruple
• Dry tartarized sulphate of iron: 1 gr.
• Dry tartaric acid: 1 oz. (or dry bisulphate of soda: 1 oz.)

Procedure:
1. Mix the bicarbonate of soda, common salt, effloresced sulphate of soda, effloresced sulphate of magnesia, and dry tartarized sulphate of iron in a container.
2. Add either dry tartaric acid or dry bisulphate of soda to the mixture.
3. Ensure all powders are previously dried and keep them in a close bottle.

Prepare Vichy Water (Soubeiran Method)
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Bicarbonate of soda: 135 grs.
• Chloride of sodium: 2 1/2 grs.
• Crystallized chloride of calcium: 2 grs.
• Sulphate of soda: 11 1/2 grs.
• Sulphate of magnesia: 3 3/4 grs.
• Tartrate of iron and potash: 1/8 gr.
• Water: 2 1/10...
Ingredients:
• Bicarbonate of soda: 135 grs.
• Chloride of sodium: 2 1/2 grs.
• Crystallized chloride of calcium: 2 grs.
• Sulphate of soda: 11 1/2 grs.
• Sulphate of magnesia: 3 3/4 grs.
• Tartrate of iron and potash: 1/8 gr.
• Water: 2 1/10 pts. (1 litre)
• Carbonic acid: 305 cubic inches (5 litres)

Procedure:
1. Dissolve the bicarbonate of soda and tartrate of iron and potash in part of the water.
2. Add the sulphate of magnesia to the mixture.
3. Dissolve the chloride of calcium in the remaining water.
4. Combine all solutions and charge with carbonic acid gas under pressure.
Vichy Water, Soubeiran Method, preparation, salts, carbonic acid
Prepare Aerated Chalybeate Water
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Sulphate of iron: 1 gr.
• Carbonate of soda: 4 grs.
• Water deprived of air and charged with carbonic acid gas: 1 pt.

Procedure:
1. Prepare water that has been deprived of air and charged with carbonic acid gas (aerated...
Ingredients:
• Sulphate of iron: 1 gr.
• Carbonate of soda: 4 grs.
• Water deprived of air and charged with carbonic acid gas: 1 pt.

Procedure:
1. Prepare water that has been deprived of air and charged with carbonic acid gas (aerated water).
2. Add sulphate of iron (1 grain) to the aerated water.
3. Add carbonate of soda (4 grains) to the mixture.
4. Ensure all ingredients are fully dissolved and mixed thoroughly.

Note: Dr. Pereira recommends 10 grs. each of sulphate of iron and bicarbonate of soda in a bottle of ordinary soda water, which is equivalent to 4 grs. of carbonate of iron.
Aerated Chalybeate Water, preparation, water remedy, sulphate of iron, carbonate of soda

Procedure:

2. Add sulphate of iron (1/2 grain) to the boiled water.

Plant Identification (15) -

Identify Water Hemlock by Visual Characteristics
Critical Source: Plant Identification
### Water Hemlock Identification Procedure

Step 1: Observe the Plant's Height
• Water Hemlock is typically 3-6 feet tall.

Step 2: Examine the Leaves
• The leaves are alternate twice-compound, with leaflets sometimes arranged in...
### Water Hemlock Identification Procedure

Step 1: Observe the Plant's Height
• Water Hemlock is typically 3-6 feet tall.

Step 2: Examine the Leaves
• The leaves are alternate twice-compound, with leaflets sometimes arranged in a haphazard manner.
• Leaflets are lance-shaped, pointed, and have numerous teeth.
• Some leaves may be doubly compound or lobed.
• Veins on the leaflets end at the notches between the teeth (a rare feature).

Step 3: Inspect the Flowers
• The flowers are small white, arranged in a flat or rounded umbel (umbrella-shaped cluster), 2-5 inches wide.
• Individual flowers are about 1/8 inch wide, with 5 petals and 5 stamens.

Step 4: Check the Stem
• The stem is smooth, stout, and often mottled or solid purple.
• It is hollow, especially in the lower part of the stem.

Step 5: Examine the Roots
• The roots have fat tuberlike branches.

Step 6: Check the Habitat
• Grows in wet open areas, such as marshes, along shores, and sometimes open swamps.

Important Notes:
• Water Hemlock is one of the most poisonous plants in North America. All parts are deadly poisonous.
• Even a small amount can be fatal to an adult.
• Avoid handling or ingesting any part of this plant, even its juice.
• It is often confused with edible lookalikes (e.g., Water Parsnip, Cowbane, Mock Bishop's Weed).
• Do not use the hollow stems as straws; many poisonings have occurred in this way.
water hemlock, plant identification, poisonous plants, umbel flowers, wet areas
Identify Bulb-bearing Water Hemlock by Flower and Growth Patterns
High Priority Source: Plant Identification
Procedure:

1. Observe the flower structure: Look for sparse, white flowers arranged in an umbel (an umbrella-shaped cluster) about 1-2" wide.
2. Check individual flower details: Each flower is approximately 1/8" wide and has 5 petals...
Procedure:

1. Observe the flower structure: Look for sparse, white flowers arranged in an umbel (an umbrella-shaped cluster) about 1-2" wide.
2. Check individual flower details: Each flower is approximately 1/8" wide and has 5 petals and 5 stamens.
3. Note the growth pattern: The plant typically grows in clusters, with widely spaced alternate compound leaves.
4. Look for bulbs along the stem: Tiny bulbs may be found growing along the stems, though they are not always visible.

Details:
• This plant is DEADLY POISONOUS and should be avoided at all costs.
• The flowers appear to be sparser than those of regular Water Hemlock, which can help in identification.
Bulb-bearing Water Hemlock, flower characteristics, umbel structure, sparse flowers, plant identification
Identify Bulb-bearing Water Hemlock by Stem Characteristics
High Priority Source: Plant Identification
Procedure:

1. Examine the stem for color and texture: Look for a purplish tinge and a 'glaucous' appearance, which is characterized by a whitish bloom that can be easily wiped off.
2. Check for hollow structure: The stems of this plant...
Procedure:

1. Examine the stem for color and texture: Look for a purplish tinge and a 'glaucous' appearance, which is characterized by a whitish bloom that can be easily wiped off.
2. Check for hollow structure: The stems of this plant are hollow, which is a key distinguishing feature.
3. Look for fine vertical lines: Observe the presence of fine vertical lines running along the stem.
4. Note the height and habitat: This plant typically grows 3-7 feet tall and thrives in wet open areas such as marshes, along shores, and sometimes open swamps.

Details:
• The hollow stems can be tempting to children, who may use them as pea shooters, leading to potential poisonings.
• It is crucial to learn this plant well during the summer months so that it can be avoided in winter when identification becomes more difficult.
Bulb-bearing Water Hemlock, stem characteristics, hollow stems, glaucous bloom, plant identification
Identify Poison Sumac by Visual Characteristics
High Priority Source: Plant Identification
### Poison Sumac Identification Procedure

Step 1: Observe the Plant's Height
• Poison Sumac is a shrub or small tree, typically 6-20 feet tall.

Step 2: Examine the Leaves
• The leaves are compound, with **7-13 pointed...
### Poison Sumac Identification Procedure

Step 1: Observe the Plant's Height
• Poison Sumac is a shrub or small tree, typically 6-20 feet tall.

Step 2: Examine the Leaves
• The leaves are compound, with 7-13 pointed leaflets.
• Leaflets have smooth edges and often angle slightly upwards from the leaf stem.
• The buds are hairless.

Step 3: Inspect the Stems and Twigs
• Stems and twigs are hairless.
• Bark is smooth, dark, and speckled with dark spots.

Step 4: Look for Berries
• The berries resemble those of Poison Ivy: spreading or drooping clusters of small white hard berries.

Step 5: Check the Habitat
• Grows in wet areas, such as marshes, swamps, and standing water.

Important Notes:
• Poison Sumac is extremely poisonous to touch.
• Its effects are similar to Poison Ivy (see related procedure for treatment).
poison sumac, plant identification, poison ivy, compound leaves, wet areas
Identify Poison Ivy by Leaf Configuration
Standard Source: Plant Identification
Procedure:

1. Look for a plant with three leaves in a compound leaf configuration.
2. Confirm that the leaves are arranged in groups of three, not more or less.
3. Note that all parts of the plant (leaves, stems, berries) are poisonous.
4....
Procedure:

1. Look for a plant with three leaves in a compound leaf configuration.
2. Confirm that the leaves are arranged in groups of three, not more or less.
3. Note that all parts of the plant (leaves, stems, berries) are poisonous.
4. Be aware that Poison Ivy can grow in various forms:
- In summer: May have toothed or smooth-edged leaves.
- In spring: Leaves may be reddish and fresh.
- In winter: Appears as a small branch with no leaves, but oils remain virulent.
5. Observe the habitat: Poison Ivy commonly grows in open woods, rocky areas, and fields.
6. Look for white berries developing on the plant during late summer or early fall.
7. Be cautious of variations in leaf shape (some may be droopy and glossy; others may be dull and toothed).

Important Notes:
• Do not touch any part of the plant, even if you believe you are immune.
• Avoid burning Poison Ivy, as the oils can become airborne and cause internal poisoning when inhaled.
poison ivy, identification, leaf configuration, three leaves, plant identification, allergic reaction, rash, oil transfer
Avoid Poisonous Plants During Foraging
Standard Source: Plant Identification
Procedure:

1. Be aware that some plants may look similar to edible plants but are poisonous.
2. Never consume any plant unless you are absolutely certain of its identity.
3. Use reliable identification methods, such as examining the tubers...
Procedure:

1. Be aware that some plants may look similar to edible plants but are poisonous.
2. Never consume any plant unless you are absolutely certain of its identity.
3. Use reliable identification methods, such as examining the tubers or bulbs directly rather than relying on flower photos.
4. If in doubt about a plant's identity, do not eat it.
5. Learn to recognize common poisonous plants and their distinguishing features.
6. Always carry a field guide or use other resources to help identify plants during foraging trips.
7. When in doubt, err on the side of caution and avoid consuming any unfamiliar plant.
poisonous plants, foraging safety, identification, edible plants, plant dangers
Identify Juniper Plants for Safe Consumption
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Look for junipers in open, dry, sunny areas throughout North America and northern Europe.
2. Check for the following characteristics:
- Small, scalelike leaves densely crowded around the branches.
- Leaves less than 1.2...
Procedure:

1. Look for junipers in open, dry, sunny areas throughout North America and northern Europe.
2. Check for the following characteristics:
- Small, scalelike leaves densely crowded around the branches.
- Leaves less than 1.2 centimeters long.
- A distinct aroma resembling cedar.
- Berrylike cones that are usually blue and covered with a whitish wax.
3. CAUTION: Many plants may be called cedars but are not related to junipers and may be harmful. Always look for the berrylike structures, needle leaves, and resinous, fragrant sap to confirm it is a juniper.
juniper, identification, edible plants, safe consumption
Identify Lotus Nelumbo Species for Safe Consumption
Standard Source: Edible Medicinal Plants
Procedure:

1. Look for lotus plants in quiet fresh water areas.
2. Identify the species by observing the flowers:
- Yellow-flowered lotus is native to North America.
- Pink-flowered lotus is widespread in the Orient and planted...
Procedure:

1. Look for lotus plants in quiet fresh water areas.
2. Identify the species by observing the flowers:
- Yellow-flowered lotus is native to North America.
- Pink-flowered lotus is widespread in the Orient and planted globally.
3. Check for large, showy flowers and leaves that may float on or rise above the water surface, often reaching 1.5 meters in radius.
4. Look for a distinctive flattened fruit containing up to 20 hard seeds.
lotus identification, edible plants, safe consumption
Harvest and Prepare Jerusalem Artichokes
Reference Source: Plant Identification
Procedure:

1. Carefully dig up the tubers from the ground.
2. Wash them thoroughly before cooking or eating raw.
3. Cook the tubers like potatoes until they are soft.
4. The skin is edible, but you may choose to peel it if desired.
5. Use the...
Procedure:

1. Carefully dig up the tubers from the ground.
2. Wash them thoroughly before cooking or eating raw.
3. Cook the tubers like potatoes until they are soft.
4. The skin is edible, but you may choose to peel it if desired.
5. Use the tubers in any recipe that calls for potatoes or other root vegetables.
6. Store any excess tubers in a cool, dry place for later use.
Jerusalem Artichoke, harvesting, preparation, cooking, foraging
Harvest and Use Wild Leeks
Reference Source: Plant Identification
Procedure:

1. Carefully dig up the onion-like bulbs of the plant.
2. Wash the leaves and bulbs thoroughly before use.
3. The leaves can be eaten raw or cooked, and are suitable for freezing or drying for later use in soups and stews.
4. The...
Procedure:

1. Carefully dig up the onion-like bulbs of the plant.
2. Wash the leaves and bulbs thoroughly before use.
3. The leaves can be eaten raw or cooked, and are suitable for freezing or drying for later use in soups and stews.
4. The bulbs can also be eaten raw or cooked.
5. Only collect when abundant, and take care not to overharvest by collecting scattered patches or individual plants.
6. Use the leaves and bulbs in recipes that call for onions or garlic, as they have a similar flavor profile.
7. Be aware that some people may experience ill effects if large amounts are eaten.
Wild Leeks, harvesting, preparation, cooking, foraging
Identify Black or Yellow Locust Tree
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Black or Yellow Locust (Robinia pseudacacia)

• A tall forest tree up to 80 feet high.
• Leaves are 8 to 14 inches long, with 9 to 19 leaflets, each 1 to 2 inches long.
• Pods are 2 to 4 inches long, containing **4 to 7...
Black or Yellow Locust (Robinia pseudacacia)

• A tall forest tree up to 80 feet high.
• Leaves are 8 to 14 inches long, with 9 to 19 leaflets, each 1 to 2 inches long.
• Pods are 2 to 4 inches long, containing 4 to 7 seeds.
• Commonly found around old lawns.
black locust,yellow locust,Robinia pseudacacia,tree identification
Identify Jerusalem Artichoke by Tubers
Reference Source: Plant Identification
Procedure:

1. Look for plants with small tubers on the roots.
2. Note that the tubers are edible and can be cooked like potatoes until they're soft.
3. The skin of the tuber is edible, but you may choose to peel it if desired.
4. The inside of...
Procedure:

1. Look for plants with small tubers on the roots.
2. Note that the tubers are edible and can be cooked like potatoes until they're soft.
3. The skin of the tuber is edible, but you may choose to peel it if desired.
4. The inside of the tuber is soft and has a mild taste.
5. Use the distinctive circular disc at the end of the stem as an additional identification feature.
6. Do not rely on photos of the flower for identification, as many plants have similar flowers.
7. Confirm the plant by examining the tubers directly.
Jerusalem Artichoke, identification, tuber, edible plants, foraging
Identify Red, Scarlet, Water, or Swamp Maple Tree
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Red, Scarlet, Water, or Swamp Maple (Acer rubrum)

• A fine, tall tree often over 100 feet high.
• Noted for its flaming crimson foliage in fall, as well as red leaf stalks, flowers, and fruit.
• Leaves are 2 to 6 inches long.
-...
Red, Scarlet, Water, or Swamp Maple (Acer rubrum)

• A fine, tall tree often over 100 feet high.
• Noted for its flaming crimson foliage in fall, as well as red leaf stalks, flowers, and fruit.
• Leaves are 2 to 6 inches long.
• Produces sugar, though not much.
• Found in Western North America.
red maple,scarlet maple,swamp maple,Acer rubrum,tree identification
Identify White Ash Tree
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
White Ash (Fraxinus Americana)

• A fine tree found on moist soil.
• Height: 70 to 130 feet.
• Leaves are yellow in autumn, and the tree is known for being last to leaf and first to shed in the forest.
• Called white due to the...
White Ash (Fraxinus Americana)

• A fine tree found on moist soil.
• Height: 70 to 130 feet.
• Leaves are yellow in autumn, and the tree is known for being last to leaf and first to shed in the forest.
• Called white due to the silvery undersides of the leaves.
• Each leaflet is 3 to 6 inches long, with 8 to 12 inch long leaves.
• Found from Nova Scotia to Texas.
white ash,Fraxinus Americana,tree identification
Identify Wild Leeks by Appearance and Smell
Reference Source: Plant Identification
Procedure:

1. Look for plants with onion-like bulbs growing in the deep woods.
2. The plant has leaves that are up to about 8" tall and have a strong onion smell when bruised or crushed.
3. The flower stem is smooth and does not have any leaves...
Procedure:

1. Look for plants with onion-like bulbs growing in the deep woods.
2. The plant has leaves that are up to about 8" tall and have a strong onion smell when bruised or crushed.
3. The flower stem is smooth and does not have any leaves on it.
4. Wild Leeks appear in the spring, usually before most other plants begin to grow.
5. The leaves die off before the flowers bloom, so you may only see the bulb and stalk during certain times of the year.
6. Only collect when abundant, and take care not to overharvest by collecting scattered patches or individual plants.
7. If they do not smell like onions, they are not Wild Leeks.
Wild Leeks, identification, onion smell, foraging, edible plants

Insect Control (13) -

Apply Coal-Oil to Eliminate Insects on Plants and Trees
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:
1. Mix 1 pt. of soft soap with half the quantity of coal-oil.
2. Stir the mixture into 7 or 8 galls. of rain-water.
3. Use a powerful syringe to apply the solution:
- Apply it after the blossoming of the tree and when...
Steps:
1. Mix 1 pt. of soft soap with half the quantity of coal-oil.
2. Stir the mixture into 7 or 8 galls. of rain-water.
3. Use a powerful syringe to apply the solution:
- Apply it after the blossoming of the tree and when immature fruit begins to fall.
- Continue the application for 3 or 4 nights in succession, then once or twice a week thereafter.
4. For cabbage plants, saturate coarse chips from a planing-mill with undiluted coal-oil and place them around each plant to prevent cut-worms.
coal-oil,insect control,plants,trees
Create an Ant Trap Using a Sponge and Sugar
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:
1. Procure a large sponge, wash it well, and press it dry to open the cells.
2. Sprinkle fine white sugar on the sponge.
3. Place the sponge near where ants are troublesome.
4. The ants will collect on the sponge and take up...
Steps:
1. Procure a large sponge, wash it well, and press it dry to open the cells.
2. Sprinkle fine white sugar on the sponge.
3. Place the sponge near where ants are troublesome.
4. The ants will collect on the sponge and take up residence in its cells.
5. To destroy the ants, dip the sponge in boiling water.
6. Note: Cyanide of potassium is used in Cuba but is a violent poison and not recommended for use.
ant trap,sponge,sugar,insect control
Destroy Body Lice Using Coculus Indicus Infusion
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:
1. Take coculus indicus: 1 oz.
2. Boil 1 pt. of water.
3. Add the coculus indicus to the boiling water and let it steep until cool.
4. Use this infusion on the affected area.
body lice,coculus indicus,insect control
Destroy Body Lice Using Corrosive Sublimate Solution
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:
1. Mix the following ingredients:
- Corrosive sublimate: 1 dr.
- Sal ammoniac: 2 drs.
- Water: 8 oz.
2. Use this solution as the first treatment, as it is more cleanly.
body lice,corrosive sublimate,insect control
Destroy Body Lice Using Mercurial Ointment
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:
1. Apply mercurial ointment well to the infected part.
2. Wash off the ointment with warm water and soap.
3. In the army, a common practice was to wear a string saturated with the ointment around the waist as a means of...
Steps:
1. Apply mercurial ointment well to the infected part.
2. Wash off the ointment with warm water and soap.
3. In the army, a common practice was to wear a string saturated with the ointment around the waist as a means of protection.
4. This method may produce salivation, so caution is advised.
body lice,mercurial ointment,insect control
Destroy Fleas on Animals Using Coculus Indicus or Coal-Oil
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:
1. Wash the animal with an infusion of coculus indicus.
2. Alternatively, use coal-oil for washing.
3. After washing, rinse the animal with soap and warm water.
fleas on animals,coculus indicus,coal-oil,insect control
Destroy Insects in Grain Using a Vacuum Method
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:
1. Place the grain into a hollow cast-iron cylinder.
2. Partially exhaust the air from the cylinder to create a vacuum.
3. No animal can live in this environment due to the lack of air and moisture.
4. Fermentation ceases as there is...
Steps:
1. Place the grain into a hollow cast-iron cylinder.
2. Partially exhaust the air from the cylinder to create a vacuum.
3. No animal can live in this environment due to the lack of air and moisture.
4. Fermentation ceases as there is no air or moisture present.
5. On a large scale, the vacuum is created by filling a communicating cylinder with steam, which is then condensed.
6. A vacuum of 20 inches is sufficient for this purpose.
grain storage,vacuum method,insect control
Destroy Slugs and Earthworms Using Carbonate of Ammonia Solution
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:
1. Prepare a solution by mixing 1 oz. of carbonate of ammonia with 1 gallon of water.
2. Water the plants with this solution.
3. The slugs and earthworms will come to the surface and perish.
4. The ammonia in the solution will also...
Steps:
1. Prepare a solution by mixing 1 oz. of carbonate of ammonia with 1 gallon of water.
2. Water the plants with this solution.
3. The slugs and earthworms will come to the surface and perish.
4. The ammonia in the solution will also promote plant growth.
slugs,earthworms,carbonate of ammonia,insect control
Make Roach Poison Using Phosphorus and Lard
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:
1. Put 1 drachm of phosphorus in a flask with 2 oz. of water.
2. Plunge the flask into hot water until the phosphorus is melted.
3. Pour the contents into a mortar with 2 or 3 oz. of lard.
4. Triturate (grind) briskly,...
Steps:
1. Put 1 drachm of phosphorus in a flask with 2 oz. of water.
2. Plunge the flask into hot water until the phosphorus is melted.
3. Pour the contents into a mortar with 2 or 3 oz. of lard.
4. Triturate (grind) briskly, adding water, and then add 1/2 lb. of flour and 1 or 2 oz. of brown sugar.
5. Plaster of Paris mixed with oatmeal is also said to destroy roaches.
roach poison,phosphorus,lard,insect control
Make Roach Wafers Using Flour, Sugar, and Red-Lead
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:
1. Combine flour, sugar, and red-lead.
2. Heat the mixture in wafer irons to create roach wafers.
roach wafers,flour,sugar,red-lead,insect control
Prevent Ants from Entering Closets or Drawers Using a Corrosive Sublimate Solution
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:
1. Prepare the following solution:
- Corrosive sublimate: 1 oz.
- Muriate of ammonia: 2 oz.
- Water: 1 pint.
2. Use a brush dipped in this solution to draw a line around the shelf or drawer to be protected.
3. The...
Steps:
1. Prepare the following solution:
- Corrosive sublimate: 1 oz.
- Muriate of ammonia: 2 oz.
- Water: 1 pint.
2. Use a brush dipped in this solution to draw a line around the shelf or drawer to be protected.
3. The ants will not cross this line.
4. This same solution can also be used to destroy bed-bugs by applying it to cracks with a feather or brush.
ant prevention,closets,drawers,corrosive sublimate,insect control
Use Chloride of Lime to Destroy Insects in Stables and Gardens
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:
1. Scatter chloride of lime on a plank in a stable to drive away biting fleas.
2. Sprinkle beds of vegetables with a weak solution of chloride of lime to protect them from caterpillars, slugs, moths, etc.
3. Apply the same...
Steps:
1. Scatter chloride of lime on a plank in a stable to drive away biting fleas.
2. Sprinkle beds of vegetables with a weak solution of chloride of lime to protect them from caterpillars, slugs, moths, etc.
3. Apply the same solution to fruit trees or shrubbery for insect control.
4. Mix chloride of lime in a paste with fatty matter, and apply it in a narrow band around trees to prevent insects from climbing up.
chloride of lime,insect control,stable,gardening
Protect Fruits from Insects Using Natural Methods
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To protect fruits from insects:

1. Use gauze or nets: Some species of insects, such as wasps and flies, can be prevented from attacking ripe fruits by covering them with gauze or nets.
2. Enclose fruits in bags: Inclosing fruits (e.g.,...
To protect fruits from insects:

1. Use gauze or nets: Some species of insects, such as wasps and flies, can be prevented from attacking ripe fruits by covering them with gauze or nets.
2. Enclose fruits in bags: Inclosing fruits (e.g., grapes) in bags is an effective method to protect them from insect infestation.
3. Use natural deterrents:
- The blossoms of the *hoya carnosa* drive wasps away from grapes in hot-houses.
- The fruit of the common yew tree has a similar effect on wasps in open air.
protect fruits,insects,gauze,nets,bags,hoya carnosa,yew fruit,wasps

Dye Preparation (13) -

Choose Quality Anatto for Dyeing
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Select anatto that is of a flame color, with the interior brighter than the exterior.
2. The paste should be soft to the touch and have a good consistence.
3. Avoid anatto that has become **hard or lost its violet-like...
Procedure:

1. Select anatto that is of a flame color, with the interior brighter than the exterior.
2. The paste should be soft to the touch and have a good consistence.
3. Avoid anatto that has become hard or lost its violet-like odor (common in Europe).

Notes:
• Quality anatto is essential for achieving vibrant dye results.
anatto,quality selection,dye,natural dye
Use of Sheep's Dung in Dyeing Processes
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Apply sheep's dung to cotton or linen fabrics before dyeing.
2. The dung impregnates the fabric with an animal mucilage, making it more receptive to certain dyes like red madder and crosswort.
3. This process mimics...
Procedure:

1. Apply sheep's dung to cotton or linen fabrics before dyeing.
2. The dung impregnates the fabric with an animal mucilage, making it more receptive to certain dyes like red madder and crosswort.
3. This process mimics the properties of wool and silk, allowing the fabric to better absorb and retain color.

Notes:
• Sheep's dung is a traditional aid in natural dyeing processes.
sheep dung,dye preparation,natural dye,pre-treatment
Give Various Tones to Lake Using Chemical Additives
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Give Various Tones to Lake:

1. To produce a beautiful tone of violet, red, or purplered in the coloring part of cochineal:
- Add a solution of chloride of tin to the colored bath.

2. Another method for achieving unique shades:
-...
To Give Various Tones to Lake:

1. To produce a beautiful tone of violet, red, or purplered in the coloring part of cochineal:
- Add a solution of chloride of tin to the colored bath.

2. Another method for achieving unique shades:
- Add arseniate of potash (neutral arsenical salt) to the lake mixture.
- This produces shades that cannot be achieved using sulphate of alumina (alum).
Tone variation,dye preparation,arseniate of potash,chloride of tin
Make a Carminated Lake from Scarlet Cloth Shearings
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Make a Carminated Lake by Extracting Coloring Part from Scarlet Cloth:

1. Prepare a solution of wood-ashes: Put 1 lb (approximately 454 grams) of fine wood-ashes into a kettle with 40 lbs (approximately 18.1 kg) of water.
2. Boil the mixture...
To Make a Carminated Lake by Extracting Coloring Part from Scarlet Cloth:

1. Prepare a solution of wood-ashes: Put 1 lb (approximately 454 grams) of fine wood-ashes into a kettle with 40 lbs (approximately 18.1 kg) of water.
2. Boil the mixture for 1/4 of an hour, then filter it through a piece of linen cloth until the liquid passes clear.

3. Place the filtered solution on the fire and bring it to ebullition again.
4. Add 2 lbs (approximately 907 grams) of shearings or shreds of scarlet cloth dyed with cochineal to the boiling mixture.
5. Boil until the cloth becomes white, then filter the liquid again and press the shreds to extract all remaining coloring matter.

6. Put the filtered liquor into a clean kettle and place it over the fire.
7. When it boils, add a solution of 10 or 12 oz (approximately 283–340 grams) of alum dissolved in 2 lbs (approximately 907 grams) of filtered spring-water.
8. Stir the mixture with a wooden spatula until any froth formed is dissipated.

9. Add 2 lbs (approximately 907 grams) of a strong decoction of Brazil-wood to the mixture and pour it onto a filter.
10. Wash the sediment with spring-water, then transfer the cloth filter containing the precipitate to plaster dryers or a bed of dry bricks.

Note: The resulting lake will be beautiful but may lack the soft velvety appearance achieved by other methods. Additionally, the presence of Brazil-wood coloring in the shreds can reduce the unalterability of cochineal's color. For better results, purified potash should be used instead of wood-ashes.
Carminated Lake,scarlet cloth,dye extraction,Brazil-wood,purified potash
Make a Lake from Madder Root
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Make a Lake from Madder:

1. Take 2 oz (approximately 57 grams) of the finest Dutch madder and enclose it in a bag made of fine, strong calico that can hold three to four times as much material.
2. Place the bag into a large marble or...
To Make a Lake from Madder:

1. Take 2 oz (approximately 57 grams) of the finest Dutch madder and enclose it in a bag made of fine, strong calico that can hold three to four times as much material.
2. Place the bag into a large marble or porcelain mortar and pour on it a pint (approximately 0.47 liters) of clear, soft water that is cold.
3. Press the bag in all directions and use a pestle to pound and rub the madder within the bag as much as possible without tearing the fabric.
4. When the water becomes loaded with color, pour it off into another container.
5. Repeat this process until the water comes off only slightly tinged; approximately 5 pints (approximately 2.37 liters) of water will be sufficient.

Processing the Madder Liquor:
1. Heat all the collected liquor in an earthen or silver vessel until it is nearly boiling.
2. Pour this hot liquid into a large basin that has already been prepared with 1 oz (approximately 28 grams) of alum dissolved in 1 pint (approximately 0.47 liters) of boiling soft water.
3. Stir the mixture thoroughly while gently adding about 1 1/2 oz (approximately 42 grams) of a saturated solution of subcarbonate of potash.
4. Allow the mixture to stand until it cools and settles.
5. Pour off the clear yellow liquor from the top.
6. Add a quart (approximately 0.95 liters) of boiling soft water to the remaining precipitate, stirring well.
7. When the mixture has cooled, separate the lake by filtration.
8. The resulting lake should weigh approximately 1 oz (approximately 28 grams).

Note: Fresh madder-root is superior to dry madder for this process.
Madder Lake,dye preparation,madder root,alum,potash
Prepare Anatto for Dyeing Using Fermentation Method
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Pound the seeds of the anatto tree.
2. Mix with water in a quantity sufficient to cover the seeds.
3. Allow the mixture to ferment for 5 to 6 days.
4. During fermentation, the liquid will become **charged with the...
Procedure:

1. Pound the seeds of the anatto tree.
2. Mix with water in a quantity sufficient to cover the seeds.
3. Allow the mixture to ferment for 5 to 6 days.
4. During fermentation, the liquid will become charged with the coloring part.
5. After fermentation, separate the superfluous moisture by either:
- Slow evaporation over a fire, or
- Exposure to the heat of the sun.

Notes:
• This method produces a larger quantity of anatto fecula but may not be as bright in color.
• The resulting product is suitable for use in dyeing.
anatto,dye,fermentation,seed preparation,natural dye
Prepare Bastard Saffron for Dyeing
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Collect dried flowers of carthamus (safflower).
2. Enclose the flowers in a linen bag and place it in a stream of running water.
3. A person wearing wooden shoes should tread on the bag every 8 to 10 hours until...
Procedure:

1. Collect dried flowers of carthamus (safflower).
2. Enclose the flowers in a linen bag and place it in a stream of running water.
3. A person wearing wooden shoes should tread on the bag every 8 to 10 hours until the water becomes colorless.
4. After squeezing out excess moisture, spread the moist flowers on canvas over a wooden box.
5. Cover with 5-6% of their weight in carbonate of soda (sodium carbonate).
6. Pour pure water over them and repeat the process several times to allow the alkali to absorb the coloring part.
7. Filter the resulting dirty red or brown liquid.
8. To release the coloring part from the solution, use an intermediate substance with higher affinity for soda (precipitation method).

Notes:
• This process is used in making Spanish vermilion and various shades of cherry, ponceau, and rose colors.
• The final product is used to dye feathers and as a natural red pigment.
bastard saffron,dye preparation,natural dye,safflower
Prepare Florentine Lake Using Cochineal Sediment
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Prepare Florentine Lake:

1. Take the sediment of cochineal remaining in the bottom of the kettle used to make carmine.
2. Boil this sediment with about 4 quarts (approximately 3.8 liters) of water.
3. Mix the red liquor left after preparing...
To Prepare Florentine Lake:

1. Take the sediment of cochineal remaining in the bottom of the kettle used to make carmine.
2. Boil this sediment with about 4 quarts (approximately 3.8 liters) of water.
3. Mix the red liquor left after preparing carmine into the boiling mixture.
4. Precipitate the mixture using a solution of tin.
5. Frequently wash the resulting red precipitate over with water.

Alternative Method Using Fresh Cochineal:
1. Take 2 oz (approximately 57 grams) of fresh cochineal and 1 oz (approximately 28 grams) of crystals of tartar.
2. Boil these ingredients in a sufficient quantity of water until clear.
3. Precipitate the mixture using a solution of tin.
4. Wash the resulting precipitate thoroughly with water.

Alum Preparation:
1. Dissolve 2 lbs (approximately 907 grams) of alum in water.
2. Precipitate this solution with a lixivium of potash.
3. Wash the white earth repeatedly with boiling water.

Final Steps:
1. Mix both precipitates together while still in their liquid state.
2. Filter the mixture and dry it to obtain Florentine Lake.

Cheaper Alternative Using Brazil Wood:
Instead of using cochineal, 1 lb (approximately 454 grams) of Brazil wood may be used following the same procedure.
Florentine Lake,cochineal sediment,Brazil wood,dye preparation,precipitation
Prepare Litmus for Dyeing and Testing
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Collect lichen or moss from the Canary or Cape de Verd islands.
2. Expose the lichen to ammonia disengaged from urine in a state of putrefaction, mixed with lime.
3. Allow the process to complete, resulting in a...
Procedure:

1. Collect lichen or moss from the Canary or Cape de Verd islands.
2. Expose the lichen to ammonia disengaged from urine in a state of putrefaction, mixed with lime.
3. Allow the process to complete, resulting in a violet-colored paste with whitish spots (marble appearance).
4. The final product is known as litmus.

Notes:
• Litmus is used for dyeing silk and wool, coloring thermometers, and testing acids/alkalies.
• It is produced on a large scale in cities like London, Paris, and Lyons.
litmus,dye preparation,testing reagent,natural dye
Extract Lakes from Other Coloring Substances
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Use the same process: Apply the same method used for Brazil-wood to extract lakes from a decoction of logwood.

2. General application: Lakes of all colors and shades can be extracted from substances that release their...
Procedure:

1. Use the same process: Apply the same method used for Brazil-wood to extract lakes from a decoction of logwood.

2. General application: Lakes of all colors and shades can be extracted from substances that release their coloring part when boiled in water. This is achieved by decomposing the tincture and communicating it to alumina precipitated from sulphate of alumina using an alkali, or by mixing with a pure, white argillaceous substance such as real Spanish white or white of Morat.

Key Point:
• The process relies on the ability of coloring substances to release their hue when boiled and then transfer it through decomposition to precipitated alumina, or mix with pure clay for final preparation.
logwood,lake extraction,dye preparation,argillaceous substances
Make Lake from Brazil-wood
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Boil the raspings: Boil 4 oz. of Brazil-wood raspings in 15 pts. of pure water until reduced to 2 pts.

2. Add alum: Add 4 or 5 oz. of alum, resulting in a hue that inclines toward rose-color.

3. Strain the decoction:...
Procedure:

1. Boil the raspings: Boil 4 oz. of Brazil-wood raspings in 15 pts. of pure water until reduced to 2 pts.

2. Add alum: Add 4 or 5 oz. of alum, resulting in a hue that inclines toward rose-color.

3. Strain the decoction: Strain through linen cloth.

4. Add carbonate of soda: Carefully add 4 oz. of carbonate of soda to cause effervescence. This will strip the mordant and allow the color to deposit as a lake, which, when washed and properly dried, results in an exceedingly rich and mellow violet red color.

Alternative Method:
• If only one-half the dose of mineral alkali is used for precipitation, the tint becomes clearer because the bath retains some undecomposed aluminous mordant.

Another Alternative:
• Follow the method used for Dutch pinks by mixing the aluminous decoction of Brazil-wood with pure clay (e.g., Spanish white or white of Morat). Deposit the mixture on a filter and wash it thoroughly to obtain a lake of very bright dark rose-color from the driers.
brazil-wood,lake preparation,dye extraction,carbonate of soda
Prepare Brownish Yellow Dutch Pink Dye
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Boil the ingredients: In 12 lbs. of water, boil for an hour:
- 1 lb. of yellow berries
- 1/2 lb. of Barberry shrub wood shavings
- 1 lb. of wood-ashes

2. Strain the decoction: Strain the mixture through a piece...
Procedure:

1. Boil the ingredients: In 12 lbs. of water, boil for an hour:
- 1 lb. of yellow berries
- 1/2 lb. of Barberry shrub wood shavings
- 1 lb. of wood-ashes

2. Strain the decoction: Strain the mixture through a piece of linen cloth.

3. Add alumina solution: At different times, add a warm solution of:
- 2 lbs. of sulphate of alumina in 5 lbs. of water
- A slight effervescence will occur as the sulphate is decomposed and alumina precipitates onto the coloring part.

4. Filter the mixture: Filter through close linen cloth, and collect the paste remaining on the cloth.

5. Dry the paste: Divide the paste into square pieces and expose them to dry on boards.

Result: This produces a brown Dutch pink dye with pure clay content. The intensity of the color indicates its quality, which is superior to other compositions.
brownish yellow dutch pink,dye preparation,natural dyes,barberry shrub,wood-ashes,sulphate of alumina
Prepare Dutch Pink for Oil Painting
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Boil the ingredients: Boil separately:
- 1 lb. of yellow-berries
- 3 oz. of sulphate of alumina in 12 lbs. of water, reducing to 4 lbs.

2. Strain and prepare mixture: Strain the decoction through linen cloth and...
Procedure:

1. Boil the ingredients: Boil separately:
- 1 lb. of yellow-berries
- 3 oz. of sulphate of alumina in 12 lbs. of water, reducing to 4 lbs.

2. Strain and prepare mixture: Strain the decoction through linen cloth and squeeze it strongly. Mix with:
- 2 lbs. of ceruse (finely ground on porphyry)
- 1 lb. of pulverized Spanish white

3. Evaporate to paste: Evaporate the mixture until it reaches a paste-like consistency.

4. Form and dry cakes: Form into small cakes and dry them in the shade.

5. Powder and reprocess: Reduce the dried cakes to powder, then mix with a new decoction of yellow-berries. Repeat this process a third time to obtain brown Dutch pink.

Important Notes:
• Decoctions must be warm when mixed with earth.
• Do not keep them long as their color changes quickly due to fermentation.
• Use a wooden spatula for stirring the mixture.
• If only one decoction is used, the resulting Dutch pink will be bright yellow and easier to mix. Using multiple decoctions results in a brown composition that is more difficult to mix, especially if argillaceous (clay-like). The artist must grind the color thoroughly, which is essential for all types of Dutch pink, even the softest ones intended for oil painting.
dutch pink,oil painting,dye preparation,ceruleum,sulphate of alumina

Cordials (12) -

Prepare Caraway Cordial
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 1 1/2 oz. of oil of caraway
• 20 drops of cassia-lignea oil
• 5 drops of essence of orange peel
• 5 drops of the essence of lemons
• 13 galls. of spirits, 1 in 5
• 8 lbs. of loaf sugar

Procedure:
1. Combine all...
Ingredients:
• 1 1/2 oz. of oil of caraway
• 20 drops of cassia-lignea oil
• 5 drops of essence of orange peel
• 5 drops of the essence of lemons
• 13 galls. of spirits, 1 in 5
• 8 lbs. of loaf sugar

Procedure:
1. Combine all ingredients.
2. Make it up and fine it down.
caraway,cordial,spirit,sugar,essence
Prepare Cedrat Cordial
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 1/4 lb. of finest loaf-sugar (powdered)
• 120 drops of essence of cedrat
• 1 gallon of fine proof spirit
• 1 quart of water

Procedure:
1. Put the powdered sugar into a glass mortar.
2. Add 120 drops of essence of cedrat and...
Ingredients:
• 1/4 lb. of finest loaf-sugar (powdered)
• 120 drops of essence of cedrat
• 1 gallon of fine proof spirit
• 1 quart of water

Procedure:
1. Put the powdered sugar into a glass mortar.
2. Add 120 drops of essence of cedrat and rub together with a glass pestle.
3. Transfer to a glass alembic along with 1 gallon of fine proof spirit and 1 quart of water.
4. Place the alembic in a bath heat.
5. Draw off 1 gallon, or until feints begin to rise.
6. Dulcify with fine sugar.

Notes:
• Ensure the spirit is clean and free from any flavor.
• This cordial is considered the finest known.
cedrat,cordial,sugar,spirit,essence
Prepare Citron Cordial
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 3 lbs. of dry yellow rinds of citrons
• 2 lbs. of orangepeel
• 3/4 lb. of bruised nutmegs
• 10 1/2 galls. of proof spirit
• 1 gallon of water

Procedure:
1. Combine all ingredients.
2. Digest with a gentle heat.
3. Draw off 10...
Ingredients:
• 3 lbs. of dry yellow rinds of citrons
• 2 lbs. of orangepeel
• 3/4 lb. of bruised nutmegs
• 10 1/2 galls. of proof spirit
• 1 gallon of water

Procedure:
1. Combine all ingredients.
2. Digest with a gentle heat.
3. Draw off 10 galls. in a bath heat.
4. Dulcify with fine sugar.
citron,cordial,sugar,spirit,nutmegs
Prepare Clove Cordial
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 4 lbs. of bruised cloves
• 1/2 lb. of pimento or allspice
• 16 galls. of proof spirit

Procedure:
1. Combine the cloves and pimento/allspice with the proof spirit.
2. Digest the mixture for 12 hours in a gentle heat.
3. Draw...
Ingredients:
• 4 lbs. of bruised cloves
• 1/2 lb. of pimento or allspice
• 16 galls. of proof spirit

Procedure:
1. Combine the cloves and pimento/allspice with the proof spirit.
2. Digest the mixture for 12 hours in a gentle heat.
3. Draw off 15 galls. with a pretty brisk fire.
4. Color the water red using a strong tincture of cochineal, alkanet, or corn poppy-flowers.
5. Dulcify at pleasure with refined sugar.
clove,cordial,sugar,spirit,pimento
Prepare Coriander Cordial
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 7 qts. of spirits
• 2 lbs. of coriander seed
• 1 oz. of caraway seed
• 6 drops of oil of orange
• 2 lbs. of sugar
• Water (to fill up)

Procedure:
1. Bruise the coriander seeds and steep them in the spirits for 10 to 12...
Ingredients:
• 7 qts. of spirits
• 2 lbs. of coriander seed
• 1 oz. of caraway seed
• 6 drops of oil of orange
• 2 lbs. of sugar
• Water (to fill up)

Procedure:
1. Bruise the coriander seeds and steep them in the spirits for 10 to 12 days.
2. Stir the mixture 2 or 3 times a day.
3. Fine it down as you would gin.
coriander,cordial,sugar,spirit,caraway
Prepare Eau de Bigarade
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Outer or yellow part of the peels of 14 bigarades (a kind of orange)
• 1/2 oz. of nutmegs
• 1/4 oz. of mace
• 1 gallon of fine proof spirit
• 2 qts. of water

Procedure:
1. Combine all ingredients in a close vessel.
2. Digest...
Ingredients:
• Outer or yellow part of the peels of 14 bigarades (a kind of orange)
• 1/2 oz. of nutmegs
• 1/4 oz. of mace
• 1 gallon of fine proof spirit
• 2 qts. of water

Procedure:
1. Combine all ingredients in a close vessel.
2. Digest for 2 days.
3. Draw off 1 gallon with a gentle fire.
4. Dulcify with fine sugar.

Prepare Gold Cordial
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 4 lbs. of sliced angelica roots
• 2 lbs. of stoned raisins
• 1/2 lb. of coriander seeds
• 1/2 lb. of caraway seeds and cinnamon, each
• 2 oz. of cloves
• 1 lb. of figs and liquorice root (sliced), each
• 11 galls. of proof...
Ingredients:
• 4 lbs. of sliced angelica roots
• 2 lbs. of stoned raisins
• 1/2 lb. of coriander seeds
• 1/2 lb. of caraway seeds and cinnamon, each
• 2 oz. of cloves
• 1 lb. of figs and liquorice root (sliced), each
• 11 galls. of proof spirit
• 2 galls. of water
• 1 oz. of English saffron
• 8 lbs. of sugar
• 3 qts. of rose-water

Procedure:
1. Combine all ingredients except the saffron and sugar.
2. Digest for 2 days.
3. Draw off by a gentle heat until feints begin to rise.
4. Hang a piece of linen, fastened to the mouth of the worm, with 1 oz. of English saffron.
5. Dissolve 8 lbs. of sugar in 3 qts. of rose-water.
6. Add the distilled liquor to the sugar solution.
gold,cordial,sugar,spirit,saffron
Prepare Lemon Cordial
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 4 lbs. of dried lemon-peel
• 10 1/2 galls. of proof spirit
• 1 gallon of water

Procedure:
1. Combine all ingredients.
2. Draw off 10 galls. by a gentle fire.
3. Dulcify with fine sugar.
lemon,cordial,sugar,spirit
Prepare Lovage Cordial
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 4 oz. each of fresh roots of lovage, valerian, celery, and sweet fennel
• 1 oz. each of essential oil of caraway and savin
• 1 pt. of spirit of wine
• 12 galls. of proof spirit
• 12 lbs. of loaf sugar

Procedure:
1. Steep the...
Ingredients:
• 4 oz. each of fresh roots of lovage, valerian, celery, and sweet fennel
• 1 oz. each of essential oil of caraway and savin
• 1 pt. of spirit of wine
• 12 galls. of proof spirit
• 12 lbs. of loaf sugar

Procedure:
1. Steep the roots and seeds in the spirits for 14 days.
2. Dissolve the oils in the spirit of wine.
3. Add them to the undulcified cordial drawn off from the other ingredients.
4. Dissolve the sugar in water for making up.
5. Fine, if necessary, with alum.
lovage,cordial,sugar,spirit,oil
Prepare Nectar
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 15 galls. of red ratafia
• 1/4 oz. of cassia-oil
• Equal quantity of oil of caraway seeds
• Half a pint of spirit of wine
• Orange wine (to fill up the cask)
• Sugar (optional, for sweetening)

Procedure:
1. Dissolve the oils...
Ingredients:
• 15 galls. of red ratafia
• 1/4 oz. of cassia-oil
• Equal quantity of oil of caraway seeds
• Half a pint of spirit of wine
• Orange wine (to fill up the cask)
• Sugar (optional, for sweetening)

Procedure:
1. Dissolve the oils in half a pint of spirit of wine.
2. Combine with 15 galls. of red ratafia and make up to fill the cask with orange wine.
3. Sweeten by adding a small lump of sugar in the glass, if desired.
nectar,cordial,sugar,wine,oil
Prepare Noyeau
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 13 galls. of French brandy (1 in 5)
• 6 oz. of best French prunes
• 2 oz. of celery
• 3 oz. of kernels of apricots, nectarines, and peaches
• 1 oz. of bitter almonds
• Essence of orange-peel and essence of lemon-peel (each 2...
Ingredients:
• 13 galls. of French brandy (1 in 5)
• 6 oz. of best French prunes
• 2 oz. of celery
• 3 oz. of kernels of apricots, nectarines, and peaches
• 1 oz. of bitter almonds
• Essence of orange-peel and essence of lemon-peel (each 2 dwts.)
• 1/2 lb. of loaf sugar
• Rose-water (to make up to 2 galls.)

Procedure:
1. Bruise the prunes, celery, apricot kernels, nectarine kernels, peach kernels, and bitter almonds.
2. Let the mixture stand for ten days or a fortnight.
3. Draw off the clear noyeau.
4. Add rose-water to make up to 2 galls.
noyeau,cordial,sugar,wine,prunes
Prepare Orange Cordial
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 5 lbs. of yellow part of fresh orange-peel
• 10 1/2 galls. of proof spirit
• 2 galls. of water

Procedure:
1. Combine all ingredients.
2. Draw off 10 galls. with a gentle fire.
orange,cordial,sugar,spirit

Essential Oils Extraction (12) -

Extracting Essence of Neroli
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Distill the flowers of the orange tree to obtain essence of neroli.
2. The yield is very low: 6 cwt. (hundredweight) of flowers yields only 1 oz. of oil.
3. A lower-quality version, known as petits grains, is obtained in the same way...
1. Distill the flowers of the orange tree to obtain essence of neroli.
2. The yield is very low: 6 cwt. (hundredweight) of flowers yields only 1 oz. of oil.
3. A lower-quality version, known as petits grains, is obtained in the same way but with less careful selection of flowers.
4. Another essence can be extracted from orange peel, which is very fragrant.
5. A third essence comes from unripe oranges, and it has a gold color.
essence of neroli, orange flowers, petits grains, orange peel, unripe oranges
Extracting Foreign Oil of Lavender
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Distill the flowers and seeds of broad-leaved lavender or French lavender (*stoechas*) using a quick fire.
2. The resulting oil is sweet-scented, but it is not as fine as the oil from narrow-leaved lavender, also known as **English...
1. Distill the flowers and seeds of broad-leaved lavender or French lavender (*stoechas*) using a quick fire.
2. The resulting oil is sweet-scented, but it is not as fine as the oil from narrow-leaved lavender, also known as English oil.
foreign oil of lavender, distillation, french lavender, english oil
Extracting Krumholz' Oil
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Distill Hungarian balsam to obtain Krumholz' oil.
2. This oil is distinguished from oil of turpentine, which is often sold as a substitute, by the following characteristics:
- Golden color
- Agreeable odor
- **Acid...
1. Distill Hungarian balsam to obtain Krumholz' oil.
2. This oil is distinguished from oil of turpentine, which is often sold as a substitute, by the following characteristics:
- Golden color
- Agreeable odor
- Acid oiliness of taste
krumholz' oil, hungarian balsam, distillation, turpentine substitute
Extracting Oil of Bitter Almonds
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Crush the kernels of bitter almonds and perform a distillation process.
2. During distillation, hydrocyanic acid is formed and passes over with the oil.
3. The resulting crude oil is poisonous due to the presence of hydrocyanic...
1. Crush the kernels of bitter almonds and perform a distillation process.
2. During distillation, hydrocyanic acid is formed and passes over with the oil.
3. The resulting crude oil is poisonous due to the presence of hydrocyanic acid.
4. Despite its toxicity, it is sometimes used in medicine for the hydrocyanic acid content, though this use is uncertain.
5. It is also used in perfumery and confectionery.
6. When used to flavor cakes, the hydrocyanic acid is driven off by heat, making it less dangerous.
oil of bitter almonds, distillation, hydrocyanic acid, perfumery, confectionery
Extracting Oil of Geranium
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Distill the leaves of *Pelargonium odoratissimum* (geranium) to obtain the essential oil.
2. The oil is used in perfumery but is often adulterated with ginger-grass oil.
3. It may also be used to adulterate attar of roses.
oil of geranium, distillation, perfumery, adulteration, ginger-grass oil
Extracting Oil of Marjoram
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Obtain oil of marjoram from the dried herb.
2. The yield is approximately 10% of the weight of the dried plant.
oil of marjoram, distillation, yield, dried herb
Extracting Oil of Meadow Sweet
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Use the plant *Spiraea ulmaria* (meadow sweet) for its stimulant and perfumery properties.
2. It is sometimes used in medicine and perfumery.
oil of meadow sweet, spiraea ulmaria, stimulant, perfumery
Extracting Oil of Mint
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Obtain oil of mint from the dried plant.
2. The yield is as follows:
- 6 lbs. of fresh leaves yield 3 1/2 drs. (drachms)
- 4 lbs. of dried leaves yield 1 1/2 oz. (ounces)
3. Oil of mint has the following properties:
-...
1. Obtain oil of mint from the dried plant.
2. The yield is as follows:
- 6 lbs. of fresh leaves yield 3 1/2 drs. (drachms)
- 4 lbs. of dried leaves yield 1 1/2 oz. (ounces)
3. Oil of mint has the following properties:
- Stimulant, carminative, and antispasmodic
oil of mint, distillation, yield, stimulant, carminative
Extracting Oil of Nutmegs
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Distill the spice nutmeg to obtain its oil.
2. The resulting oil is liquid and pale yellow.
3. During distillation, a sebaceous, insipid matter floats on the water in the still.
oil of nutmegs, distillation, pale yellow, sebaceous matter
Preparing Dippel's Oil
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Distill the oil from hatshorn without adding any substances.
2. Rectify the oil using one of these methods:
- Use a slow distillation in a retort no larger than necessary, and save only the first portion that comes over.
-...
1. Distill the oil from hatshorn without adding any substances.
2. Rectify the oil using one of these methods:
- Use a slow distillation in a retort no larger than necessary, and save only the first portion that comes over.
- Use water in a common still.
3. The resulting oil is very fine and thin, but it must be stored properly to maintain its quality:
- Keep it in an opaque vessel, or in a drawer or dark place to prevent discoloration by light.
4. Dippel's Oil has the following properties:
- Antispasmodic, anodyne, and diaphoretic
- Used in doses of 10 to 30 drops, taken in water.
dippel's oil, distillation, hatshorn, rectification, storage
Producing Essence of Lavender
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Extract the oil from the flowers of lavender.
2. To preserve its delicate odor and prevent it from becoming glutinous, draw the oil over in a water-bath with a small quantity of alcohol to create the essence.
3. Store the essence in a...
1. Extract the oil from the flowers of lavender.
2. To preserve its delicate odor and prevent it from becoming glutinous, draw the oil over in a water-bath with a small quantity of alcohol to create the essence.
3. Store the essence in a closely corked container for about 7 years.
4. After this time, the essence will have a peculiarly fine and delicate odor, free from any empyreuma (burnt smell).
essence of lavender, distillation, preservation, alcohol infusion
Extracting Camphor from Essential Oils
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Distill the essential oils from labiate plants (such as rosemary, marjoram, sage, or lavender) without adding 1/3 of the oil.
2. After distillation, separate the crystals of camphor that form in the residuum.
3. **Redistill the...
1. Distill the essential oils from labiate plants (such as rosemary, marjoram, sage, or lavender) without adding 1/3 of the oil.
2. After distillation, separate the crystals of camphor that form in the residuum.
3. Redistill the remaining oil 2 to 3 times to extract any additional camphor.
4. The yield varies depending on the plant:
- Oil of rosemary or sweet marjoram: 1 oz. of camphor from 10 oz. of oil
- Sage: 1 oz. of camphor from 8 oz. of oil
- Lavender: 1 oz. of camphor from 4 oz. or less of oil
5. Camphor obtained from marjoram is not volatile and will not sustain a flame for long.
6. To increase the yield, keep the essential oils in slightly stopped bottles in a cool place.
camphor, essential oils, distillation, rosemary, marjoram, sage, lavender

Fragrances Perfumes (12) -

Prepare Bouquet Royale Perfume
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Extract of rose (from pomade), 1 pt.
• Esprit de rose (triple), 1/2 pt.
• Extract of jasmin, extract of violet (from pomade), each 1/2 pt.
• Extract of verbena, extract of cassia, each 2 1/2 oz.
• Otto of lemons, otto of bergamot,...
Ingredients:
• Extract of rose (from pomade), 1 pt.
• Esprit de rose (triple), 1/2 pt.
• Extract of jasmin, extract of violet (from pomade), each 1/2 pt.
• Extract of verbena, extract of cassia, each 2 1/2 oz.
• Otto of lemons, otto of bergamot, each 1/2 oz.
• Extract of musk, extract of ambergris, each 1 oz.

Procedure:
1. Combine all the listed ingredients in a large mixing vessel.
2. Stir thoroughly to ensure even distribution of all components.
3. Allow the mixture to rest for several days to allow the scents to blend and mature.
4. Filter the final product through fine muslin or a strainer to remove any particulate matter.
5. Store in an airtight, dark glass bottle away from direct sunlight and heat.
bouquet royale, perfume, extract of rose, esprit de rose, jasmin, violet, verbena, cassia, otto of lemons, musk, ambergris
Prepare Cheap Cologne
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Alcohol at 85°, 10 qts.
• Essence of lemon, 5 oz.
• Essence of cedrat, 12 1/2 drs.
• Essence of bergamot, 4 oz.
• Essence of lavender, 1 oz.
• Tincture of benzoin, 1 oz.

Procedure:
1. Take alcohol at 85° (10 qts.).
2. Add the...
Ingredients:
• Alcohol at 85°, 10 qts.
• Essence of lemon, 5 oz.
• Essence of cedrat, 12 1/2 drs.
• Essence of bergamot, 4 oz.
• Essence of lavender, 1 oz.
• Tincture of benzoin, 1 oz.

Procedure:
1. Take alcohol at 85° (10 qts.).
2. Add the following essences and oils to the alcohol:
- Essence of lemon: 5 oz.
- Essence of cedrat: 12 1/2 drs.
- Essence of bergamot: 4 oz.
- Essence of lavender: 1 oz.
- Tincture of benzoin: 1 oz.
3. Allow the mixture to blend thoroughly and store in a cool, dark place.
cheap cologne,essence of lemon,cologne making
Prepare Cologne Water (Redwood Gray's Formula 2)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Oil of bergamot, 3 oz.
• Oil of lemon, 2 oz.
• Oil of lavender, 3 1/2 drs.
• Oil of neroli, 2 1/2 drs.
• Oil of origanum, 2 drs.
• Oil of rosemary, 1 dr.
• Essence of vanilla, 2 drs.
• Musk, 10 grs.
• Rectified spirits, 13 pts.
-...
Ingredients:
• Oil of bergamot, 3 oz.
• Oil of lemon, 2 oz.
• Oil of lavender, 3 1/2 drs.
• Oil of neroli, 2 1/2 drs.
• Oil of origanum, 2 drs.
• Oil of rosemary, 1 dr.
• Essence of vanilla, 2 drs.
• Musk, 10 grs.
• Rectified spirits, 13 pts.
• Rosewater, 1 qt.
• Orange-flower water, 1 pt.

Procedure:
1. Combine the following oils:
- Oil of bergamot: 3 oz.
- Oil of lemon: 2 oz.
- Oil of lavender: 3 1/2 drs.
- Oil of neroli: 2 1/2 drs.
- Oil of origanum: 2 drs.
- Oil of rosemary: 1 dr.
- Essence of vanilla: 2 drs.
- Musk: 10 grs.
2. Dissolve the oils in 10 pts. of rectified spirits.
3. Add rosewater (1 qt.) and orange-flower water (1 pt.), previously mixed with the remainder of the spirits.
4. Allow the mixture to stand for 2 weeks in a cool, dark place.
5. Filter the mixture before bottling and storing.
cologne water,redwood gray formula 2,musk,rosewater

Ingredients:

• Extract of vanilla, 2 oz.
• Extract of orris, 8 oz.
• Otto of lemons, 1/4 oz.
• Otto of bergamot, 1 oz.

Procedure:
1. Combine all the listed ingredients in a large mixing vessel.
2. Stir...

• Otto of bergamot, 1 oz.

Procedure:

2. Stir thoroughly to ensure even distribution of all components.
3. Allow the mixture to rest for several days to allow the scents to blend and mature.
4. Filter the final product through fine muslin or a strainer to remove any particulate matter.
5. Store in an airtight, dark glass bottle away from direct sunlight and heat.

Prepare Extract of Verbena

Ingredients:

• Oil of lemon-grass, 3 drs.

• Oil of orange-peel, 1/2 oz.

Procedure:
1. Combine the following ingredients:
- Rectified spirits: 1 pt.
- Oil of lemon-grass: 3 drs.
...
Ingredients:

• Oil of lemon-grass, 3 drs.
• Oil of lemon-peel, 2 oz.
• Oil of orange-peel, 1/2 oz.

Procedure:
1. Combine the following ingredients:
- Rectified spirits: 1 pt.
- Oil of lemon-grass: 3 drs.
- Oil of lemon-peel: 2 oz.
- Oil of orange-peel: 1/2 oz.
2. Allow the mixture to stand together for a few hours in a cool, dark place.
3. Filter the mixture and store it in a clean, sealed container for sale.

• Oil of lemongrass, 2 1/2 drs.

• Oil of lemongrass, 2 1/2 drs.

• Extrait de tubereuse, 7 oz.

Prepare Fine Cologne Water
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Alcohol at 85°, 10 qts.
• Essence of neroli petit grain, 1/2 oz.
• Essence of rosemary, 2 1/2 drs.
• Essence of lavender, 1 1/4 drs.
• Essence of clove, 1/2 dr.
• Essence of peppermint, 1/2 dr.
• Essence of bergamot, 12 1/2 drs.
-...
Ingredients:
• Alcohol at 85°, 10 qts.
• Essence of neroli petit grain, 1/2 oz.
• Essence of rosemary, 2 1/2 drs.
• Essence of lavender, 1 1/4 drs.
• Essence of clove, 1/2 dr.
• Essence of peppermint, 1/2 dr.
• Essence of bergamot, 12 1/2 drs.
• Lemon, 12 1/2 drs.
• Essence of Portugal, 7 1/2 drs.
• Tincture of benzoin, 1 1/4 drs.

Procedure:
1. Take alcohol at 85° (10 qts.).
2. Dissolve the following essences and oils in the alcohol:
- Essence of neroli petit grain: 1/2 oz.
- Essence of rosemary: 2 1/2 drs.
- Essence of lavender: 1 1/4 drs.
- Essence of clove: 1/2 dr.
- Essence of peppermint: 1/2 dr.
- Essence of bergamot: 12 1/2 drs.
- Lemon: 12 1/2 drs.
- Essence of Portugal: 7 1/2 drs.
- Tincture of benzoin: 1 1/4 drs.
3. Allow the mixture to blend thoroughly and store in a cool, dark place.
cologne water,fine cologne,essence of neroli,alcohol,perfume making

1. Combine all the listed...
Ingredients:
• Extract of rose, extract of violet (from pomade), each 1 pt.
• Extract of rose (triple), 2 1/2 oz.
• Extract of cassia, 2 1/2 oz.
• Otto of bergamot, 2 drs.
• Extract of ambergris, 1 oz.

Procedure:
1. Combine all the listed ingredients in a large mixing vessel.
2. Stir thoroughly to ensure even distribution of all components.
3. Allow the mixture to rest for several days to allow the scents to blend and mature.
4. Filter the final product through fine muslin or a strainer to remove any particulate matter.
5. Store in an airtight, dark glass bottle away from direct sunlight and heat.

Hair Care (12) -

Remove Superfluous Hair (Method 2 - Arsenic-Based)
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Remove Superfluous Hair

1. Take 8 oz of freshly slaked lime and 1/2 oz of orpiment.
2. Mix the ingredients thoroughly and store in a well-closed bottle.
3. When ready to use, apply as a paste with water until it begins to...
To Remove Superfluous Hair

1. Take 8 oz of freshly slaked lime and 1/2 oz of orpiment.
2. Mix the ingredients thoroughly and store in a well-closed bottle.
3. When ready to use, apply as a paste with water until it begins to burn the skin.
4. Use an ordinary paper-knife to shave off the hair.
5. The time required will vary depending on the type of hair (e.g., black hair takes longer than fair hair).
6. Warning: This method contains arsenic, and must be used with caution.
remove superfluous hair,slaked lime,orpiment,arsenic,paste,application
Remove Superfluous Hair (Method 1)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Remove Superfluous Hair

1. Take 16 parts of quicklime, 2 parts of pearlash, and 2 parts of liver of sulphur.
2. Mix the ingredients thoroughly and store in a tight bottle.
3. When ready to use, mix the powder into a **paste...
To Remove Superfluous Hair

1. Take 16 parts of quicklime, 2 parts of pearlash, and 2 parts of liver of sulphur.
2. Mix the ingredients thoroughly and store in a tight bottle.
3. When ready to use, mix the powder into a paste consistency.
4. Apply the paste to the area with superfluous hair.
5. Allow it to remain for 2 to 3 minutes.
6. Remove the paste using a wooden knife.
remove superfluous hair,quicklime,pearlash,liver of sulphur,paste,application

• Rum, 3 qts.
• Spirit of wine, 1 pt.
• Water, 1 pt.
• Tincture of cantharides, 1/2 oz.
• Carbonate of ammonia, 1/2 oz.
• Salt of tartar, 1 oz.

Procedure:
1. Combine all the listed ingredients in a large mixing vessel.
2. Stir...
Ingredients:
• Rum, 3 qts.
• Spirit of wine, 1 pt.
• Water, 1 pt.
• Tincture of cantharides, 1/2 oz.
• Carbonate of ammonia, 1/2 oz.
• Salt of tartar, 1 oz.

Procedure:
1. Combine all the listed ingredients in a large mixing vessel.
2. Stir thoroughly to ensure even distribution of all components.
3. Apply the mixture directly to the scalp and hair, massaging gently into the skin.
4. After application, rinse with clean water.
5. If desired, omit the salt of tartar for a formulation similar to Balm of Columbia.
american shampoo liquor, rum, spirit of wine, tincture of cantharides, carbonate of ammonia, salt of tartar

2. Allow the solution to cool completely.
3. Filter the mixture before use to ensure...

• Camphor, 1/2 oz.
• Boiling water, 1 qt.

Procedure:

2. Allow the solution to cool completely.
3. Filter the mixture before use to ensure it is free of any particulate matter.
4. Apply this solution as a hair wash for cleansing and nourishing the scalp.

Procedure:

Procedure:

2. Distill the mixture with gentle heat until a...

• Honey, 4 lbs.
• Very dry sand, 5 lbs.

Procedure:

2. Distill the mixture with gentle heat until a yellowish acid water is produced.
3. Use this water for hair care, applying it to the scalp and rinsing thoroughly afterward.

Prepare Orfila's Hair-Dye
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Orfila's Hair-Dye

1. Take 3 parts of litharge and 2 parts of quicklime, mix thoroughly.
2. Store the mixture in a well-stopped bottle.
3. When ready to use, mix with water or milk.
4. Apply the mixture to the hair.
5. Cover the...
Orfila's Hair-Dye

1. Take 3 parts of litharge and 2 parts of quicklime, mix thoroughly.
2. Store the mixture in a well-stopped bottle.
3. When ready to use, mix with water or milk.
4. Apply the mixture to the hair.
5. Cover the hair with an oil-skin cap and leave it on for 5 to 6 hours.
Orfila's Hair-Dye,litharge,quicklime,hair-dye,application
