• Southernwood, 2 oz.
• Box-leaves, 6 oz.
• Water, 4 pts.

Procedure:
1. Boil the southernwood and box-leaves gently in water for 1/4 of an hour.
2. Strain the mixture to remove any solid particles.
3. To each pint of the...
Ingredients:
• Southernwood, 2 oz.
• Box-leaves, 6 oz.
• Water, 4 pts.

Procedure:
1. Boil the southernwood and box-leaves gently in water for 1/4 of an hour.
2. Strain the mixture to remove any solid particles.
3. To each pint of the liquid, add 2 oz. spirit of rosemary and 1/2 dr. of salt of tartar or 1 dr. of Naples soap.
4. Use this solution as a hair wash for cleansing and nourishing the scalp.
vegetable hair wash, southernwood, box-leaves, spirit of rosemary, salt of tartar, naples soap

Color Preparation (12) -

Fine Black Color from Camphor and Candle Buttons
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Take camphor and set it on fire.
2. Collect the dense smoke produced by holding a common saucer over the flame.
3. Mix this black with gum arabic for use in painting.

Alternative Method:
• Miniature painters can obtain a...
Procedure:

1. Take camphor and set it on fire.
2. Collect the dense smoke produced by holding a common saucer over the flame.
3. Mix this black with gum arabic for use in painting.

Alternative Method:
• Miniature painters can obtain a beautiful black by collecting buttons formed on the snuff of a candle when allowed to burn undisturbed.
• Collect these buttons into a small thimble or similar vessel and cover it immediately with the thumb to exclude air.
• This black is free from grease and has desirable qualities for painting.
camphor, candle buttons, gum arabic, miniature painters, fine black color
Make Black from Wine-lees
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Use wine-lees and tartar.
2. Place them in large cylindrical vessels or pots with an aperture in the cover to allow passage of smoke, acid, and alkaline vapors during calcination.
3. Continue the process until no more smoke is...
Procedure:

1. Use wine-lees and tartar.
2. Place them in large cylindrical vessels or pots with an aperture in the cover to allow passage of smoke, acid, and alkaline vapors during calcination.
3. Continue the process until no more smoke is observed.
4. Wash the remaining matter (a mixture of salts and carbonaceous material) several times in boiling water.
5. Reduce it to the proper fineness by grinding it on porphyry.

Notes:
• If extracted from dry lees, the black will be coarser due to earthy matters mixed with the carbonaceous part.
• This black has a velvety appearance and is used mainly by copper-plate printers.
• Another method: Burn peach-stones in a close vessel. The resulting charcoal, when ground on porphyry, produces an old gray color for painting.
• Vine twigs reduced to charcoal give a bluish black that can be mixed with white to produce a silver white not achievable by other blacks.
black, wine-lees, tartar, calcination, porphyry, peach-stones, vine twigs, charcoal, copper-plate printers
Make Brunswick Green
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make Brunswick Green:

1. Prepare a solution of precipitate of copper in tartar and water.
2. By evaporation, this solution yields a transparent cupreous tartar.
3. This results in a product similar to the **superfine Brunswick...
To make Brunswick Green:

1. Prepare a solution of precipitate of copper in tartar and water.
2. By evaporation, this solution yields a transparent cupreous tartar.
3. This results in a product similar to the superfine Brunswick green.
Brunswick Green, copper precipitate, tartar, evaporation
Make Green Colors Free from Arsenic (Elsner Green)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make Elsner Green (a green color free from arsenic):

1. Add to a solution of sulphate of copper, a decoction of fustic, previously clarified by a solution of gelatine.
2. To this mixture, add **10 or 11 per cent. of protochloride...
To make Elsner Green (a green color free from arsenic):

1. Add to a solution of sulphate of copper, a decoction of fustic, previously clarified by a solution of gelatine.
2. To this mixture, add 10 or 11 per cent. of protochloride of tin.
3. Finally, add an excess of caustic potash soda.
4. The precipitate is then washed and dried, whereupon it assumes a green color with a tint of blue.
Elsner Green, sulphate of copper, fustic, protochloride of tin, caustic potash
Make Green Using Acetic Copper (Verdigris)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make Green using acetic copper (verdigris):

1. Dissolve acetic copper (verdigris) in acetic acid.
2. This forms an elegant green color.
Verdigris, acetic acid, green color
Make Ivory and Boneblack
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Place fragments or turnings of ivory or osseous parts of animals into a crucible.
2. Surround the crucible with burning coals.
3. Cover the crucible closely to prevent heat loss.
4. Allow the ivory or bones to be reduced to...
Procedure:

1. Place fragments or turnings of ivory or osseous parts of animals into a crucible.
2. Surround the crucible with burning coals.
3. Cover the crucible closely to prevent heat loss.
4. Allow the ivory or bones to be reduced to charcoal by exposure to heat.
5. When no more smoke is seen escaping from the cover, leave the crucible over the fire for half an hour or until it has completely cooled.
6. Retrieve the hard carbonaceous matter produced.
7. Pound and grind it on porphyry with water.
8. Wash the material on a filter using warm water and then dry it.
9. Before use, subject it again to the same process.

Notes:
• Boneblack is reddish in color, while ivoryblack is more beautiful and brighter than black obtained from peach-stones.
• Ivoryblack mixed with white oxide of lead produces a beautiful pearl gray.
• Cologne and Cassel-black are made from ivory.
ivory, boneblack, crucible, coals, carbonaceous matter, porphyry, pearl gray, white oxide of lead
Make Schweinfurth or Emerald Green Color
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make Schweinfurth or Emerald Green Color:

1. Dissolve 6 parts of sulphate of copper in a small quantity of hot water.
2. In another vessel, boil 6 parts of oxide of arsenic with 8 parts of potash, until it throws out no more...
To make Schweinfurth or Emerald Green Color:

1. Dissolve 6 parts of sulphate of copper in a small quantity of hot water.
2. In another vessel, boil 6 parts of oxide of arsenic with 8 parts of potash, until it throws out no more carbonic acid.
3. Mix the hot solution from step 2 by degrees with the first solution (sulphate of copper), agitating continually until the effervescence has entirely ceased.
4. This forms a precipitate of a dirty greenish yellow, very abundant.
5. Add about 3 parts of acetic acid, or such a quantity that there may be a slight excess perceptible to the smell after the mixture.
6. The precipitate will diminish in bulk, and in a few hours, it will deposit spontaneously at the bottom of the liquor entirely discolored, forming a powder of a contexture slightly crystalline, and of a very beautiful green.
7. Separate the floating liquor afterward.
Schweinfurth Green, Emerald Green, sulphate of copper, oxide of arsenic, potash, acetic acid
Make Tin-Copper Green (Stannate of Copper)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make Tin-Copper Green (a stannate of copper):

1. Heap 59 parts of tin in a Hessian crucible, with 100 parts of nitrate of soda.
2. Dissolve the mass when cold in a caustic alkali.
3. When clear, dilute this solution with...
To make Tin-Copper Green (a stannate of copper):

1. Heap 59 parts of tin in a Hessian crucible, with 100 parts of nitrate of soda.
2. Dissolve the mass when cold in a caustic alkali.
3. When clear, dilute this solution with water and add a cold solution of sulphate of copper.
4. A reddish yellow precipitate falls.
5. Wash and dry the precipitate, which becomes a beautiful green.
Tin-Copper Green, stannate of copper, tin, nitrate of soda, caustic alkali, sulphate of copper
Make Titanium Green
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make Titanium Green:

1. Fuse Iserin (titaniferous iron) in a Hessian crucible with 12 times its weight of sulphate of potash.
2. When cold, treat the fused mass with hydrochloric acid, heated to 50° C., and filter hot.
3....
To make Titanium Green:

1. Fuse Iserin (titaniferous iron) in a Hessian crucible with 12 times its weight of sulphate of potash.
2. When cold, treat the fused mass with hydrochloric acid, heated to 50° C., and filter hot.
3. Evaporate the filtrate until a drop placed on a glass plate solidifies.
4. Allow it to cool, then pour over it a concentrated solution of sal ammoniac.
5. Stir well and filter.
6. The titanic acid which remains behind is digested at 50° or 70° with dilute hydrochloric acid.
7. Add some solution of prussiate of potash, then quickly heat to boiling.
8. A green precipitate falls, which must be washed with water acidulated with hydrochloric acid and dried under 100° C.
9. The result is a beautiful dark green powder.
Titanium Green, Iserin, sulphate of potash, hydrochloric acid, sal ammoniac, prussiate of potash
Make a Safe Green Color for Confectionary Use
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make a green color safe for confectionary use:

1. Infuse 0.32 grammes of saffron in 7 grammes of distilled water for 24 hours.
2. Take 0.26 grammes of carmine of indigo and infuse it in 15.6 grammes of distilled water.
3....
To make a green color safe for confectionary use:

1. Infuse 0.32 grammes of saffron in 7 grammes of distilled water for 24 hours.
2. Take 0.26 grammes of carmine of indigo and infuse it in 15.6 grammes of distilled water.
3. Mix the two liquids to obtain a beautiful green color, which is harmless.
4. Ten parts of this mixture will color 1000 parts of sugar.
5. To preserve the color for a long time, either evaporate the liquid to dryness or make it into a syrup.
safe green color, confectionary, saffron, carmine of indigo, distilled water, sugar syrup
Prepare Bistre Color for Herborizations
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• 1 pound of calcined manganese
• 6 oz. of burnt iron straw or 1 lb of iron ore
• 3 oz. of flint powder

Procedure:
1. Pound Separately: The manganese and straw (or iron ore) must be pounded separately in a mortar.
2....
Materials Required:
• 1 pound of calcined manganese
• 6 oz. of burnt iron straw or 1 lb of iron ore
• 3 oz. of flint powder

Procedure:
1. Pound Separately: The manganese and straw (or iron ore) must be pounded separately in a mortar.
2. Calcine Mixture: Combine the pounded materials with the flint powder, then calcine together in an earthen pot.
3. Prepare Paste: Once calcined, the mixture is pounded again and then mixed in a small tub of water to create a paste.

Application Notes:
• This prepared color can be used for herborizations on various kinds of ware.
• For application, use mordants such as urine or essence of tobacco instead of diluting with water.
bistre,color preparation,herborizations,manganese,iron ore,flint powder
Make Scheele's Green
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make Scheele's Green:

1. Dissolve 2 lbs. of blue vitriol in 6 lbs. of water in a copper vessel.
2. In another vessel, dissolve 2 lbs. of dry white potash and 11 oz. of white arsenic in 2 lbs. of water.
3. When the...
To make Scheele's Green:

1. Dissolve 2 lbs. of blue vitriol in 6 lbs. of water in a copper vessel.
2. In another vessel, dissolve 2 lbs. of dry white potash and 11 oz. of white arsenic in 2 lbs. of water.
3. When the solutions are perfect, pour the arsenical lye into the other solution gradually.
4. About 1 lb. 6 oz. of good green precipitate will be obtained.
Scheele's Green, blue vitriol, white potash, arsenic, copper vessel

Foraging Mushrooms (12) -

Identify Boletes (_Boletaceae_)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Examine the cap and stalk: Look for a thick, spongy cap that resembles a hamburger bun on top of a thick stalk.
2. Check the pore surface: Confirm there are downward-facing tubes (pores) instead of gills or ridges.
3....
Procedure:

1. Examine the cap and stalk: Look for a thick, spongy cap that resembles a hamburger bun on top of a thick stalk.
2. Check the pore surface: Confirm there are downward-facing tubes (pores) instead of gills or ridges.
3. Observe coloration: The caps are usually brownish or reddish-brown while the pores may be white, yellow, orange, red, olive, or brown.
4. Size check: Boletes can grow up to 10 inches tall with caps ranging from 1–10 inches wide.
5. Verify growth location: Found growing on the ground near or under trees during summer and fall (May–November).
6. Check for habit: Individuals are found scattered, not in compacted groups.

Notes:
• Boletes are generally safe to eat but should be identified carefully.
• Avoid any boletes with orange or red pores or that bruise blue.
• Confirm identification using multiple field characteristics before consumption.
bolete mushroom, identification, foraging mushrooms, edible boletes
Identify Chicken of the Woods (_Laetiporus_)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Examine the cap: Look for bright-orange caps that are flat and shelflike with a fleshy texture.
2. Check for stalks: Confirm there is no stalk; the mushroom has a flat, shelflike structure.
3. Observe color changes:...
Procedure:

1. Examine the cap: Look for bright-orange caps that are flat and shelflike with a fleshy texture.
2. Check for stalks: Confirm there is no stalk; the mushroom has a flat, shelflike structure.
3. Observe color changes: Young mushrooms have vibrant orange color but fade to peach or salmon as they age.
4. Spore print: The spore print should be white.
5. Size check: Chicken of the woods can range in size from 2–12 inches wide.
6. Verify growth location: Found on dead or dying hardwood trees, stumps, buried roots, or living trees during summer and fall (May–November), rarely found in spring.
7. Check for habit: Usually found growing in large clusters of overlapping caps.

Notes:
• Chicken of the woods is easily identifiable due to its bright coloration.
• It is generally safe to eat but should be confirmed before consumption.
chicken of the woods, laetiporus, identification, foraging mushrooms, edible mushrooms
Identify Oyster Mushrooms (_Pleurotus ostreatus_ or _P. pulmonarius_)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Examine the cap: Look for a gilled, whitish, grayish, or tan cap that is shell-shaped, semicircular to elongated like an oyster. The margin should be smooth and sometimes wavy.
2. Check the stalk: Ensure the stalk is...
Procedure:

1. Examine the cap: Look for a gilled, whitish, grayish, or tan cap that is shell-shaped, semicircular to elongated like an oyster. The margin should be smooth and sometimes wavy.
2. Check the stalk: Ensure the stalk is stubby and offcenter with narrow gills that descend along it.
3. Observe spores: Confirm the spores are white to grayish-lilac.
4. Determine species:
- _P. ostreatus_ has a cap that ranges from pale to dark brown and is found in cooler weather (spring and fall).
- _P. pulmonarius_ has a cap that is white to pale tan and appears during warm summer months.
5. Verify growth location: Oyster mushrooms grow on wood, sometimes appearing as if they are growing out of the ground but are actually attached to tree roots beneath the soil surface.
6. Check for clusters: They usually grow in large clusters of overlapping, shelflike caps.
7. Use a spore print: To confirm identification, make a spore print and compare it to known samples.
8. Watch for pests: Be cautious of small black beetles that may infest the mushroom.

Notes:
• Both species are edible but should be confirmed before consumption.
• Avoid consuming similar-looking species unless positively identified.
oyster mushroom, pleurotus ostreatus, pleurotus pulmonarius, identification, foraging, edible mushrooms
Identify and Handle Ash Tree Bolete (_Boletinellus merulioides_)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Examine the cap and stalk: Look for a thick, spongy cap that resembles a hamburger bun on top of a thick stalk.
2. Check the pore surface: Confirm there are downward-facing tubes (pores) instead of gills or ridges.
3....
Procedure:

1. Examine the cap and stalk: Look for a thick, spongy cap that resembles a hamburger bun on top of a thick stalk.
2. Check the pore surface: Confirm there are downward-facing tubes (pores) instead of gills or ridges.
3. Observe coloration: The caps are usually brownish or reddish-brown while the pores may be white, yellow, orange, red, olive, or brown.
4. Size check: Boletes can grow up to 10 inches tall with caps ranging from 1–10 inches wide.
5. Verify growth location: Found growing on the ground near or under trees during summer and fall (May–November), often associated with ash trees.
6. Check for habit: Individuals are found scattered, not in compacted groups.
7. Avoid consumption: Do not eat any boletes that have orange or red pores or that bruise blue.
8. Confirm identification: Use multiple field characteristics to confirm the species before consuming.
9. Inspect for slime: Check if the cap is slimy and remove this layer before cooking, as it can cause diarrhea.
10. Check for pests: Look for bugs within the cap of the mushroom.

Notes:
• Ash tree boletes are generally safe but should be handled with care.
• Always confirm identification using multiple characteristics to avoid confusion with similar species.
ash tree bolete, identification, foraging mushrooms, edible boletes
Identify and Harvest Coral Fungi (Artomyces, Ramaria)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Inspect the mushroom: Look for clumps of branching stems that point upward, giving a coral-like structure.
2. Check color: Most are tan, whitish, or yellowish, with some being pinkish or purple.
3. Size check: The...
Procedure:

1. Inspect the mushroom: Look for clumps of branching stems that point upward, giving a coral-like structure.
2. Check color: Most are tan, whitish, or yellowish, with some being pinkish or purple.
3. Size check: The clusters may grow up to 8 inches tall.
4. Confirm habitat: Coral mushrooms grow in wooded areas on the ground or on decaying logs and can be found in spring and summer (June–September).
5. Avoid look-alikes: Be cautious of similar-looking mushrooms that may cause gastrointestinal upset or are known to be poisonous.
6. Check for characteristics: Avoid coral fungi that taste bitter, bruise brown when handled, or have gelatinous bases.

Cooking hints:
• The mushroom tips and upper branches are most tender.
• Sauté and add to vegetables or white sauce.
coral fungi, artomyces, ramaria, mushroom identification, foraging, edible mushrooms
Identify and Harvest Crown-tipped Coral (Artomyces pyxidatus)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Inspect the mushroom: Look for a coral-like structure with crown-shaped tips.
2. Check color: The mushrooms are typically tan, whitish, or yellowish.
3. Confirm habitat: Crown-tipped coral grows in wooded areas on the...
Procedure:

1. Inspect the mushroom: Look for a coral-like structure with crown-shaped tips.
2. Check color: The mushrooms are typically tan, whitish, or yellowish.
3. Confirm habitat: Crown-tipped coral grows in wooded areas on the ground or on decaying logs and can be found in spring and summer (June–September).
4. Avoid look-alikes: Be cautious of similar-looking mushrooms that may cause gastrointestinal upset or are known to be poisonous.
5. Check for characteristics: Avoid coral fungi that taste bitter, bruise brown when handled, or have gelatinous bases. A look-alike mushroom is the jellied false coral ( _Tremellodendron pallidum_ ), which has tough branches, unlike the brittle branches of true coral mushrooms, which break easily.

Cooking hints:
• The mushroom tips and upper branches are most tender.
• Sauté and add to vegetables or white sauce.
crown-tipped coral, artomyces pyxidatus, mushroom identification, foraging, edible mushrooms
Identify and Harvest Gem-studded Puffball (Lycoperdon perlatum)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Inspect the mushroom: Slice each puffball from top to bottom and examine the interior.
2. Check for color: The interior should be completely white and featureless, like a marshmallow, with no trace of yellow or brown.
3....
Procedure:

1. Inspect the mushroom: Slice each puffball from top to bottom and examine the interior.
2. Check for color: The interior should be completely white and featureless, like a marshmallow, with no trace of yellow or brown.
3. Avoid look-alikes: Be cautious of other mushrooms, including the deadly destroying angel ( _Amanita bisporigera_ ), which may have round, “button” stages that resemble puffballs.
4. Confirm identity: If a sliced specimen shows any sign of a developing cap or stem inside, it means the mushroom is not a puffball and should not be eaten.

Cooking hints:
• Remove outer skin if it is tough.
• Slice, dip in batter, and fry.
gem-studded puffball, lycoperdon perlatum, mushroom identification, foraging, edible mushrooms
Identify and Harvest Morels
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Inspect the mushroom: Look for a distinctive, honeycomb-like cap with a hollow stem.
2. Check color: Morels are typically yellowish to dark brown in color.
3. Confirm habitat: Morels grow in wooded areas on the ground...
Procedure:

1. Inspect the mushroom: Look for a distinctive, honeycomb-like cap with a hollow stem.
2. Check color: Morels are typically yellowish to dark brown in color.
3. Confirm habitat: Morels grow in wooded areas on the ground and can be found in spring and early summer (April–June).
4. Avoid look-alikes: Be cautious of other mushrooms that may resemble morels but could be poisonous.
5. Check for characteristics: Ensure the cap has a honeycomb pattern and the stem is hollow.
morels, mushroom identification, foraging, edible mushrooms
Identify and Harvest Pear-shaped Puffball (Lycoperdon pyriforme)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Inspect the mushroom: Look for a pear-shaped structure with a smooth surface.
2. Check for color: Slice each puffball from top to bottom and examine the interior. It should be completely white and featureless, like a...
Procedure:

1. Inspect the mushroom: Look for a pear-shaped structure with a smooth surface.
2. Check for color: Slice each puffball from top to bottom and examine the interior. It should be completely white and featureless, like a marshmallow, with no trace of yellow or brown.
3. Avoid look-alikes: Be cautious of other mushrooms, including the deadly destroying angel ( _Amanita bisporigera_ ), which may have round, “button” stages that resemble puffballs.
4. Confirm identity: If a sliced specimen shows any sign of a developing cap or stem inside, it means the mushroom is not a puffball and should not be eaten.
pear-shaped puffball, lycoperdon pyriforme, mushroom identification, foraging, edible mushrooms
Identify and Harvest Shaggy Mane (Coprinus comatus)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Inspect the mushroom: Look for a white mushroom with a shaggy, cylindrical cap that turns black and inky with age.
2. Check for color changes: As the mushroom matures, the cap gradually expands from a cylindrical shape to...
Procedure:

1. Inspect the mushroom: Look for a white mushroom with a shaggy, cylindrical cap that turns black and inky with age.
2. Check for color changes: As the mushroom matures, the cap gradually expands from a cylindrical shape to a bell shape, with the cap’s white scales turning brownish and becoming more upturned, giving the cap a shaggier texture.
3. Confirm spore print: The spore print should be black. If unsure, make a spore print to confirm that it has black spores.
4. Avoid look-alikes: Be cautious of other white mushrooms that may grow in similar habitats and could be poisonous.

Cooking hints:
• Pick only young, fresh specimens.
• Cook them as soon as possible after harvesting, then refrigerate.
• Sauté mushrooms in butter and season with nutmeg or garlic.
• Add to scrambled eggs or chicken dishes.
shaggy mane, coprinus comatus, mushroom identification, foraging, edible mushrooms
Prepare and Cook Boletes
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Clean the mushrooms: Gently brush off any dirt or debris from the caps and stalks.
2. Remove pests: Check for bugs within the cap of the mushroom and remove them if present.
3. Trim the stems: Remove tough stems,...
Procedure:

1. Clean the mushrooms: Gently brush off any dirt or debris from the caps and stalks.
2. Remove pests: Check for bugs within the cap of the mushroom and remove them if present.
3. Trim the stems: Remove tough stems, especially in older specimens.
4. Peel the pore layer: In all but the youngest boletes, peel off the pore layer.
5. Cooking method:
- Sauté in butter.
- Add to any cheese dish.
- Dried boletes can be used in soups.

Notes:
• Boletes are best eaten the same day they are collected.
• Avoid consuming boletes that have been stored for long periods or show signs of decay.
cooking boletes, preparing mushrooms, edible mushroom recipes
Prepare and Cook Oyster Mushrooms
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Clean the mushrooms: Gently brush off any dirt or debris from the caps and stalks.
2. Remove pests: Check for small black beetles and remove them if present.
3. Cut the mushrooms: Slice the mushrooms into pieces as...
Procedure:

1. Clean the mushrooms: Gently brush off any dirt or debris from the caps and stalks.
2. Remove pests: Check for small black beetles and remove them if present.
3. Cut the mushrooms: Slice the mushrooms into pieces as desired for cooking.
4. Cooking method:
- Dip in beaten egg.
- Roll in cracker crumbs.
- Fry until golden brown.

Notes:
• Oyster mushrooms are best consumed fresh, ideally within a day of harvesting.
• They can be stored in the refrigerator for up to 2 days if not cooked immediately.
cooking oyster mushrooms, preparing mushrooms, edible mushroom recipes

2. Before applying, size the drawing or print with a solution of isinglass in water.
3. Once dry, apply...
Ingredients:

• Spirit of turpentine: 2 oz
Procedure:
1. Mix the Canada balsam and spirit of turpentine together.
2. Before applying, size the drawing or print with a solution of isinglass in water.
3. Once dry, apply the varnish using a camel's-hair brush.

Prepare Drying Fat Oils Using Lead Compounds and Garlic
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• 8 lbs. nut-oil or linseed oil
• 1 oz. white lead (slightly calcined)
• 1 oz. yellow acetate of lead (sal saturni) (calcined)
• 1 oz. sulphate of zinc (white vitriol)
• 12 oz. vitreous oxide of lead (litharge)
• A head of...
Materials Required:
• 8 lbs. nut-oil or linseed oil
• 1 oz. white lead (slightly calcined)
• 1 oz. yellow acetate of lead (sal saturni) (calcined)
• 1 oz. sulphate of zinc (white vitriol)
• 12 oz. vitreous oxide of lead (litharge)
• A head of garlic or a small onion
• Fire capable of maintaining slight ebullition
• Wide-mouthed bottles for storage

Steps:
1. Prepare Dry Ingredients:
- Pulverize the white lead, yellow acetate of lead, and sulphate of zinc.

2. Combine with Oil and Garlic:
- Mix the dry ingredients with a head of garlic or small onion in the oil over a fire that maintains a slight state of ebullition.

3. Heat Until Ready:
- Continue heating until the oil no longer produces scum, turns reddish in color, and the garlic becomes brown.
- A pellicle (thin film) will form on the surface, indicating completion of the process.

4. Settle and Separate:
- Remove from heat and let the mixture rest until the pellicle settles to the bottom, carrying away impurities.
- Decant the clear oil into wide-mouthed bottles.

5. Final Clarification:
- Allow the oil to clarify completely over time in its storage bottle.
fat oils,drying oil,lead compounds,garlic
Prepare Drying Poppy Oil Using Sulphate of Zinc
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• 3 lbs. pure water
• 1 oz. sulphate of zinc (white vitriol)
• 2 lbs. oil of pinks or poppy oil
• Earthen vessel capable of withstanding heat
• Glass bottle or jar
• Glass funnel with cork stopper

Steps:
1. **Mix...
Materials Required:
• 3 lbs. pure water
• 1 oz. sulphate of zinc (white vitriol)
• 2 lbs. oil of pinks or poppy oil
• Earthen vessel capable of withstanding heat
• Glass bottle or jar
• Glass funnel with cork stopper

Steps:
1. Mix Ingredients:
- In an earthen vessel, combine 3 lbs. of pure water with 1 oz. of sulphate of zinc (white vitriol) and 2 lbs. of poppy oil or oil of pinks.

2. Heat the Mixture:
- Place the vessel over a fire that maintains a slight state of ebullition (gentle boiling).
- Continue heating until one-half to two-thirds of the water has evaporated.

3. Transfer and Settle:
- Pour the mixture into a large glass bottle or jar and let it rest until the oil becomes clear.

4. Decant and Separate:
- Use a glass funnel with a cork stopper to decant the clearest portion of the oil.
- Replace the cork with your forefinger, allowing water to escape while retaining only the oil.

5. Final Clarification:
- Allow the oil to rest for several weeks until it becomes limpid and colorless.
poppy oil,drying oil,sulphate of zinc,clarification
Remove Old Paint or Putty Using Caustic Soda
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Concentrated lye (caustic soda)
• Brush or broom made of vegetable matter

Steps:
1. Apply Lye Solution:
- Use a brush or broom made from vegetable fibers to apply concentrated caustic soda (lye) to the surface...
Materials Required:
• Concentrated lye (caustic soda)
• Brush or broom made of vegetable matter

Steps:
1. Apply Lye Solution:
- Use a brush or broom made from vegetable fibers to apply concentrated caustic soda (lye) to the surface with old paint or putty.
- Allow it to sit for sufficient time to break down the paint or putty.

2. Scrape Off Residue:
- After the lye has softened the material, scrape off the loosened paint or putty using a suitable tool.
caustic soda,paint removal,putty removal
Gild Silk, Satin with Decorative Ornaments Using Hydrogen Gas
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Paint flowers or other ornaments on silk or satin using a fine camel-hair pencil dipped in the gold chloride solution (1 part chloride to 3 parts distilled water).
2. Hold the painted item over a Florence flask from which hydrogen...
Procedure:

1. Paint flowers or other ornaments on silk or satin using a fine camel-hair pencil dipped in the gold chloride solution (1 part chloride to 3 parts distilled water).
2. Hold the painted item over a Florence flask from which hydrogen gas is being released during the decomposition of water by sulfuric acid and iron filings.
3. Within minutes, the painted areas will be covered with a layer of pure gold that shines brightly.
4. This coating is resistant to tarnishing when exposed to air or washing.
gild,silk,satin,decorative ornaments,camel-hair pencil,chloride of gold,hydrogen gas,Florence flask
Gild Silk, Satin, Ivory Using Hydrogen Gas
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Immerse a piece of white satin, silk, or ivory in a solution of chloride of gold (1 part chloride to 3 parts distilled water).
2. While the item is still wet, immerse it into a jar containing hydrogen gas.
3. The item will be...
Procedure:

1. Immerse a piece of white satin, silk, or ivory in a solution of chloride of gold (1 part chloride to 3 parts distilled water).
2. While the item is still wet, immerse it into a jar containing hydrogen gas.
3. The item will be coated with a complete layer of gold within a short time.
gild,silk,satin,ivory,chloride of gold,hydrogen gas

2. Use a brush to apply this mixture and write the letters.
3. Allow the mixture to dry almost completely.
4. Apply gold leaf onto the surface.
5. Burnish the surface...
Procedure:

2. Use a brush to apply this mixture and write the letters.
3. Allow the mixture to dry almost completely.
4. Apply gold leaf onto the surface.
5. Burnish the surface to achieve a smooth finish.

Gild the Edges of Paper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Prepare a composition by mixing four parts Armenian bole with one part candied sugar, ground together with water to form a proper consistency.
2. Apply this mixture onto the edges of paper using a brush and mix it with the white...
Procedure:

1. Prepare a composition by mixing four parts Armenian bole with one part candied sugar, ground together with water to form a proper consistency.
2. Apply this mixture onto the edges of paper using a brush and mix it with the white of an egg.
3. Allow the coating to dry nearly completely.
4. Smooth the surface using a burnisher made from a crooked piece of agate, fixed in a handle.
5. Slightly moisten the surface by pressing a sponge dipped in clean water into the hand and squeezing it.
6. Take gold leaf from a leathern cushion using cotton wool (preferred for its pliability, smoothness, softness, and slight moisture).
7. Apply the gold leaf onto the moistened surface.
8. Once dry, burnish the surface repeatedly with the agate burnisher from end to end, taking care not to damage it.
9. Interpose a piece of silk or India paper between the gold and the burnisher during this process.
gild,paper,edges,Armenian bole,candied sugar,eggs,burnish,agate,cotton wool
Oil Gilding on Wood
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Prime the wood surface with 2 or 3 coatings of boiled linseed oil and carbonate of lead to fill pores and conceal irregularities.
2. Allow the priming to dry completely.
3. Prepare gold size by grinding red oxide of lead with the...
Procedure:

1. Prime the wood surface with 2 or 3 coatings of boiled linseed oil and carbonate of lead to fill pores and conceal irregularities.
2. Allow the priming to dry completely.
3. Prepare gold size by grinding red oxide of lead with the thickest drying oil (the older, the better), then mix it with a small amount of turpentine until it reaches the proper consistency.
4. Apply a thin coat of this gold size onto the wood surface.
5. Allow the gold size to dry for 12 hours or more until it is sufficiently firm.
6. Spread a leaf of gold on a cushion made from folded flannel secured in leather and cut into strips using a blunt pallet knife.
7. Take each strip with a fine brush and apply it to the area intended for gilding.
8. Gently press down the gold onto the surface using a ball of soft cotton.
9. Use a large camel's-hair brush to sweep away loose particles of gold leaf without disturbing the adhered portion.
10. Allow the size to dry completely over one or two days, then complete the process.
gild,wood,oil gilding,boiled linseed oil,carbonate of lead,gold size,red oxide of lead,turpentine,cotton wool,camel's-hair brush
Prepare Grecian Wax Paint Base
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• 1 oz. white wax
• 1 oz. gum mastic (in drops, made into powder)
• Glazed pan
• Water
• Mortar and pestle
• Linen cloth
• Strong gum-water
• Colored pigments
• Hard brush
• White wax for varnish
• Fire source
• Linen cloth...
Materials Required:
• 1 oz. white wax
• 1 oz. gum mastic (in drops, made into powder)
• Glazed pan
• Water
• Mortar and pestle
• Linen cloth
• Strong gum-water
• Colored pigments
• Hard brush
• White wax for varnish
• Fire source
• Linen cloth for rubbing

Steps:
1. Melt Wax and Mastic:
- Place 1 oz of white wax into a glazed pan over a slow fire.
- When the wax is melted, add 1 oz of gum mastic (in powder form) and stir until both are fully incorporated.

2. Cool and Process Paste:
- Pour the mixture into water to cool and harden.
- Once hardened, remove it from the water and wipe dry.
- Beat the paste in a mortar until it becomes a coarse powder.
- Continue pounding in a linen cloth until reduced to a fine powder.

3. Prepare Paint Mixture:
- Make a strong gum-water solution.
- When painting, mix a small amount of the prepared wax powder with colored pigments and gum-water.
- Lighter colors require less wax powder; darker colors (especially black) require nearly equal parts of wax powder and pigment.

4. Apply Paint:
- Ensure the wooden surface is pre-painted with a proper ground color, as described in the text.
- Apply the paint mixture using water, similar to watercolor techniques.

5. Dry and Varnish:
- Allow the painting to dry completely.
- Use a hard brush to apply white wax varnish (melted over a slow fire) evenly across the surface.
- Ensure the wax does not boil during melting.

6. Final Treatment:
- Hold the picture near a fire until the wax is melted but not runny.
- Allow the varnish to cool and harden completely.
- Gently rub the surface with a linen cloth.

7. Fix Blistering Issues:
- If blisters form, warm the painting very slowly near a fire until the bubbles subside.
grecian method,wax paint,varnish,painting techniques
Prepare Solvents for India-Rubber and Gutta Percha
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Benzine (from coal or petroleum)
• Bisulphide of Carbon
• Chloroform
• Turpentine
• India rubber (cut into fine strings or shreds)

Steps:
1. Benzine Solvent:
- Use either true benzine (distilled from coal or...
Materials Required:
• Benzine (from coal or petroleum)
• Bisulphide of Carbon
• Chloroform
• Turpentine
• India rubber (cut into fine strings or shreds)

Steps:
1. Benzine Solvent:
- Use either true benzine (distilled from coal or coal-tar) or petroleum-based benzine.
- True benzine is suitable for varnishes, while the cheaper petroleum version can be used in mixing paints.

2. Bisulphide of Carbon:
- An excellent rubber solvent that works at room temperature.
- Produced by passing sulfur vapor over red-hot charcoal.

3. Chloroform:
- A highly effective but costly solvent for India-rubber.

4. Turpentine:
- Acts slowly and takes longer to dry.
- Always cut India rubber into fine strings or shreds before using any solvent.
solvents,india-rubber,gutta-percha,benzine,chloroform

Wildlife Identification (11) -

Identify Red Fox Physical Characteristics
Reference Source: Student manual 2005
Procedure:

1. Observe size and weight:
- Red fox weigh between 10 to 12 pounds, with heavier individuals in the north reaching up to 14 pounds.
- Total body length ranges from 35 to 41 inches.

2. **Examine fur...
Procedure:

1. Observe size and weight:
- Red fox weigh between 10 to 12 pounds, with heavier individuals in the north reaching up to 14 pounds.
- Total body length ranges from 35 to 41 inches.

2. Examine fur coloration:
- Typically, they have a red top coat, transitioning to gray or white on the lower body.
- Black markings are present on the ears, lower legs, and feet.
- Other color variations include black, silver, and crosses between red and silver.

3. Inspect tail features:
- The tail is bushy, measuring 14 to 17 inches in length, with a white tip.

4. Note behavioral traits:
- Red fox are nocturnal and have the ability to detect small prey underground due to their sensitivity to low-frequency sounds.
red fox, identification, physical characteristics, wildlife, animal behavior
Identify Ringtail/Bassarisk Physical Characteristics
Reference Source: Student manual 2005
Procedure:

1. Observe size and weight:
- Ringtails weigh between 2 to 2.5 pounds.

2. Examine physical features:
- They have a cat-like body shape, with a long, bushy tail featuring **14 to 16 distinct black and white...
Procedure:

1. Observe size and weight:
- Ringtails weigh between 2 to 2.5 pounds.

2. Examine physical features:
- They have a cat-like body shape, with a long, bushy tail featuring 14 to 16 distinct black and white bands.

3. Note behavioral traits:
- Ringtails are nocturnal, staying in dens during the day.
- They are excellent climbers and can scale trees or walls.
- They are also strong leapers.

4. Identify common names:
- Sometimes referred to as “miner’s cats” due to their historical use in mines for rat control.
ringtail, bassarisk, identification, physical characteristics, wildlife behavior
Identify Mule Deer by Physical Characteristics
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Look for the following distinguishing features of a mule deer:
- Horns: Double-forked.
- Ears: Large in size.
- Forehead: Dark patch with the rest of the face being whitish.
- Tail: White with a black...
Procedure:

1. Look for the following distinguishing features of a mule deer:
- Horns: Double-forked.
- Ears: Large in size.
- Forehead: Dark patch with the rest of the face being whitish.
- Tail: White with a black bunch on the end.

2. Note that mule deer are generally larger than white-tailed deer.

3. Recognize regional distribution:
- Found in mountainous regions from Mexico to British Columbia and northeasterly Saskatchewan and the Lake of the Woods.

4. Be aware of variations: several varieties exist in the South and West.
mule deer, identification, wildlife, physical characteristics, scouting
Identify White-tailed Deer by Physical Characteristics
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Look for the following distinguishing features of a white-tailed deer:
- Horns: Forward bend with snags pointing backward.
- Tail: Long, brown or blackish above and pure white below.
- Face: Gray in color.
...
Procedure:

1. Look for the following distinguishing features of a white-tailed deer:
- Horns: Forward bend with snags pointing backward.
- Tail: Long, brown or blackish above and pure white below.
- Face: Gray in color.
- Throat: White in appearance.

2. Note the weight range of a fair-sized buck: approximately 200 pounds live weight, with some individuals reaching over 350 pounds.

3. Recognize regional variations:
- In the Southern states, white-tailed deer are generally smaller.
- Historically found in all timber states east of the Rockies, as well as in Ontario, Quebec, and New Brunswick.
- Currently exterminated in highly cultivated states of the Middle West but has spread into northern Ontario, New Brunswick, and Manitoba.
white-tailed deer, identification, wildlife, physical characteristics, scouting
Identify a Beaver
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Title: Identify a Beaver

Description: The beaver can be identified by the following characteristics:

• Appearance: Large size (weighing from 25 to 50 pounds), chestnut color with darker fur on the crown, webbed feet, and a broad, flat,...
Title: Identify a Beaver

Description: The beaver can be identified by the following characteristics:

• Appearance: Large size (weighing from 25 to 50 pounds), chestnut color with darker fur on the crown, webbed feet, and a broad, flat, naked, scaly tail.
• Behavior: Famous for building dams and digging canals. Its pelt is highly valued as a fur.
• Habitat: Found wherever there was water and timber in North America north of Mexico; however, it has been exterminated in most highly settled regions.
beaver, identification, wildlife, animal characteristics
Identify a Cottontail Rabbit
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Title: Identify a Cottontail Rabbit

Description: The common eastern cottontail can be identified by the following characteristics:

• Appearance: Distinguished from the snowshoe hare by its smaller feet and much larger, longer tail...
Title: Identify a Cottontail Rabbit

Description: The common eastern cottontail can be identified by the following characteristics:

• Appearance: Distinguished from the snowshoe hare by its smaller feet and much larger, longer tail (gray above and snow-white underneath).
• Tail Coloration: Unlike the tame rabbit, the cottontail has a black top on its tail.
• Habitat: Found in most parts of the United States; enters Canada only in the Ontario peninsula and southern Saskatchewan. They do not turn white in winter.
cottontail rabbit, identification, wildlife, animal characteristics
Identify a Cougar
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Title: Identify a Cougar

Description: The cougar can be identified by the following characteristics:

• Appearance: Large cat with distinctive features such as long legs and a short tail.
• Behavior: Known for its stealth and power,...
Title: Identify a Cougar

Description: The cougar can be identified by the following characteristics:

• Appearance: Large cat with distinctive features such as long legs and a short tail.
• Behavior: Known for its stealth and power, it is an apex predator in its habitat.
• Habitat: Found in various regions across North America, including mountainous areas and forests.
cougar, identification, wildlife, animal characteristics
Identify a Jack Rabbit
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Title: Identify a Jack Rabbit

Description: The jack-rabbit can be identified by the following characteristics:

• Appearance: Known for its speed and large ears. Its size is about double that of a common rabbit.
• Markings: Has a...
Title: Identify a Jack Rabbit

Description: The jack-rabbit can be identified by the following characteristics:

• Appearance: Known for its speed and large ears. Its size is about double that of a common rabbit.
• Markings: Has a jet black stripe running from its back into its tail.
• Habitat: Found on the plains from Nebraska to Oregon and south to Mexico. There are several different varieties.
jack rabbit, identification, wildlife, animal characteristics
Identify a Mountain Goat
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Title: Identify a Mountain Goat

Description: The mountain goat can be identified by the following characteristics:

• Appearance: Pure white coat of wool and hair, black horns, and a peculiar shape.
• Size: Larger than a common...
Title: Identify a Mountain Goat

Description: The mountain goat can be identified by the following characteristics:

• Appearance: Pure white coat of wool and hair, black horns, and a peculiar shape.
• Size: Larger than a common deer. A full-grown male weighs between 250 to 300 pounds; females weigh about one-third less.
• Habitat: Found in the higher Rockies, primarily above timber lines, from central Idaho to Alaska.
• Behavior: Known for its incredible ability as a rock climber and mountaineer.
mountain goat, identification, wildlife, animal characteristics
Identify a Muskrat
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Title: Identify a Muskrat

Description: The muskrat can be identified by the following characteristics:

• Appearance: About the size of a cat (21 inches long, with a tail measuring about 10 inches). Resembles the beaver in color but has...
Title: Identify a Muskrat

Description: The muskrat can be identified by the following characteristics:

• Appearance: About the size of a cat (21 inches long, with a tail measuring about 10 inches). Resembles the beaver in color but has less conspicuously webbed feet.
• Tail: Long and flattened vertically, not horizontally as in the beaver.
• Habitat: Found throughout North America within the limit of trees wherever there is fresh water. It is the most abundant fur on the market.
muskrat, identification, wildlife, animal characteristics
Identify a Woodchuck
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Title: Identify a Woodchuck

Description: The common woodchuck can be identified by the following characteristics:

• Appearance: Grizzly brown on the back, chestnut on the breast, blackish on the crown and paws, and whitish on the...
Title: Identify a Woodchuck

Description: The common woodchuck can be identified by the following characteristics:

• Appearance: Grizzly brown on the back, chestnut on the breast, blackish on the crown and paws, and whitish on the cheeks.
• Physical Features: Short ears and a bushy tail.
• Size: Approximately 24 inches in length (including the tail), with the tail measuring about 5.5 inches.
• Weight: Ranges from 5 to 10 pounds.
• Habitat: Found in all wooded parts of Canada, from the Rockies to the Atlantic, and south in the eastern states to about 40 degrees latitude.
woodchuck, identification, wildlife, animal characteristics

Skincare Products (10) -

Prepare Amandine Paste
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Gum arabic: 2 oz
• White honey: 6 oz
• Neutral white soap: 3 oz
• Fresh coldpressed oil of sweet almonds: 2 oz
• Yolks of eggs: 5 eggs

Procedure:
1. In a mortar, mix gum arabic and white honey until they form a thick...
Ingredients:
• Gum arabic: 2 oz
• White honey: 6 oz
• Neutral white soap: 3 oz
• Fresh coldpressed oil of sweet almonds: 2 oz
• Yolks of eggs: 5 eggs

Procedure:
1. In a mortar, mix gum arabic and white honey until they form a thick mass.
2. Gradually incorporate the neutral white soap into the mixture.
3. Add the fresh coldpressed oil of sweet almonds, mixing thoroughly.
4. Finally, add the yolks of 5 eggs to complete the paste.

Additional Preparation:
Mix 4 oz of fresh peeled pistach nuts with 4 oz of distilled rose-water to create a thick milk of pistachio. Add 1/2 dr of bitter almonds per lb of paste for aromatization. To use, mix a small portion with warm water to produce a white lather with an agreeable odor.
amandine paste, skincare, natural remedies, preparation

• Spirit of geranium: 2 1/2 drs

Prepare Cold Cream No. 1
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:

• Almond oil: 10 oz
• Rose-water: 3 1/2 oz
• Essence of roses: 15 grs
• Potash lye: a few drops

Procedure:
1. Liquefy spermaceti and white wax over a water-bath.
2. Pour the melted...
Ingredients:
• Spermaceti: 5 drs
• White wax: 5 drs
• Almond oil: 10 oz
• Rose-water: 3 1/2 oz
• Essence of roses: 15 grs
• Potash lye: a few drops

Procedure:
1. Liquefy spermaceti and white wax over a water-bath.
2. Pour the melted mixture into a marble mortar heated by boiling water.
3. Stir quickly, then add almond oil.
4. Add rose-water, essence of roses, and potash lye to the mixture.
5. Continue stirring until well combined.

• Bichloride of mercury: 5 grs per 8 oz bottle
• Rose or violet scent: as desired

Procedure:
Mix blanched bitter almonds and rose-water thoroughly, then strain. Add...
Ingredients:
• Blanched bitter almonds: 1 part
• Rose-water: 16 parts
• Bichloride of mercury: 5 grs per 8 oz bottle
• Rose or violet scent: as desired

Procedure:
Mix blanched bitter almonds and rose-water thoroughly, then strain. Add bichloride of mercury to every 8 oz bottle, and scent with rose or violet.

Procedure:

• Spirit of Portugal: 2 oz

Procedure:

Prepare Milk of Roses
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Spermaceti: 1 oz
• White wax: 1 oz
• Soap: 1 oz
• Bitter almonds: 2 oz
• Sweet almonds: 1 lb
• Distilled water: 1 qt
• Rose-water: 1/2 pt
• Spirit of rose: 1/2 pt

Procedure:
1. Melt spermaceti, white wax, and soap...
Ingredients:
• Spermaceti: 1 oz
• White wax: 1 oz
• Soap: 1 oz
• Bitter almonds: 2 oz
• Sweet almonds: 1 lb
• Distilled water: 1 qt
• Rose-water: 1/2 pt
• Spirit of rose: 1/2 pt

Procedure:
1. Melt spermaceti, white wax, and soap together in a stoneware vessel over a water-bath.
2. In a marble mortar, rub bitter almonds until well incorporated.
3. Take out 3/4 of the almonds, then pour the melted mixture onto the remaining 1/4, continuing to rub thoroughly.
4. Gradually add the other 3/4 of the almonds, always pestling to ensure thorough incorporation.
5. In a white glass bottle, mix distilled water, rose-water, and spirit of rose.
6. Reserve 1 pt of the liquid, then gradually pour the almond mixture into the remaining liquid.
7. Rub and strain through a cloth, returning the residue to the mortar.
8. Triturate with the reserved mixture, strain again, and mix with the other liquid.
milk of roses, skincare, natural remedies, preparation

Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia

• Lycopodium powder: 1 oz
• Pomade rosat: 2 lbs

Procedure:
Mix oxide of zinc, lycopodium powder, and pomade rosat thoroughly to create a perfectly homogeneous pomade. This is an excellent...
Ingredients:
• Oxide of zinc: 1 oz
• Lycopodium powder: 1 oz
• Pomade rosat: 2 lbs

Procedure:
Mix oxide of zinc, lycopodium powder, and pomade rosat thoroughly to create a perfectly homogeneous pomade. This is an excellent remedy for chapped lips and beneficial in cases of ulceration of the nails of the feet.
pomade for chapped lips, skincare, natural remedies, preparation

Enamel Preparation (10) -

Cautions in Making Colored Enamel
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
General Cautions:
1. Ensure that the pots are glazed with white glass and are suitable to bear the fire.
2. Mix the matter of enamels very nicely with the colors.
3. When the enamel is good, and the color well incorporated, remove it from the...
General Cautions:
1. Ensure that the pots are glazed with white glass and are suitable to bear the fire.
2. Mix the matter of enamels very nicely with the colors.
3. When the enamel is good, and the color well incorporated, remove it from the fire using a pair of tongs.
cautions, enamel preparation, white glass, tongs, mixing colors

Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Red-lead: 1 oz.
• Calcined iron: 1 oz.
• Antimony: 2 oz.
• Litharge: 2 oz.
• Zaffre: 1 oz.
• Sand: 2 oz.

Procedure:
1. Take red-lead, calcined iron, antimony, litharge, zaffre, and sand as specified above.
2. Calcine...
Materials Required:
• Red-lead: 1 oz.
• Calcined iron: 1 oz.
• Antimony: 2 oz.
• Litharge: 2 oz.
• Zaffre: 1 oz.
• Sand: 2 oz.

Procedure:
1. Take red-lead, calcined iron, antimony, litharge, zaffre, and sand as specified above.
2. Calcine or melt together, or use raw, as may be most expedient.
3. Vary the proportions of any or all the materials as the tint or quality may require.

2. Grind them together for use.
3. If necessary, add flux to make the...

2. Grind them together for use.

Prepare Purple Enamel
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Opaque or transparent enamel: 12 parts
• Purple of Cassius: 1 to 2 parts
• Sal ammoniac: as needed
• Sulphur: 6 times the weight of the powder
• Mercury: twice the weight of the powder
• Flux: 10 times the weight of the...
Materials Required:
• Opaque or transparent enamel: 12 parts
• Purple of Cassius: 1 to 2 parts
• Sal ammoniac: as needed
• Sulphur: 6 times the weight of the powder
• Mercury: twice the weight of the powder
• Flux: 10 times the weight of the final powder

Procedure:
1. Take opaque or transparent enamel, 12 parts.
2. Mix with purple of Cassius, 1 to 2 parts, regulated with sal ammoniac.
3. Put it in a sand-heat for about 48 hours to digest the gold.
4. Collect the powder and grind it with 6 times its weight of sulphur.
5. Place the mixture into a crucible on the fire until the sulphur is evaporated.
6. Amalgamate the powder with twice its weight of mercury.
7. Put the mixture into a mortar or other vessel and rub it together for about 6 hours with a small quantity of water, changing the water frequently.
8. Evaporate the remaining mercury in a crucible.
9. Add to the powder 10 times its weight of flux, or more or less as the hardness or softness of the color may require.
purple enamel, purple of Cassius, sal ammoniac, sulphur, mercury, flux
Prepare Rose-colored Enamel
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Purple enamel (prepared above): as needed
• Flux: 30 times the weight of the purple enamel
• Silverleaf or silver preparation: 1/100th part of the weight of the purple enamel

Procedure:
1. Take the purple enamel...
Materials Required:
• Purple enamel (prepared above): as needed
• Flux: 30 times the weight of the purple enamel
• Silverleaf or silver preparation: 1/100th part of the weight of the purple enamel

Procedure:
1. Take the purple enamel prepared above.
2. Mix it with 30 times its weight of flux and 1/100th part of its weight of silverleaf, or any preparation of silver.
3. Vary the proportion of the flux and silver as the quality of the color may require.
4. Alternatively, use other preparations for purple enamel, varying the proportions of the flux and silver as above.
5. Any materials from which purple can be produced, with the addition of silver and flux, will also answer.
rose-colored enamel, purple enamel, flux, silverleaf, silver preparation
Prepare White Enamel
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Tin prepared by aqua-fortis: 1 oz.
• Red-lead: 1 oz.
• White pebble-stone or natural crystal: 2 oz.
• Nitre: 1 oz.
• Arsenic: 1 dr.

Procedure:
1. Take the tin prepared by aqua-fortis and red-lead, each 1 oz.
2. Add...
Materials Required:
• Tin prepared by aqua-fortis: 1 oz.
• Red-lead: 1 oz.
• White pebble-stone or natural crystal: 2 oz.
• Nitre: 1 oz.
• Arsenic: 1 dr.

Procedure:
1. Take the tin prepared by aqua-fortis and red-lead, each 1 oz.
2. Add white pebble-stone or natural crystal, 2 oz.
3. Include nitre, 1 oz., and arsenic, 1 dr.
4. Mix with equal parts of flux, or more or less as the softness or opacity may require.
5. Melt together, calcine, or use raw.
white enamel, tin aqua-fortis, red-lead, white pebble-stone, nitre, arsenic, flux
General Method of Making Colored Enamels
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps to make colored enamels:

1. Prepare the colors: Powder, sift, and grind all the colors very finely.
2. Mix the colors: First mix the colors with one another, then combine them with the common matter of enamels.
3. **Heat in a...
Steps to make colored enamels:

1. Prepare the colors: Powder, sift, and grind all the colors very finely.
2. Mix the colors: First mix the colors with one another, then combine them with the common matter of enamels.
3. Heat in a furnace: Place the mixture in pots and set them in a furnace until they are well mixed and incorporated.
4. Cast into water: Once well mixed, cast the mixture into water and allow it to dry.
5. Reheat in a furnace: Set the dried mixture back into a furnace to melt.
6. Test the result: Take a proof of the melted enamel.
- If the color is too deep: Add more of the common matter of enamels.
- If the color is too pale: Add more of the colors.
colored enamel, preparation, furnace, mixing, grinding
To Make Enamel Called Niella
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps to make niella enamel:

1. Prepare the metals: Take 1 part pure silver, 2 parts copper, and 3 parts pure lead.
2. Fuse the metals: Melt them together in a furnace.
3. Pour into a matrass: Pour the molten amalgam into a...
Steps to make niella enamel:

1. Prepare the metals: Take 1 part pure silver, 2 parts copper, and 3 parts pure lead.
2. Fuse the metals: Melt them together in a furnace.
3. Pour into a matrass: Pour the molten amalgam into a long-necked earthenware matrass that is half-filled with levigated sulphur.
4. Seal and cool: Immediately close the mouth of the vessel and allow it to cool.
5. Process the mass: Once cooled, levigate (grind finely) and wash the resulting mass.
6. Apply to a plate: Fill cavities left by fusion with the prepared niella enamel.
7. Heat the plate: Hold the plate over a small furnace fueled with a mixture of charcoal and wood.
8. Distribute the enamel: Use an appropriate instrument to distribute the enamel evenly.
9. Remove and cool: Remove the plate from the heat once fusion has occurred, then allow it to cool sufficiently.
10. Finish the surface: Clear any excess with a file and polish using fine pumice and tripoli.
niella enamel, silver, copper, lead, sulphur
To Obtain Black Enamel with Platina
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps to obtain black enamel using platina:

1. Mix chloride of platina: Dissolve some chloride of platina in water.
2. Combine with nitrate of mercury: Mix the solution with neutral nitrate of mercury.
3. Form a precipitate: Allow a...
Steps to obtain black enamel using platina:

1. Mix chloride of platina: Dissolve some chloride of platina in water.
2. Combine with nitrate of mercury: Mix the solution with neutral nitrate of mercury.
3. Form a precipitate: Allow a precipitate to form.
4. Heat the precipitate: Expose the precipitate to heat sufficient to volatilize the proto-chloride of mercury.
5. Resulting black powder: A black powder will be obtained, which can be applied with a flux to create a beautiful black enamel.
black enamel, platina, nitrate of mercury, precipitate
To Paint in Enamel
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps to paint in enamel:

1. Understand the process: The enamel painter works with mixtures of metallic oxides and vitrescent (glass-like) materials, not actual colors.
2. Knowledge required: Requires experience in knowing how different...
Steps to paint in enamel:

1. Understand the process: The enamel painter works with mixtures of metallic oxides and vitrescent (glass-like) materials, not actual colors.
2. Knowledge required: Requires experience in knowing how different metallic oxides interact chemically under heat, their fusibility, and the precise temperatures needed to retain color accuracy and figure detail.
3. Multiple firings: Painting in enamel requires a series of firings:
- First, fire the ground (base) that will receive the design, which itself needs two firings.
- Then, fire each part of the design separately.
4. Prepare the ground: Apply the ground using methods similar to those used for common watch-face enameling.
5. Mix colors: Use different metallic oxides melted with a vitrescent mixture and ground to extreme fineness.
6. Prepare paint mixtures: Work up the powdered enamel with an essential oil (spikenard is preferred, followed by lavender oil) to achieve the proper consistency of oil paints.
7. Apply with brush: Use a very fine hair brush to apply the enamel mixture onto the prepared surface.
8. Use pure essential oils: Ensure that the essential oil used is very pure; this allows it to evaporate completely in moderate heat, leaving no carbonaceous matter that could affect oxidation and color accuracy.
9. Order of application: Apply colors based on their required firing temperatures:
- First apply those that require the highest temperature for fixation.
- Finish with those that need the least heat.
10. Outline and fill: Trace the outline of the design first, then gradually fill in the details through repeated firings until achieving the final, delicate touches.
painting enamel, metallic oxides, firing process, oil colors

Dyeing And Staining (9) -

Another Method to Stain Wood
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Another Method to Stain Wood:

1. Prepare wood: After forming the wood into its destined figure, rub it with slightly diluted aquafortis.
2. Remove threads: Small threads of wood will rise during drying; rub them off with...
Another Method to Stain Wood:

1. Prepare wood: After forming the wood into its destined figure, rub it with slightly diluted aquafortis.
2. Remove threads: Small threads of wood will rise during drying; rub them off with pumice-stone.
3. Repeat process: Repeat this process again.
4. Make solution: In a glazed earthen vessel, mix 1 pint of strong vinegar, 2 oz. of fine iron filings, and 1/2 lb. of pounded galls. Let it infuse for 3–4 hours on hot cinders.
5. Add copperas and borax: Add 4 oz. of copperas (sulphate of iron) and a chopin of water with 1/2 oz. of borax and as much indigo dissolved in it. Boil until froth rises.
6. Apply solution: Rub several layers of this solution onto the wood.
7. Polish: When dry, polish the wood with leather on which a little tripoli has been applied.
wood,aquafortis,pumice-stone,vinegar,iron filings,galls,copperas,borax,indigo,tripoli,leather
Another Method to Stain Wood with Logwood Extract
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Another Method to Stain Wood with Logwood Extract:

1. Prepare solution: Pour 2 qts. of boiling water over 1 oz. commercial extract of logwood.
2. Add chromate: When dissolved, add 1 dr. of yellow chromate of potash and stir well.
3....
Another Method to Stain Wood with Logwood Extract:

1. Prepare solution: Pour 2 qts. of boiling water over 1 oz. commercial extract of logwood.
2. Add chromate: When dissolved, add 1 dr. of yellow chromate of potash and stir well.
3. Apply cold: This stain is cheap, keeps well, and can be applied cold with a brush without any preparation.
logwood,extract,stain,chromate,potash,cold,application
Dye Gloves Resembling Limerick
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Dye Gloves Resembling Limerick:

1. Prepare the dye solution: Steep saffron in boiling soft water for about 12 hours.
2. Prepare gloves: Slightly sew up the tops of the gloves to prevent the dye from staining the insides.
3. **Apply...
To Dye Gloves Resembling Limerick:

1. Prepare the dye solution: Steep saffron in boiling soft water for about 12 hours.
2. Prepare gloves: Slightly sew up the tops of the gloves to prevent the dye from staining the insides.
3. Apply dye: Wet the gloves over with a sponge or soft brush dipped into the saffron solution.
4. Adjust quantities: The quantity of saffron and water will depend on how much dye is desired and the depth of color required. A common teacup's worth of solution is sufficient for one pair of gloves.
saffron,dye,gloves,limerick,brown,tan
Dye White Gloves Purple
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Dye White Gloves Purple:

1. Prepare the dye solution: Boil 4 oz. of logwood and 2 oz. of roche-alum in 8 pts. of soft water until half wasted.
2. Cool the solution: Let the liquor stand to cool after straining.
3. Prepare gloves:...
To Dye White Gloves Purple:

1. Prepare the dye solution: Boil 4 oz. of logwood and 2 oz. of roche-alum in 8 pts. of soft water until half wasted.
2. Cool the solution: Let the liquor stand to cool after straining.
3. Prepare gloves: Ensure the gloves are nicely mended.
4. Apply dye: With a brush, rub the gloves over with the cooled dye solution.
5. Dry and repeat: When dry, repeat the process. Twice is sufficient unless a very dark color is desired.
6. Remove loose dye: When dry, rub off the loose dye with a coarse cloth.
7. Apply egg solution: Beat up the white of an egg and use a sponge to rub it over the leather.
8. Clean hands: The dye will stain the hands; wet them with vinegar before washing to remove the stain.
logwood,roche-alum,dye,gloves,purple
Prepare Wood for Dyeing
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Prepare Wood for Dyeing:

1. Choose wood: Use fresh pear-tree, holly, or beech wood; avoid aged wood.
2. Boil veneers: Boil the veneers for 1 hour, then let them cool.
3. Dry naturally: After dyeing, dry the wood in the air, not...
To Prepare Wood for Dyeing:

1. Choose wood: Use fresh pear-tree, holly, or beech wood; avoid aged wood.
2. Boil veneers: Boil the veneers for 1 hour, then let them cool.
3. Dry naturally: After dyeing, dry the wood in the air, not by fire or kiln, as artificial heat can destroy color.

To Dye Blue, Green, Red, or Other Colors:

1. Use clear holly: Put veneers into a box or trough with clear water and let them remain for 4–5 days, changing the water once or twice as needed.
2. Dry before dyeing: Let the wood dry for about 12 hours before applying dye to ensure a brighter hue.
wood,dye,pear-tree,holly,beech,blue,green,red,clear,water,veneers
Stain Beech-wood a Mahogany Color
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Stain Beech-wood a Mahogany Color:

1. Prepare dragon's blood: Break 2 oz. of dragon's blood into pieces and put them into a quart of rectified spirit of wine.
2. Store in warm place: Let the bottle stand in a warm place and shake it...
To Stain Beech-wood a Mahogany Color:

1. Prepare dragon's blood: Break 2 oz. of dragon's blood into pieces and put them into a quart of rectified spirit of wine.
2. Store in warm place: Let the bottle stand in a warm place and shake it frequently until dissolved.
3. Apply solution: When dissolved, the solution is ready for use to stain beech-wood a mahogany color.
beech-wood,mahogany,color,dragon's blood,sprit,wine,rectified
Stain Bone or Ivory
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Stain Bone or Ivory:

1. Use mordant: First steep the body in a mordant.
2. Apply coloring material: Then immerse it in a hot bath of coloring material.
3. Mordants and colors:
- Bichloride of tin as a mordant will give red...
To Stain Bone or Ivory:

1. Use mordant: First steep the body in a mordant.
2. Apply coloring material: Then immerse it in a hot bath of coloring material.
3. Mordants and colors:
- Bichloride of tin as a mordant will give red with Brazil-wood or cochineal, yellow with fustic, violet with logwood.
- Black is given by nitrate of silver.
- Gilding is achieved by immersion in a fresh solution of copperas, followed by a solution of chloride of gold.
- Bleaching is done using a solution of sulphurous acid.
bone,ivory,stain,mordant,bichloride,tin,Brazil-wood,cochineal,fustic,logwood,nitrate,silver,copperas,chloride,gold,sulphurous,acid
Stain Oak a Mahogany Color
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Stain Oak a Mahogany Color:

1. Boil Brazil-wood and alum: Boil together Brazil-wood and alum.
2. Add potash: Before applying to the wood, add a little potash to the solution.
3. Prepare varnish: A suitable varnish can be made by...
To Stain Oak a Mahogany Color:

1. Boil Brazil-wood and alum: Boil together Brazil-wood and alum.
2. Add potash: Before applying to the wood, add a little potash to the solution.
3. Prepare varnish: A suitable varnish can be made by dissolving amber in oil of turpentine mixed with a small portion of linseed oil.
oak,mahogany,color,Brazil-wood,alum,potash,varnish,amber,turpentine,linseed,oil
Stain Wood Ebony-Black
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Stain Wood Ebony-Black:

1. Soak wood: Steep the wood for 2–3 days in lukewarm water with a little alum dissolved.
2. Prepare logwood solution: Put a handful of logwood, cut small, into a pint of water and boil it down to less than...
To Stain Wood Ebony-Black:

1. Soak wood: Steep the wood for 2–3 days in lukewarm water with a little alum dissolved.
2. Prepare logwood solution: Put a handful of logwood, cut small, into a pint of water and boil it down to less than half a pint.
3. Add indigo: If desired, add a little indigo for a more beautiful color.
4. Apply layers: Spread the hot liquor on the wood with a pencil to give it a violet color. When dry, apply another layer; repeat until three layers are applied.
5. Boil verdigris: Boil verdigris in its own vinegar and spread a layer of it on the wood when dry.
6. Rub and polish: Rub the wood with a brush and then with oiled chamois skin for a fine black finish that imitates ebony.
wood,ebony-black,logwood,alum,indigo,verdigris,vinegar,chamois,skin,polish

Cosmetics And Dyes (9) -

Alternative Method for Carminated Lake Using Alkaline Liquor
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Another method to prepare carminated lake:

• If decomposition is used in the process, mix the bath with an alkaline liquor, which will decompose the alum and remove its mordant properties.
• Use 7 or 8 oz. of alum for each pound of...
Another method to prepare carminated lake:

• If decomposition is used in the process, mix the bath with an alkaline liquor, which will decompose the alum and remove its mordant properties.
• Use 7 or 8 oz. of alum for each pound of madder during this process.
• The resulting lake will appear as a reddish brown without any additional colorants.
• To achieve a brighter red, mix the washed precipitate with alum-water before drying.
carminated lake,alkaline liquor,decomposition,reddish brown
Enhance Carminated Lake with Acetate of Lead or Arseniate of Potash
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Improvement on the above method:

• If the aluminated madder bath is sharpened with acetate of lead or arseniate of potash, a rosecolored lake can be produced by adding carbonate of soda.
• The resulting color's strength may...
Improvement on the above method:

• If the aluminated madder bath is sharpened with acetate of lead or arseniate of potash, a rosecolored lake can be produced by adding carbonate of soda.
• The resulting color's strength may vary depending on the amount of additives used.
acetate of lead,arseniate of potash,rosecolored lake
Make Fine Red Lake Using Stick-Lac and Water
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make a fine red lake:

• Boil stick-lac in water, then filter the resulting decoction.
• Evaporate the clear liquid over a gentle fire until it reaches dryness.
• The reason for this easy separation is that the beautiful red color adheres...
To make a fine red lake:

• Boil stick-lac in water, then filter the resulting decoction.
• Evaporate the clear liquid over a gentle fire until it reaches dryness.
• The reason for this easy separation is that the beautiful red color adheres only slightly to the outsides of the sticks from which the gum-lac was obtained and readily communicates itself to boiling water.
• Some of the sticking matter also adheres to the gum itself, so it is necessary to boil the whole together.
• The gum does not prejudice the color or dissolve in boiling water, making it as suitable for sealing-wax and other uses after this process as before.
fine red lake,stick-lac,evaporation,sealing-wax
Make Pink Saucers Using Safflower Extract and Talc
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make Pink Saucers:

• Prepare extract of safflower (carthamus) by:
1. Washing the safflower with cold water.
2. Digesting it in a solution of carbonate of soda.
3. Precipitating the extract using citric acid.
• The resulting...
To make Pink Saucers:

• Prepare extract of safflower (carthamus) by:
1. Washing the safflower with cold water.
2. Digesting it in a solution of carbonate of soda.
3. Precipitating the extract using citric acid.
• The resulting extract can be used to dye silk and wool without a mordant.
• Evaporate the extract onto saucers as a dye-stuff.
• Mix the evaporated extract with powdered talc to form a variety of rouge.
pink saucers,safflower extract,carbonate of soda,citric acid,talc,dye-stuff
Make a Beautiful Red Lake Using Cochineal and Tin Solution
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make a beautiful red lake:

• Take any quantity of cochineal, and pour over it twice its weight of alcohol and an equal amount of distilled water.
• Infuse the mixture near a gentle fire for several days, then filter it.
• To the...
To make a beautiful red lake:

• Take any quantity of cochineal, and pour over it twice its weight of alcohol and an equal amount of distilled water.
• Infuse the mixture near a gentle fire for several days, then filter it.
• To the filtered liquid, add a few drops of a solution of tin, which will cause a fine red precipitate to form.
• Continue adding small amounts of the solution of tin every 2 hours until all the coloring matter is precipitated.
• Finally, wash the precipitate thoroughly in a large quantity of distilled water and dry it.
beautiful red lake,cochineal,tin solution,distilled water
Prepare Carminated Lake from Madder
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare carminated lake from madder:

• Boil 1 part of madder in 12 to 15 pints of water, and continue boiling until the mixture is reduced to about 2 lbs.
• Strain the decoction through a piece of strong linen cloth, ensuring...
To prepare carminated lake from madder:

• Boil 1 part of madder in 12 to 15 pints of water, and continue boiling until the mixture is reduced to about 2 lbs.
• Strain the decoction through a piece of strong linen cloth, ensuring it is well squeezed.
• Add 4 oz. of alum to the decoction.
• The resulting tint will be a bright red, which can be preserved if mixed with appropriate clay.
• Expose the thick liquor produced on a linen filter and subject it to one washing to remove the alum.
• Once removed from the driers, the lake will retain its bright primitive color given by the alum.
carminated lake,madder,alum,bright red,dye preparation
Prepare Dark-Red Using Dragon's Blood and Varnish
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To make dark-red:

• Infuse dragon's blood in varnish while warm to produce reds of varying darkness, depending on the quantity of coloring resin used.
• The artist can adjust the tone by controlling how much dragon's blood is combined...
To make dark-red:

• Infuse dragon's blood in varnish while warm to produce reds of varying darkness, depending on the quantity of coloring resin used.
• The artist can adjust the tone by controlling how much dragon's blood is combined with the varnish.
• Cochineal, when divided, provides minimal color to essence compared to its effect in water.
• However, carmine may be added to a varnish colored by dragon's blood to produce a purple red, from which various shades can be easily formed.
dark-red,dragon's blood,varnish,carmine,purple red
Prepare Rouge Using Carmine and Talc
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare Rouge:

• Combine carmine with talc in varying proportions to create rouge suitable for use in the toilette.
• Talc, also known as Briancon chalk, is primarily composed of clay combined naturally with silex.
• Carmine...
To prepare Rouge:

• Combine carmine with talc in varying proportions to create rouge suitable for use in the toilette.
• Talc, also known as Briancon chalk, is primarily composed of clay combined naturally with silex.
• Carmine and carminated lakes (which derive their color from cochineal) are highly valued because they maintain their color without degradation.
• In some cases, caustic ammonia may be added to enhance the color, even though it alters many other coloring substances.
rouge,carmine,talc,Briancon chalk,cochineal,caustic ammonia
Prepare Violet Using Carminated Varnish and Prussiate of Iron
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To prepare violet:

• Mix carminated varnish with dragon's blood, then add it to a solution colored by prussiate of iron.
• This combination produces violet.
violet,carminated varnish,dragon's blood,prussiate of iron

Animal Health (8) -

Differentiate Between Types of Colic in Horses and Administer Appropriate Treatment
High Priority Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Identify the type of colic: Distinguish between flatulent (windy), spasmodic, or inflammatory colic.
2. For flatulent or spasmodic colic: Use anodynes like laudanum or opium-based remedies as they are effective in these...
Procedure:
1. Identify the type of colic: Distinguish between flatulent (windy), spasmodic, or inflammatory colic.
2. For flatulent or spasmodic colic: Use anodynes like laudanum or opium-based remedies as they are effective in these cases.
3. For inflammatory colic: Avoid using opium or similar medicines as they may worsen the condition.
4. If costiveness is present: Administer a laxative drench after the paroxysm to prevent recurrence.
5. If the horse appears dull and heavy before an attack: Consider bleeding as a treatment option.
6. Always use clysters (enemas) to empty the bowels in cases of colic.
Colic Differentiation,horse remedies,inflammatory colic,flatulent colic
Administer Laudanum Draught for Wet or Lax Gripes
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Beer: 1 quart
• Powdered ginger: 1 tablespoonful
• Laudanum: 1 small wineglassful

Procedure:
1. Warm the beer to just above blood heat.
2. Add the powdered ginger and laudanum to the warm beer immediately before...
Ingredients:
• Beer: 1 quart
• Powdered ginger: 1 tablespoonful
• Laudanum: 1 small wineglassful

Procedure:
1. Warm the beer to just above blood heat.
2. Add the powdered ginger and laudanum to the warm beer immediately before administration.
3. Administer this mixture to the horse in most cases of wet or lax gripes.
4. If the complaint is extremely violent, give about half the quantity every 15-20 minutes until symptoms subside.

Post-Administration Considerations:
