• If the belly is costive (constipated): Give a purgative after pain subsides.
• If there is looseness (diarrhea): Do not give any purgative, as laudanum has a binding effect and will correct it.

Important Notes:
• Laudanum should be used with caution in cases of inflammation; opium-based medicines may worsen inflammatory conditions such as bowel inflammation.
• However, in cases of spasm or irritation (e.g., flatulent or spasmodic colic), laudanum is highly effective.
Laudanum Draught,wet gripes,horse remedies,constipation,diarrhea
Prepare Emergency Colic Remedy for Travel or When Medicines Are Unavailable
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Option 1:
• Pint of strong peppermint water
• 4 ounces of gin
• Any kind of spice

Option 2:
• Pint of port wine
• Spice or ginger

Option 3:
• Half a pint of gin diluted with 4 ounces of water and a little ginger

Procedure:
1. Mix...
Option 1:
• Pint of strong peppermint water
• 4 ounces of gin
• Any kind of spice

Option 2:
• Pint of port wine
• Spice or ginger

Option 3:
• Half a pint of gin diluted with 4 ounces of water and a little ginger

Procedure:
1. Mix the ingredients for any of the above options.
2. Administer as needed during emergencies when proper medicines are not available.

Alternative Preparation (More Complex):
Ingredients:
• Epsom salt: 6 ounces
• Castile soap, sliced: 2 ounces
• Warm gruel: 1 1/2 pints
• Tincture of opium: 1/2 ounce
• Oil of juniper: 2 drachms

Procedure:
1. Dissolve Epsom salt and Castile soap in warm gruel.
2. Add tincture of opium and oil of juniper to the mixture.
3. Stir thoroughly and administer while still warm (new-milk temperature).
4. Repeat this drink every 4-5 hours until symptoms begin to abate.
Emergency Colic Remedy,horse remedies,Epsom salt,Castile soap,tincture of opium
Prepare Travel-Ready Anodyne Drink for Colic and Flatulent Pains
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Tincture of opium: 2 drachms
• Oil of juniper: 2 drachms
• Sweet spirit of nitre: 1/2 ounce
• Tincture of benzoin: 1/2 ounce
• Aromatic spirit of ammonia: 1/2 ounce

Procedure:
1. Mix all the above ingredients in a bottle for...
Ingredients:
• Tincture of opium: 2 drachms
• Oil of juniper: 2 drachms
• Sweet spirit of nitre: 1/2 ounce
• Tincture of benzoin: 1/2 ounce
• Aromatic spirit of ammonia: 1/2 ounce

Procedure:
1. Mix all the above ingredients in a bottle for one dose.
2. Administer this mixture in a pint of warm gruel.
3. Repeat every two hours until symptoms abate.

Alternative Use Case: This drink is particularly useful for colic, flatulency, and colicky pains of the intestines during travel or when immediate medical attention is not available.
Travel-Ready Anodyne Drink,colic,horse remedies,tincture of opium,oil of juniper
Administer Warm Beer and Ginger or Cordial Ball for Colic Relief
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Prepare a mixture of warm beer and ginger.
2. Alternatively, mix a cordial ball with warm beer.
3. Administer this to the horse as needed for colic relief.

Important Notes: This is a simple remedy that can be used in...
Procedure:
1. Prepare a mixture of warm beer and ginger.
2. Alternatively, mix a cordial ball with warm beer.
3. Administer this to the horse as needed for colic relief.

Important Notes: This is a simple remedy that can be used in emergencies when more complex preparations are not available.
Warm Beer and Ginger,colic,horse remedies,cordial ball
Prepare Another Anodyne Medicine for Colic in Horses
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Opium: 1 drachm (60 grains)
• Castile soap: 2 drachms
• Powdered aniseed: 1/2 ounce (4 drachms)

Procedure:
1. Combine all the ingredients listed above.
2. Make into a ball using syrup for one dose.

Use Case: This...
Ingredients:
• Opium: 1 drachm (60 grains)
• Castile soap: 2 drachms
• Powdered aniseed: 1/2 ounce (4 drachms)

Procedure:
1. Combine all the ingredients listed above.
2. Make into a ball using syrup for one dose.

Use Case: This preparation is suitable for horses suffering from colic or when anodynes are required.
Anodyne Medicine,colic,horse remedies,opium,Castile soap
Prepare White's Ball for Gripes
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Castile soap: 3 drachms
• Camphor: 2 drachms
• Ginger: 1 drachm and a half
• Venice turpentine: 6 drachms

Procedure:
1. Combine all the ingredients listed above.
2. Make into a ball for one dose.
3. Wrap the ball closely in a...
Ingredients:
• Castile soap: 3 drachms
• Camphor: 2 drachms
• Ginger: 1 drachm and a half
• Venice turpentine: 6 drachms

Procedure:
1. Combine all the ingredients listed above.
2. Make into a ball for one dose.
3. Wrap the ball closely in a piece of bladder to preserve its potency over time.

Use Case: This preparation is particularly useful for travelers who may need it during journeys where liquid medicine is not readily available.
White's Ball,gripes,horse remedies,camphor,Venice turpentine,Castile soap
Prepare Diuretic Balls for Horses (Method 2)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 3 pounds of nitre
• 3 pounds of resin
• 1 1/2 pounds of soap
• 1 pound of juniper berries
• 1 1/2 ounces of oil of juniper

Procedure:
1. Mix Ingredients: Combine all the listed ingredients thoroughly.
2. **Form into...
Ingredients:
• 3 pounds of nitre
• 3 pounds of resin
• 1 1/2 pounds of soap
• 1 pound of juniper berries
• 1 1/2 ounces of oil of juniper

Procedure:
1. Mix Ingredients: Combine all the listed ingredients thoroughly.
2. Form into Balls: Shape the mixture into balls of common size.
3. Use Spirits of Turpentine: Use spirits of turpentine to assist in forming the balls or as a binding agent.

Notes: This is an alternative method for preparing diuretic balls for horses.
diuretic balls,horse remedies,nitre,resin,soap,juniper berries,oil of juniper,spirits of turpentine

Cooking Recipes (8) -

Fry Sweetbreads
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Fry Sweetbreads

1. Boil the sweetbreads in salt and water for 15 minutes.
2. Remove them from the boiling water and let them cool.
3. Skin the sweetbreads and cut them in half.
4. Season with pepper and salt.
5. Dust a little flour over...
To Fry Sweetbreads

1. Boil the sweetbreads in salt and water for 15 minutes.
2. Remove them from the boiling water and let them cool.
3. Skin the sweetbreads and cut them in half.
4. Season with pepper and salt.
5. Dust a little flour over the sweetbreads.
6. Fry them slowly in equal portions of butter and lard until they are a fine brown.
7. Puree the sweetbreads on a dish.
8. In the same pan, dust a little flour into the fat they were fried in.
9. Stir well and pour in about a gill of hot water (approximately 1/4 pint or 59 ml).
10. Season the gravy to your taste with salt and pepper.
11. As soon as it boils, pour the gravy over the sweetbreads and serve them hot.
sweetbreads, frying, cooking recipe, seasoning
Prepare Beef Sanders
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Beef Sanders

1. Mince cold beef small with onions.
2. Add:
- Pepper
- Salt
- A little gravy
3. Put the mixture into a pie-dish or scallop-shells until about three parts full.
4. Fill up the remaining space with mashed potatoes.
5....
Beef Sanders

1. Mince cold beef small with onions.
2. Add:
- Pepper
- Salt
- A little gravy
3. Put the mixture into a pie-dish or scallop-shells until about three parts full.
4. Fill up the remaining space with mashed potatoes.
5. Bake in an oven or before the fire until done and lightly browned.
6. Mutton may be cooked in the same way.
beef sander, cooking recipe, pie dish, mashed potatoes
Prepare Beef's Tongue aux Champignons
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Beef's Tongue aux Champignons

1. Wash the tongue thoroughly.
2. Boil the tongue for 30 minutes.
3. Prepare a larding mixture with:
- Salt
- Pepper
- All kinds of spice
- Shallots
- Chopped parsley
4. Lard the tongue across...
Beef's Tongue aux Champignons

1. Wash the tongue thoroughly.
2. Boil the tongue for 30 minutes.
3. Prepare a larding mixture with:
- Salt
- Pepper
- All kinds of spice
- Shallots
- Chopped parsley
4. Lard the tongue across with this mixture.
5. Place the tongue in a stewpan with:
- A few slices of bacon and beef
- Carrots
- Onions
- Thyme
- 3 bay leaves
- 3 cloves
6. Cover with bouillon and stew gently for 4 hours.
7. Once done, skin the tongue.
8. Cut it up lengthways in the middle and under part, but not through, so that you can bend it up and lay it on your dish in the shape of a heart.
9. Prepare button mushrooms by:
- Frying them in butter
- Sprinkling with lemon juice moistened with bouillon
- Bubbling to the proper consistency
10. Pour the mushroom mixture over the tongue and serve hot.
beef tongue, champignons, cooking recipe, stewing, seasoning
Prepare Fish en Matelotte
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Fish en Matelotte

1. Scale, clean, and cut the fish into pieces.
2. Place them in a pan with:
- A handful of small onions previously fried whole in butter
- Two bay leaves
- A bunch of shallots and parsley
- Small mushrooms
-...
Fish en Matelotte

1. Scale, clean, and cut the fish into pieces.
2. Place them in a pan with:
- A handful of small onions previously fried whole in butter
- Two bay leaves
- A bunch of shallots and parsley
- Small mushrooms
- Thyme
- Salt and pepper
3. Pour over the fish enough red wine to cover it completely.
4. Set the pan on a quick fire.
5. When the wine is half gone, mix:
- A spoonful of flour with a lump of butter
- Roll into little balls
6. Add these flour-butter balls one by one into your sauce, stirring constantly.
7. Arrange the fish handsomely on a deep dish.
8. Pour the sauce over the fish.
9. Garnish with slices of lemon.
fish, matelotte, cooking recipe, red wine, seasoning
Prepare Kidneys a la Brochette
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Kidneys a la Brochette

1. Let the kidneys steep in cold water for 5 minutes to soften the skin.
2. Remove the skin and split each kidney.
3. Insert a wooden or silver skewer through the middle of each kidney.
4. Season the kidneys with...
Kidneys a la Brochette

1. Let the kidneys steep in cold water for 5 minutes to soften the skin.
2. Remove the skin and split each kidney.
3. Insert a wooden or silver skewer through the middle of each kidney.
4. Season the kidneys with pepper and salt.
5. Dip each kidney into oil or melted butter.
6. Broil them on a gridiron until cooked to your liking.
7. Before serving, remove the skewers unless they are made of silver.
8. Serve the kidneys on a dish with butter and fine herbs.
kidneys, brochette, cooking recipe, skewering
Prepare Steak a la Soyer
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steak a la Soyer

1. Broil the rump-steak.
2. Dress the steak with:
- Pepper
- Salt
- Cayenne pepper
- Flour (all in a dredge-box together)
3. Keep turning the steak and dredging it constantly until it is sufficiently done.
4. Chop...
Steak a la Soyer

1. Broil the rump-steak.
2. Dress the steak with:
- Pepper
- Salt
- Cayenne pepper
- Flour (all in a dredge-box together)
3. Keep turning the steak and dredging it constantly until it is sufficiently done.
4. Chop up 1 small shallot.
5. Put the chopped shallot into a stewpan with a little catsup.
6. When the steak is sufficiently done, add a little butter to the sauce in the stewpan.
7. Strain the sauce through a small sieve.
8. Serve the steak and sauce very hot.
steak, soyer, broiling, catsup, seasoning
Prepare Timballe of Macaroni with Chicken and Ham
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Timballe of Macaroni, with Chicken and Ham

1. Simmer 1/2 pound macaroni in plenty of water and a tablespoonful of salt until it is tender but not too soft.
2. Strain the water from the macaroni.
3. Beat up 5 egg yolks and the whites of...
Timballe of Macaroni, with Chicken and Ham

1. Simmer 1/2 pound macaroni in plenty of water and a tablespoonful of salt until it is tender but not too soft.
2. Strain the water from the macaroni.
3. Beat up 5 egg yolks and the whites of 2 eggs.
4. Take a pint of the best cream, the breast of a fowl, and some slices of ham.
5. Mince the breast of the fowl and some slices of ham.
6. Add to the mixture:
- From 2 to 3 tablespoonfuls of finely grated Parmesan cheese
- Season with pepper and salt
7. Mix all these ingredients together with the macaroni.
8. Put the mixture into a well-buttered pudding-mould.
9. Let it strain in a stewpan of boiling water for about 1 hour.
10. Serve the timballe quite hot with rich gravy.
11. It is also very good cold.
timballe, macaroni, chicken, ham, cooking recipe
Prepare Veal Cutlets
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Veal Cutlets

1. Pound the veal cutlets well with a rolling-pin or potato masher.
2. Wash and dry them on a clean towel.
3. Season with pepper and salt.
4. Prepare a mixture of:
- A pint of fine powdered cracker, seasoned with salt and...
Veal Cutlets

1. Pound the veal cutlets well with a rolling-pin or potato masher.
2. Wash and dry them on a clean towel.
3. Season with pepper and salt.
4. Prepare a mixture of:
- A pint of fine powdered cracker, seasoned with salt and pepper
5. Whisk 2 eggs with 1 gill of milk (approximately 59 ml).
6. Pour the egg-milk mixture over the cutlets.
7. Take one cutlet at a time and place it in the cracker mixture, patting well with the back of a spoon to ensure the cracker adheres closely to the meat.
8. Fry the cutlets in hot lard, turning occasionally until they are well done and handsomely browned on both sides.
veal cutlets, cooking recipe, cracker coating, frying

Distillation (8) -

Expeditious Method for Distilling Simple Water Using a Warming-Pan and Earthen Pot
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Tie a piece of muslin or gauze over the mouth of a glazed earthen pot, ensuring it is just large enough to receive the bottom of a warming-pan.

2. Place the herb (clipped) on top of the muslin or gauze.

3. Position the warming-pan...
1. Tie a piece of muslin or gauze over the mouth of a glazed earthen pot, ensuring it is just large enough to receive the bottom of a warming-pan.

2. Place the herb (clipped) on top of the muslin or gauze.

3. Position the warming-pan with live coals over the herbs, using heat that is just warm enough to prevent burning.

4. The steam produced by the heat will be unable to rise and must descend into the water at the bottom of the receiver, which becomes strongly impregnated with essential oils and vegetable salts.

5. If spirituous or compound water is desired, simply add good spirits or French brandy to the distilled liquid for preservation.

6. Use charcoal as fuel for better regulation of heat; manage the fire by lifting or lowering the lid as needed to increase or decrease the temperature.

7. The depth of the earthen pot and the season will influence the initial amount of fire used, which should be gradually increased over time.

8. This method produces a small quantity of distilled water but with greater strength compared to other methods.
expeditious distillation, warming-pan, earthen pot
Preparing Plants for Distillation of Simple Water
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Use fresh plants and their parts whenever possible.

2. If dry plants are used, ensure they are easily procurable at all times of the year. Note that fresher, more fragrant waters can be obtained from green plants.

3. Bruise the...
1. Use fresh plants and their parts whenever possible.

2. If dry plants are used, ensure they are easily procurable at all times of the year. Note that fresher, more fragrant waters can be obtained from green plants.

3. Bruise the plants slightly before use to facilitate extraction.

4. Pour thrice the quantity of spring water over the bruised plant material. Adjust this amount based on the juiciness of the plants:
- Fresh and juicy herbs: Use thrice their weight in water.
- Dry herbs: Use a larger quantity of water to ensure sufficient liquid remains after distillation.

5. Ensure there is enough water left after distillation to prevent burning or scorching the still.
distillation, simple water, preparation, plants, herbs
Using a Cold Still for Distilling Simple Waters
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. The cold still is preferred when extracting volatile compounds from fresh plants that are valued for their fine flavor.

2. This method preserves the volatile components of the plant, which may be lost during drying or exposure to high...
1. The cold still is preferred when extracting volatile compounds from fresh plants that are valued for their fine flavor.

2. This method preserves the volatile components of the plant, which may be lost during drying or exposure to high heat.

3. In a cold still, the plant is placed in an instrument where it is dried slowly using moderate warmth.

4. All vapors and essential oils are collected and preserved without excessive heat.

5. This method results in more fragrant and fully impregnated distilled water compared to the hot still.
cold still, distillation, preservation
Fermentation of Vegetables for Distillation
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Some vegetables may be slightly fermented with the addition of yeast prior to distillation.

2. This step is optional and was historically used to enhance the properties of the final product.
fermentation, vegetables, distillation, yeast
Preserving Distilled Liquors with Spirits
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. To ensure long-term preservation of distilled liquors, cork them tightly to prevent exposure to air.

2. Adding about one-twentieth part of any spirits (such as alcohol) will further enhance the longevity and potency of the distilled...
1. To ensure long-term preservation of distilled liquors, cork them tightly to prevent exposure to air.

2. Adding about one-twentieth part of any spirits (such as alcohol) will further enhance the longevity and potency of the distilled product.
preservation, spirits, distilled liquors
Preserving Distilled Waters with Alcohol
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. After distillation, add about one-twentieth part of proof spirit (alcohol) to the distilled water to enhance preservation.

2. This addition helps maintain the potency and longevity of the distilled water.
preservation, alcohol, distilled water
Removing Oil from Distilled Water
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. If any drops of oil are observed floating on the surface of the water during or after distillation, they must be carefully removed.

2. This step is crucial to ensure the purity and quality of the distilled water.
oil removal, distillation, water purification
Understanding the Components of Distilled Water from Plants
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. The more volatile parts of plants include aqueous, oily, gummy, resinous, and saline components.

2. These are typically found in distilled water obtained through distillation processes that use sufficient heat to burst plant...
1. The more volatile parts of plants include aqueous, oily, gummy, resinous, and saline components.

2. These are typically found in distilled water obtained through distillation processes that use sufficient heat to burst plant cells.

3. Some plants contain a large quantity of oil, which may be visible as floating droplets on the surface of the distilled water.

4. This process ensures that volatile compounds are effectively extracted and preserved in the final product.
volatile components, plant distillation, oil extraction

Oil Preparation (8) -

Prepare Castor Oil
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Castor beans.

Procedure:
1. Press the castor beans cold or slightly warmed to extract the oil.
2. To render the oil colorless and odorless, filter it through animal charcoal and magnesia.
3. It is soluble in strong alcohol...
Ingredients:
• Castor beans.

Procedure:
1. Press the castor beans cold or slightly warmed to extract the oil.
2. To render the oil colorless and odorless, filter it through animal charcoal and magnesia.
3. It is soluble in strong alcohol and used as a basis for many hair oils (see PERFUMERY).
Castor Oil, preparation, oil remedy, castor beans, animal charcoal
Prepare Nut Oil
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Hazelnuts: kernels.

Procedure:
1. Extract the oil from the kernel of the hazelnut.
2. This oil is very fine and has been proposed as a substitute for almond oil due to its better preservation qualities.
3. It is used in China...
Ingredients:
• Hazelnuts: kernels.

Procedure:
1. Extract the oil from the kernel of the hazelnut.
2. This oil is very fine and has been proposed as a substitute for almond oil due to its better preservation qualities.
3. It is used in China with tea, likely in lieu of cream, and by painters as a superior vehicle for their colors.
Nut Oil, preparation, oil remedy, hazelnut kernel, painter's medium
Prepare Oil of Croton
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Molucca grains (purging nuts).

Procedure:
1. Extract oil from Molucca grains or purging nuts.
2. This oil is chemically similar to castor oil but more active as a cathartic; even a single drop of genuine oil can be a powerful...
Ingredients:
• Molucca grains (purging nuts).

Procedure:
1. Extract oil from Molucca grains or purging nuts.
2. This oil is chemically similar to castor oil but more active as a cathartic; even a single drop of genuine oil can be a powerful laxative.
Oil of Croton, preparation, oil remedy, Molucca grains, cathartic oil
Prepare Oil of Mace
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Nutmegs: 2 lbs.

Procedure:
1. Extract oil from nutmegs using a press.
2. The resulting oil is buttery in texture, with the smell and color of mace.
3. It becomes paler and harder over time.
4. In Europe, 2 lbs. of nutmegs...
Ingredients:
• Nutmegs: 2 lbs.

Procedure:
1. Extract oil from nutmegs using a press.
2. The resulting oil is buttery in texture, with the smell and color of mace.
3. It becomes paler and harder over time.
4. In Europe, 2 lbs. of nutmegs yield approximately 6 oz. of this oil.
Oil of Mace, preparation, oil remedy, nutmegs, press extraction
Prepare Oil of Sweet Almonds
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Bitter almonds: 1 cwt.

Procedure:
1. Use bitter almonds for cheapness or old Jordan almonds; fresh Barbary almonds may be used for a longer-lasting oil.
2. Optionally, blanch the almonds by dipping in boiling water or soaking...
Ingredients:
• Bitter almonds: 1 cwt.

Procedure:
1. Use bitter almonds for cheapness or old Jordan almonds; fresh Barbary almonds may be used for a longer-lasting oil.
2. Optionally, blanch the almonds by dipping in boiling water or soaking in cold water to remove their skins easily.
3. Grind the almonds into a paste and place them in canvas bags.
4. Press the almond paste between iron plates using a screw press or wedge to extract the oil.
5. The yield from 1 cwt. of bitter almonds is approximately 46 lbs. of oil, with the cake produced being sufficient to pay for pressing.
Oil of Sweet Almonds, preparation, oil remedy, bitter almonds, screw press
Prepare Olive, Salad, or Sweet Oil
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Olive: kernels.

Procedure:
1. Extract oil from olive kernels to produce olive, salad, or sweet oil.
2. This oil is demulcent, emollient, gently laxative, and used as an emetic with warm water; dose: 1 oz. troy (a large...
Ingredients:
• Olive: kernels.

Procedure:
1. Extract oil from olive kernels to produce olive, salad, or sweet oil.
2. This oil is demulcent, emollient, gently laxative, and used as an emetic with warm water; dose: 1 oz. troy (a large spoonful).
3. It can be applied externally when warm for serpent bites and when cold for tumors and dropsies.
4. Rank oil is best for plasters, while fresh oil makes the best hard soap.
Olive Oil, preparation, oil remedy, olive kernel, demulcent oil
Prepare True Oil of Mace by Expression
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Mace: 1 1/2 lbs.

Procedure:
1. Extract oil from mace using expression (cold pressing).
2. The resulting oil is red, remains liquid or soft, has a strong smell of mace, and a subacid taste.
3. It is imported in jars or...
Ingredients:
• Mace: 1 1/2 lbs.

Procedure:
1. Extract oil from mace using expression (cold pressing).
2. The resulting oil is red, remains liquid or soft, has a strong smell of mace, and a subacid taste.
3. It is imported in jars or bottles, with the lower part being thicker than the top.
4. In Europe, 1 1/2 lbs. of mace yield approximately 1 1/2 oz. troy of oil.
True Oil of Mace, preparation, oil remedy, mace expression, red oil
Manufacture of Drying Linseed Oil without Heat
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Manufacturing Drying Linseed Oil Without Heat:

1. In a bottle containing 4 1/2 pts. of rain-water, add 18 oz. of neutral acetate of lead.
2. Allow the solution to complete, then add **18 oz. of litharge in very fine...
Procedure for Manufacturing Drying Linseed Oil Without Heat:

1. In a bottle containing 4 1/2 pts. of rain-water, add 18 oz. of neutral acetate of lead.
2. Allow the solution to complete, then add 18 oz. of litharge in very fine powder.
3. Let the mixture stand in a moderately warm place and frequently agitate it to assist the dissolution of the litharge.
4. The process is considered complete when no more small scales are apparent.
5. Separate the deposit of shining white color (sexbasic acetate of lead) by filtration.
6. This solution can be used for preparing 22 lbs. of drying oil.
7. Dilute the vinegar of lead solution with an equal volume of rain-water.
8. Gradually add 22 lbs. of linseed oil, which has had 18 oz. of litharge previously mixed in, while constantly agitating.
9. Renew the points of contact between the lead solution and the oil by agitation 3 or 4 times a day.
10. Allow the mixture to settle in a warm place until the limpid straw-colored oil rises to the surface, leaving a copious white deposit.
11. Filter the watery solution to render it clear; this can be reused for subsequent operations after adding 18 oz. of litharge again.
12. By filtration through paper or cotton, obtain the oil as limpid as water.
13. Expose the oil to sunlight to bleach it if desired.

To produce drying oil free from lead:
• Add dilute sulphuric acid to the solution.
• Allow it to stand until a deposit of sulphate of lead forms.
• The clear oil obtained is free from all trace of lead.
drying linseed oil, vinegar of lead, litharge, rain-water, acetate of lead, filtration

Oil Purification (8) -

Purifying Oil Using Lead and Sunlight
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:

1. Take a white glass bottle filled with pure olive or almond oil.
2. Insert a coiled strip of lead into the bottle.
3. Expose the bottle to the sun's rays until a white curdy matter ceases to be deposited.
lead purification,sunlight,oil purification
Bleach Palm Oil Using Chloride of Lime
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Take 7 to 14 lbs. of chloride of lime.
2. Triturate the chloride of lime in a mortar, adding gradually 12 times the quantity of water, forming a smooth cream.
3. Liquefy 112 lbs. of palm oil and remove it from the...
Procedure:

1. Take 7 to 14 lbs. of chloride of lime.
2. Triturate the chloride of lime in a mortar, adding gradually 12 times the quantity of water, forming a smooth cream.
3. Liquefy 112 lbs. of palm oil and remove it from the fire.
4. Add the solution of chloride of lime to the oil and stir well with a wooden stirrer.
5. Allow the mixture to cool until it becomes solid, then break it into small fragments.
6. Expose the fragments to air for 2 or 3 weeks.
7. Put the oil into a cast-iron boiler lined with lead, diluted with 20 parts of water.
8. Boil with moderate heat until the oil drops clear from the stirrer.
9. Let it cool.

Details:
• This method is also used for removing foetor from fish oils (using only 1 lb. of chloride of lime to 112 lbs. of oil).
• It does not remove the natural smell of the oil.
chloride of lime,palm oil purification,bleaching oil
Bleach Palm Oil Using Chromic Acid (Mr. Watt's Method)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Heat the palm oil in a steam vessel until it cools down to 130° Fahr..
2. Transfer the oil into wooden vessels, ensuring no water or sediment is present.
3. Prepare a saturated solution of 25 lbs. of bichromate of potash,...
Procedure:

1. Heat the palm oil in a steam vessel until it cools down to 130° Fahr..
2. Transfer the oil into wooden vessels, ensuring no water or sediment is present.
3. Prepare a saturated solution of 25 lbs. of bichromate of potash, add 8 lbs. of sulphuric acid, and 60 lbs. of muriatic acid (or equivalent quantity of salt and sulphuric acid).
4. Add the mixture to the oil and constantly stir until it turns a light-green color.
5. If not sufficiently decolored, add more of the mixture.
6. Let the oil settle for half an hour.
7. Pump the oil into a wooden vat and boil it with fresh water using a steam pipe.
8. Allow the oil to settle again.

Details:
• For linseed, rape, and mustard oils: use a dilute solution of chromic acid with a little muriatic acid.
• For olive, almond, and castor oils: no muriatic acid is required.
• Fish oils and fats are first boiled in a steam apparatus with a weak soda lye (1/2 lb. of soda for every ton of fat) for 30 minutes.
9. Add 1/2 lb. of sulphuric acid diluted with 3 lbs. of water, boil for 16 minutes, and allow to settle for one hour or more.
10. Draw off the water and sediment, then further bleach the oil using a solution of 4 lbs. of bichromate of potash and 2 lbs. of sulphuric acid, properly diluted.
chromic acid,bichromate of potash,palm oil purification,oil bleaching
Bleach and Purify Fixed Oils Using Steam and Air
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Heat the oil using steam to 170° or 200°.
2. Force a current of air through the oil under a chimney until it is bleached and purified.

Details:
• This method improves the smell and color of fish and other fat oils.
• The...
Procedure:

1. Heat the oil using steam to 170° or 200°.
2. Force a current of air through the oil under a chimney until it is bleached and purified.

Details:
• This method improves the smell and color of fish and other fat oils.
• The process involves continuous agitation during heating.
bleach oil,purify oil,steam treatment,oil purification
Purify Castor Oil Using Animal Charcoal and Calcined Magnesia
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Mix 1000 parts of castor oil with 25 parts of animal charcoal and 10 parts of calcined magnesia.
2. Leave the mixture together for 3 days at a temperature of 68° to 78° Fahr., often stirring or shaking the...
Procedure:

1. Mix 1000 parts of castor oil with 25 parts of animal charcoal and 10 parts of calcined magnesia.
2. Leave the mixture together for 3 days at a temperature of 68° to 78° Fahr., often stirring or shaking the mixture.
3. Filter the oil after this period.

Results:
• The purified castor oil is limpid, colorless, without taste, and easily soluble in alcohol.
• It congeals at a lower temperature than before, making it superior to ordinary oil.
castor oil purification,animal charcoal,calcined magnesia
Purify Oils Using Common Salt Solution
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Agitate the oils (especially lamp oils) with a strong solution of common salt.

Details:
• This method is used for purifying various types of oil, particularly lamp oil.
common salt,oil purification,lamp oil
Refine Oils Using Caustic Soda Lye (Mr. Bancroft's Method)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Agitate the oil with 3 1/2 to 8 per cent. of caustic soda lye of 12 specific gravity.
2. If, on trial of a small quantity, the lye is found to settle clear at the bottom, enough has been added.
3. Allow the oil to rest for...
Procedure:

1. Agitate the oil with 3 1/2 to 8 per cent. of caustic soda lye of 12 specific gravity.
2. If, on trial of a small quantity, the lye is found to settle clear at the bottom, enough has been added.
3. Allow the oil to rest for 24 hours to let the soapy matter subside.
4. Filter the supernatant oil.

Details:
• This method is suitable for refining common olive oil, lard oil, and similar oils for lubricating purposes.
caustic soda lye,oil refining,lubricating oil
Refine Oils Using Sulphuric Acid and Water (Mr. Davidson's Method)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Treat whale oil first with a solution of tan.
2. Next treat it with water and chloride of lime.
3. Finally, treat it with diluted sulphuric acid and warm water.
4. Rape and other seed oils are refined using sulphuric acid and...
Procedure:

1. Treat whale oil first with a solution of tan.
2. Next treat it with water and chloride of lime.
3. Finally, treat it with diluted sulphuric acid and warm water.
4. Rape and other seed oils are refined using sulphuric acid and twice as much water.
5. For 112 lbs. of oil, use 2 lbs. of oil of vitriol (sulphuric acid).
6. Carefully wash the oil from the acid and filter it.

Details:
• This method is suitable for various oils including whale oil, rape oil, and others.
sulphuric acid,oil refining,tan solution,chloride of lime

Cosmetics (8) -

Make Economical Pomade
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Economical Pomade

1. Take 2 lbs of white grease, 1 1/2 dr of essence of bergamot or lemon, and 1 dr of essence of cloves.
2. Color the pomade with alkanet or carmine lake.
economical pomade,white grease,essence of bergamot,essence of cloves,alkanet,carmine lake
Make Philicomes and Cosmetics (Winter)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Philicomes and Cosmetics (Winter)

1. Use equal parts of lard and earthnut oil.
2. Melt the greases over a water-bath, and pass them through a cloth.
3. When the products begin to solidify, add the oils to the mixture.
4. Run the...
Philicomes and Cosmetics (Winter)

1. Use equal parts of lard and earthnut oil.
2. Melt the greases over a water-bath, and pass them through a cloth.
3. When the products begin to solidify, add the oils to the mixture.
4. Run the final product into jars or bottles.

For Fine Philicomes: Substitute pommades a la rose, orange-flower, au bouquet, geranium, a la tubereuse, etc., for the lard.
philicomes and cosmetics,winter,lard,earthnut oil,water-bath,cloth,pommades a la rose
Make Pomade Divine
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Pomade Divine

1. Take 3 lbs of beef marrow, and place it in an earthen vessel.
2. Cover the marrow with cold water and change the water daily for a few days, using rose-water on the last day.
3. Pour off and press out the...
Pomade Divine

1. Take 3 lbs of beef marrow, and place it in an earthen vessel.
2. Cover the marrow with cold water and change the water daily for a few days, using rose-water on the last day.
3. Pour off and press out the water from the marrow.
4. Add to the marrow:
- 4 oz of styrax
- 4 oz of benzoin
- 4 oz of Chio turpentine
- 1 oz of orris powder
- 1/2 oz of powdered cinnamon
- 1/2 oz of powdered cloves
- 1/2 oz of powdered nutmeg
5. Place the vessel in hot water, and keep the water boiling for 3 hours.
6. Strain the mixture thoroughly.
pomade divine,beef marrow,styrax,benzoin,chio turpentine,orris powder,cinnamon,clove,nutmeg
Make Pomade a la Rose
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Pomade a la Rose

1. Take 1 lb of white grease, 3 1/2 oz of nut-oil, and 1 1/3 oz of spermaceti or white wax.
2. Melt the ingredients together and add the oil.
3. Pass the warm mixture through a clean cloth.
4. Stir the mixture...
Pomade a la Rose

1. Take 1 lb of white grease, 3 1/2 oz of nut-oil, and 1 1/3 oz of spermaceti or white wax.
2. Melt the ingredients together and add the oil.
3. Pass the warm mixture through a clean cloth.
4. Stir the mixture in a mortar until cold.
5. If the pomade must be white, do not add any coloring matter.
6. For red color, soak 1/2 dr of powdered alkanet in the oil previously warmed and add it to the mixture.
7. For yellow color, add 1/4 or 1/2 dr of annotto to the melted grease mixture.
8. Pass through a clean cloth again.
9. Perfume with:
- Essence of geranium: 1 1/4 dr
- Essence of rose: 1/4 dr
- Essence of canella: 1-16 dr
10. Introduce the essences into the grease when it is half fluid.
pomade a la rose,white grease,nut-oil,spermaceti,alkanet,annotto,essence of geranium,essence of rose
Make Pomade of Bitter Almonds
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Pomade of Bitter Almonds

1. Take 2 lbs of pure white grease, 1 1/2 dr of natural essence of bitter almonds, and 1 1/2 dr of essence of lemon, cedrat, or Portugal.
2. Mix all the ingredients thoroughly.
pomade of bitter almonds,pure white grease,essence of bitter almonds,essence of lemon
Make Stick Pomatum
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Stick Pomatum

1. Use mutton suets, or alternatively use hard body with the addition of 1 oz of wax for every pound of body in summer.
2. In winter, lard body can also be used but increase the proportion of wax...
Stick Pomatum

1. Use mutton suets, or alternatively use hard body with the addition of 1 oz of wax for every pound of body in summer.
2. In winter, lard body can also be used but increase the proportion of wax accordingly.
3. Always melt the least fusible body first.
4. When moulding, ensure the pomade is not too hot to avoid cavities forming in the centre, which may cause the sticks to break.
5. Perfume with:
- Essence of bergamot: 1 dr per lb of pomade
- Essence of lavender: 1 dr per lb of pomade
- Essence of thyme: 1 dr per lb of pomade
- Essence of orange-peel: 1 dr per lb of pomade
6. Color with annotto or saffron.

Alternative Method for Stick Pomatum (Cosmetic):

1. Melt 2 parts of tallow and 1 part of wax in sheet-iron moulds, the size desired for the stick.
2. The mass should be perfumed and colored before being poured into the moulds.
stick pomatum,mutton suets,lard body,wax,essence of bergamot,annotto,sheet-iron moulds,tallow
Make Violet Powder
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Violet Powder

1. Take 12 lbs of wheat starch, 2 1/2 lbs of powdered orris-root, 1/2 oz of oil of lemon, 1/4 oz of oil of bergamot, and 2 dr of oil of cloves.
2. Mix all the ingredients thoroughly.
violet powder,wheat starch,orris-root,oil of lemon,oil of bergamot
Prepare Bloom of Roses
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Bloom of Roses

1. Dissolve 1/4 oz of cinnamon in 1/2 oz of strong ammonia.
2. After 2 days, add 1 pt of rose-water and 1/2 oz of Esprit de Rose (triple).
3. Mix thoroughly and set aside for a week.
4. Pour off the liquid...
Bloom of Roses

1. Dissolve 1/4 oz of cinnamon in 1/2 oz of strong ammonia.
2. After 2 days, add 1 pt of rose-water and 1/2 oz of Esprit de Rose (triple).
3. Mix thoroughly and set aside for a week.
4. Pour off the liquid from any sediment that may have formed.
bloom of roses,cinnamon,strong ammonia,rose-water,esprit de rose

Vinegar Preparation (8) -

Prepare Aromatic Vinegar
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Concentrated acetic acid: 8 oz
• Oil of English lavender: 2 drs
• Oil of English rosemary: 1 dr
• Oil of cloves: 1 dr
• Oil of camphor: 1 oz

Procedure:
1. Combine all ingredients in a container.
2. Mix thoroughly.
aromatic vinegar, concentrated acetic acid, lavender oil, rosemary oil, camphor oil
Prepare Camphorated Vinegar
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Camphor: 6 drs
• Alcohol: 2 oz
• Strong vinegar: 1 lb

Procedure:
1. Powder the camphor using alcohol in a mortar.
2. Add the strong vinegar to the powdered camphor and mix thoroughly.
camphorated vinegar, camphor powder, alcohol mixing
Prepare Cologne Vinegar
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Cologne water: 1 quart
• No. 8 vinegar: 1 oz

Procedure:
1. Add 1 oz of No. 8 vinegar to each quart of cologne water.
2. Mix thoroughly.
cologne vinegar, no.8 vinegar, cologne water preparation
Prepare Henry's Vinegar
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Dried leaves of rosemary: 1/2 oz
• Dried leaves of rue: 1/2 oz
• Dried wormwood: 1/2 oz
• Dried sage: 1/2 oz
• Dried mint: 1/2 oz
• Lavender flowers: 1/2 oz
• Bruised nutmeg: 1/4 oz
• Cloves: 1/4 oz
• Angelica root: 1/4 oz
-...
Ingredients:
• Dried leaves of rosemary: 1/2 oz
• Dried leaves of rue: 1/2 oz
• Dried wormwood: 1/2 oz
• Dried sage: 1/2 oz
• Dried mint: 1/2 oz
• Lavender flowers: 1/2 oz
• Bruised nutmeg: 1/4 oz
• Cloves: 1/4 oz
• Angelica root: 1/4 oz
• Camphor: 1/4 oz
• Alcohol, rectified: 4 oz
• Concentrated acetic acid: 16 oz

Procedure:
1. Macerate the materials (rosemary, rue, wormwood, sage, mint, lavender flowers, nutmeg, cloves, angelica root, camphor) in the alcohol for a day.
2. After maceration, add concentrated acetic acid to the mixture.
3. Digest the mixture for a week, keeping it at a temperature of about 70 ° Fahr (approximately 21 °C).
4. Once digestion is complete, press out the now aromatized acid and filter it thoroughly.
henry's vinegar, rosemary, wormwood, sage, camphor, alcohol maceration
Prepare Hygienic Vinegar
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Brandy: 1 pint
• Oil of cloves: 1 dr
• Oil of lavender: 1 dr
• Oil of marjoram: 1/2 dr
• Gum benzoin: 1 oz
• Brown vinegar: 2 pts (approximately 1 gallon)

Procedure:
1. **Macerate brandy, oil of cloves, oil of lavender, oil...
Ingredients:
• Brandy: 1 pint
• Oil of cloves: 1 dr
• Oil of lavender: 1 dr
• Oil of marjoram: 1/2 dr
• Gum benzoin: 1 oz
• Brown vinegar: 2 pts (approximately 1 gallon)

Procedure:
1. Macerate brandy, oil of cloves, oil of lavender, oil of marjoram, and gum benzoin together for a few hours.
2. After maceration, add brown vinegar to the mixture.
3. If desired, strain or filter the final product to make it bright.
hygienic vinegar, brandy maceration, gum benzoin, marjoram oil
Prepare Toilet Vinegar (a la Rose)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Dried rose leaves: 4 oz
• Esprit de rose, triple: 1/2 pt
• White wine vinegar: 2 pts

Procedure:
1. Macerate dried rose leaves in esprit de rose and white wine vinegar for a fortnight (approximate time not specified) in a...
Ingredients:
• Dried rose leaves: 4 oz
• Esprit de rose, triple: 1/2 pt
• White wine vinegar: 2 pts

Procedure:
1. Macerate dried rose leaves in esprit de rose and white wine vinegar for a fortnight (approximate time not specified) in a close vessel.
2. After maceration, filter the mixture thoroughly.
3. Bottle the filtered product for sale.
toilet vinegar a la rose, dried rose leaves, esprit de rose maceration
Prepare Vinagre des Quatre Voleurs (Four Thieves' Vinegar)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Fresh tops of common wormwood: 3/4 oz
• Fresh tops of Roman wormwood: 3/4 oz
• Fresh rosemary: 3/4 oz
• Fresh sage: 3/4 oz
• Fresh mint: 3/4 oz
• Fresh rue: 3/4 oz
• Lavender flowers: 1 oz
• Garlic: 1 dr
• Calamus aromaticus: 1...
Ingredients:
• Fresh tops of common wormwood: 3/4 oz
• Fresh tops of Roman wormwood: 3/4 oz
• Fresh rosemary: 3/4 oz
• Fresh sage: 3/4 oz
• Fresh mint: 3/4 oz
• Fresh rue: 3/4 oz
• Lavender flowers: 1 oz
• Garlic: 1 dr
• Calamus aromaticus: 1 dr
• Cinnamon: 1 dr
• Cloves: 1 dr
• Nutmeg: 1 dr
• Camphor: 1/2 oz
• Alcohol or brandy: 1 oz
• Strong vinegar: 4 pts (approximately 2 gallons)

Procedure:
1. Combine all ingredients except camphor and spirit in a closely covered vessel.
2. Digest the mixture for a fortnight, keeping it at a summer heat (approximate temperature not specified).
3. After digestion, express and filter the vinaigre produced.
4. Dissolve camphor in alcohol or brandy separately.
5. Add the dissolved camphor to the filtered vinaigre.
four thieves vinegar, wormwood, rosemary, sage, camphor, alcohol digestion
Prepare Vinaigre aux Fines Herbes
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Tarragon: 12 oz
• Basil: 4 oz
• Laurel leaves: 4 oz
• Shallots: 2 oz
• Strong vinegar: 1/2 gal

Procedure:
1. Combine all ingredients in a container.
2. Let the mixture soak for a week or two.
3. After soaking, strain...
Ingredients:
• Tarragon: 12 oz
• Basil: 4 oz
• Laurel leaves: 4 oz
• Shallots: 2 oz
• Strong vinegar: 1/2 gal

Procedure:
1. Combine all ingredients in a container.
2. Let the mixture soak for a week or two.
3. After soaking, strain the liquid.
4. The resulting vinaigre is too strong for direct use but can be added to table vinegar to improve its flavor.
vinaigre aux fines herbes, tarragon, basil, laurel leaves, shallots, vinegar preparation

Leather Care (8) -

Another Method for Blacking
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Ivory-black: 1/4 lb
• Moist sugar: 1/4 lb
• Flour: 1 tablespoonful
• Tallow (size of a walnut): 1 piece
• Gum Arabic: small piece
• Water: 1 quart
Procedure:
1. Make a paste from the flour.
2. While hot, add the tallow to the...
Ingredients:
• Ivory-black: 1/4 lb
• Moist sugar: 1/4 lb
• Flour: 1 tablespoonful
• Tallow (size of a walnut): 1 piece
• Gum Arabic: small piece
• Water: 1 quart
Procedure:
1. Make a paste from the flour.
2. While hot, add the tallow to the paste.
3. Add the moist sugar to the mixture.
4. Mix all ingredients well together in a quart of water.
another blacking method, leather care, tallow, flour, gum arabic
Cheap Method for Blacking
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Ivory-black: 2 oz
• Brown sugar: 1 1/2 oz
• Sweet oil: 1/2 tablespoonful
• Small beer: 1/2 pt
Procedure:
1. Mix the ivory-black, brown sugar, and sweet oil well together.
2. Gradually add small beer to the mixture.
cheap blacking, leather care, brown sugar, sweet oil, beer
India Rubber Blacking (Patent)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Ivory-black: 60 lbs
• Treacle: 45 lbs
• Vinegar No. 24: 20 galls
• Powdered gum: 1 lb
• India-rubber oil: 9 lbs (made by dissolving 18 oz of India rubber in 9 lbs of rape-oil)
Procedure:
1. Grind all ingredients smooth in a...
Ingredients:
• Ivory-black: 60 lbs
• Treacle: 45 lbs
• Vinegar No. 24: 20 galls
• Powdered gum: 1 lb
• India-rubber oil: 9 lbs (made by dissolving 18 oz of India rubber in 9 lbs of rape-oil)
Procedure:
1. Grind all ingredients smooth in a paintmill.
2. Add 12 lbs of oil of vitriol gradually, stirring strongly for 1/2 an hour a day over the course of a fortnight.
india rubber blacking, leather care, treacle, vinegar, india rubber oil
Make Bailey's Composition for Blacking-cakes
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Gum tragacanth: 1 oz
• Neat's-foot oil: 2 oz
• Superfine ivory-black: 2 oz
• Deep blue prepared from iron and copper: 2 oz
• Brown sugar candy: 4 oz
• River-water: 4 oz
Procedure:
1. Mix all ingredients well together.
2....
Ingredients:
• Gum tragacanth: 1 oz
• Neat's-foot oil: 2 oz
• Superfine ivory-black: 2 oz
• Deep blue prepared from iron and copper: 2 oz
• Brown sugar candy: 4 oz
• River-water: 4 oz
Procedure:
1. Mix all ingredients well together.
2. Evaporate the river-water until it is fully incorporated into the mixture.
3. Form the mixture into cakes.
black, leather care, gum tragacanth, sugar candy, river water
Make Blacking
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Ivory black: 12 oz
• Treacle: 12 oz
• Spermaceti oil: 4 oz
• White wine vinegar: 4 pts
Procedure:
1. Mix all ingredients together.
black, leather care, vinegar, treacle, spermaceti oil
Make Blacking Balls for Shoes
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Mutton suet: 4 oz
• Bees-wax: 1 oz
• Sweet oil: 1 oz
• Sugar candy: 1 dr (in fine powder)
• Gum Arabic: 1 dr (in fine powder)
• Lampblack: sufficient to give a good black color
• Turpentine: about a spoonful
Procedure:
1. Melt...
Ingredients:
• Mutton suet: 4 oz
• Bees-wax: 1 oz
• Sweet oil: 1 oz
• Sugar candy: 1 dr (in fine powder)
• Gum Arabic: 1 dr (in fine powder)
• Lampblack: sufficient to give a good black color
• Turpentine: about a spoonful
Procedure:
1. Melt the mutton suet, bees-wax, sweet oil, sugar candy, and gum Arabic together over a gentle fire.
2. Add turpentine and lampblack to achieve a good black color.
3. While hot enough to run, pour the mixture into a tin mould to form balls.
4. Alternatively, let it cool almost completely before shaping by hand.
black, leather care, bees wax, mutton suet, turpentine
Make Liquid Japan Blacking
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Ivory-black: 3 oz
• Coarse sugar: 2 oz
• Sulphuric acid: 1 oz
• Muriatic acid: 1 oz
• Sweet oil: 1 tablespoonful
• Lemon acid: 1 tablespoonful
• Vinegar: 1 pt
Procedure:
1. Mix the ivory-black and sweet oil together.
2....
Ingredients:
• Ivory-black: 3 oz
• Coarse sugar: 2 oz
• Sulphuric acid: 1 oz
• Muriatic acid: 1 oz
• Sweet oil: 1 tablespoonful
• Lemon acid: 1 tablespoonful
• Vinegar: 1 pt
Procedure:
1. Mix the ivory-black and sweet oil together.
2. Combine the lemon acid and coarse sugar, adding a little vinegar to qualify the blacking.
3. Add the sulphuric and muriatic acids to the mixture.
4. Mix all ingredients well together.
Observation: The sugar, oil, and vinegar prevent the acids from damaging the leather and enhance the lustre of the blacking.
liquid japan blacking, leather care, sulphuric acid, muriatic acid, vinegar
Render Leather Water-proof
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Drying oils
• Oxides of lead, copper, or iron
Procedure:
1. Rub or brush a mixture of drying oils and any of the oxides (lead, copper, or iron) onto the leather to make it water-proof.
2. Alternatively, substitute gummy resins...
Ingredients:
• Drying oils
• Oxides of lead, copper, or iron
Procedure:
1. Rub or brush a mixture of drying oils and any of the oxides (lead, copper, or iron) onto the leather to make it water-proof.
2. Alternatively, substitute gummy resins for the metallic oxides.
waterproof leather, drying oils, lead oxide, copper oxide, iron oxide

Metals Processing (8) -

Prepare Gold Powder Using Mercury Amalgam Method
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Prepare an amalgam of gold and mercury.
2. Place the amalgam into a clean, open crucible.
3. Apply strong heat to the crucible while continuously stirring the amalgam with a glass rod.
4. Continue heating until all the mercury has...
Procedure:

1. Prepare an amalgam of gold and mercury.
2. Place the amalgam into a clean, open crucible.
3. Apply strong heat to the crucible while continuously stirring the amalgam with a glass rod.
4. Continue heating until all the mercury has completely evaporated.
5. Once the mercury is gone, grind the remaining gold powder in a Wedgwood mortar with a small amount of water.
6. Dry the ground powder thoroughly for use.

Warnings:
• This method involves the sublimation of mercury, which is highly toxic and dangerous to health.
• It is strongly advised to avoid this method unless absolutely necessary due to the significant health risks involved.
gold,powder,mercury,amalgam,evaporation,gilding,toxicity
Cover Copper Bars with Gold to Make Sheets (Turner's Method)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Prepare ingots or pieces of copper or brass in convenient lengths and sizes.
2. Clean the surfaces of the metal thoroughly and make them level.
3. Prepare plates of pure gold or a gold alloy, ensuring they are the same size as the...
Procedure:

1. Prepare ingots or pieces of copper or brass in convenient lengths and sizes.
2. Clean the surfaces of the metal thoroughly and make them level.
3. Prepare plates of pure gold or a gold alloy, ensuring they are the same size as the ingots and of suitable thickness.
4. Place a piece of gold onto an ingot intended to be plated.
5. Hammer and compress both the gold and the ingot together until their surfaces are as nearly equal as possible.
6. Bind them together with wire to maintain contact during the process.
7. Mix silver-filings with borax to assist in fusion.
8. Apply this mixture along the edge of the gold plate, next to the metal ingot.
9. Place the assembly into a stove or furnace and heat until the silver and borax melt, ensuring perfect adhesion between the gold and the metal.
10. Carefully remove the ingot from the heat source once the process is complete.

Notes:
• The plated ingot can then be rolled out into sheets of gilded copper.
copper,gold,plating,sheet,turner,method
Dissolve Gold in Aqua Regia
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Prepare aqua regia, a solution composed of 2 parts nitric acid and 1 part muriatic acid.
2. Granulate the gold to be dissolved.
3. Place the granulated gold into a sufficient quantity of aqua regia.
4. Heat the mixture gently...
Procedure:

1. Prepare aqua regia, a solution composed of 2 parts nitric acid and 1 part muriatic acid.
2. Granulate the gold to be dissolved.
3. Place the granulated gold into a sufficient quantity of aqua regia.
4. Heat the mixture gently (moderate degree of heat).
5. Observe the reaction: effervescence will occur, and the solution will acquire a beautiful yellow color that becomes more intense over time, eventually turning golden or even orange.
6. Continue heating until the aqua regia is saturated with dissolved gold.
7. The resulting solution will be clear and transparent once the process is complete.

Notes:
• Aqua regia is a highly corrosive mixture that can dissolve gold, which is otherwise resistant to most acids.
• This method is used for dissolving gold in preparation for further processing or analysis.
gold,aqua,regia,dissolution,nitric,muriatic,acid
French-Plating Copper, Iron, and Brass with Leaf-Silver
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Heat the copper, iron, or brass to a suitable temperature.
2. Burnish leaf-silver onto the heated surface of the metal using appropriate tools.

Notes:
• This method is called French-plating and is used for plating metals...
Procedure:

1. Heat the copper, iron, or brass to a suitable temperature.
2. Burnish leaf-silver onto the heated surface of the metal using appropriate tools.

Notes:
• This method is called French-plating and is used for plating metals with silver.
• It is simpler than other methods but may not be as durable.
copper,iron,brass,silver,french-plating,burnishing
Grecian Gilding Using Sal-Ammoniac and Corrosive Sublimate
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Dissolve equal parts of sal-ammoniac and corrosive sublimate in spirit of nitre.
2. Prepare a solution of gold using this mixture as the menstruum (solvent).
3. Brush the silver surface with the prepared solution, which will turn...
Procedure:

1. Dissolve equal parts of sal-ammoniac and corrosive sublimate in spirit of nitre.
2. Prepare a solution of gold using this mixture as the menstruum (solvent).
3. Brush the silver surface with the prepared solution, which will turn it black.
4. Expose the treated silver to a red heat until it assumes the color of gold.

Notes:
• This method is known as Grecian Gilding and produces a gilded effect on silver.
• The process involves chemical reactions that change the surface color of the metal.
grecian,gilding,sal-ammoniac,corrosive,sublimate,spirit,nitre
Prepare Gold Powder Using Honey/Gum Method
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Place some gold-leaf into an earthen mortar.
2. Add a small amount of honey or thick gum-water to the mortar.
3. Grind the mixture thoroughly until the gold is reduced to extremely minute particles.
4. Wash the mixture with warm...
Procedure:

1. Place some gold-leaf into an earthen mortar.
2. Add a small amount of honey or thick gum-water to the mortar.
3. Grind the mixture thoroughly until the gold is reduced to extremely minute particles.
4. Wash the mixture with warm water to remove the honey or gum.
5. Allow the gold to dry, leaving it in a pulverulent (powdered) state.

Notes:
• This method produces fine gold powder suitable for gilding.
• The resulting powder is less refined than other methods but still usable.
gold,powder,honey,gum,processing,gilding
Prepare Gold Powder Using Nitro-Muriatic Acid Method
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Dissolve pure gold (or gold leaf) in nitro-muriatic acid.
2. Precipitate the dissolved gold using a piece of copper or a solution of sulphate of iron.
3. If precipitating with copper, digest the precipitate in distilled vinegar...
Procedure:

1. Dissolve pure gold (or gold leaf) in nitro-muriatic acid.
2. Precipitate the dissolved gold using a piece of copper or a solution of sulphate of iron.
3. If precipitating with copper, digest the precipitate in distilled vinegar and wash it repeatedly with water until clean.
4. Dry the washed precipitate thoroughly.

Notes:
• This method produces very fine gold powder that works better and is more easily burnished than other methods.
• The resulting powder is highly refined and suitable for high-quality gilding.
gold,powder,nitro-muriatic,acid,precipitation,gilding
Silver Copper Ingots Using Flux and Heat
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. File the surface of the copper to make it flat and leave it slightly rough.
2. Anneal the silver, then pickle it in weak muriatic acid.
3. Planish and scrape the silver on the surface that will be attached to the copper.
4. Anoint...
Procedure:

1. File the surface of the copper to make it flat and leave it slightly rough.
2. Anneal the silver, then pickle it in weak muriatic acid.
3. Planish and scrape the silver on the surface that will be attached to the copper.
4. Anoint or sprinkle a solution of borax or fine powdered borax onto both surfaces.
5. Bind the silver and copper together with wire to keep them in contact.
6. Expose the assembly to sufficient heat until the flux causes the surfaces to fuse simultaneously.
7. Allow the assembly to cool, after which the silver will be firmly united with the copper.

Notes:
• This method is used for plating copper ingots with silver.
• Fluxes are essential to ensure proper fusion and prevent sealing of the copper.
copper,silver,plating,flux,heat,fusion

Tree Health (7) -

Cleanse Orchard Trees Using Lime Application
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Slake some fresh lime with water to create a mixture.
2. Use a brush to apply this lime mixture to old, moss-eaten orchard trees.
3. Ensure the application is thorough and covers all affected areas.

Details:
• This method...
Procedure:
1. Slake some fresh lime with water to create a mixture.
2. Use a brush to apply this lime mixture to old, moss-eaten orchard trees.
3. Ensure the application is thorough and covers all affected areas.

Details:
• This method helps destroy insects and moss on the tree's surface.
• It also removes the outer rind and promotes the formation of a new, smooth, clear, healthy rind.
• Even twenty-year-old trees can regain a healthy appearance through this treatment.
lime application, orchard tree cleaning, natural remedy
Cure Apple-Tree Infection Using Cowdung and Human Urine Mixture
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Mix a quantity of cowdung with human urine to achieve the consistency of paint.
2. Apply this mixture to infected apple-trees around the beginning of March.

Details:
• This method is used for orchards occasionally injured by...
Procedure:
1. Mix a quantity of cowdung with human urine to achieve the consistency of paint.
2. Apply this mixture to infected apple-trees around the beginning of March.

Details:
• This method is used for orchards occasionally injured by an insect resembling white efflorescence that emits a blood-red fluid when bruised.
• The mixture should be applied early in the spring.
apple tree infection, cowdung, human urine, natural remedy
Cure Blight in Fruit Trees Using Smothering Straw-Fire and Lime-Dung Solution
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Make a smothering straw-fire early in October under each tree during calm weather.
2. Keep the fire burning for an hour or more.
3. After the fire, scrape off moss and other impurities from the trunk of the tree and from every...
Procedure:
1. Make a smothering straw-fire early in October under each tree during calm weather.
2. Keep the fire burning for an hour or more.
3. After the fire, scrape off moss and other impurities from the trunk of the tree and from every obscure hole and corner.
4. Use ladders to reach branches and clean them thoroughly, removing any webs or insect nests.
5. If necessary, wash the trunk and larger wood with a solution of lime and dung.
6. Destroy any insects or eggs that may have fallen on the ground around the tree.
7. Loosen the soil in the circumference of the tree to aid in the process.
8. In the spring or early blighting season, repeat this procedure by making a careful survey of every branch and acting accordingly.
9. Monthly, pick off all blights by band and use the water-engine where ablution may be necessary.

Details:
• This method is highly recommended for orchards and gardens to ensure healthy fruit production.
• The process involves multiple steps, including fire, cleaning, washing, and regular monitoring.
blight cure, straw-fire, lime-dung solution, tree health
Cure Canker in Apple-Trees Using Tar Streaks
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Encircle the apple-tree about knee-high with a streak of tar early in the spring.
2. Occasionally add a fresh coat of tar to maintain its effectiveness.

Details:
• This method is used to prevent canker worm infestation, which...
Procedure:
1. Encircle the apple-tree about knee-high with a streak of tar early in the spring.
2. Occasionally add a fresh coat of tar to maintain its effectiveness.

Details:
• This method is used to prevent canker worm infestation, which destroys young fruit and endangers the life of the tree.
• The application of tar acts as a barrier against the canker worm.
apple tree canker, tar, natural remedy
Cure Canker in Other Trees Using Sound Bark and Flannel Roller
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Cut off the affected parts of the tree to the quick.
2. Apply a piece of sound bark from any other tree to the cut area.
3. Bind the applied bark securely with a flannel roller.
4. Remove the canker and allow a new shoot to...
Procedure:
1. Cut off the affected parts of the tree to the quick.
2. Apply a piece of sound bark from any other tree to the cut area.
3. Bind the applied bark securely with a flannel roller.
4. Remove the canker and allow a new shoot to grow.

Details:
• This method is used for trees other than apple-trees affected by canker.
• However, in some cases, the new growth may become cankered again within one or two years.
canker treatment, sound bark, flannel roller, natural remedy
Cure Disease in Apple-Trees Using Train Oil and Scotch Snuff Mixture
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Brush off the white down from the infected areas of the apple-tree.
2. Clear off the red stain underneath the white down.
3. Anoint the infected places with a liquid mixture of train oil and Scotch snuff.

Details:
• This...
Procedure:
1. Brush off the white down from the infected areas of the apple-tree.
2. Clear off the red stain underneath the white down.
3. Anoint the infected places with a liquid mixture of train oil and Scotch snuff.

Details:
• This method is used to treat diseases in apple-trees.
• The combination of train oil and Scotch snuff is applied directly to the affected areas.
apple tree disease, train oil, scotch snuff, natural remedy
Cure Ulcers in Elm-Trees Using Boring and Tube Application
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Bore every elm tree attacked by ulcers at the ulcer itself.
2. Apply a tube to the hole created by the borer, penetrating about nine lines in depth.
3. Repeat this process for sound trees as well, even though they may not produce...
Procedure:
1. Bore every elm tree attacked by ulcers at the ulcer itself.
2. Apply a tube to the hole created by the borer, penetrating about nine lines in depth.
3. Repeat this process for sound trees as well, even though they may not produce any liquid.
4. The application of the tube should be done carefully to ensure that it is properly seated and sealed.

Details:
• This method helps dry and heal ulcers within forty-eight hours.
• The effusion from ulcerated trees increases in fine weather when the wound is exposed to the south, but stormy weather and great winds stop the effusion.
elm tree ulcer, boring, tube application, natural remedy

Oil Extraction (7) -

Distillation of Turpentine Oil Using a Retort
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Fill the retort with essence (turpentine).
2. Apply a small square of paper moistened with saliva to the tubular receiver to allow free passage of vapors.
3. Gradually increase the fire to carry out distillation very slowly.
4....
Procedure:
1. Fill the retort with essence (turpentine).
2. Apply a small square of paper moistened with saliva to the tubular receiver to allow free passage of vapors.
3. Gradually increase the fire to carry out distillation very slowly.
4. Continue until slightly more than 1/2 of the oil contained in the retort is obtained.
5. Separate a small quantity of exceedingly acid and reddish water that passes at the same time as the ethereous essence.
6. The remaining oil in the retort will be highly colored and thicker than the original essence.
7. This oil can be used for extending fat, varnish, or coarse oil painting.
turpentine oil,distillation,retort,essence,extraction
Extracting Watchmaker's Oil Using Porpoise Oil
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:

1. Expose the finest porpoise oil to the lowest natural temperature attainable.
2. The oil will separate into two portions: a thick, solid mass at the bottom and a thin, oily supernatant liquid.
3. Pour off the thin, oily supernatant...
Steps:

1. Expose the finest porpoise oil to the lowest natural temperature attainable.
2. The oil will separate into two portions: a thick, solid mass at the bottom and a thin, oily supernatant liquid.
3. Pour off the thin, oily supernatant liquid while it is still at the low temperature.
4. This liquid is now fit for use.
watchmaker's oil,porpoise oil,oil extraction,separation
Extraction of Essence of Vitivert
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Distill the kus-kus (rhizome of an East Indian grass) to obtain essence of vitivert.
Use:
• Used in perfumery.
essence of vitivert,distillation,kus-kus,perfumery
Extraction of Oil of Wintergreen
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Distill the leaves of *Gaultheria procumbens* to obtain oil of wintergreen.
Properties and Uses:
• Stimulant and carminative.
• Used in medicine, confectionary, and perfumery.
oil of wintergreen,gaultheria procumbens,distillation,medicine,perfumery
Extraction of Oil of Wormwood
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Distill the herb wormwood to obtain oil.
Yield Information:
• 25 lbs. of green wormwood will yield from 6 to 10 drs. of oil.
• 4 lbs. of dry wormwood will yield 1 oz. of oil.
• 18 lbs. of wormwood will yield 1 1/2 troy oz. of...
Procedure:
1. Distill the herb wormwood to obtain oil.
Yield Information:
• 25 lbs. of green wormwood will yield from 6 to 10 drs. of oil.
• 4 lbs. of dry wormwood will yield 1 oz. of oil.
• 18 lbs. of wormwood will yield 1 1/2 troy oz. of oil.
oil of wormwood,wormwood,distillation,yield,herb extraction
Production of Balsam of Turpentine (Dutch-drops)
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Distill oil of turpentine in a glass retort until a red balsam is left.
2. Alternatively, distill resin and separate the oils as they come over:
- First, a white oil.
- Then, yellow oil.
- Finally, a thick red oil, which...
Procedure:
1. Distill oil of turpentine in a glass retort until a red balsam is left.
2. Alternatively, distill resin and separate the oils as they come over:
- First, a white oil.
- Then, yellow oil.
- Finally, a thick red oil, which is the balsam.
Properties:
• Stimulant and diuretic.
balsam of turpentine,dutch-drops,distillation,resin,oil extraction
Refining Oil Using Alcohol and Heat
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:

1. Put a portion of any fine oil into a matrass or glass flask.
2. Add 7 or 8 times its weight of alcohol to the flask.
3. Heat the mixture almost to boiling.
4. Decant the clear upper stratum of fluid from the mixture.
5. Allow the...
Steps:

1. Put a portion of any fine oil into a matrass or glass flask.
2. Add 7 or 8 times its weight of alcohol to the flask.
3. Heat the mixture almost to boiling.
4. Decant the clear upper stratum of fluid from the mixture.
5. Allow the decanted fluid to cool.
6. A solid portion of fatty matter will separate; remove it.
7. Evaporate the alcoholic solution in a retort or basin until reduced to one-fifth of its bulk.
8. The resulting fluid part of the oil should be colorless, tasteless, almost free from smell, without action on infusion of litmus, and have the consistence of white olive oil. It should not easily congeal.
refining oil,alcohol extraction,oil purification

Dyeing And Coloring (7) -

Separate Red Coloring Principle of Madder
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps to Separate the Red Coloring Principle of Madder:
1. Prepare Tubs: Use three tubs labeled A, B, and C.
- Tub A: 2 feet 8 inches deep, 2 feet 6 inches in diameter (capacity for 55 lbs. of madder).
- Tub B: 5 1/2 feet high, 3 feet...
Steps to Separate the Red Coloring Principle of Madder:
1. Prepare Tubs: Use three tubs labeled A, B, and C.
- Tub A: 2 feet 8 inches deep, 2 feet 6 inches in diameter (capacity for 55 lbs. of madder).
- Tub B: 5 1/2 feet high, 3 feet in diameter, with three cocks placed at 2, 3, and 4 feet above the bottom.
- Tub C: Used as a deposit tub.
2. Location Setup: In summer, place the tubs near each other under a shed; in winter, store them in a cellar kept between 66° to 70°.
3. Add Madder to Tub A: Put 50–55 lbs. of ground madder into Tub A and add water until it is covered by an inch and a half of water. Stir the mixture continuously.
4. Fermentation Process: Allow the mixture to rest for 36–48 hours, during which fermentation will occur and raise a crust of madder to the surface.
5. Transfer Mixture to Tub B: Transfer the fermented mass from Tub A into Tub B, then fill it with water and let it repose for 2 hours.
6. Drain Liquor: Open the cocks in order: first at 2 feet, next at 3 feet, and finally at 4 feet above the bottom of Tub B. Collect the liquor from the second and third cocks into Tub C.
7. Washing Process: Repeat washing in Tub B until the water is no longer colored.
8. Prevent Re-Fermentation: In summer, take care to prevent re-fermentation of madder.
9. Precipitation Completion: The madder in Tub C will be washed and precipitated, resulting in a product equally good as other methods.
madder,red coloring,separation,tubs,washing
Prepare Green Dye Solution
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps to Prepare a Green Dye Solution:
1. Mix Acids: To 12 quarts of muriatic acid, add 1 quart of nitric acid gradually.
2. Add Grain Tin: Saturate the entire mixture with grain tin and boil it in an appropriate vessel until two-thirds...
Steps to Prepare a Green Dye Solution:
1. Mix Acids: To 12 quarts of muriatic acid, add 1 quart of nitric acid gradually.
2. Add Grain Tin: Saturate the entire mixture with grain tin and boil it in an appropriate vessel until two-thirds of the solution has evaporated.
3. Prepare Indigo Mixture: Take 9 lbs. of indigo and 1/2 pound of orange orpiment, grind them together in about 4 quarts of water.
4. Combine Solutions: Mix the prepared indigo mixture thoroughly with the green dye solution obtained from the acid-grain tin reaction.
green dye,muriatic acid,nitric acid,grain tin
Prepare Indigo Dye Solution
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps to Prepare Indigo Dye Solution:
1. Combine Ingredients: Boil together 9 lbs. of indigo with 1/2 pound of orange orpiment in about 4 qts. of water.
2. Grind Mixture: Mix the solution thoroughly and continue grinding it in the usual...
Steps to Prepare Indigo Dye Solution:
1. Combine Ingredients: Boil together 9 lbs. of indigo with 1/2 pound of orange orpiment in about 4 qts. of water.
2. Grind Mixture: Mix the solution thoroughly and continue grinding it in the usual way until a uniform consistency is achieved.
3. Thicken Solution: Thickening agent (gum) should be added to the solution to improve its application properties.
4. Store Properly: Ensure that the solution is carefully excluded from air exposure, as contact with air can cause the indigo to regenerate and render the solution useless.
Notes:
• Dr. Bancroft proposed using coarse brown sugar instead of orpiment for decomposing indigo; it serves equally well in making the dye soluble and acts as a thickening agent.
indigo,dye solution,preparation,orpiment,gum
Print Blue on Fabric
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps to Print Blue on Fabric:
1. Apply Wax Composition: Besmear the printing block with a composition partly consisting of wax, which will cover all parts of the fabric that are intended to remain white.
2. Dyeing Process: Immerse the...
Steps to Print Blue on Fabric:
1. Apply Wax Composition: Besmear the printing block with a composition partly consisting of wax, which will cover all parts of the fabric that are intended to remain white.
2. Dyeing Process: Immerse the treated fabric into a cold indigo vat for dyeing.
3. Dry Fabric: Allow the dyed fabric to dry completely.
4. Remove Wax Composition: Use hot water to remove the wax composition from the fabric, revealing the blue color on the areas not covered by the wax.
blue printing,wax composition,cold indigo vat
Print Yellow on Fabric
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps to Print Yellow on Fabric:
1. Apply Mordant: Besmear the printing block with acetate of alumina.
2. Treat Fabric: Apply the mordant to the fabric, ensuring even coverage.
3. Dyeing Process: After receiving the mordant, dye the...
Steps to Print Yellow on Fabric:
1. Apply Mordant: Besmear the printing block with acetate of alumina.
2. Treat Fabric: Apply the mordant to the fabric, ensuring even coverage.
3. Dyeing Process: After receiving the mordant, dye the fabric using quercitron bark as the coloring agent.
4. Bleaching Step: Finally, bleach the fabric to achieve the desired yellow color.
yellow printing,mordant,quercitron bark
Produce Lilac and Brown on Fabric
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps to Produce Lilac, Flea Brown, and Blackish Brown on Fabric:
1. Use Acetate of Iron: Apply acetate of iron to the fabric in quantities proportioned to the desired depth of color.
2. Add Sumach for Deep Colors: For very deep shades,...
Steps to Produce Lilac, Flea Brown, and Blackish Brown on Fabric:
1. Use Acetate of Iron: Apply acetate of iron to the fabric in quantities proportioned to the desired depth of color.
2. Add Sumach for Deep Colors: For very deep shades, add a small amount of sumach to enhance the intensity of the color.
3. Dyeing Process: After applying the mordant and any additional agents (like sumach), dye the fabric in the usual manner using madder as the coloring agent.
4. Bleaching Step: Bleach the fabric if necessary for a more defined or lighter shade of lilac, flea brown, or blackish brown.
lilac,brown,dyeing,madder,sumach
Produce Nankeen Yellow on Cotton Prints
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps to Produce Nankeen Yellow on Cotton Prints:
1. Prepare Block: Besmear the printing block (cut into the desired print pattern) with acetate of iron, thickened with gum or flour.
2. Apply to Fabric: Apply this mordant to the cotton...
Steps to Produce Nankeen Yellow on Cotton Prints:
1. Prepare Block: Besmear the printing block (cut into the desired print pattern) with acetate of iron, thickened with gum or flour.
2. Apply to Fabric: Apply this mordant to the cotton fabric.
3. Dry and Cleanse: Dry the fabric and cleanse it in the usual manner.
4. Dyeing Process: Plunge the treated fabric into a potash lye solution.
5. Adjust Shade Depth: The quantity of acetate of iron used is proportioned to the desired depth of the yellow shade.
nankeen yellow,acetate of iron,potash lye

Jewelry Preparation (7) -

Color Foils Using Smoke or Pigment
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Methods for Coloring Foils:
• Method 1: Tingeing with Smoke:
- Use smoke to tint the surface of copper to the desired color.
• Method 2: Painting with Pigments:
- Stain or paint the foil using pigments, dyes, or coloring...
Methods for Coloring Foils:
• Method 1: Tingeing with Smoke:
- Use smoke to tint the surface of copper to the desired color.
• Method 2: Painting with Pigments:
- Stain or paint the foil using pigments, dyes, or coloring substances.

Pigment Preparation:
• Colors may be tempered with oil, water made viscous by gum Arabic, size, or varnish.
• For Deep Colors:
- Oil is most suitable because some pigments become transparent in it (e.g., lake or Prussian blue).
• For Yellow and Green:
- Varnish is preferable as these colors can be obtained in perfection from a tinge dissolved in spirit of wine, similar to lacquers.
• For Green:
- Distilled verdigris produces the most beautiful green but may turn black with oil.
• Common Cases:
- Isinglass size is suitable for most colors and can be used similarly to glazing colors in miniature painting.
color foils, pigment preparation, coloring methods, oil, varnish, gum arabic, isinglass size
Create Amethyst Color for Foils
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Needed:
• Lake
• Prussian blue
• Oil

Steps:
1. For Amethyst Color:
- Mix lake with a small amount of Prussian blue.
- Use oil as the medium and apply it very thinly on the foil.
amethyst color, lake, prussian blue, oil application
Create Eagle Marine Color for Foils
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Needed:
• Common verdigris
• Prussian blue
• Shellac varnish

Steps:
1. For Eagle Marine Color:
- Mix common verdigris with a small amount of Prussian blue.
- Temper the mixture in shellac varnish for application on foils.
eagle marine color, verdigris, prussian blue, shellac varnish
Create Garnet Red for Foils
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Needed:
• Dragon's blood
• Seedlac varnish
• Orange lake
• Shellac varnish

Steps:
1. For Garnet Red:
- Use dragon’s blood dissolved in seedlac varnish.
2. For Vinegar Garnet:
- Mix orange lake with shellac varnish...
Materials Needed:
• Dragon's blood
• Seedlac varnish
• Orange lake
• Shellac varnish

Steps:
1. For Garnet Red:
- Use dragon’s blood dissolved in seedlac varnish.
2. For Vinegar Garnet:
- Mix orange lake with shellac varnish for an excellent result.
garnet red, dragon's blood, seedlac varnish, orange lake, shellac varnish
Create Ruby Colors for Foils
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Needed:
• Carmine
• Lake (used in isinglass size)
• Shellac varnish

Steps:
1. For Red (Imitating Ruby):
- Use carmine or a small amount of lake mixed with isinglass size.
- Alternatively, use shellac varnish if the glass...
Materials Needed:
• Carmine
• Lake (used in isinglass size)
• Shellac varnish

Steps:
1. For Red (Imitating Ruby):
- Use carmine or a small amount of lake mixed with isinglass size.
- Alternatively, use shellac varnish if the glass or paste is of a full crimson verging toward purple.
2. For Scarlet or Orange Tones:
- Use very bright lake (not purple) in oil alone.
ruby colors, carmine, lake, shellac varnish, isinglass size
Create Yellow Color for Foils
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Needed:
• Yellow lacquer

Steps:
1. For Full Yellow:
