- Use yellow lacquer and apply it on the foil as you would for other purposes.
2. For Slighter Yellow (Topazes):
- The burnish and foil itself may be sufficient...
Materials Needed:
• Yellow lacquer

Steps:
1. For Full Yellow:
- Use yellow lacquer and apply it on the foil as you would for other purposes.
2. For Slighter Yellow (Topazes):
- The burnish and foil itself may be sufficient without any additional color.
yellow color, yellow lacquer, topazes, foil preparation
Prepare Foils for Crystals, Pebbles, or Paste to Give the Lustre and Play of Diamonds
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Needed:
• Leaves of tin (prepared as for silvering looking-glasses)
• Thin gum-water
• Warm quicksilver
• Socket or setting for stones

Steps:
1. Prepare the Tin Foils:
- Take leaves of tin that have been prepared in the...
Materials Needed:
• Leaves of tin (prepared as for silvering looking-glasses)
• Thin gum-water
• Warm quicksilver
• Socket or setting for stones

Steps:
1. Prepare the Tin Foils:
- Take leaves of tin that have been prepared in the same manner as for silvering looking-glasses.
- Cut them into small pieces large enough to cover the surface of the sockets or stones to be set.
2. Apply Gum-Water and Dry:
- Moisten the inside of the socket with thin gum-water.
- Allow it to dry until only a slight stickiness remains.
3. Insert Tin Foils into Socket:
- Place three pieces of tin leaves, one upon another, into the socket.
- Ensure they are laid as evenly as possible on the surface.
4. Heat and Apply Quicksilver:
- Heat the socket.
- Fill it with warm quicksilver and let it remain for 3 to 4 minutes.
- Gently pour out the quicksilver.
5. Insert Stone into Socket:
- Insert the stone into the socket, ensuring there is enough room so that it can be placed without stripping off the tin or quicksilver from any part of the furnace.
6. Secure the Foil and Stone:
- Close the work around the stone to prevent the tin and quicksilver from being shaken out by any violence.

Benefits:
• The lustre of stones set in this manner will last longer than when set in the common way, as moisture cannot penetrate the cavity surrounding them.
foils, crystals, pebbles, paste, lustre, diamonds, quicksilver, tin leaves, socket preparation

Metalworking (7) -

Voltaic Protection of Metals
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To protect metals using voltaic protection, follow these steps:

1. Understand the principle: When two metals are united and exposed to a corrosive agent, the metal that would be most affected by the agent receives the majority of the...
To protect metals using voltaic protection, follow these steps:

1. Understand the principle: When two metals are united and exposed to a corrosive agent, the metal that would be most affected by the agent receives the majority of the corrosion force, while the other remains protected.
2. Example with galvanized iron: Iron coated with zinc (galvanized iron) will last for years when exposed to the atmosphere.
3. Example with lightning rods: Copper points on lightning rods remain bright for a long time when screwed into a zinc ball.
voltaic protection, galvanized iron, corrosion prevention, metal coating
Brass Deposition Procedure
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To deposit brass, follow these steps:

1. Prepare copper solution: Dissolve 5 oz. of powdered acetate of copper in 1/2 gallon of water.
2. Add ammonia solution: Add 1 pint of a solution of ammonia to the copper solution.
3. **Prepare...
To deposit brass, follow these steps:

1. Prepare copper solution: Dissolve 5 oz. of powdered acetate of copper in 1/2 gallon of water.
2. Add ammonia solution: Add 1 pint of a solution of ammonia to the copper solution.
3. Prepare zinc solution: Dissolve 10 oz. of sulphate of zinc (white vitriol) in 1 gallon of water at 180 ° Fahrenheit. When cooled, add 1 pint of a solution of ammonia.
4. Prepare potash solution: Dissolve 4 1/2 lbs. of potassa in 1 gallon of water.
5. Prepare cyanide solution: Dissolve 8 oz. of cyanide of potassium in 1 gallon of hot water.
6. Mix solutions: Combine the solutions in this order:
- Add the copper solution to the zinc solution.
- Then add the potash and cyanide solutions.
7. Digest mixture: Allow the mixture to digest for about an hour.
8. Adjust volume: Add water to make up a total of 8 gallons.
9. Electrolysis setup: Use a brass anode and an active battery power during electrolysis. Occasionally add more ammonia and cyanide as needed.
brass deposition, copper solution, zinc solution, potash solution
Bronzing Copper Medals
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To bronze copper medals, follow these steps:

### Brown Bronzing
1. Clean surface: Clean the surface of the medal with weak nitric acid.
2. Dry and apply heat: Allow it to dry, then apply gentle heat to achieve a brown color.

###...
To bronze copper medals, follow these steps:

### Brown Bronzing
1. Clean surface: Clean the surface of the medal with weak nitric acid.
2. Dry and apply heat: Allow it to dry, then apply gentle heat to achieve a brown color.

### Black Bronzing
1. Use alternative solution: Instead of nitric acid, use sulphydrate of ammonia or liver of sulphur for black bronzing.

### Green Bronzing
1. Expose to fumes: Place the medal in a closed box and expose it to the fumes of chloride of lime, or to the vapor of acetic or muriatic acid to achieve a green color.

### General Bronzing for Fine Work
1. Use bichloride of platinum: For bronzing fine copper or brass work, use a weak solution of bichloride of platinum.
2. Control temperature and color: By varying the temperature and exposure time, you can achieve different shades ranging from steel gray to deep black.
bronzing copper medals, bichloride of platinum, nitric acid, liver of sulphur
Copying Medals Using Electrolysis
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To copy medals, follow these steps:

1. Create casts: Make casts of the medals using fusible metal, plaster, wax, or other suitable materials.
2. Prepare non-metallic molds: If using a non-metallic mold, brush its face with black...
To copy medals, follow these steps:

1. Create casts: Make casts of the medals using fusible metal, plaster, wax, or other suitable materials.
2. Prepare non-metallic molds: If using a non-metallic mold, brush its face with black lead.
3. Coat metallic molds: Coated the back of metallic molds with wax or varnish to prevent unwanted reactions.
4. Attach wire: Attach the wire to the edge of the medal by soldering or twisting it in place.
5. Set up electrolysis chamber: Use a water-tight box divided by a porous partition (made from plaster or leather).
6. Fill compartments:
- On one side, immerse a plate of zinc in diluted sulphuric acid (1 to 20 ratio).
- On the other side, place a solution of saturated sulphato of copper.
7. Connect wires: Connect a wire from the zinc plate to a copper rod. Hang the medals on this rod so they dip into the copper solution.
copying medals, electrolysis setup, sulphuric acid, copper solution
Depositing Copper on Iron Using Electrolysis
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To deposit copper on iron, follow these steps:

1. Prepare cyanide solution: Create a solution of cyanide of copper by either:
- Dissolving oxide of copper in cyanide of potassium, or
- Adding cyanide of potassium to a solution of...
To deposit copper on iron, follow these steps:

1. Prepare cyanide solution: Create a solution of cyanide of copper by either:
- Dissolving oxide of copper in cyanide of potassium, or
- Adding cyanide of potassium to a solution of sulphate of copper and redissolving the precipitate formed.
2. Electrolysis setup: Use a strong battery power during the process.
3. Ensure current strength: The copper will not deposit unless the current is strong enough to evolve hydrogen at the cathode.
4. Avoid hydrogen evolution: Always avoid the evolution of hydrogen when depositing other metals.
copper deposition, iron plating, cyanide solution, battery power
Electro-Gilding Procedure
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Electro-gilding is done as follows:

1. Dissolve gold: Dissolve the gold in nitro-hydrochloric acid.
2. Wash and digest: Wash the dissolved gold with boiling nitric acid, then digest it with calcined magnesia.
3. Deposit as oxide:...
Electro-gilding is done as follows:

1. Dissolve gold: Dissolve the gold in nitro-hydrochloric acid.
2. Wash and digest: Wash the dissolved gold with boiling nitric acid, then digest it with calcined magnesia.
3. Deposit as oxide: The gold is deposited in the form of an oxide.
4. Further processing: After washing in boiling nitric acid, dissolve the gold in a solution of cyanide of potassium.
5. Prepare articles for plating: Articles to be plated with gold must be prepared properly and placed into the cyanide of potassium solution.
6. Special preparation for certain metals: Iron, steel, lead, and some other metals that do not readily receive the gold deposit require to be first lightly plated with copper or dipped in a solution containing:
- Nitrate of silver: 1 part
- Nitrate of mercury: 1 part
- Nitric acid (specific gravity 1.384): 4 parts
- Water: 120 parts
7. Battery setup: The positive plate of the battery must be made of gold, and the other plate should be made of iron or copper.
8. Apply current: Use a feeble current during the electro-gilding process.
electro-gilding, gold plating, battery setup, metal preparation
Enamelling Process on Metallic Surfaces
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Enamelling involves applying a smooth coating of vitrified matter to a bright polished metallic surface. This process is similar to applying a varnish made of glass, which is melted onto the surface, providing a fine uniform ground for ornaments...
Enamelling involves applying a smooth coating of vitrified matter to a bright polished metallic surface. This process is similar to applying a varnish made of glass, which is melted onto the surface, providing a fine uniform ground for ornaments that are also fixed by heat.

### Materials and Preparation:
• Metals suitable for enamelling: Only gold and copper can be used. Opaque enamels are typically used on copper.
• Transparent colored enamels: Require metals that remain unaltered when fully red hot and are not chemically altered by melted glass containing metallic oxides.

### Key Considerations:
• Silver is impractical for colored enamelling because the interaction between silver and colored enamel (e.g., yellow from lead or antimony oxide) causes color changes, such as from yellow to orange and eventually to a dirty olive.
• Copper is also chemically altered by colored enamels, making it unsuitable for this type of work.
• Gold is the only metal that can withstand prolonged contact with colored glass at full red heat without being chemically altered.
enamelling, vitrified matter, gold, copper, silver, transparent enamel, opaque enamel, metallic oxides

Glassmaking Glazes (7) -

Prepare Fine Blue Glazing
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• 1 lb red-lead
• 2 lbs powdered flints
• 2 lbs common salt
• 1 lb tartar
• 1/2 lb white flint-glass
• 1/2 lb zaffre

Procedure:
1. Calcination Process:
- Calcine the mixture until it is almost white.
2. **Fusion...
Materials Required:
• 1 lb red-lead
• 2 lbs powdered flints
• 2 lbs common salt
• 1 lb tartar
• 1/2 lb white flint-glass
• 1/2 lb zaffre

Procedure:
1. Calcination Process:
- Calcine the mixture until it is almost white.
2. Fusion and Quenching:
- Fuse the mixture with white flint-glass and zaffre, then quench the melted mass in water.
3. Repeat Process:
- Repeat this operation several times for optimal results.

Alternative Method:
• Use 1 lb tartar, 1/4 lb red-lead, 1/2 oz zaffre, and 1/4 lb powdered flints.
• Follow the same procedure as above.
fine blue glazing,tartar,zaffre,fusion
Prepare Gold-colored Glazing
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• 3 parts red-lead
• 2 parts antimony
• 1 part colcothar

Procedure:
1. Fusion Process:
- Fuse the mixture.
2. Powder and Refuse:
- Powder the mass, then melt it again.
3. Repeat Process:
- Repeat...
Materials Required:
• 3 parts red-lead
• 2 parts antimony
• 1 part colcothar

Procedure:
1. Fusion Process:
- Fuse the mixture.
2. Powder and Refuse:
- Powder the mass, then melt it again.
3. Repeat Process:
- Repeat this operation up to four times to produce a fine gold-colored yellow glazing.

Alternative Method:
• Use 12 parts red lead, 12 parts white flints, and 1 part filings of iron.
• Fuse the mixture twice to obtain a transparent, faintly yellowish glazing.
gold-colored glazing,red-lead,colcothar,fusion
Prepare Green Glazing on a White Ground
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• 1 part calcined copper
• 2 parts yellow glazing (from any of the preceding recipes)

Procedure:
1. Combine Ingredients:
- Mix the calcined copper with the yellow glazing.
2. Fusion Process:
- Fuse the...
Materials Required:
• 1 part calcined copper
• 2 parts yellow glazing (from any of the preceding recipes)

Procedure:
1. Combine Ingredients:
- Mix the calcined copper with the yellow glazing.
2. Fusion Process:
- Fuse the mixture twice.
3. Application Notes:
- Apply the glazing thinly to avoid a deep color.
- The glazing should be applied on a white ground for optimal results.
green glazing,calcined copper,yellow glazing,fusion
Prepare Lemon-colored Glazing
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• 3 parts red-lead
• 3 1/2 parts powdered bricks (very red and brittle)
• 1 part antimony

Procedure:
1. Calcination Process:
- Calcine the mixture day and night for 4 days in the ashhole of a glass-house...
Materials Required:
• 3 parts red-lead
• 3 1/2 parts powdered bricks (very red and brittle)
• 1 part antimony

Procedure:
1. Calcination Process:
- Calcine the mixture day and night for 4 days in the ashhole of a glass-house furnace.
2. Fusion Process:
- Fuse the mixture until it becomes liquid.
3. Result:
- The glazing will produce a very fine lemon-colored finish.

Important Notes:
• Use only finely red and brittle bricks for optimal results.
• Gray bricks are not suitable for this preparation.
lemon-colored glazing,red-lead,powdered bricks,antimony,fusion
Prepare Light Yellow Glazing

• 4 parts red-lead
• 3 parts antimony
• 8 parts mixture of calxes of lead and tin (from masticot for white glazing)
• 3 parts glass

Alternative Composition:
• 10 parts red-lead
• 3 parts antimony
• 3 parts glass
• 2...

• 4 parts red-lead
• 3 parts antimony
• 8 parts mixture of calxes of lead and tin (from masticot for white glazing)
• 3 parts glass

Alternative Composition:
• 10 parts red-lead
• 3 parts antimony
• 3 parts glass
• 2 parts calcined tin

Procedure:
1. Combine Ingredients:
- Mix the specified quantities of materials together.
2. Fusion Process:
- Fuse the mixture to produce a light yellow glazing.

• 12 parts tartar

Prepare Yellow Glazing Using Tin, Antimony, and Lead
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• 2 lbs tin
• 2 lbs antimony
• 3 lbs lead

Procedure:
1. Calcine the materials:
- Calcine the tin, lead, and antimony together.
- Alternatively, use equal quantities of all three ingredients as per some...
Materials Required:
• 2 lbs tin
• 2 lbs antimony
• 3 lbs lead

Procedure:
1. Calcine the materials:
- Calcine the tin, lead, and antimony together.
- Alternatively, use equal quantities of all three ingredients as per some methods.
2. Fusion Process:
- Place the calcined mixture into fusion to vitrify it.
3. Result:
- The glazing will run quickly and produce a fine yellow color.

Alternative Method:
• Use 5 parts red-lead, 2 parts powdered bark, 1 part sand, 1 part white glazing, and 2 parts antimony.
• Calcine the mixture and then fuse it to obtain a fine yellow glazing.
yellow glazing,tin,antimony,lead,calcine,fusion

Alcohol Preparation (6) -

Determine Alcohol Content in Wine and Beer
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Alcohol Content Determination in Wine and Beer:

1. Distillation Process:
- Carefully distill a small quantity of the liquid until approximately 1/2 to 3/4 has passed over.

2. Reconstitution:
- Add enough water to the distillate...
Alcohol Content Determination in Wine and Beer:

1. Distillation Process:
- Carefully distill a small quantity of the liquid until approximately 1/2 to 3/4 has passed over.

2. Reconstitution:
- Add enough water to the distillate to make it up to its original bulk.
- Place the mixture in a well-stopped bottle and shake it well.

3. Standing Period:
- Let the mixture stand aside for one or two days.

4. Specific Gravity Measurement:
- Take the specific gravity of the mixture using a hydrometer or specific gravity bottle.
alcohol,detection,wine,beer,specific,gravity,test
Alternative Raspberry Brandy Preparation
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Alternative Raspberry Brandy Method:

1. Infusion Process:
- Prepare the raspberry brandy in a manner similar to cherry brandy.
- Use about the same addition of brandy for each infusion (first, second, and third).
- Adjust sweetness...
Alternative Raspberry Brandy Method:

1. Infusion Process:
- Prepare the raspberry brandy in a manner similar to cherry brandy.
- Use about the same addition of brandy for each infusion (first, second, and third).
- Adjust sweetness accordingly for each batch.

2. Color Adjustment:
- The first infusion should be bright and deep in color; omit cinnamon and cloves during this step.
- For the second infusion, include cinnamon and cloves but note that it will be somewhat paler than the first.
- Enhance its color by adding approximately 1 quart (qt.) of cherry brandy along with 10 or more gallons (galls.) of raspberry brandy.

3. Third Infusion:
- The third infusion will require even more cherry brandy to achieve the desired color.

4. Flavoring:
- Flavor the final product with the juice of elderberries.
raspberry,brandy,syrup,infusion,color,flavoring
Flavoring Materials for Spirits and Wines
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Flavoring Materials:

The following materials are used to flavor spirits and wines:

• Acetic, butyric ethers,
• Acetate of the oxyde of amyl (see CONFECTIONARY for Flavoring Extracts),
• Sweet spirit of nitre,
• Oil of bitter almonds,
• Oil of...
Flavoring Materials:

The following materials are used to flavor spirits and wines:

• Acetic, butyric ethers,
• Acetate of the oxyde of amyl (see CONFECTIONARY for Flavoring Extracts),
• Sweet spirit of nitre,
• Oil of bitter almonds,
• Oil of cognac,
• Light oil of wine,
• Various essential oils,
• Tincture of benzoin,
• Citric, tartaric, and sulphuric acid.

Other materials used to give the appearance of age include:

• Nut kernels,
• Mucilage of various kinds,
• Slippery elm,
• Almond oil,
• Green tea,
• Sugar.

Materials used to provide pungency and intoxicating qualities include:

• Coculus indicus,
• Guinea pepper,
• Mustard,
• Horse radish,
• Pellitory.

Substances used for astringency and color include:

• Catechu,
• Green tea,
• Logwood,
• Oak bark, etc.

Materials used to give color include:

• Caramel,
• Burned sugar.
flavoring,spirit,wine,materials,essence,additives
Prepare Neutral Spirits (Sweet Liquor)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Neutral Spirits Preparation:

1. Filtration Process:
- Filter ordinary whiskey through bone-black to remove impurities.
- Further filter the mixture through wood charcoal to eliminate all fusel oil and other odorous matter.
neutral,spirit,sweet,liquor,filtration
Prepare Orange Brandy
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Orange Brandy Preparation:

1. Preparation of Oranges and Brandy:
- Take chips from 18 Seville oranges.
- Place them in 3 quarts (qt.) of brandy.
- Allow the mixture to steep for a fortnight (approximately two weeks) in a stone...
Orange Brandy Preparation:

1. Preparation of Oranges and Brandy:
- Take chips from 18 Seville oranges.
- Place them in 3 quarts (qt.) of brandy.
- Allow the mixture to steep for a fortnight (approximately two weeks) in a stone bottle that is tightly closed.

2. Preparation of Syrup:
- Boil 2 quarts (qt.) of spring water with 1 1/2 lbs. of the finest sugar, gently for nearly an hour.
- Clarify the water and sugar mixture using the white of an egg.
- Strain the clarified syrup through a jelly-bag.
- Boil the syrup until it is nearly half its original volume.

3. Combining Brandy and Syrup:
- Once the syrup has cooled, strain the brandy into the syrup.
orange,brandy,syrup,preparation,spirit,recipe
Prepare Whiskey Cordial
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Whiskey Cordial Preparation:

1. Spice and Spirit Mixture:
- Take 3 oz. each of cinnamon, ginger, and coriander-seed.
- Add 1 1/2 oz. each of mace, cloves, and cubebs.
- Combine these with 11 gallons (galls.) of proof spirit and 2...
Whiskey Cordial Preparation:

1. Spice and Spirit Mixture:
- Take 3 oz. each of cinnamon, ginger, and coriander-seed.
- Add 1 1/2 oz. each of mace, cloves, and cubebs.
- Combine these with 11 gallons (galls.) of proof spirit and 2 gallons (galls.) of water.
- Distill the mixture.

2. Addition of Flavoring Ingredients:
- Tie up 5 oz. of English saffron.
- Add 4 1/2 lbs. of stoned raisins, 3 lbs. of dates, and 2 lbs. of liquorice root.
- Let these ingredients stand for 12 hours in 2 gallons (galls.) of water.

3. Combining Ingredients:
- Strain the mixture after standing and add it to the distilled spirit mixture.

4. Sweetening:
- Dulcify the entire mixture with fine sugar.
whiskey,cordial,spirit,recipe,sweetening

Flavor Extraction (6) -

Artificial Fruit Essences
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Artificial Fruit Essences:

1. Use the following compounds to create artificial fruit essences:
- Pineapple flavor: butyrate of ethyloxide (butyric ether)
- Apple flavor: valerianate of amyloxide
- Quince flavor: pelargonate of...
Artificial Fruit Essences:

1. Use the following compounds to create artificial fruit essences:
- Pineapple flavor: butyrate of ethyloxide (butyric ether)
- Apple flavor: valerianate of amyloxide
- Quince flavor: pelargonate of ethyloxide
- Jargonelle pear flavor: acetate of amyloxide
- Ordinary pear flavor: acetate of amyloxide with acetate of ethyloxide
- Melon flavor: cocinate of ethyloxide
2. Adjust the proportions and dilution levels with alcohol to achieve desired flavors.
artificial essences,fruit flavoring,chemical compounds
Make Butyric Ether
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Make Butyric Ether:

1. Combine the following ingredients:
- 8 oz. of butyric acid
- 6 oz. of alcohol
- 2 oz. of sulphuric acid
2. Distill the mixture in a glass retort.
3. Re-distill the distillate over chloride of calcium to purify...
To Make Butyric Ether:

1. Combine the following ingredients:
- 8 oz. of butyric acid
- 6 oz. of alcohol
- 2 oz. of sulphuric acid
2. Distill the mixture in a glass retort.
3. Re-distill the distillate over chloride of calcium to purify it.
4. This ether is known as pineapple oil and is used for flavoring syrup, creams, fruit drops, and cheap brandy.
5. Dilute the ether with deodorized alcohol to develop its true flavor.
butyric ether,pineapple oil,flavoring preparation
Make Jargonelle Pear Oil
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Make Jargonelle Pear Oil:

1. Use heavy fusel oil obtained from the last portion of distillation.
2. Purify the fusel oil by washing it with soda and water, then distill between 254° and 284° Fahr.
3. Take 1 lb. of this purified fusel oil and...
To Make Jargonelle Pear Oil:

1. Use heavy fusel oil obtained from the last portion of distillation.
2. Purify the fusel oil by washing it with soda and water, then distill between 254° and 284° Fahr.
3. Take 1 lb. of this purified fusel oil and combine it with:
- 1 lb. of glacial acetic acid
- 1/2 lb. of sulphuric acid
4. Digest the mixture for several hours at 254° Fahr.
5. Add water to separate the ether, then purify it by washing with soda and water again.
6. Mix the purified ether with:
- 1-30th part of acetic ether
- 7 parts of deodorized alcohol
7. This mixture produces the essence of pears.
jargonelle pear oil,fusel oil,pear flavoring
Make Oil of Quince
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Make Oil of Quince:

1. Prepare pelargonic ether by treating oil of rue with double its volume of dilute nitric acid.
2. Heat the mixture until it begins to boil, and allow it to stand for some time until two layers form.
3. Separate the...
To Make Oil of Quince:

1. Prepare pelargonic ether by treating oil of rue with double its volume of dilute nitric acid.
2. Heat the mixture until it begins to boil, and allow it to stand for some time until two layers form.
3. Separate the lower layer using a pipette and remove any residual nitric acid by evaporation in a chloride of zinc bath.
4. Filter the ether and mix it with deodorized alcohol.
5. Digest the mixture at a gentle heat until a fruity odor is noticed.
6. This ether is identical to ethereal oil of wine, which gives bouquet to wines and is sometimes sold as oil of Cognac.
oil of quince,pelargonic ether,wine bouquet
Preparation of Rum Ether
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Preparation of Rum Ether:

1. Gather the following ingredients:
- Black oxide of manganese: 12 lbs.
- Sulphuric acid: 12 lbs.
- Alcohol: 26 lbs.
- Strong acetic acid: 10 lbs.
2. Combine all ingredients and prepare the ether.
3. This...
Preparation of Rum Ether:

1. Gather the following ingredients:
- Black oxide of manganese: 12 lbs.
- Sulphuric acid: 12 lbs.
- Alcohol: 26 lbs.
- Strong acetic acid: 10 lbs.
2. Combine all ingredients and prepare the ether.
3. This ether is responsible for giving rum its distinctive flavor and is also used in making cheap brandy.
rum ether,flavor extraction,spirit preparation
Procure Oil of Wine
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Procure the Oil of Wine:

1. Distill the oil from the thick lees of French wines for optimal flavor.
2. Store the oil in a bottle and mix it with pure spirits of wine (such as alcohol) to preserve it for an extended period.
3. Shake the...
To Procure the Oil of Wine:

1. Distill the oil from the thick lees of French wines for optimal flavor.
2. Store the oil in a bottle and mix it with pure spirits of wine (such as alcohol) to preserve it for an extended period.
3. Shake the bottle before using the oil to ensure even distribution.
oil of wine,flavor extraction,wine lees,preservation

• Lavender flowers (ground): 1 lb
• Gum benzoin (powdered): 1/4 lb
• Otto of lavender: 1/4 oz

Procedure:
Mix lavender flowers, gum benzoin, and otto of lavender thoroughly.

Ingredients:
• Ground santal wood: as required

Procedure:
Purchase santal wood from wholesale dry-salters, and have it reduced to powder by drug-grinders. Any attempt to grind it at home will be found ineffective due to its toughness.

Fragrance (6) -

Make Cachou Aromatise
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Extract of liquorice: 3 1/2 oz.
• Water: 3 1/2 oz.
• Bengal catechu (powdered): 462 grs.
• Gum arabic (powdered): 231 grs.
• Mastic: 30 grs.
• Cascarilla: 30 grs.
• Charcoal: 30 grs.
• Orrisroot: 30 grs.
• English oil of...
Ingredients:
• Extract of liquorice: 3 1/2 oz.
• Water: 3 1/2 oz.
• Bengal catechu (powdered): 462 grs.
• Gum arabic (powdered): 231 grs.
• Mastic: 30 grs.
• Cascarilla: 30 grs.
• Charcoal: 30 grs.
• Orrisroot: 30 grs.
• English oil of peppermint: 30 drops.
• Tincture of ambergris: 5 drops.
• Tincture of musk: 5 drops.

Procedure:
1. Dissolve the extract of liquorice and water together using a water-bath until fully dissolved.
2. Add the powdered Bengal catechu and gum arabic to the mixture and continue heating until it reaches the consistency of an extract.
3. Reduce the mastic, cascarilla, charcoal, and orrisroot to fine powder by sifting them.
4. Incorporate these powders into the extract mixture.
5. Remove from heat and add the English oil of peppermint, tincture of ambergris, and tincture of musk to the mixture.
6. Pour the mixture onto an oiled slab and spread it out evenly using a roller until it reaches the thickness of a sixpenny piece.
7. Allow the mixture to cool completely.
8. Apply folds of blotting paper over the surface to absorb any excess oil.
9. Moisten the surface with water, then cover it with sheets of silver-leaf.
10. Allow the mixture to dry thoroughly.
11. Once fully dried, cut it into very thin strips and further divide these strips into small pieces about the size of a fenugreek seed.
cachou aromatise, liquorice extract, bengal catechu, gum arabic, mastic, cascarilla, charcoal, orrisroot, peppermint oil, ambergris tincture, musk tincture
Make Frangipanni Sachet
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Orris-root: 1 lb.
• Rose-leaves: 1 lb.
• Sandalwood: 1/4 lb.
• Tonqua beans: 1/4 lb.
• Musk: 1 dr.
• Civet: 1/4 dr.
• Essence of roses: 1/2 dr.

Procedure:
1. Triturate the musk, civet, and essence of roses together in a...
Ingredients:
• Orris-root: 1 lb.
• Rose-leaves: 1 lb.
• Sandalwood: 1/4 lb.
• Tonqua beans: 1/4 lb.
• Musk: 1 dr.
• Civet: 1/4 dr.
• Essence of roses: 1/2 dr.

Procedure:
1. Triturate the musk, civet, and essence of roses together in a mortar until well mixed.
2. Reduce the orris-root, rose-leaves, sandalwood, and Tonqua beans to a fine powder by sifting them.
3. Mix the tritured musk, civet, and essence of roses with the powdered substances.
4. Store the mixture in an airtight container for use in sachets.
frangipanni sachet, orris root, rose leaves, sandalwood, tonqua beans, musk, civet, essence of roses
Make Indian or Yellow Pastils
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Santal-wood (powdered): 1 lb.
• Gum benzoin: 1 1/2 lbs.
• Gum tolu: 1/4 lb.
• Nitrate of potassa: 1 1/2 lbs.
• Otto of santal: 3 drs.
• Otto of cassia: 3 drs.
• Otto of cloves: 3 drs.
• Mucilage of tragacanth: q.s. (quantity...
Ingredients:
• Santal-wood (powdered): 1 lb.
• Gum benzoin: 1 1/2 lbs.
• Gum tolu: 1/4 lb.
• Nitrate of potassa: 1 1/2 lbs.
• Otto of santal: 3 drs.
• Otto of cassia: 3 drs.
• Otto of cloves: 3 drs.
• Mucilage of tragacanth: q.s. (quantity sufficient) to make a thick paste

Procedure:
1. Powder and sift the santalwood, gum benzoin, and gum tolu until they are finely ground.
2. Add the otto of santal, otto of cassia, and otto of cloves to the powdered mixture.
3. Dissolve the nitrate of potassa in the mucilage of tragacanth.
4. Combine all ingredients thoroughly by beating them in a mortar until well mixed.
5. Shape the mixture into pastils using a pastil mould.
6. Gradually dry the pastils over time.
indian pastils, yellow pastils, santal wood, gum benzoin, gum tolu, nitrate of potassa, otto, mucilage of tragacanth
Make Perfumer's Pastils
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Well-burned charcoal: 1 lb.
• Tolu: 1/4 lb.
• Vanilla pods: 1/4 lb.
• Cloves: 1/4 lb.
• Benzoin: 3/4 lb.
• Otto of santal: 2 drs.
• Otto of neroli: 2 drs.
• Nitre: 1 1/2 oz.
• Mucilage of tragacanth: q.s. (quantity sufficient) to...
Ingredients:
• Well-burned charcoal: 1 lb.
• Tolu: 1/4 lb.
• Vanilla pods: 1/4 lb.
• Cloves: 1/4 lb.
• Benzoin: 3/4 lb.
• Otto of santal: 2 drs.
• Otto of neroli: 2 drs.
• Nitre: 1 1/2 oz.
• Mucilage of tragacanth: q.s. (quantity sufficient) to bind the mixture

Procedure:
1. Combine the well-burned charcoal, tolu, vanilla pods, and cloves in a mortar or mixing container.
2. Add the benzoin, otto of santal, and otto of neroli to the mixture.
3. Dissolve the nitre in a suitable medium (not specified) and add it to the mixture.
4. Mix all ingredients thoroughly using a sieve for even distribution.
5. Add enough mucilage of tragacanth to bind the mixture together, ensuring that only as much is used as necessary to achieve proper consistency.
6. Shape the mixture into pastils using a pastil mould.
7. Gradually dry the pastils over time.
perfumer's pastils, charcoal, tolu, vanilla pods, cloves, benzoin, otto of santal, nitre, mucilage of tragacanth
Make Piesse's Pastils
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Willow charcoal: 1/2 lb.
• Benzoic acid: 6 oz.
• Otto of thyme: 1/2 dr.
• Otto of caraway: 1/2 dr.
• Otto of rose: 1/2 dr.
• Otto of lavender: 1/2 dr.
• Otto of cloves: 1/2 dr.
• Otto of santal: 1/2 dr.
• Nitre: 3/4 oz.
-...
Ingredients:
• Willow charcoal: 1/2 lb.
• Benzoic acid: 6 oz.
• Otto of thyme: 1/2 dr.
• Otto of caraway: 1/2 dr.
• Otto of rose: 1/2 dr.
• Otto of lavender: 1/2 dr.
• Otto of cloves: 1/2 dr.
• Otto of santal: 1/2 dr.
• Nitre: 3/4 oz.
• Distilled or ordinary rosewater: 1/2 pt.
• Mucilage of tragacanth: q.s. (quantity sufficient) to bind the mixture
• Flowers of benzoin: as needed

Procedure:
1. Dissolve 3/4 oz. of nitre in 1/2 pt. of distilled or ordinary rosewater.
2. Thoroughly wet the willow charcoal with this solution and allow it to dry in a warm place until completely dry.
3. Once the nitrated charcoal is fully dry, pour over it the mixed ottoes (thyme, caraway, rose, lavender, cloves, santal) and stir thoroughly.
4. Add the flowers of benzoin to the mixture and combine well by sifting through a sieve for even distribution.
5. Add enough mucilage of tragacanth to bind the mixture together, using as little as possible to achieve proper consistency.
6. Shape the mixture into pastils using a pastil mould.
7. Gradually dry the pastils over time.
piesse's pastils, willow charcoal, benzoic acid, otto of thyme, nitre, rosewater, mucilage of tragacanth, flowers of benzoin
Prepare Peau d'Espagne (Spanish Skin)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Oil of rose: 1/2 oz.
• Neroli: 1/2 oz.
• Santal: 1/2 oz.
• Oil of lavender: 1/4 oz.
• Verbena: 1/4 oz.
• Bergamot: 1/4 oz.
• Oil of cloves: 2 drs.
• Cinnamon: 2 drs.
• Gum benzoin: 2 oz.
• Waste leather: sufficient...
Ingredients:
• Oil of rose: 1/2 oz.
• Neroli: 1/2 oz.
• Santal: 1/2 oz.
• Oil of lavender: 1/4 oz.
• Verbena: 1/4 oz.
• Bergamot: 1/4 oz.
• Oil of cloves: 2 drs.
• Cinnamon: 2 drs.
• Gum benzoin: 2 oz.
• Waste leather: sufficient quantity

Procedure:
1. In a container, dissolve the gum benzoin in a mixture of oils (rose, neroli, santal, lavender, verbena, and bergamot), along with the oil of cloves and cinnamon.
2. Steep good pieces of waste leather in this solution for one or two days.
3. After steeping, dry the leather over a line until it is completely dry.
4. Prepare a paste by rubbing 1 dr. of civet with 1 dr. of grain musk and enough gum tragacanth mucilage to achieve the proper consistency.
5. Cut the dried leather into pieces about 4 inches square.
6. Paste two pieces together using the prepared paste, place them between two sheets of paper, and weight or press until dry.
7. Once dry, enclose the pasted leather in silk or satin for storage.
peau d'espagne, spanish skin, oil of rose, neroli, santal, gum benzoin, waste leather, civet, musk, tragacanth mucilage

Textile Processing (6) -

Alum Silk Preparation
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Aluming Silk:

1. Silk should be alumed cold, as hot alum treatment can reduce its lustre.
2. Use a strong alum solution when preparing silk, as it will take dye more readily afterward.
alum silk,textile processing,dyeing
Discharge Colors from Dyed Fabrics
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Discharging Colors from Dyed Fabrics:

1. Place all colored silks to be discharged into a copper vat.
2. Dissolve 1/2 lb. or 1 lb. of white soap in the copper and boil the fabrics.
3. When the copper becomes too full of color,...
Procedure for Discharging Colors from Dyed Fabrics:

1. Place all colored silks to be discharged into a copper vat.
2. Dissolve 1/2 lb. or 1 lb. of white soap in the copper and boil the fabrics.
3. When the copper becomes too full of color, remove the silks and rinse them in warm water.
4. Add a fresh solution of soap to the copper and repeat the process until all the color is discharged.

For Specific Colors:
• Greys, cinnamons, etc.: Use tartar instead of soap if soap does not work.
• Slate colors, greenish drabs, olive drabs: Use oil of vitriol in warm water.
• For other colors: Boil alum in the copper, cool it down, and then immerse the silks. Rinse them before re-dyeing.
• Some fast colors may require a small quantity of muriatic acid diluted in warm water for discharge. Afterward, rinse the goods thoroughly in both warm and cold water to prevent damage to the fabric.
discharge colors,dyeing,textile processing,soap,tartar,oil of vitriol,alum,muriatic acid
Dye Cotton and Linen Blue
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Dyeing Cotton and Linen Blue:

1. Prepare a solution with the following ingredients:
- 1 part indigo
- 1 part green sulphate of iron
- 2 parts quicklime

2. Immerse the cotton or linen in this solution to...
Procedure for Dyeing Cotton and Linen Blue:

1. Prepare a solution with the following ingredients:
- 1 part indigo
- 1 part green sulphate of iron
- 2 parts quicklime

2. Immerse the cotton or linen in this solution to achieve a blue dye.
dye cotton blue,textile processing,indigo,sulphate of iron,quicklime
Dye Silk Blue
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Dyeing Silk Blue:

1. Prepare a ferment using the following ingredients:
- 6 parts bran
- 6 parts indigo
- 6 parts potassa
- 1 part madder
2. Immerse the silk in this mixture to achieve a **light...
Procedure for Dyeing Silk Blue:

1. Prepare a ferment using the following ingredients:
- 6 parts bran
- 6 parts indigo
- 6 parts potassa
- 1 part madder
2. Immerse the silk in this mixture to achieve a light blue dye.
3. For a dark blue, first apply a ground-color using a red dyestuff called archil before proceeding with the main dyeing process.
dye silk blue,textile processing,indigo,potassa,madder
Prussian Blue Dyeing Process
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Prussian Blue Dyeing:

1. Prepare a mordant solution with the following ingredients:
- 1 pint of nitrate of iron
- 8 oz. of bichloride of tin crystals
- 1/2 oz. of oil of vitriol
- 10 gallons of...
Procedure for Prussian Blue Dyeing:

1. Prepare a mordant solution with the following ingredients:
- 1 pint of nitrate of iron
- 8 oz. of bichloride of tin crystals
- 1/2 oz. of oil of vitriol
- 10 gallons of water

2. Prepare a second solution by dissolving 4 oz. of red or yellow prussiate of potash, depending on the desired shade.

3. Immerse the silks in the mordant solution for 10 minutes, then transfer them to the prussiate of potash solution for another 10 minutes. Repeat this process 6 times, alternating between the two solutions.

4. After each immersion, wash the fabric in cream of tartar water to ensure proper fixation of the dye.
prussian blue,dyeing,textile processing,nitrate of iron,bichloride of tin,cream of tartar
Re-Dye or Change the Color of Garments
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Re-Dyeing or Changing the Color of Garments:

1. The success of re-dyeing depends on the original dye used and how well the fabric has been cleaned.
2. If the fabric is not fully discharged, additional dye may be required to...
Procedure for Re-Dyeing or Changing the Color of Garments:

1. The success of re-dyeing depends on the original dye used and how well the fabric has been cleaned.
2. If the fabric is not fully discharged, additional dye may be required to achieve the desired color.
3. In some cases, the existing color must be completely discharged before applying a new dye.

Color Behavior Notes:
• All colors can be dyed black, and black will always remain black when re-dyed.
• Colors generally take on the same hue again if not fully discharged.
• Blues can become green or black upon re-dyeing.
• Green may turn brown, and brown may turn green.
• Re-dyeing typically results in a darker tint than the original color.

Dischargeability of Colors:
• Yellows, browns, and blues are not easily discharged.
• Maroons, reds (some kinds), olives, etc., can be discharged.

Specific Discharge Methods:
• For maroons: Boil a small quantity of alum in a copper until dissolved, then immerse the fabric and boil it to discharge the color.
• For olives, grays, etc.: Use 2 or 3 tablespoonfuls of oil of vitriol in warm water; immerse the garment and boil it until it turns white.
• Chemic green: Can be discharged with alum, pearlash, or soap to a yellow. This yellow can then be boiled off with soap if the fabric is prepared for taking chemic blue.
• Muriatic acid used at hand heat will discharge most colors.

Re-Dyeing Black Fabrics:
• A black garment may be dyed maroon, claret, green, or a dark brown. It is common for black to turn into claret, green, or dark brown, with green being the principal color it changes to.
re-dye,color change,textile processing,dyeing,black fabric,dischargeability

Textile Coloring (6) -

Mixing Solution of Tin with Prepared Indigo
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Mix the Solution of Tin with Prepared Indigo:

1. Take 2 galls. of the indigo prepared as above.
2. Stir into it, by degrees, 1 gall. of the solution of tin.
3. Neutralize the solution of tin with as much caustic alkali as can be added...
To Mix the Solution of Tin with Prepared Indigo:

1. Take 2 galls. of the indigo prepared as above.
2. Stir into it, by degrees, 1 gall. of the solution of tin.
3. Neutralize the solution of tin with as much caustic alkali as can be added without precipitating the tin from the acids.

Application to Goods:
• The goods are to be dipped in the way of dipping China blues.
• They must not however, be allowed to drain, but moved from one vat to another as quickly as possible.
• After dipping, they are to be cleansed in the usual way, in a sour vat of about 150 galls. of water to 1 gall. of sulphuric acid.
• They are then to be well washed in decoctions of weld, and other yellow color drugs.
• Finally, they are to be branned or bleached till they become white in those parts which are required colorless.
indigo,tin,solution,dyeing,textiles,coloring
Preparing a Substitute for Gum Used in Calico Printing
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Prepare a Substitute for Gum Used in Calico Printing:

1. Collect 1/2 a ton weight of scraps of pelts or skins, or pieces of rabbit or sheep-skins.
2. Boil them together for 7 or 8 hours in 350 galls. of water until it becomes a strong...
To Prepare a Substitute for Gum Used in Calico Printing:

1. Collect 1/2 a ton weight of scraps of pelts or skins, or pieces of rabbit or sheep-skins.
2. Boil them together for 7 or 8 hours in 350 galls. of water until it becomes a strong size.
3. Draw off the solution and weigh it when cold.
4. Warm it again, and to every 1 cwt., add 4 galls. of the strongest sweet wort that can be made from malt or 20 lbs. weight of sugar.
5. When incorporated, take it off and put it into a cask for use.

Usage:
• This substitute for gum may be used by calico printers in mixing up nearly all kinds of colors.
• Using a sixth part only of gum with it will also improve the gum.
• It improves and preserves the paste so much used by printers.
gum substitute,calico printing,textiles,dyeing,coloring
Printing Different Colors on Textiles
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Print different Colors:

• When different colors are to appear in the same print, a greater number of operations is necessary.
• Use two or more blocks or rollers; each block or roller should have only that part of the print cut which is to...
To Print different Colors:

• When different colors are to appear in the same print, a greater number of operations is necessary.
• Use two or more blocks or rollers; each block or roller should have only that part of the print cut which is to be of some particular color.
• Besmear each block or roller with different mordants and apply them to the cloth, then dye as usual.

Example Process for Cotton:
1. Apply one block with acetate of alumina.
2. Apply another block with acetate of iron.
3. Apply a third block with a mixture of those two mordants.
4. Dye the cotton with quercitron bark and bleached.

Color Results:
• Acetate of alumina: yellow.
• Acetate of iron: olive, drab, dove.
• Mixture of acetate of alumina and iron: olive green.
• If the yellow part is covered with indigo liquor applied with a pencil, it will be converted into green.
• Indigo liquor can also be used to give blue to other parts of the print.

Alternative Dyeing with Madder:
• Acetate of alumina: red.
• Acetate of iron: brown, black.
• Mixture: purple.

For Multiple Colors (e.g., Bark and Madder):
1. Apply mordants for parts of the pattern to cotton.
2. Dye in the madder bath and bleached.
3. Add remaining mordants to fill up the pattern, then dye with quercitron-bark and bleached.
4. The second dyeing does not much affect the madder colors because the mordants are already saturated.
5. Yellow tinge is easily removed by subsequent bleaching.
6. A new mordant may be applied to some of the madder colors, resulting in a new permanent color from the bark.
7. After the last bleaching, use indigo liquor to add new colors.
textiles,coloring,mordants,dyeing,cotton
Printing Dove Color and Drab
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Print Dove color and Drab:

• Use acetate of iron and quercitron bark.
• The cloth is afterwards prepared in the usual manner.
dove color,drab,textiles,coloring
Printing Green with Aniline
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Print Green with Aniline:

• Print the design with a thickened solution of chlorate of potassa.
• Pass through a solution of an aniline salt.
• In 2 or 3 days, the green color will be developed.
• The color may be changed to dark blue by...
To Print Green with Aniline:

• Print the design with a thickened solution of chlorate of potassa.
• Pass through a solution of an aniline salt.
• In 2 or 3 days, the green color will be developed.
• The color may be changed to dark blue by using soap or an alkaline liquid.

Alternative Method:
• Use alternately aniline blue and picric acid.
aniline,green,textiles,dyeing,coloring
Printing in Coal-tar Colors
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Print in Coal-tar Colors:

• Mix the colors with albumen and print them on the fibre.
• Coagulate the albumen to fix the color.

Alternative Method:
1. Print with tannin on fabric previously impregnated with stannate of soda.
2. Then dye...
To Print in Coal-tar Colors:

• Mix the colors with albumen and print them on the fibre.
• Coagulate the albumen to fix the color.

Alternative Method:
1. Print with tannin on fabric previously impregnated with stannate of soda.
2. Then dye with a hot, dilute acid bath.
3. The color on the unmordanted parts is easily discharged.
• This preparation is not necessary for silk and wool.
coal-tar colors,textiles,albumen,tannin,dyeing

Book Restoration (6) -

Black the Edges of Paper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To black the edges of paper, follow these steps:

Materials Required:
• Black lead
• Ink
• Fine cloth
• Dog's-tooth
• Glass or iron scraper with a flat edge

Procedure:
1. Mix black lead with ink.
2. When the paper is cut, color it thinly...
To black the edges of paper, follow these steps:

Materials Required:
• Black lead
• Ink
• Fine cloth
• Dog's-tooth
• Glass or iron scraper with a flat edge

Procedure:
1. Mix black lead with ink.
2. When the paper is cut, color it thinly over with black ink using a piece of fine cloth.
3. Rub on the black lead, covering every part of the edge.
4. If the edge appears rough after cutting, scrape it over with a piece of glass or an iron scraper with a flat edge.
5. Use the dog's-tooth to burnish the edge until it becomes well polished.
black paper edges, black lead, ink, burnishing
Color Vellum Green
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To color vellum green, follow these steps:

Materials Required:
• 1/2 pt. of the best white wine vinegar
• 1 oz. of verdigris
• 1 1/2 oz. of sap-green

Procedure:
1. Dissolve the verdigris and sap-green in the vinegar for a few days, having...
To color vellum green, follow these steps:

Materials Required:
• 1/2 pt. of the best white wine vinegar
• 1 oz. of verdigris
• 1 1/2 oz. of sap-green

Procedure:
1. Dissolve the verdigris and sap-green in the vinegar for a few days, having been heated by the fire.
2. Shake the bottle frequently before it is used.
3. Wash the vellum over with weak potash water.
4. When dry, color the vellum with the green solution 3 or 4 times until it has a good color.
5. When dry, wash the vellum over with thin paste water to give it a gloss.
color vellum green, verdigris, sap-green, vinegar
Dye Horns of Different Colors
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To dye horns in different colors, follow these steps:

Black Dye:
1. Steep brass in aquafortis until it turns green.
2. Wash the horn once or twice with this solution.
3. Put the horn into a warmed decoction of logwood and water.

**Green...
To dye horns in different colors, follow these steps:

Black Dye:
1. Steep brass in aquafortis until it turns green.
2. Wash the horn once or twice with this solution.
3. Put the horn into a warmed decoction of logwood and water.

Green Dye:
1. Boil the horn in alum-water.
2. Treat it with verdigris, ammoniac, and white wine vinegar, keeping it hot until sufficiently green.

Red Dye:
1. Boil the horn in alum-water.
2. Treat it with verdigris and ammoniac.
3. Finish by boiling it in a decoction of quicklime steeped in rainwater (strained), to which an ounce of Brazil-wood is added per pint.

Deep Black Stain:
1. Use a solution of nitrate of silver, diluted to avoid corrosion.
2. Apply the solution 2 or 3 times at considerable intervals.
3. Expose the horn to sunlight as much as possible to hasten and deepen the color.
dye horns black green red, aquafortis, logwood, Brazil wood
Dye or Stain Horn Tortoise-shell Color
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To dye or stain horn to resemble tortoise-shell, follow these steps:

Materials Required:
• Quicklime
• Litharge
• Soap lye
• Whiting (optional)
• Foil (orsedue type)

Procedure:
1. Press the horn into proper plates, scales, or other flat...
To dye or stain horn to resemble tortoise-shell, follow these steps:

Materials Required:
• Quicklime
• Litharge
• Soap lye
• Whiting (optional)
• Foil (orsedue type)

Procedure:
1. Press the horn into proper plates, scales, or other flat forms.
2. Prepare a mixture of quicklime (two parts) and litharge (one part), tempered together to the consistency of a soft paste with soap lye.
3. Apply this paste over all parts of the horn except those that should remain transparent.
4. Allow the horn to remain covered with the paste until it is thoroughly dry.
5. Brush off the paste, and the horn will be partly opaque and partly transparent, resembling tortoise-shell.
6. To enhance the resemblance, add semi-transparent parts by mixing whiting with some of the paste in specific areas, producing reddish-brown spots that can be interspersed for greater beauty.
dye horn tortoise shell, quicklime, litharge, soap lye
Marble the Covers of Books
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To marble the covers of books, perform the following steps:

1. Form clouds with aquafortis (oil of vitriol) mixed with ink.
2. Glaze the covers using this mixture.
marble, book covers, aquafortis, oil of vitriol
Sprinkle the Edges of Books
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To sprinkle the edges of books, follow these steps:

Materials Required:
• Russia hogs' bristles
• Cord
• Leather
• Wax cord
• Color (prepared in a cup)
• Folding-stick
• Cutting press

Procedure for Brush Preparation:
1. Make brushes from...
To sprinkle the edges of books, follow these steps:

Materials Required:
• Russia hogs' bristles
• Cord
• Leather
• Wax cord
• Color (prepared in a cup)
• Folding-stick
• Cutting press

Procedure for Brush Preparation:
1. Make brushes from Russia hogs' bristles of good thickness.
2. Tie the bristles round with cord and glue at the thick end.
3. Half cover the brush with a piece of leather.
4. When dry, tie the brush again with waxed cord within half an inch of the soft part.
5. Cut the brush very smooth and even.

Procedure for Sprinkling:
1. Prepare the color in a cup.
2. Dip the brush into the color until it is charged.
3. Press out the excess until it will no longer drop.
4. Screw the book tight in the cutting press.
5. Hold the brush in the left hand and use a folding-stick in the right to rub over the brush, causing the color to sprinkle finely on the edges.
6. Move the brush up and down over the edge as you sprinkle to ensure it is regular on every part.
7. After sprinkling is done, carefully wash the brushes in water, especially after using blue, which can otherwise destroy the brush.
sprinkle book edges, Russia hogs bristles, color preparation

Coatings And Preservatives (6) -

Apply Black Varnish for Zinc
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• 2 parts by weight of nitrate of copper
• 3 parts of crystallized chloride
• 64 parts of distilled water
• 8 parts of hydrochloric acid (110 density)
• Zinc, previously scoured with fine sand

Procedure:
1. Dissolve **2...
Materials Required:
• 2 parts by weight of nitrate of copper
• 3 parts of crystallized chloride
• 64 parts of distilled water
• 8 parts of hydrochloric acid (110 density)
• Zinc, previously scoured with fine sand

Procedure:
1. Dissolve 2 parts by weight of nitrate of copper and 3 parts of crystallized chloride in 64 parts of distilled water.
2. Add 8 parts of hydrochloric acid with a density of 110 to the solution.
3. Submerge the zinc, which has been previously scoured with fine sand, into this liquid.
4. Wash the metal thoroughly with water after removal from the solution.
5. Dry the zinc rapidly.

Notes:
• This process creates a velvet-black varnish that adheres chemically to zinc.
zinc,varnish,black,nitrate of copper,chloride,hydrochloric acid
Apply Wax Coating for Iron and Steel Protection
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Benzine
• Wax
• Brush

Procedure:
1. Heat benzine to a moderate temperature.
2. Dissolve half its weight of wax in the heated benzine.
3. Carefully apply this solution to the tool using a brush.
4. Allow the...
Materials Required:
• Benzine
• Wax
• Brush

Procedure:
1. Heat benzine to a moderate temperature.
2. Dissolve half its weight of wax in the heated benzine.
3. Carefully apply this solution to the tool using a brush.
4. Allow the solvent (benzine) to evaporate, leaving behind a very adhesive and permanent coating of wax.

Benefits:
• This wax coating preserves the metal even from the action of acid vapors.
iron,steel,wax,benzine,protection
Polish Varnished Surfaces with Pumice and Tripoli
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Pumice-stone, reduced to impalpable powder
• Tripoli earth, reduced to fine powder
• Serge cloth, moistened with water
• Clean woollen cloth, moistened with olive oil
• Soft linen cloth
• **Starch or...
Materials Required:
• Pumice-stone, reduced to impalpable powder
• Tripoli earth, reduced to fine powder
• Serge cloth, moistened with water
• Clean woollen cloth, moistened with olive oil
• Soft linen cloth
• Starch or Spanish white

Procedure:
1. Reduce pumice-stone to a fine, impalpable powder.
2. Mix the pumice powder with water and apply it to a piece of serge cloth, then use this to lightly rub the varnished surface in an even manner.
3. Reduce tripoli earth to a very fine powder.
4. Apply the tripoli powder to a clean woollen cloth that has been moistened with olive oil and use it for polishing the surface.
5. Wipe off the varnish with soft linen after polishing.
6. Once the surface is completely dry, clean it using starch or Spanish white, then rub gently with the palm of your hand to achieve a smooth finish.
polish,varnish,pumice,tripoli,serge cloth,woollen cloth,starch
Prepare Black Varnish for Indian Shields
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Nuts of Semecarpus anacardium (markingnut)
• Berries of Holigarna longifolia
• Clear water
• Mill for pressing
• Agate or smooth pebble for polishing

Procedure:
1. Soak the nuts of Semecarpus anacardium and...
Materials Required:
• Nuts of Semecarpus anacardium (markingnut)
• Berries of Holigarna longifolia
• Clear water
• Mill for pressing
• Agate or smooth pebble for polishing

Procedure:
1. Soak the nuts of Semecarpus anacardium and berries of Holigarna longifolia in clear water for a month.
2. Cut the soaked nuts and berries transversely, then press them in a mill to extract their juices.
3. Keep the expressed juice from each fruit separately for several months, removing scum periodically.
4. Decant the liquid from both fruits.
5. Mix two parts of Semecarpus anacardium juice with one part of Holigarna longifolia juice to create the varnish.
6. Apply this mixture like paint onto the shield or object.
7. Once dry, polish the surface using an agate or smooth pebble for a glossy finish.

Notes:
• This varnish also protects wood from destruction by white ants (termites).
shield,varnish,semecarpus anacardium,holigarna longifolia,polish,wood protection
Recover Varnish Using Potash Lye
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Potash (potassium hydroxide)
• Ashes of the lees of wine
• Water

Procedure:
1. Clear off any filth from the surface using a lye made of potash and ashes of the lees of wine.
2. Prepare the lye by mixing:
- 48...
Materials Required:
• Potash (potassium hydroxide)
• Ashes of the lees of wine
• Water

Procedure:
1. Clear off any filth from the surface using a lye made of potash and ashes of the lees of wine.
2. Prepare the lye by mixing:
- 48 oz. (approximately 1.4 kg) of potash
- 16 oz. (approximately 0.5 kg) of ashes of the lees of wine
3. Add these ingredients to 6 quarts (approximately 5.7 liters) of water.
4. Stir until fully dissolved, completing the lye.

Use:
• This lye is used for cleaning and recovering varnish from surfaces.
recover,varnish,potash,lye,wine lees
Varnish Silver Leaf to Resemble Gold
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Silver leaf
• Glutinous matter (e.g., size or adhesive)
• Varnish
• Rectified spirit of wine
• Tin foil (optional)
• Emery, fine linen cloth, putty, lacquer

Procedure:
1. Fix the silver leaf onto the surface using an...
Materials Required:
• Silver leaf
• Glutinous matter (e.g., size or adhesive)
• Varnish
• Rectified spirit of wine
• Tin foil (optional)
• Emery, fine linen cloth, putty, lacquer

Procedure:
1. Fix the silver leaf onto the surface using an appropriate glutinous matter, similar to how gold leaf is applied.
2. Apply a thin coat of varnish with a pencil over the silver leaf.
3. Allow the first coat to dry completely.
4. Wash and reapply the varnish repeatedly until the color appears sufficiently deep.
5. To test if this gilding method has been used, apply rectified spirit of wine; it will dissolve the varnish, revealing the silver leaf's natural whiteness.
6. If using tin foil instead of silver leaf:
- Fix the tin-leaf on the surface with glue.
- Burnish it with emery and a fine linen cloth.
- Polish further with putty applied in the same manner.
- Apply lacquer over the surface 5 or 6 times, giving it a finish resembling burnished gold.
7. A less concentrated version of this varnish can also be used on brass to enhance its color and protect it from tarnishing by air.
silver leaf,gold,varnish,tin foil,brass,polish

Insect Study (6) -

Collecting and Preserving Insects for Study
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Objective: Learn how to properly collect and preserve insects for further study.

Procedure:
1. Use a small container or vial with a lid to safely capture insects without damaging them.
2. Gently place the insect into the container, ensuring...
Objective: Learn how to properly collect and preserve insects for further study.

Procedure:
1. Use a small container or vial with a lid to safely capture insects without damaging them.
2. Gently place the insect into the container, ensuring it is not harmed during collection.
3. For preservation, use methods such as pinning (for larger insects) or placing in alcohol (for smaller or delicate specimens).
4. Label each specimen with details such as date, location, and species if known.
5. Store preserved specimens in a secure, dry place to prevent damage.

Notes: Refer to the United States National Museum’s bulletin *"Directions for Collecting and Preserving Insects"* by Mr. Nathan Banks for detailed instructions on proper collection techniques.
insect preservation, entomology, collecting insects, specimen storage
Observing Ant Communities
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Objective: Observe ant communities in their natural habitat.

Procedure:
1. Locate an area where ants are actively foraging, such as near food sources or along trails.
2. Use a magnifying glass or hand lens to closely examine the structure...
Objective: Observe ant communities in their natural habitat.

Procedure:
1. Locate an area where ants are actively foraging, such as near food sources or along trails.
2. Use a magnifying glass or hand lens to closely examine the structure of the colony and the behavior of individual ants.
3. Note the roles different ants play within the community (e.g., workers, soldiers, queens).
4. Document observations in a notebook, including details such as the time of day, weather conditions, and types of activities observed.
5. If possible, refer to Dr. W. M. Wheeler’s book *"Ants, their Structure, Development, and Behavior"* for further insight into ant behavior and biology.

Notes: This activity is ideal for developing observational skills and understanding social insect behavior.
ant observation, ant communities, entomology, insect study
Observing Insects and Butterflies for Study
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Choose a location with grass or vegetation where insects are likely to be found.
2. Sit quietly on the ground or a low surface to avoid startling insects.
3. Observe carefully using your eyes, and if available, use...
Procedure:

1. Choose a location with grass or vegetation where insects are likely to be found.
2. Sit quietly on the ground or a low surface to avoid startling insects.
3. Observe carefully using your eyes, and if available, use magnifying tools for closer inspection.
4. Identify species by noting color patterns, wing shapes, and behaviors.
5. Take notes on what you see, including time of day, weather conditions, and types of insects observed.
6. Use illustrations or field guides to help with identification, such as Comstock's "How to Know the Butterflies."
insect study, butterfly observation, entomology, field guide, natural study
Observing Wasp Nesting Behavior
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Objective: Study the nesting and foraging behaviors of wasps.

Procedure:
1. Locate areas where wasps are active, such as near paper nests, in ground burrows, or on tree trunks.
2. Use a magnifying glass or hand lens to closely examine wasp...
Objective: Study the nesting and foraging behaviors of wasps.

Procedure:
1. Locate areas where wasps are active, such as near paper nests, in ground burrows, or on tree trunks.
2. Use a magnifying glass or hand lens to closely examine wasp nests and their construction materials (e.g., paper, mud).
3. Observe the behavior of wasps as they forage for food, including how they collect prey such as grasshoppers or other insects.
4. Note any interactions between wasps and other organisms in their environment.
5. Document observations in a notebook, including details such as time of day, weather conditions, and nest locations.

Notes: This activity provides insight into the complex behaviors of wasps and their ecological roles.
wasp study, entomology, insect behavior, nesting habits
Setting Up Aquaria for Studying Aquatic Insects
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Objective: Create a home-made aquarium to observe the life histories of aquatic insects.

Procedure:
1. Select a small container with a lid, such as a glass jar or plastic aquarium.
2. Fill the container with water from a stagnant pool or...
Objective: Create a home-made aquarium to observe the life histories of aquatic insects.

Procedure:
1. Select a small container with a lid, such as a glass jar or plastic aquarium.
2. Fill the container with water from a stagnant pool or other natural body of water (ensure it is safe for handling).
3. Collect larvae or "wigglers" of mosquitoes and other aquatic insects from the same area where the water was taken.
4. Place the collected larvae into the aquaria, along with some plant matter or small stones to mimic their natural habitat.
5. Observe the development stages of the insects over time, noting changes in appearance and behavior.
6. Record observations in a notebook, including details such as temperature, water conditions, and any interactions between organisms.

Notes: This activity provides an opportunity to learn about aquatic ecosystems and insect life cycles.
aquatic insects, aquarium setup, entomology, insect study
Studying Beetle Habitats and Behavior
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Objective: Observe and understand the diverse habitats and behaviors of beetles.

Procedure:
1. Identify areas where beetles are likely to be found, such as under logs, in leaf litter, or on flowers.
2. Use a magnifying glass or hand lens to...
Objective: Observe and understand the diverse habitats and behaviors of beetles.

Procedure:
1. Identify areas where beetles are likely to be found, such as under logs, in leaf litter, or on flowers.
2. Use a magnifying glass or hand lens to closely examine beetles and their surroundings.
3. Note the specific habitat each beetle species occupies (e.g., wood-boring, flower-feeding, leaf-eating).
4. Document observations in a notebook, including details such as time of day, weather conditions, and any interactions between beetles and other organisms.
5. If possible, refer to entomology resources or the United States National Museum’s bulletin on insect classification for further study.

Notes: This activity is ideal for learning about beetle diversity and ecological roles.
beetle study, entomology, insect behavior, Coleoptera

Bleaching (5) -

Estimate Bleaching Power Using Mr. Graham's Test
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Purpose: To estimate the bleaching power of chloride of lime by measuring its reaction with protosulphate of iron.

Materials Required:
• 78 grains of powdered and dried green vitriol (protosulphate of iron)
• 2 oz. water
• Few drops of...
Purpose: To estimate the bleaching power of chloride of lime by measuring its reaction with protosulphate of iron.

Materials Required:
• 78 grains of powdered and dried green vitriol (protosulphate of iron)
• 2 oz. water
• Few drops of sulphuric or muriatic acid
• 50 grains of bleaching salt to be tested
• Tepid water
• Graduated glass vessel
• Ferricyanide of potassium solution (red prussiate of potash)

Procedure:
1. Dissolve 78 grains of green vitriol in about 2 oz. of water, acidulated with a few drops of sulphuric or muriatic acid.
2. Dissolve 50 grains of bleaching salt in about 2 oz. of tepid water using trituration in a mortar.
3. Transfer the solution to a graduated glass vessel and fill it to its zero mark with pure water.
4. Gradually pour the bleaching salt solution into the green vitriol solution until the iron is completely peroxidized.
5. To test for complete oxidation, use a solution of ferricyanide of potassium (red prussiate of potash), which gives a precipitate only with salts of protoxide of iron.
6. Spatter drops of the ferricyanide solution onto a plate and apply a drop of the iron solution to the end of a rod after each addition of the chlorine solution.
7. When no deep blue precipitate is formed, 10 grains of chlorine have been used.
8. Determine the percentage of chlorine by noting what portion of the solution was used.
chlorimetry, bleaching power, protosulphate of iron, ferricyanide solution
Bleach Cotton Using Alkaline Solution and Sulphuric Acid
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Purpose: To bleach cotton by removing impurities and earthy deposits.

Materials Required:
• Alkaline solution or steam
• Running water
• Weak sulphuric acid solution
• Water for rinsing

Procedure:
1. Scour the cotton in a slight...
Purpose: To bleach cotton by removing impurities and earthy deposits.

Materials Required:
• Alkaline solution or steam
• Running water
• Weak sulphuric acid solution
• Water for rinsing

Procedure:
1. Scour the cotton in a slight alkaline solution, or expose it to steam for initial cleaning.
2. Rinse the cotton thoroughly in running water after scouring.
3. Immerse the cotton in an alkaline lye (even with thorough rinsing), which may leave an earthy deposit on the fabric.
4. To remove this deposit, press the cotton down in a very weak solution of sulphuric acid.
5. Wash the cotton thoroughly to remove any remaining acid and prevent damage from prolonged exposure.
bleach cotton, alkaline solution, sulphuric acid, scouring
Bleach Wool Using Ammoniacal Lye and Sulphurous Acid Gas
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Purpose: To bleach wool by removing grease and using sulphurous acid gas.

Materials Required:
• Ammoniacal lye (5 measures of river water + 1 measure of stale urine)
• Wool
• Running water
• Bisulphide of carbon or benzine (optional)
-...
Purpose: To bleach wool by removing grease and using sulphurous acid gas.

Materials Required:
• Ammoniacal lye (5 measures of river water + 1 measure of stale urine)
• Wool
• Running water
• Bisulphide of carbon or benzine (optional)
• Sulphurous acid gas

Procedure:
1. Scour the wool by immersing it in an ammoniacal lye made from 5 measures of river water and 1 measure of stale urine, heated to 56 °F for about 20 minutes.
2. Drain the wool and rinse it thoroughly in running water.
3. Repeat this process a second or third time until the wool achieves the desired whiteness.
4. For valuable articles, use water slightly impregnated with soap instead of ammoniacal lye; however, this method is more expensive.
5. Alternatively, use bisulphide of carbon or benzine to clean the wool and save the fat by distilling off the solvent for reuse.
6. Use sulphurous acid gas (generated from burning sulphur in a moist room) to bleach wool and silk. Hang the moistened fabric in the room during two exposures of 24 hours each.
bleach wool, ammoniacal lye, sulphurous acid gas, scouring wool
Prepare Bleaching Salt (Chloride of Lime)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Purpose: To produce bleaching salt by reacting chlorine gas with lime.

Materials Required:
• Chlorine gas
• Moistened lime

Procedure:
1. Pass chlorine gas over moistened lime.
2. The resulting product is a moist grayish powder, soluble...
Purpose: To produce bleaching salt by reacting chlorine gas with lime.

Materials Required:
• Chlorine gas
• Moistened lime

Procedure:
1. Pass chlorine gas over moistened lime.
2. The resulting product is a moist grayish powder, soluble in 10 parts of water.
3. Any excess hydrate of lime remains undissolved after dissolution.
4. Freshly made bleaching salt may contain up to 30% chlorine; however, it often contains less than 10% due to deterioration over time.
5. It is decomposed by acids, yielding chlorine gas.
6. The composition includes hypochlorite of lime, chloride of calcium, water, and excess lime.
chloride of lime, bleaching salt, chlorine gas, lime, hypochlorite of lime
Prepare Sulphurous Acid Solution for Bleaching
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Purpose: To prepare a solution of sulphurous acid for use in bleaching.

Materials Required:
• 12 oz. of sulphuric acid
• 2 oz. of sulphur
• Glass retort
• Large bottle or series of bottles partially filled with water

Procedure:
1. Heat...
Purpose: To prepare a solution of sulphurous acid for use in bleaching.

Materials Required:
• 12 oz. of sulphuric acid
• 2 oz. of sulphur
• Glass retort
• Large bottle or series of bottles partially filled with water

Procedure:
1. Heat 12 oz. of sulphuric acid and 2 oz. of sulphur in a glass retort.
2. Collect the gas that is released by placing a large bottle (or a series of connected bottles) partially filled with water beneath the retort.
3. The gas will dissolve in the water, forming a solution of sulphurous acid.
4. This method is considered the cheapest and most effective for preparing the solution.
sulphurous acid, bleaching solution, sulphuric acid, glass retort

Dyeing Preparation (5) -

Determine Effects of Salts or Mordants on Colors
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Use different salts or mordants to observe their effects on colors.
2. This helps in understanding how various compounds influence dyeing outcomes.
mordant testing, color effects, chemical preparation
Preparation for Dyeing Fabrics
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Wool requires scouring.
2. Raw silk must be ungummed.
3. Cotton needs to be sheared, singed, and bleached.
4. Refer to the section on BLEACHING AND SCOURING for detailed instructions.
dyeing preparation, fabric treatment, scouring, bleaching
Prepare Acetate of Iron for Dyeing
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Dissolve iron scraps in acetic or pyroligneous acid.
2. This produces acetate of iron, which is preferred for dyeing vegetable fibres.
acetate of iron, dyeing, chemical preparation
Prepare Copperas for Dyeing
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Dissolve clean iron in dilute sulphuric acid.
2. Crystallize the solution to produce copperas.
3. An inferior kind can be made from pyrites, which contains iron in the form of protoxide.
4. On exposure to air, more oxygen is taken...
Procedure:

1. Dissolve clean iron in dilute sulphuric acid.
2. Crystallize the solution to produce copperas.
3. An inferior kind can be made from pyrites, which contains iron in the form of protoxide.
4. On exposure to air, more oxygen is taken up, forming sesquioxide of iron.
5. This compound acts as a powerful mordant, causing iron mould to adhere strongly to fabrics.
copperas, dyeing, mordant, chemical preparation
Prepare Madder Red Dye for Woolens
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Use 1/2 the weight of madder compared to the weight of woolens to be dyed.
2. For 16 parts of fabric, use:
- 5 parts alum
- 1 part red tartar
3. Adjusting the proportions of salts changes the color produced:
- Less alum...
Procedure:

1. Use 1/2 the weight of madder compared to the weight of woolens to be dyed.
2. For 16 parts of fabric, use:
- 5 parts alum
- 1 part red tartar
3. Adjusting the proportions of salts changes the color produced:
- Less alum and more tartar produces a red cinnamon.
- Omitting alum entirely results in a durable tawny cinnamon.
4. Boiling woolens in weak pearlash and water destroys much of the color.
5. A solution of soap discharges part of the color, leaving the remaining more beautiful.
6. Volatile alkalies enhance the red color but make it fugitive (not long-lasting).
madder red, wool dyeing, alum, tartar, chemical preparation

Painting Color Preparation (5) -

Create Chestnut-color for Painting
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• English red or red ochre of Auvergne
• Ochre de rue
• Black
• Drying nut-oil (if using English red)
• Mastic gallipot varnish
• Linseed oil
• Litharge (ground on porphyry)

Procedure:
1. To create chestnut color, mix...
Materials Required:
• English red or red ochre of Auvergne
• Ochre de rue
• Black
• Drying nut-oil (if using English red)
• Mastic gallipot varnish
• Linseed oil
• Litharge (ground on porphyry)

Procedure:
1. To create chestnut color, mix red, yellow, and black.
2. Use English red or red ochre of Auvergne, along with ochre de rue and a small amount of black for a dark chestnut shade suitable for all types of painting.
3. If using English red (which dries faster than Auvergne red), grind it with drying nut-oil when intended for varnish.
4. For ochre of Auvergne, grind it with mastic gallipot and mix with gallipot varnish.
5. Experienced artists may use linseed oil to grind dark colors if the situation allows, as it dries more quickly.
6. Nut-oil is preferable for articles without doors.
7. For oak-wood, walnut-tree, chestnut, olive, and yellow colors, add a small amount of litharge (ground on porphyry) to hasten desiccation and give the color body.
8. If covering these colors with varnish (as is common in wainscoting), mix them with essence to which a little oil has been added for better dispersion under the varnish.
chestnut color, english red, ochre de rue, black, drying nut-oil, mastic gallipot varnish, linseed oil, litharge
Create Violet-color for Varnish
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Vermilion
• Camphorated mastic varnish
• Boiled oil (1/4 part)
• Ceruse
• Prussian blue (ground in oil)

Procedure:
1. Take vermilion and grind it with camphorated mastic varnish.
2. Add a fourth part of boiled oil and...
Materials Required:
• Vermilion
• Camphorated mastic varnish
• Boiled oil (1/4 part)
• Ceruse
• Prussian blue (ground in oil)

Procedure:
1. Take vermilion and grind it with camphorated mastic varnish.
2. Add a fourth part of boiled oil and a small amount of ceruse to the mixture.
3. Incorporate a little Prussian blue that has been ground in oil.
4. Adjust the proportions based on the desired intensity of the color through experience.
5. The addition of white brightens the tint.
6. Vermilion and Prussian blue, when separated or mixed, produce hard tones; these should be softened with an intermediate substance to enhance light reflections.
violet color, vermilion, camphorated mastic varnish, boiled oil, ceruse, prussian blue
Make Red Distemper for Tiles
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Common lye or soapy water
• Water charged with a 20th part of carbonate of potash (pearlash)
• Flanders glue
• Red ochre
• Drying linseed oil
• Size
• Wax

Procedure:
1. Clean the tiles by dipping a brush in common lye,...
Materials Required:
• Common lye or soapy water
• Water charged with a 20th part of carbonate of potash (pearlash)
• Flanders glue
• Red ochre
• Drying linseed oil
• Size
• Wax

Procedure:
1. Clean the tiles by dipping a brush in common lye, soapy water, or water charged with a 20th part of carbonate of potash (pearlash), and draw it over the tiles to thoroughly cleanse them.
2. Once dry, dissolve 1/2 lb of Flanders glue in 8 pts of water.
3. While the mixture is boiling, add 2 lbs of red ochre and mix carefully.
4. Apply a first layer of this mixture to the pavement and allow it to dry.
5. Apply a second layer with drying linseed oil and a third with the same red mixed up with size.
6. Once fully dry, rub the surface with wax for finishing.
red distemper, tiles, lye, soapy water, carbonate of potash, flanders glue, red ochre, drying linseed oil, size, wax
Make a Dryer for Painting Using Litharge
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Vitreous oxide of lead (litharge)
• Linseed oil

Procedure:
1. Litharge is used to free oils from their greasy particles and impart drying qualities.
2. Red litharge should be preferred over greenish yellow as it is...
Materials Required:
• Vitreous oxide of lead (litharge)
• Linseed oil

Procedure:
1. Litharge is used to free oils from their greasy particles and impart drying qualities.
2. Red litharge should be preferred over greenish yellow as it is softer and more effective for this purpose.
