3. If boiled oil is unavailable, painters can use linseed oil not freed from its greasy particles by mixing with 2 or 3 parts of litharge (ground on porphyry with water, dried, and reduced to fine powder) per 16 parts of oil.
4. This mixture gives the color a great deal of body and dries as quickly as if mixed with drying oil.
litharge, dryer for painting, linseed oil, vitreous oxide of lead
Prepare Siccitive Oil for Painting
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Litharge (1/2 oz)
• Calcined ceruse (1/2 oz)
• Terre d'ombre (1/2 oz)
• Talc (1/2 oz)
• Linseed oil (1 lb)

Procedure:
1. Boil together for 2 hours on a slow and equal fire: 1/2 oz of litharge, 1/2 oz of calcined...
Materials Required:
• Litharge (1/2 oz)
• Calcined ceruse (1/2 oz)
• Terre d'ombre (1/2 oz)
• Talc (1/2 oz)
• Linseed oil (1 lb)

Procedure:
1. Boil together for 2 hours on a slow and equal fire: 1/2 oz of litharge, 1/2 oz of calcined ceruse, 1/2 oz of terre d'ombre, and 1/2 oz of talc with 1 lb of linseed oil.
2. Stir the mixture continuously during boiling.
3. Carefully skim and clarify the oil throughout the process.
4. The longer it is aged, the better the siccitive oil becomes.
5. Use a quarter of a pint of this dryer for every pound of color.
siccitive oil, litharge, calcined ceruse, terre d'ombre, talc, linseed oil

Pigment Preparation (5) -

Make Montpellier Yellow
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• 4 lbs. of litharge (well sifted)
• 1 lb. of sea-salt
• 4 lbs. of water
• Strong wooden spatula
• Glazed earthen vessels
• Strong linen cloth
• Press
• Flat vessels

Procedure:
1. Divide the litharge: Split into four...
Materials Required:
• 4 lbs. of litharge (well sifted)
• 1 lb. of sea-salt
• 4 lbs. of water
• Strong wooden spatula
• Glazed earthen vessels
• Strong linen cloth
• Press
• Flat vessels

Procedure:
1. Divide the litharge: Split into four equal portions and place each in a glazed earthen vessel.
2. Prepare solution: Dissolve 1 lb. of sea-salt in about 4 lbs. of water.
3. Mix with solution: Pour one-fourth of the salt solution into each vessel to form a light paste.
4. Rest and stir: Let rest for several hours until the surface begins to turn white. Stir the mixture with a strong wooden spatula at regular intervals; this prevents excessive hardness and ensures proper decomposition.
5. Dilute as needed: If consistency increases, dilute with more of the salt solution or simple water to maintain uniformity.
6. Washing and pressing: After 24 hours, wash the paste thoroughly to remove caustic soda (soda deprived of carbonic acid). Place it in strong linen cloth and press it to extract excess liquid.
7. Distribute and calcinate: Spread the remaining paste into flat vessels and expose them to heat for proper oxidation (calcination), which converts it into a solid, yellow, brilliant matter, sometimes crystallized in transverse striae.

Notes:
• Montpellier yellow can be used similarly to Naples yellow.
Montpellier yellow,litharge,sea-salt,washing,pigment preparation
Make Naples Yellow
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• 12 oz. ceruse
• 2 oz. sulphuret of antimony
• 1/2 oz. calcined alum
• 1 oz. sal ammoniac

Procedure:
1. Pulverize all the ingredients thoroughly.
2. Mix them together and place into a capsule or crucible made of...
Materials Required:
• 12 oz. ceruse
• 2 oz. sulphuret of antimony
• 1/2 oz. calcined alum
• 1 oz. sal ammoniac

Procedure:
1. Pulverize all the ingredients thoroughly.
2. Mix them together and place into a capsule or crucible made of earth.
3. Cover the capsule with the same material (earth).
4. Expose to heat: Start with a gentle heat, gradually increasing it until the capsule is moderately red.
5. Calcination time: Allow at least 5 hours of exposure to heat for complete oxidation.
6. Grinding: Once calcined, grind the resulting Naples yellow in water on a porphyry slab using an ivory spatula (iron alters the color).
7. Drying and preservation: Dry the paste thoroughly and store it for use.

Notes:
• The final product is a yellow oxide of lead and antimony.
• Adjust proportions if a golden or more fusible color is desired: increase sulphuret of antimony, muriate of ammonia, or calcined sulphate of alumina accordingly.
Naples yellow,ceruse,sulphuret of antimony,calcined alum,sal ammoniac,pigment preparation
Prepare Carmine from Cochineal
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• 36 grs. of chosen seed
• 18 grs. of autour bark
• 18 grs. of alum
• 5 grs. of pulverized cochineal
• 5 lbs. of water

Procedure:
1. Decoction preparation: Combine the chosen seed, autour bark, and alum in a...
Materials Required:
• 36 grs. of chosen seed
• 18 grs. of autour bark
• 18 grs. of alum
• 5 grs. of pulverized cochineal
• 5 lbs. of water

Procedure:
1. Decoction preparation: Combine the chosen seed, autour bark, and alum in a decoction with 5 grs. of pulverized cochineal and 5 lbs. of water.
2. Resting period: Allow the mixture to rest for 5–10 days until it produces a red fecula (carmine).
3. Drying: Once dried, the fecula weighs between 40–48 grs.

Notes:
• The remaining decoction is used for preparing carminated lakes.
carmine,cochineal,pulverized cochineal,fecula preparation
Prepare Dutch Pink from Yellow Berries
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Seeds of yellow berries (from blackthorn)
• Alum-water
• Clay or marl

Procedure:
1. Boil seeds: Boil the seeds of yellow berries in alum-water.
2. Mix with earthy matter: Combine the resulting decoction with a...
Materials Required:
• Seeds of yellow berries (from blackthorn)
• Alum-water
• Clay or marl

Procedure:
1. Boil seeds: Boil the seeds of yellow berries in alum-water.
2. Mix with earthy matter: Combine the resulting decoction with a certain quantity of clay or marl to allow the coloring part of the berries to unite with the earthy matter, giving it a beautiful yellow color.

Notes:
• This method produces a superior Dutch pink compared to that made from woad.
dutch pink,yellow berries,blackthorn,clay preparation
Prepare Superfine Carmine of Amsterdam
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• 6 buckets of rain-water
• 2 lbs. of finely-powdered cochineal
• 3 oz. of pure water
• 4 oz. of binoxalate of potash
• Syphon
• Basins
• Shelf
• Whalebone or sponge on a stick
• Shade for drying

Procedure:
1....
Materials Required:
• 6 buckets of rain-water
• 2 lbs. of finely-powdered cochineal
• 3 oz. of pure water
• 4 oz. of binoxalate of potash
• Syphon
• Basins
• Shelf
• Whalebone or sponge on a stick
• Shade for drying

Procedure:
1. Boiling: Heat 6 buckets of rain-water until it begins to boil, then add 2 lbs. of finely-powdered cochineal and continue boiling for 2 hours.
2. Add water and binoxalate: After boiling, add 3 oz. of pure water followed by 4 oz. of binoxalate of potash. Boil again for 1 minute.
3. Cooling and syphoning: Remove from heat and let the decoction stand for 4 hours. Use a syphon to draw off the supernatant liquid into numerous basins, then place them on a shelf for about 3 weeks.
4. Remove pellicle: After 3 weeks, a mouldy pellicle will form; carefully remove it using a whalebone or sponge attached to a stick.
5. Drain and dry: Run off the water through a syphon that reaches the bottom of the pans. Dry the carmine in the shade for an intensely brilliant hue.
superfine carmine,amsterdam,carmine preparation,binoxalate of potash

Crafts Tools (5) -

Prepare Aniline Black Ink
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Composition:
• Made from aniline black.

Properties:
• This ink is nearly indelible, meaning it cannot be easily removed.
• It may turn slightly yellowish when exposed to chlorine, but the color change does not result in...
Composition:
• Made from aniline black.

Properties:
• This ink is nearly indelible, meaning it cannot be easily removed.
• It may turn slightly yellowish when exposed to chlorine, but the color change does not result in complete removal.
aniline black ink, chlorine exposure, indelible ink
Prepare Blue Writing Fluids (Stark's, Stephens's, Arnold's)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Composition:
• Contains sulphate of indigo or soluble Prussian blue.

Properties:
• This ink is a true solution, not merely a suspended precipitate.
• It remains blue when first used and does not fade as easily as other inks.
blue ink, indigo sulphate, prussian blue
Prepare Carbon Inks (Coal-tar Based)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Composition:
• Made from coal-tar diluted with naphtha.

Properties:
• These inks are indelible, meaning they cannot be easily removed.
carbon ink, coal-tar, naphtha dilution
Prepare Marking Ink (Nitrate of Silver)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Composition:
• Contains nitrate of silver.

Properties:
• This ink is not indelible and can be removed using cyanide of potassium.
marking ink, silver nitrate, cyanide removal
Prepare Writing Ink (General Information)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Composition:
• Ordinary black writing ink contains a mixture of tannates and gallates of the proto and sesquioxide of iron.
• These are insoluble in water, so they are suspended using gum.
• Creosote or essential oils are...
Composition:
• Ordinary black writing ink contains a mixture of tannates and gallates of the proto and sesquioxide of iron.
• These are insoluble in water, so they are suspended using gum.
• Creosote or essential oils are added to prevent moulding.

Recommendations:
• Avoid using vinegar, logwood, and salts of copper as they may cause fading and damage to pens.
• Inks that are initially pale become darker due to oxidation, which improves their durability.
• Faded ink can be restored by brushing with an infusion of galls or a solution of ferrocyanide of potassium.
• The use of steel pens impairs the durability of any ink.
writing ink, iron compounds, gum suspension, oxidation

Sympathetic Inks (5) -

Creating Sympathetic Ink Using Chloride of Cobalt
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Dilute solution of chloride of cobalt
• Common salt (optional)
• Paper
• Heat source

Procedure:
1. Write with a dilute solution of chloride of cobalt on paper.
2. If the solution contains nickel, it will appear...
Materials Required:
• Dilute solution of chloride of cobalt
• Common salt (optional)
• Paper
• Heat source

Procedure:
1. Write with a dilute solution of chloride of cobalt on paper.
2. If the solution contains nickel, it will appear green when exposed to heat; otherwise, it will be blue.
3. Add a little common salt to the solution so that it remains more on the paper.
4. Expose the writing to gentle heat to make it visible.
5. The ink can be made visible and faded multiple times.
chloride of cobalt, nickel, sympathetic ink, blue/green writing
Creating Sympathetic Ink Using Nitrate of Bismuth and Infusion of Galls
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Solution of nitrate of bismuth
• Paper
• Feather
• Infusion of galls

Procedure:
1. Write on paper with a solution of nitrate of bismuth; the writing will be invisible.
2. Use a feather dipped in infusion of galls to...
Materials Required:
• Solution of nitrate of bismuth
• Paper
• Feather
• Infusion of galls

Procedure:
1. Write on paper with a solution of nitrate of bismuth; the writing will be invisible.
2. Use a feather dipped in infusion of galls to smear over the writing.
3. The letters will now appear brown.
4. If the use of nitrate of bismuth is concealed, this can create an impressive effect by revealing writing with just a feather and solution.
nitrate of bismuth, infusion of galls, sympathetic ink, brown writing
Creating Sympathetic Ink Using Starch and Iodide
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Weak boiled starch
• Paper
• Weak solution of iodine

Procedure:
1. Write with weak boiled starch on paper.
2. When the writing is required to appear, brush over it with a weak solution of iodine.
3. The letters will...
Materials Required:
• Weak boiled starch
• Paper
• Weak solution of iodine

Procedure:
1. Write with weak boiled starch on paper.
2. When the writing is required to appear, brush over it with a weak solution of iodine.
3. The letters will appear blue.
starch, iodide, sympathetic ink, blue writing
Creating Sympathetic Ink Using Sugar of Lead or Tartar Emetic and Sulphuretted Hydrogen Gas
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Solution of sugar of lead or tartar emetic
• Paper
• Sulphuretted hydrogen gas

Procedure:
1. Write on paper with a solution of sugar of lead or tartar emetic; the writing will be invisible.
2. Moistened the writing and...
Materials Required:
• Solution of sugar of lead or tartar emetic
• Paper
• Sulphuretted hydrogen gas

Procedure:
1. Write on paper with a solution of sugar of lead or tartar emetic; the writing will be invisible.
2. Moistened the writing and expose it to a current of sulphuretted hydrogen gas.
3. The lead will turn black, and the antimony will become orange brown.
sugar of lead, tartar emetic, sulphuretted hydrogen gas, sympathetic ink, black/orange writing
Creating Sympathetic Ink Using Sulphate of Iron and Prussiate of Potassa
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Solution of sulphate of iron
• Paper
• Feather
• Solution of prussiate of potassa

Procedure:
1. Write on paper with a solution of sulphate of iron; the writing will be invisible.
2. Rub over the writing with a feather...
Materials Required:
• Solution of sulphate of iron
• Paper
• Feather
• Solution of prussiate of potassa

Procedure:
1. Write on paper with a solution of sulphate of iron; the writing will be invisible.
2. Rub over the writing with a feather dipped in a solution of prussiate of potassa.
3. The writing will appear blue.
sulphate of iron, prussiate of potassa, sympathetic ink, blue writing

Metalworking And Gilding (5) -

Apply Size Coating for Burnishing Gold Leaf
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Gild by Burnishing:

1. Prepare a strong size by boiling down pieces of white leather or clippings of parchment until they are reduced to a stiff jelly.
2. Carefully cover the surface to be gilt with this size coating and allow it to dry...
To Gild by Burnishing:

1. Prepare a strong size by boiling down pieces of white leather or clippings of parchment until they are reduced to a stiff jelly.
2. Carefully cover the surface to be gilt with this size coating and allow it to dry completely.
3. Apply 8 to 10 additional layers, consisting of the same size mixed with fine Paris plaster or washed chalk.
4. Once sufficient layers have been applied and the entire surface is dry, apply a moderately thick layer composed of size and Armenian bole, or yellow oxide of lead.
5. While this final layer is still moist, place gold-leaf on the surface in the usual manner.
6. Press the gold leaf onto the surface using a cotton ball to ensure it adheres properly.
7. Before the size becomes completely dry, carefully burnish the parts intended to be most brilliant using an agate or a dog's tooth fixed in a handle.
gilding,burnishing,gold leaf,size coating,Armenian bole
Burnish Gilded Surfaces with Agate or Dog's Tooth
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Gild by Burnishing:

1. After applying the final layer of size and Armenian bole, press gold leaf onto the surface using a cotton ball to ensure adhesion.
2. Before the size becomes completely dry, carefully burnish the parts intended to be...
To Gild by Burnishing:

1. After applying the final layer of size and Armenian bole, press gold leaf onto the surface using a cotton ball to ensure adhesion.
2. Before the size becomes completely dry, carefully burnish the parts intended to be most brilliant using an agate or a dog's tooth fixed in a handle.
3. Avoid using shortcuts such as slightly burnishing some areas and deadening others with a brush dipped in size, as this can lead to inferior results and potential staining from water exposure.
4. This method is suitable only for indoor work, as rain or high humidity may cause the gold to peel off.
5. To clean dirty gilded surfaces, use a soft brush with hot spirit of wine or oil of turpentine.
burnishing,gold leaf,cleaning,gilding
Gild Copper Using Amalgam Method
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Gild Copper, etc., by Amalgam:

1. Immerse a very clean bright piece of copper in a diluted solution of nitrate of mercury.
2. The mercury will be precipitated due to the affinity of copper for nitric acid.
3. Spread the amalgam of gold...
To Gild Copper, etc., by Amalgam:

1. Immerse a very clean bright piece of copper in a diluted solution of nitrate of mercury.
2. The mercury will be precipitated due to the affinity of copper for nitric acid.
3. Spread the amalgam of gold rather thinly over the coat of mercury on the copper.
4. Place the piece or pieces so treated in a clean oven or furnace with no smoke.
5. Heat the copper until the temperature reaches slightly above 660°, which will cause the mercury of the amalgam to be volatilized, leaving the copper beautifully gilt.
6. In large-scale operations, furnaces are designed to condense and preserve the volatilized mercury for reuse, minimizing waste.
7. Additional contrivances may be used to prevent volatile particles of mercury from injuring the gilders.
gilding,copper,amalgam,nitrate of mercury
Gild Steel Using Ethereal Solution of Chloride of Gold
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Gild Steel:

1. Pour some of the ethereal solution of chloride of gold into a wineglass.
2. Dip the blade of a new penknife, lancet, or razor into the solution and allow it to absorb the liquid.
3. Withdraw the instrument and let the ether...
To Gild Steel:

1. Pour some of the ethereal solution of chloride of gold into a wineglass.
2. Dip the blade of a new penknife, lancet, or razor into the solution and allow it to absorb the liquid.
3. Withdraw the instrument and let the ether evaporate; the blade will be covered with a beautiful coat of gold.
4. Alternatively, use a clean rag or small piece of very dry sponge dipped in the ethereal solution to moisten the blade for the same effect.
5. To avoid evaporation loss, apply ether directly to the mouth of the phial and moisten the rag or sponge with it instead of pouring the liquid into a glass.
6. This gilded coating will remain on the steel for an extended period and help preserve it from rusting.
7. This method is commonly used to ornament swords and other cutlery, as well as to protect lancets from rust.
gilding,steel,chloride of gold,ethereal solution
Matting Process for Burnished Gilded Surfaces
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Matting:

1. Cover the parts to be burnished (in gilding on metals) with the usual guarding.
2. Fasten the piece by five iron wires to the end of an iron rod.
3. Heat the piece until the guarding becomes brown, which will cause the gilding to...
Matting:

1. Cover the parts to be burnished (in gilding on metals) with the usual guarding.
2. Fasten the piece by five iron wires to the end of an iron rod.
3. Heat the piece until the guarding becomes brown, which will cause the gilding to acquire a fine gold color.
4. Cover the piece with a mixture of common salt, nitre, and alum liquefied in their water of crystallization.
5. Carry the piece back to the fire and heat it until the saline coating is in a state of fusion and becomes nearly transparent.
6. Withdraw the piece and suddenly plunge it into cold water to remove both the coating and guarding.
7. Dip the piece in very weak nitric acid, then wash it repeatedly in several separate tubs of water.
8. Dry the piece either by exposure to air or gently wiping it with clean soft, dry linen.
matting,gilding,saline coating,nitric acid

Mushroom Foraging (5) -

Identify and Avoid Poisonous Look-Alikes of Chanterelles
Standard Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Jack-o’-lantern mushrooms (_Omphalotus illudens_):
- Have true gills, not ridges.
- Orange inner flesh or orange to rust-colored spore print.
- Usually grow in tightly compacted clusters instead of scattered...
Procedure:

1. Jack-o’-lantern mushrooms (_Omphalotus illudens_):
- Have true gills, not ridges.
- Orange inner flesh or orange to rust-colored spore print.
- Usually grow in tightly compacted clusters instead of scattered individuals.

2. Big laughing gym (_Gymnopilus spectabilis_):
- Also has true gills and an orange spore print.
- Grows in tightly compacted clusters.

Notes:
• All chanterelles are edible, but look-alikes can be poisonous.
• Be cautious when foraging to avoid mistaking these mushrooms for chanterelles.
poisonous mushrooms, mushroom identification, look-alikes, foraging safety
Identify and Avoid Comb Tooth Mushroom (_Hericium coralloides_)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Description:
- Closely related to bearded tooth but more open with branched spines.
- Also edible when young and white, but becomes sour as it ages (turns yellow).

2. Identification tips:
- Look for branched...
Procedure:

1. Description:
- Closely related to bearded tooth but more open with branched spines.
- Also edible when young and white, but becomes sour as it ages (turns yellow).

2. Identification tips:
- Look for branched spines instead of the unbranched spines of bearded tooth.

Notes:
• Comb tooth is a good edible when young but should not be consumed once it turns yellow.
• Bearded tooth and comb tooth are similar, so careful identification is necessary.
comb tooth, mushroom identification, foraging safety
Cook Bearded Tooth Mushroom (_Hericium erinaceus_)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Preparation steps:
- Slice the mushroom into pieces.
- Parboil until tender (taste a piece to test for doneness).
- Drain thoroughly.
- Serve with cheese sauce.

Notes:
• Bearded tooth mushrooms can be prepared...
Procedure:

1. Preparation steps:
- Slice the mushroom into pieces.
- Parboil until tender (taste a piece to test for doneness).
- Drain thoroughly.
- Serve with cheese sauce.

Notes:
• Bearded tooth mushrooms can be prepared in various ways, but parboiling is recommended to ensure tenderness.
bearded tooth cooking, foraging recipes
Identify Bearded Tooth Mushroom (_Hericium erinaceus_)
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Description:
- Round, beardlike mushroom with long, hanging toothlike spines.
- White when young; yellows with age.
- No stalk; spines hang evenly from a central base.
- Spore print is white.

2. Size:
- Can...
Procedure:

1. Description:
- Round, beardlike mushroom with long, hanging toothlike spines.
- White when young; yellows with age.
- No stalk; spines hang evenly from a central base.
- Spore print is white.

2. Size:
- Can grow up to 1 foot across.
- Each spine is ½–2 inches long.

3. Habitat and season:
- Found on trunks of living hardwood trees, fallen logs, or stumps.
- Grows during late summer and fall (August–November).

4. Growth pattern:
- Single to several individuals.

Notes:
• Bearded tooth mushrooms are also known as lion’s mane or hedgehog mushroom.
bearded tooth, lion's mane, foraging, edible mushrooms
Identify Chanterelle Species in Missouri
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Common chanterelle (_C. cibarius_):
- Grows up to 6 inches tall and wide.
- Has ridges on the underside of the cap that run down the stem.

2. Smooth chanterelle (_C. lateritius_):
- Looks similar to common...
Procedure:

1. Common chanterelle (_C. cibarius_):
- Grows up to 6 inches tall and wide.
- Has ridges on the underside of the cap that run down the stem.

2. Smooth chanterelle (_C. lateritius_):
- Looks similar to common chanterelle but has smooth or very shallow wrinkles on the cap instead of ridges.

3. Tiny chanterelle (_C. minor_):
- Has well-developed ridges on the underside of the cap.
- Grows up to 1 ½ inches tall.
- The stalk makes up a larger proportion of the total length compared to common chanterelle.

4. Red chanterelle or cinnabar chanterelle (_C. cinnabariunus_):
- Only reddish-orange colored chanterelle.
- Shares all other identifying characteristics: vase shape, ridges, and white center.

Notes:
• Chanterelles are usually found in large numbers of scattered individuals.
• They are especially common when the weather has been hot and humid.
chanterelle, mushroom identification, foraging, Missouri mushrooms, edible mushrooms

Herbal Gardening (5) -

Grow Aloe Vera for Medicinal Use
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Purpose: Grow Aloe Vera for topical use on burns, cuts, and inflammation.

Steps:
1. Obtain a potted Aloe Vera plant from a grocery store, nursery, or neighbor.
2. Place the plant in a sunny location with well-drained soil.
3. Water...
Purpose: Grow Aloe Vera for topical use on burns, cuts, and inflammation.

Steps:
1. Obtain a potted Aloe Vera plant from a grocery store, nursery, or neighbor.
2. Place the plant in a sunny location with well-drained soil.
3. Water sparingly; allow the soil to dry between waterings.
4. To propagate new plants:
- Wait until shoots appear at the base of the mother plant.
- Carefully separate these shoots from the roots when they are 1-2" tall.
- Pot them separately in well-drained soil and continue care as above.

Use:
• When needed, cut a leaf close to the bottom of the plant.
• Split the leaf open and apply the gel directly to burns, minor cuts, or radiation burns.
• Aloe Vera has antibacterial, wound-healing, and anti-inflammatory properties.

Notes:
• Aloe plants can live for many years with proper care.
• Keep a plant in your kitchen window for easy access.
aloe vera,growing herbs,home garden,topical medicine,burn treatment,wound healing
Grow Catnip for Medicinal Use
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Purpose: Grow Catnip to treat muscle pain, cramps, and digestive issues.

Steps:
1. Obtain catnip plants from a local nursery or start from root divisions in the spring.
2. Plant in well-drained soil with full or partial sun.
3. Space plants...
Purpose: Grow Catnip to treat muscle pain, cramps, and digestive issues.

Steps:
1. Obtain catnip plants from a local nursery or start from root divisions in the spring.
2. Plant in well-drained soil with full or partial sun.
3. Space plants about 2' apart as they can grow up to 4' tall.
4. Water moderately and allow the plant to self-seed if desired.

Harvesting:
• Harvest whole flowering tops and leaves for drying or use in infusions/tinctures.
• Can be grown from seed sown in the fall, but germination can be inconsistent.

Use:
• Use dried catnip to make water infusions or tinctures.
• Acts as a muscle relaxer, pain reliever for children, and stomach settler.

Notes:
• Catnip is easy to grow and maintain in the garden.
• Leaves can be used fresh or dried.
catnip,growing herbs,muscle pain relief,stomach remedy
Grow Comfrey for Wound Healing and Pain Relief
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Purpose: Grow Comfrey to treat wounds, ulcers, and inflammation.

Steps:
1. Obtain comfrey root pieces from a nursery or grow from root divisions in the spring.
2. Plant in well-drained soil with partial sun.
3. Water moderately; avoid...
Purpose: Grow Comfrey to treat wounds, ulcers, and inflammation.

Steps:
1. Obtain comfrey root pieces from a nursery or grow from root divisions in the spring.
2. Plant in well-drained soil with partial sun.
3. Water moderately; avoid overwatering.
4. Harvest leaves before flowering, up to 2-3 times per season, taking no more than 1/3 of the total leaves each time.

Use:
• Use fresh or dried comfrey leaves for poultices, compresses, salves, and decoctions.
• Apply as a compress or poultice to wounds, ulcers, or inflamed areas daily until resolved.
• Make water infusions for stomach and bowel discomforts.

Notes:
• Comfrey is highly effective in promoting new cell growth and reducing swelling.
• A must-have herb for any home garden due to its versatility.
comfrey,wound healing,stomach remedy,ulcer treatment
Grow Dill for Digestive and Lactation Support
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Purpose: Grow Dill to aid digestion and support lactation in nursing mothers.

Steps:
1. Obtain dill seeds or plants from a nursery or start from seed indoors or outdoors.
2. Plant in partial sun with warm temperatures.
3. Allow the plant to...
Purpose: Grow Dill to aid digestion and support lactation in nursing mothers.

Steps:
1. Obtain dill seeds or plants from a nursery or start from seed indoors or outdoors.
2. Plant in partial sun with warm temperatures.
3. Allow the plant to self-seed if not harvesting all flower heads for pickles or medicinal use.
4. Harvest leaves when the plant is about 1' tall, taking no more than 1/3 of the leaves at a time.
5. Harvest seeds in mid-summer when ripe.

Use:
• Use fresh or dried dill leaves to make water infusions or tinctures.
• Drink several cups of infusion daily for digestive relief or take a dropperful of tincture.
• Helps dispel flatulence, increase milk production in nursing mothers, and prevent breast congestion.

Notes:
• Dill is easy to grow and maintain in the garden.
• Can be used fresh or dried for various preparations.
dill,digestive aid,lactation support,growing herbs
Grow Garlic for Antibiotic and Cardiovascular Benefits
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Purpose: Grow Garlic to use as an antibiotic and cardiovascular support.

Steps:
1. Obtain garlic bulbs or cloves from a nursery or grow from seed if available.
2. Plant in well-drained soil with full sun and moderate moisture.
3. Harvest...
Purpose: Grow Garlic to use as an antibiotic and cardiovascular support.

Steps:
1. Obtain garlic bulbs or cloves from a nursery or grow from seed if available.
2. Plant in well-drained soil with full sun and moderate moisture.
3. Harvest bulbs when the top has died back, usually in late summer.
4. Cure the harvested bulbs by letting them air dry for a few days in the shade.
5. Store cured bulbs inside in a cool, dry place.

Use:
• Use fresh or cured garlic cloves to make water infusions, oil infusions, syrups, or tinctures.
• Garlic has broad-spectrum antibiotic properties and is effective for lowering blood pressure and cholesterol.

Notes:
• Garlic has been used medicinally for over 5000 years.
• Extracts from garlic have shown effectiveness against a wide range of bacteria in laboratory studies.
garlic,antibiotic properties,cardiovascular health,growing herbs

Insect Cultivation (4) -

Arranging Linen for Egg Deposition
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Arranging Linen on a Frame:

1. Frame Setup:
- Use a tressel or frame sized according to the number of moths.
- Attach two boards horizontally on each side of the length, with one edge nailed to the tressel about 5 inches...
Procedure for Arranging Linen on a Frame:

1. Frame Setup:
- Use a tressel or frame sized according to the number of moths.
- Attach two boards horizontally on each side of the length, with one edge nailed to the tressel about 5 inches and a half above the ground.
- The other edge should be slightly higher and project outward.

2. Cloth Placement:
- Lay a cloth over the frame so that it hangs evenly on both sides.
- Ensure the ends of the cloth cover the boards below to prevent soiling from moth secretions.
- The more vertical the lateral parts of the tressel, the less likely the cloth will be stained.

3. Moth Placement:
- After six hours of union, gently separate the moths and place females on the frame.
- Carry them to the tressel and position them on the cloth one over another, starting at the top and moving downward.
- Note the time each female is placed on the cloth to avoid confusion.

Notes:
• The same procedure applies to females that have mated with a virgin male or those previously paired for five hours.
• Leave the females undisturbed on the cloth for 36–40 hours to allow egg deposition.
moth,cultivation,egg laying,linen arrangement
Hatching Moths at Specific Temperatures
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Hatching and Preserving Moths:

1. Temperature Control:
- Cocoons kept in a temperature of 66° begin to be hatched after fifteen days.
- Those kept in a heat between 71° and 73°, begin to come forth after eleven or twelve...
Procedure for Hatching and Preserving Moths:

1. Temperature Control:
- Cocoons kept in a temperature of 66° begin to be hatched after fifteen days.
- Those kept in a heat between 71° and 73°, begin to come forth after eleven or twelve days.

2. Lighting Conditions:
- The room in which the moths are produced should be dark, or at least there should be only sufficient light to distinguish objects. This is an important rule and must be carefully attended to.

3. Hatching Timeline:
- The moths do not come forth in great numbers on the first or second day.
- They are chiefly hatched on the fourth, fifth, sixth, and seventh days, depending on the temperature of the cocoons.

4. Optimal Hatching Hours:
- The hours when the moths burst the cocoons in the greatest numbers are three and four hours after sunrise if the temperature is between 64° to 66°.

5. Handling Mating Moths:
- When male and female moths unite, they must be placed on frames covered with linen that can be changed when soiled.
- Much care must be taken in raising the united moths; they must be held by the wings to avoid separating them.

6. Storage of Mated Moths:
- When one small table is filled with mated moths, they are to be carried into a small room that is sufficiently airy and fresh and can be made very dark.

7. Separate Moths Handling:
- During the first hours of the day, select and carry the united moths.
- Males and females found separate on the tables should be brought into contact, placed on frames, and carried into the dark room.

8. Identifying Gender Imbalance:
- The body of the female is nearly double the size of that of the male.
- A single male beats its wings at the least approach of light.

9. Timing for Dark Room Placement:
- Note the hour when the tables containing the united moths are placed in the dark room.

10. Handling Remaining Moths:
- If any moths remain after this operation, place them in a small box with a perforated cover until the favorable moment for their union arrives.
- From time to time, check if they separate and bring them back into contact.

11. Lighting During Dark Chamber Operations:
- When performing any task in the dark chamber, admit as little light as possible—only enough to distinguish objects.
- Excessive light disturbs and troubles the moths, as it is too stimulating for them.

12. Box Usage for Males:
- Boxes are convenient for keeping quiet males that remain, preventing fine powder from adhering to their wings and causing damage.

13. Cocoon Removal:
- Remove cocoons as soon as they are pierced by the moth, as being moist, they communicate humidity to those still intact.

14. Paper Maintenance on Trays:
- When the paper on the trays becomes soiled, remove it and supply fresh paper.

15. Constant Attention Required:
- Constant attention is required throughout the day due to the continuous process of hatching and union of moths, which occasionally vary in relative proportion to one another.

16. Alternative for Egg Receiving:
- Instead of a frame, paper may be used for receiving the eggs.

17. Cocoon Quality Check:
- A few good cocoons will not produce moths due to their hardness, which prevents the moth from making a hole by which to come forth.
moth,hatching,preservation,cocoons,temperature,lighting,mating,frames,dark room
Monitoring Egg Deposition and Temperature Control
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Monitoring Egg Deposition and Maintaining Optimal Temperature:

1. Egg Inspection:
- After 36–40 hours, check the linen to see if it is adequately stocked with eggs.
- If not sufficiently covered, add more females to...
Procedure for Monitoring Egg Deposition and Maintaining Optimal Temperature:

1. Egg Inspection:
- After 36–40 hours, check the linen to see if it is adequately stocked with eggs.
- If not sufficiently covered, add more females to ensure even distribution of eggs.

2. Temperature Regulation:
- Maintain room temperature between 63° and 79° for proper egg development.
- At temperatures of 77° or 79°, the eggs will be yellow (unimpregnated) or reddish (imperfectly impregnated), which will not produce worms.

3. Egg Development Monitoring:
- Eight to ten days after egg deposition, observe for color changes:
- The initial jonquil color will shift to a reddish gray and then to a pale clayish hue.
- Eggs are lenticular in shape with slight depressions on both surfaces.

Notes:
• Some females may escape their mates before impregnation, resulting in the production of worthless eggs.
• Female cocoons are generally larger than male cocoons and lack the ring or depression found in male cocoons.
moth,cultivation,egg development,temperature control
Separating Moth Couples for Egg Laying
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Separating Moth Couples:

1. Determine Gender Ratio:
- If there are more males than females, remove the excess males.
- If there are more females, allocate additional males to them who have already mated.

2. **Separation...
Procedure for Separating Moth Couples:

1. Determine Gender Ratio:
- If there are more males than females, remove the excess males.
- If there are more females, allocate additional males to them who have already mated.

2. Separation Technique:
- When separating couples, take care not to injure the males.
- Males should remain united with females for no longer than six hours.
- After six hours, gently separate the moths by grasping them by their wings and body.

3. Handling Separated Males:
- Place all separated males on a frame.
- Select the most vigorous males to pair again with females who have not yet mated.
- Keep other vigorous males in a separate box and store them in darkness.

4. Adjustment for Male Shortage:
- If there are fewer males, allow the first union between male and female to last only five hours instead of six.
- Females can wait longer without harm; however, some eggs may not be impregnated.

5. Preparation for Egg Laying:
- Prepare a cool, dry, airy chamber with linen where the moth will deposit its eggs.

Notes:
• Males used multiple times (up to six or even thirteen couplings) have produced high-quality eggs, as demonstrated by Mr. Delonchamps in 1824.
• The union time should not be less than twenty to twenty-four hours for optimal results.
• Disunion must always be done manually and never allowed to occur spontaneously.
moth,cultivation,egg laying,separation,temperature control

Dyeing Techniques (4) -

Create Black Dye Using Iron Oxide and Tannin
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Black Dyes:

The substance employed to give a black color to cloth are red oxide of iron and tannin, also bichromate of potassa and logwood. These substances have a strong affinity for each other, and when combined assume a deep...
Black Dyes:

The substance employed to give a black color to cloth are red oxide of iron and tannin, also bichromate of potassa and logwood. These substances have a strong affinity for each other, and when combined assume a deep black color not liable to be destroyed by the action of air or light.

### Logwood Usage:
• Logwood is usually employed as an auxiliary because it communicates lustre, and adds considerably to the fulness of the black.
• It is the wood of a tree native to several of the West India islands and parts of Mexico surrounding the bay of Honduras.
• It yields its coloring matter to water. The decoction is at first a fine red, bordering on violet, but if left to itself it gradually assumes a black color.

### Effects of Chemicals on Logwood:
• Acids give it a deep red color.
• Alkalies give it a deep violet, inclining to brown.
• Sulphate of iron renders it as black as ink and occasions a precipitate of the same color.

### Pre-Dyeing Process:
• Cloth before receiving a black color is usually dyed blue, which renders the color much fuller and finer than it would otherwise be.
• If the cloth is coarse, the blue dye may be too expensive; in that case, a brown color is given by means of walnut-peels.
black dye, iron oxide, tannin, logwood, bichromate potassa, walnut peels, blue dye
Dye Linens and Cottons Red Using Madder
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Dye Linens and Cottons Red, Scarlet, etc.:

Cotton and linen are dyed red with madder. The process was borrowed from the East; hence the color is often called Adrianople, or Turkey red.

### Steps for Turkey Red on...
To Dye Linens and Cottons Red, Scarlet, etc.:

Cotton and linen are dyed red with madder. The process was borrowed from the East; hence the color is often called Adrianople, or Turkey red.

### Steps for Turkey Red on Linen/Cotton:
1. First impregnate the cloth with oil.
2. Then with galls.
3. Lastly with alum.
4. Boil the cloth for an hour in a decoction of madder, which is commonly mixed with a quantity of blood.
5. After the cloth is dyed, plunge it into a soda lye to brighten the color.

• The red given by this process is very permanent and when properly conducted is exceedingly beautiful.
• The whole difficulty consists in the application of the mordant, which is by far the most complicated employed in the whole art of dyeing.
madder, turkey red, dye linen, dye cotton, galls, alum, blood
Dye Silk Red Using Cochineal or Carthamus
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Dye Silks Red, Crimson, etc.:

Silk is usually dyed red with cochineal or carthamus, and sometimes with Brazil-wood. Kermes does not answer for silk; madder is scarcely ever used for that purpose because it does not yield...
To Dye Silks Red, Crimson, etc.:

Silk is usually dyed red with cochineal or carthamus, and sometimes with Brazil-wood. Kermes does not answer for silk; madder is scarcely ever used for that purpose because it does not yield a color bright enough. Archil is employed to give silk a bloom, but it is scarcely ever used by itself unless when the color wanted is lilac.

### Dyeing Silk Crimson:
1. Steep the silk in a solution of alum.
2. Then dye it in the usual way in a cochineal bath.

### Dyeing Silk with Carthamus:
• The colors known by the names of poppy, cherry, rose, and flesh-color are given to silk by means of carthamus.
• The process consists merely in keeping the silk as long as it extracts any color in an alkaline solution of carthamus, into which as much lemon-juice as gives it a fine cherry-red color has been poured.

### Dyeing Silk接近Scarlet:
1. First impregnate the stuff with protochloride of tin.
2. Then dye it in a bath composed of 4 parts of cochineal and 4 parts of quercitron hark.
3. To give the color more body, both the mordant and the dye may be repeated.

### Alternative Method for接近Scarlet:
1. First dye the silk in crimson.
2. Then dye it with carthamus.
3. Lastly dye it with yellow, without heat.
dye silk, cochineal, carthamus, red, crimson, scarlet, mordant, alum
Turkey-Red Dyeing Process for Cotton Goods
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Turkey-red on Cotton:

### Preparation of the Fabric:
1. Clean the cotton goods regularly with soap made from cocoanut or palm oil, using a copper proportioned to the quantity of work (from 10 yds. to 100 yds.).
2. Boil the soap in water,...
Turkey-red on Cotton:

### Preparation of the Fabric:
1. Clean the cotton goods regularly with soap made from cocoanut or palm oil, using a copper proportioned to the quantity of work (from 10 yds. to 100 yds.).
2. Boil the soap in water, softened with pearlash.
3. Add some palm-oil soap to make a soap liquor.
4. Put the cotton into this and boil for 1/2 hour.
5. Transfer the cotton to a clean tub of hot soap-liquor, handle it well, wring it out, and hang it up to dry.

### Making the Dye Paste:
1. Beat up sheep and cow-dung, ash, and water together until you have a paste.
2. Work this through a sieve into a clean copper.
3. Add one-fourth of its bulk of sweet oil.
4. Stir all together with a fire under the copper until a soap is formed.
5. Add double its bulk of water.

### Dyeing Process:
1. Handle the cotton in this mixture and allow it to dry overnight.
2. In the morning, wring out the cotton well with palm soap liquor, dry it, and clean it again in cocoanut-oil soap, drying it once more.
3. Give it a strong nut-gall liquor.
4. Then a strong, hot alum bath for an hour.
5. Return to the nut gall liquor again for an hour.
6. Then another hour in the alum; wring it out and dry it.

### Final Steps:
1. In a clean copper, put for every 3 pails of water: 1 lb. of the best madder and 1 qt. of horses', sheep's, pig's, or bullock's blood.
2. Put the copper on to a scald and handle in it (do not boil), keeping it in an hour.
3. Give it a good strong alum and hot water for 1/2 an hour.
4. Rinse in two waters and return to the blood and madder copper for half an hour.
5. Rinse, dry, and clean in very hot and strong soap liquors.
6. Dry again and give them a weak starch for a finish.
turkey red, cotton dyeing, madder, blood, alum, nut gall, soap liquor

Paint Preparation (4) -

Make White Distemper Paint
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Grind Bougival white (a type of marl or chalky clay) in water.
2. Mix it with size to create the distemper paint.
3. Brighten the color by adding a small quantity of indigo or charcoal-black.
white distemper, Bougival white, marl, chalky clay, size, indigo, charcoal-black
Make White Paint for Varnish or Oil
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Use metallic oxide to give body to the color.
2. Take ceruse (a type of white lead) and reduce it to powder.
3. Grind it with oil of pinks and 1/4 oz. of sulphate of zinc for each pound of oil.
4. Apply a second coating without...
Procedure:

1. Use metallic oxide to give body to the color.
2. Take ceruse (a type of white lead) and reduce it to powder.
3. Grind it with oil of pinks and 1/4 oz. of sulphate of zinc for each pound of oil.
4. Apply a second coating without the sulphate of zinc, allowing it to dry.
5. Cover the whole with a stratum of sandarach varnish.

Notes:
• Boiled linseed oil may be used instead of oil of pinks, but it might slightly affect the purity of the white color.

Alternative Method:
1. Prepare white using pure white oxide of lead.
2. Grind it with a little essence and mix it with oil of pinks and gallipot varnish.
3. The color may also be mixed with essence diluted with oil, without varnish for the last two coatings.
4. For a lively white, heighten the color with a small amount of Prussian blue or indigo, or with prepared black (which gives it a gray cast).
5. Pure white lead is more expensive and reserved for valuable articles.
6. If a very fine and durable white is required, grind pure white lead with a little essence and mix it with sandarach or varnish.
white paint, ceruse, oil of pinks, sulphate of zinc, sandarach varnish, white oxide of lead, Prussian blue, indigo
Prepare Drying Oil for Zinc Paint Using Manganese Oxide
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Oxide of manganese: 10 parts (for every 100 parts of oil)
• Linseed or nut-oil: 100 parts

Procedure:
1. Break the oxide of manganese into pieces about the size of peas and dry them.
2. Sieve the powder to separate it...
Materials Required:
• Oxide of manganese: 10 parts (for every 100 parts of oil)
• Linseed or nut-oil: 100 parts

Procedure:
1. Break the oxide of manganese into pieces about the size of peas and dry them.
2. Sieve the powder to separate it from larger fragments.
3. Place the manganese in a bag made of iron-wire gauze.
4. Hang the bag in an iron or copper vessel containing the oil.
5. Heat the mixture gently for 24 to 36 hours, ensuring that the oil does not boil (to avoid overflow).
6. Once the oil has acquired a reddish color, pour it into an appropriate vessel for clearing.
7. Note on Manganese Oxide Usage:
- Fresh oxide of manganese should be introduced into the oil on the second day of the process.
- The process takes longer with fresh oxide, and care must be taken to avoid overheating the oil.
8. If the oil becomes too thick due to boiling, add turpentine after it has cooled completely.
drying oil,manganese oxide,zinc paint,paint preparation
Prepare Drying Oil with Litharge and Zinc Sulphate
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Litharge: 1 1/2 oz.
• Sulphate of zinc: 3/8 oz.
• Linseed or nut-oil: 16 oz.

Procedure:
1. Combine the litharge and sulphate of zinc in a vessel.
2. Add the linseed or nut-oil to the mixture.
3. Conduct the operation...
Materials Required:
• Litharge: 1 1/2 oz.
• Sulphate of zinc: 3/8 oz.
• Linseed or nut-oil: 16 oz.

Procedure:
1. Combine the litharge and sulphate of zinc in a vessel.
2. Add the linseed or nut-oil to the mixture.
3. Conduct the operation as described in the preceding case (refer to earlier instructions for details on preparation).
4. Oil Selection Considerations:
- Use nut-oil or poppy-oil for painting articles exposed to external air or delicate painting.
- Use linseed-oil for coarse painting that is sheltered from rain and sun.
5. Fire Management:
- Avoid negligence in fire management, as it may affect the oil's color and drying quality.
- If a pellicle forms (a thin film on the surface), it will appear more slowly when using this method.
6. Alternative Method for Drying Quality:
- For each pound of linseed or nut-oil, add 3 oz. of litharge reduced to fine powder.
7. Special Considerations for Paint Preparation:
- Use this preparation for floor-cloths and large paintings with argillaceous colors (e.g., yellow and red boles, Dutch pink) where faster drying is needed.
- For metallic oxide-based paints (e.g., lead, copper), the previously mentioned doses are sufficient as these oxides contain oxygen, which enhances the oil's drying quality.
drying oil,litharge,zinc sulphate,nut-oil,paint preparation

Painting (4) -

Prepare Flaxen Gray Paint
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Needed: Ceruse (white lead), lake, Prussian blue, oil of pinks, mastic gallipot varnish, common gallipot varnish, resinous drying nut-oil

Procedure:
1. Grind ceruse separately.
2. Mix it with ground lake and Prussian blue,...
Materials Needed: Ceruse (white lead), lake, Prussian blue, oil of pinks, mastic gallipot varnish, common gallipot varnish, resinous drying nut-oil

Procedure:
1. Grind ceruse separately.
2. Mix it with ground lake and Prussian blue, adjusting the quantities to achieve the desired tone.
3. For distemper, mix the color with mastic gallipot varnish (with a little oil of pinks added) and common gallipot varnish.
4. For oil painting, grind the mixture with unprepared oil of pinks, then mix it with resinous drying nut-oil.

Notes:
• This color is brilliant and solid when applied properly.
• Before beginning work, fill nail holes in wainscoting with putty to avoid imperfections.
• Avoid sizing on firwood as it can be harmful; use a plain stratum of strong glue and water for plaster instead.
flaxen gray,ceruse,lake,Prussian blue,varnish,oil painting,paint preparation
Prepare Light Gray Paint for Distemper
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Needed: Ceruse, lamp-black

Procedure:
1. Mix ceruse with a small quantity of lamp-black to create light gray.
2. Adjust the shade by varying the amount of lamp-black added (more black = darker gray).
3. If intended for...
Materials Needed: Ceruse, lamp-black

Procedure:
1. Mix ceruse with a small quantity of lamp-black to create light gray.
2. Adjust the shade by varying the amount of lamp-black added (more black = darker gray).
3. If intended for distemper, mix the color with water.
4. For oil painting, grind the mixture with nut-oil or oil of pinks, and add essence to the oil if used as varnish.

Notes:
• This color is durable and pure when mixed with camphorated mastic varnish.
• Gallipot varnish makes it solid enough to withstand being struck with a hammer after the first coat.
• For the final coating, use sandarach varnish or camphorated varnish.
• The darkest gray should be applied with spirituous sandarac varnish.
ceruse,lamp-black,distemper,oil painting,varnish,paint preparation
Prepare Oak-wood Color Paint
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Needed: Ceruse, ochre de rue, umber earth, yellow de Berri

Procedure:
1. Combine three-fourths of ceruse with one-fourth each of ochre de rue, umber earth, and yellow de Berri.
2. Adjust the proportions of ochre de rue, umber...
Materials Needed: Ceruse, ochre de rue, umber earth, yellow de Berri

Procedure:
1. Combine three-fourths of ceruse with one-fourth each of ochre de rue, umber earth, and yellow de Berri.
2. Adjust the proportions of ochre de rue, umber earth, and yellow de Berri to achieve the desired tint.
3. This mixture is suitable for distemper, varnish, and oil painting.

Notes:
• The resulting color mimics oak wood's natural appearance.
• Ensure all ingredients are thoroughly mixed.
ceruse,ochre de rue,umber earth,yellow de Berri,oak-wood color,paint preparation
Prepare Walnut-wood Color Paint
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Needed: Ceruse, ochre de rue, umber earth, red ochre, yellow ochre de Berri, drying nut-oil, gallipot varnish

Procedure:
1. Combine a given quantity of ceruse with half that amount of ochre de rue, a small amount of umber earth,...
Materials Needed: Ceruse, ochre de rue, umber earth, red ochre, yellow ochre de Berri, drying nut-oil, gallipot varnish

Procedure:
1. Combine a given quantity of ceruse with half that amount of ochre de rue, a small amount of umber earth, red ochre, and yellow ochre de Berri.
2. Adjust the quantities to achieve the desired tone for distemper, varnish, or oil painting.
3. For varnish, grind the mixture with a little drying nut-oil and mix it with gallipot varnish.
4. For oil painting, grind the mixture with fat oil of pinks added to drying oil or essence, then mix it with plain drying oil or resinous drying oil.

Notes:
• This color mimics walnut wood's natural appearance.
• Ensure all ingredients are thoroughly mixed and applied in thin layers for best results.
ceruse,ochre de rue,umber earth,red ochre,yellow ochre de Berri,walnut-wood color,paint preparation

Artistic Chemistry (4) -

Extracting Remaining Ultramarine from Paste
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Mix with Linseed Oil:
- Take the paste that has been kneaded in water and mix it with four times its weight of linseed oil.

2. Prepare the Vessel:
- Pour the mixture into a conical glass vessel.

3. **Use Alembic...
Procedure:

1. Mix with Linseed Oil:
- Take the paste that has been kneaded in water and mix it with four times its weight of linseed oil.

2. Prepare the Vessel:
- Pour the mixture into a conical glass vessel.

3. Use Alembic Setup:
- Expose the vessel to the balneum maria (a water bath) of an alembic.

4. Heat the Mixture:
- Keep the water in the alembic at a state of ebullition for several hours.

5. Decant the Oil:
- The liquidity allows ultramarine to separate itself from the mixture.
- Decant the supernatant oil.

6. Repeat Immersion:
- Repeat the immersion in oil to remove any remaining resinous parts adhering to the ultramarine.

7. Final Boiling Step:
- Boil the mixture in water to separate the oil completely.

8. Collect Ultramarine:
- The resulting deposit is ultramarine, though it may be of lower quality compared to that extracted by the first washing.
ultramarine extraction, remaining pigment, linseed oil, alembic
Extracting Ultramarine from Lapis Lazuli Using Porphyry and Wax Paste
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Prepare the Blue Parts:
- Separate the blue parts of lapis lazuli.
- Reduce them to an impalpable powder on a piece of porphyry.
- Besprinkle the powder with linseed oil.

2. Create the Paste Mixture:
- Combine...
Procedure:

1. Prepare the Blue Parts:
- Separate the blue parts of lapis lazuli.
- Reduce them to an impalpable powder on a piece of porphyry.
- Besprinkle the powder with linseed oil.

2. Create the Paste Mixture:
- Combine equal parts of yellow wax, pine resin, and colophonium (8 oz. of each).
- Add 1/2 oz. of linseed oil, 2 oz. of oil of turpentine, and an equivalent amount of mastic.

3. Mix with Lapis Lazuli:
- Take 4 parts of the mixture above and 1 part of lapis lazuli ground with oil on porphyry.
- Mix the whole warm together.

4. Digest the Mixture:
- Allow the mixture to digest for a month.

5. Knead in Warm Water:
- Knead the mixture thoroughly in warm water until the blue part separates from it.

6. Decant the Liquor:
- After several days, decant the liquor. The resulting ultramarine is exceedingly beautiful.

Notes:
• This method differs slightly from other processes by the preliminary preparation of Kinckel, which involves bringing lapis lazuli to a red heat and immersing it in vinegar. However, this may be harmful to certain kinds of azure stone.
• Morgraff's observations suggest that calcination (heating) can affect the purity of ultramarine.
ultramarine, lapis lazuli, extraction, wax paste, porphyry
Preparing Artificial Ultramarine Using Chemicals
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Combine Ingredients in Crucible:
- Place 2 parts of sulfur and 1 part of dry carbonate of soda into a Hessian crucible.
- Cover the crucible securely.

2. Apply Heat Until Fusion:
- Apply heat until the mixture...
Procedure:

1. Combine Ingredients in Crucible:
- Place 2 parts of sulfur and 1 part of dry carbonate of soda into a Hessian crucible.
- Cover the crucible securely.

2. Apply Heat Until Fusion:
- Apply heat until the mixture fuses together.

3. Add Silicate and Aluminate Mixtures:
- Gradually sprinkle in a mixture of silicate of soda (containing 72 parts of silica) and aluminate of soda (containing 70 parts of alumina).

4. Calcine the Mixture:
- Calcine (heat strongly) for 1 hour.

5. Wash in Pure Water:
- Wash the resulting mass thoroughly in pure water to obtain artificial ultramarine.
artificial ultramarine, chemical synthesis, silicate of soda
Testing for Adulteration in Ultramarine
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Heat Test:
- Take a small quantity of ultramarine and subject it to a red heat in a crucible.

2. Observe Color Change:
- If the ultramarine remains unchanged in color, it is pure.
- If it turns blackish or paler,...
Procedure:

1. Heat Test:
- Take a small quantity of ultramarine and subject it to a red heat in a crucible.

2. Observe Color Change:
- If the ultramarine remains unchanged in color, it is pure.
- If it turns blackish or paler, it is adulterated.

3. Comparison Test:
- Compare the tested sample with an unexposed portion of the same material to ensure accuracy.

4. Alternative Test Using Oil:
- Treat a small amount of ultramarine with oil; if it assumes a brown tint, this may indicate adulteration or low quality.

5. Visual Inspection:
- If ultramarine is mixed with azure (a less valuable blue pigment), it will appear duller than pure ultramarine, similar to sand ground on porphyry.
ultramarine adulteration, purity test, crucible heating

Lacquer Preparation (4) -

Prepare Gold-Colored Lacquer for Brass Watchcases
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:

• Seed-lac: 6 oz.
• Amber: 2 oz.
• Gum guttae: 2 oz.
• Extract of red sandalwood in water: 24 grs.
• Dragon's blood: 60 grs.
• Oriental saffron: 36 grs.
• Pounded glass: 4 oz.
• Pure alcohol: 36 oz.

Procedure:

1. Grind...
Ingredients:

• Seed-lac: 6 oz.
• Amber: 2 oz.
• Gum guttae: 2 oz.
• Extract of red sandalwood in water: 24 grs.
• Dragon's blood: 60 grs.
• Oriental saffron: 36 grs.
• Pounded glass: 4 oz.
• Pure alcohol: 36 oz.

Procedure:

1. Grind amber, seed-lac, gum guttae, and dragon's blood on a piece of porphyry until finely powdered.
2. Mix the ground resins with pounded glass.
3. Prepare an infusion of oriental saffron in alcohol, and prepare an extract of red sandalwood in water.
4. Combine all ingredients by adding the prepared infusions to the mixture of resins and glass.
5. Follow the directions for completing varnish as previously described.

Application:
• Heat metal articles (such as brass watchcases) before applying this lacquer.
• Immerse them in packets or apply directly for a gold-colored finish.

Notes:
• The tint of the varnish can be varied by adjusting the quantities of coloring substances.
gold lacquer,brass watchcases,varnish,amber,saffron,dragon's blood
Prepare Lacquer of a Less Drying Quality
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:

• Seed-lac: 4 oz.
• Sandarac or mastic: 4 oz.
• Dragon's blood: 1/2 oz.
• Terra merita: 30 grs.
• Gum guttae: 30 grs.
• Pounded glass: 5 oz.
• Clear turpentine: 8 oz.
• Essence of turpentine: 32 oz.

Procedure:

1. Extract a...
Ingredients:

• Seed-lac: 4 oz.
• Sandarac or mastic: 4 oz.
• Dragon's blood: 1/2 oz.
• Terra merita: 30 grs.
• Gum guttae: 30 grs.
• Pounded glass: 5 oz.
• Clear turpentine: 8 oz.
• Essence of turpentine: 32 oz.

Procedure:

1. Extract a tincture from the coloring substances (terra merita, gum guttae) by infusion in essence of turpentine or another suitable solvent.
2. Mix seed-lac, sandarac or mastic, and dragon's blood with pounded glass.
3. Add the tincture to the mixture, following the directions for compound mastic varnish.

Application:
• This lacquer is suitable for use on metals (copper, brass, hammered tin) and wooden boxes or furniture.
• It imparts a more agreeable color and enhances luster, resembling that of precious metals.
• Used in the manufacturing of gilt leather, papier-mâché, and confection d'hyacinthe for decorative purposes.

Notes:
• Artists can vary the tint by adjusting the amounts of coloring agents or adding anatto to mixtures containing dragon's blood, saffron, etc.
less drying lacquer,varnish,seed-lac,sandarac,mastic,dragon's blood
Prepare Lacquers of Various Tints
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:

• Gum guttae: 4 oz.
• Dragon's blood: 4 oz.
• Annatto: 1 oz.
• Essence of turpentine: sufficient quantity for each infusion.

Procedure:

1. Infuse gum guttae, dragon's blood, and annatto separately in **essence of...
Ingredients:

• Gum guttae: 4 oz.
• Dragon's blood: 4 oz.
• Annatto: 1 oz.
• Essence of turpentine: sufficient quantity for each infusion.

Procedure:

1. Infuse gum guttae, dragon's blood, and annatto separately in essence of turpentine (4 oz. of each ingredient per 32 oz. of essence).
2. Allow the infusions to sit for 15 days in the sun.
3. After infusion, pour a measured quantity of each liquor into a flask.
4. By varying the proportions of the infusions, different shades and tints can be achieved.

Application:
• These infusions may be used to alter the color of alcoholic varnishes.
• Saffron and red sandalwood extract are useful for imitating gold tones in other tinctures.

Notes:
• This method allows artists to create a wide range of colors by adjusting the ratios of ingredients.
various tint lacquers,essence of turpentine,gum guttae,dragon's blood,annatto
Prepare Philosophical Instruments Lacquer
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:

• Gum guttae (gamboge): 3/4 oz.
• Gum sandarac: 2 oz.
• Gum elemi: 2 oz.
• Dragon's blood (best quality): 1 oz.
• Seed-lac: 1 oz.
• Terra merita: 3/4 oz.
• Oriental saffron: 2 grs.
• Pounded glass: 3 oz.
• Pure alcohol: 20...
Ingredients:

• Gum guttae (gamboge): 3/4 oz.
• Gum sandarac: 2 oz.
• Gum elemi: 2 oz.
• Dragon's blood (best quality): 1 oz.
• Seed-lac: 1 oz.
• Terra merita: 3/4 oz.
• Oriental saffron: 2 grs.
• Pounded glass: 3 oz.
• Pure alcohol: 20 oz.

Procedure:

1. Prepare a tincture of terra merita and oriental saffron by infusing them in pure alcohol for 24 hours, or expose them to the heat of the sun during summer.
2. Strain the tincture through clean linen cloth, squeezing it thoroughly to extract all liquid.
3. Pound and mix dragon's blood, gum elemi, seed-lac, and gum guttae with pounded glass.
4. Pour the strained tincture over the mixture of resins and glass.
5. Follow the directions for making compound varnish as previously described.

Application:
• Apply this lacquer to philosophical instruments for protection and color modification.
• It may also be used on cast or molded articles in furniture decoration.

Notes:
• If dragon's blood is of high quality, it may produce a too intense color; reduce its quantity as needed, along with other coloring agents.
philosophical instruments,lacquer,varnish,gum guttae,dragon's blood,terra merita,saffron

Art Supplies (4) -

Prepare Alternative Mordants for Gilding and Bronzing
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Option 1:
• Use Jew's pitch and drying oil diluted with essence of turpentine.

Option 2:
• Mix colors (yellow, red) into the mordant to assist in achieving desired tones on gold.

Option 3:
• Use fat varnish with a little red oxide of...
Option 1:
• Use Jew's pitch and drying oil diluted with essence of turpentine.

Option 2:
• Mix colors (yellow, red) into the mordant to assist in achieving desired tones on gold.

Option 3:
• Use fat varnish with a little red oxide of lead (minium).

Option 4:
• Use thick glue dissolved with honey, known as *batture*, for heightening the color of gold and strong adhesion to gold leaf.

Option 5:
• Boil oil in a pan until black smoke is released. Set it on fire briefly, then extinguish. Pour into a heated bottle and add essence of turpentine. This mordant dries quickly and adheres strongly to wood, metals, and other surfaces.
mordant,varnish,gilding,batture,bronzing
Prepare Composition for Colored Drawings and Prints Resembling Oil Paintings
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 1 oz Canada balsam
• 2 oz spirit of turpentine

Procedure:
1. Mix the Canada balsam with the spirit of turpentine.
2. Before applying, size the drawing or print with a solution of isinglass in water.
3. Allow it to dry...
Ingredients:
• 1 oz Canada balsam
• 2 oz spirit of turpentine

Procedure:
1. Mix the Canada balsam with the spirit of turpentine.
2. Before applying, size the drawing or print with a solution of isinglass in water.
3. Allow it to dry completely.
4. Apply the varnish using a camel's-hair brush.
varnish,drawings,prints,oil painting
Prepare Mordant Varnish for Gilding
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 1 oz mastic
• 1 oz gum sandarach
• 1/2 oz gum guttae
• 1/4 oz turpentine
• 6 oz essence of turpentine

Alternative: Some artists substitute 1 oz of essence of lavender for the turpentine, which makes the composition less...
Ingredients:
• 1 oz mastic
• 1 oz gum sandarach
• 1/2 oz gum guttae
• 1/4 oz turpentine
• 6 oz essence of turpentine

Alternative: Some artists substitute 1 oz of essence of lavender for the turpentine, which makes the composition less drying.

Application Notes:
• The mordant should be neither too thick nor too fluid to ensure delicacy in strokes.
• It must not dry until the artist completes their design.
• This composition is primarily used for gold leaf on various grounds.
mordant,varnish,gilding,art supplies
Prepare Varnish to Color Baskets
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 2 oz sealing-wax (red, black, or white, depending on desired color)
• 1 oz spirit of wine

Procedure:
1. Pound the wax fine and sift it through a fine lawn sieve until extremely fine.
2. Place the wax in a large phial with the...
Ingredients:
• 2 oz sealing-wax (red, black, or white, depending on desired color)
• 1 oz spirit of wine

Procedure:
1. Pound the wax fine and sift it through a fine lawn sieve until extremely fine.
2. Place the wax in a large phial with the spirit of wine.
3. Shake the mixture frequently and let it stand near the fire for 48 hours.
4. Use a brush to apply the varnish all over the baskets.
5. Allow them to dry, then repeat the process for a second application.
varnish,baskets,coloring

Material Processing (4) -

Creating Buttons from Talc or Steatite
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Talc or steatite
• Kiln or furnace capable of reaching nearly white heat
• Emery, tripoli, jeweller's putty
• Mineral or organic coloring agents (e.g., chloride of gold for purple, nitrate of silver for...
Materials Required:
• Talc or steatite
• Kiln or furnace capable of reaching nearly white heat
• Emery, tripoli, jeweller's putty
• Mineral or organic coloring agents (e.g., chloride of gold for purple, nitrate of silver for black)

Procedure:
1. Shape the talc or steatite into buttons or cameos.
2. Heat the shaped pieces in a kiln or furnace at nearly white heat for several hours to achieve extreme hardness and durability.
3. After cooling, polish the surface using emery, tripoli, and jeweller's putty.
4. Apply color by immersing the buttons in solutions of mineral or organic matter:
- Chloride of gold produces a purple hue.
- Nitrate of silver results in a black coloration.
5. Exposure to a reducing flame can enhance the brilliancy of the applied colors.

Result:
Buttons and cameos made from talc or steatite will be extremely hard, capable of striking fire with flints, and resistant to even the best tempered files.
buttons, steatite, talc, calcination, polishing, coloring agents
Bleach Yellowed Ivory Knife Handles
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Ingredients:
- A watery solution of sulphurous acid.
2. Steps:
- Soak yellowed ivory knife-handles in the sulphurous acid solution for 2–4 hours until they become white again.
3. Warning:
- Using **gaseous...
Procedure:

1. Ingredients:
- A watery solution of sulphurous acid.
2. Steps:
- Soak yellowed ivory knife-handles in the sulphurous acid solution for 2–4 hours until they become white again.
3. Warning:
- Using gaseous sulphurous acid may cause the ivory to crack.
bleaching ivory,sulphurous acid,yellowed knife handles
Make Transparent and Flexible Ivory
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Ingredients:
- Liquid phosphoric acid (specific gravity 1.131).
- Ivory cut into thin pieces (not thicker than the twentieth part of an inch).
2. Steps:
- Immerse the ivory in liquid phosphoric acid until it...
Procedure:

1. Ingredients:
- Liquid phosphoric acid (specific gravity 1.131).
- Ivory cut into thin pieces (not thicker than the twentieth part of an inch).
2. Steps:
- Immerse the ivory in liquid phosphoric acid until it becomes transparent.
- Remove the ivory from the bath and wash it thoroughly with water.
- Dry it using a clean linen cloth.
3. Drying:
- The ivory dries naturally in the air without heat application.
4. Reversibility:
- It softens again when exposed to warm water.
transparent ivory,phosphoric acid,flexible material
Make Turpentine Varnish for General Use
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Ingredients:
- 1 gallon of oil of turpentine.
- 5 lbs. of powdered resin.
2. Steps:
- Mix the turpentine and powdered resin in a tin can.
- Place the can on a stove and let it boil for **30...
Procedure:

1. Ingredients:
- 1 gallon of oil of turpentine.
- 5 lbs. of powdered resin.
2. Steps:
- Mix the turpentine and powdered resin in a tin can.
- Place the can on a stove and let it boil for 30 minutes.
3. Cooling and Use:
- Allow the mixture to cool before using it as varnish.
turpentine,varnish,resin,boiling

Colored Inks (4) -

Preparing Brown Ink Using Powdered Catechu and Bichromate of Potassa
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Powdered catechu: 4 parts
• Water: 60 parts
• Bichromate of potassa: 1 part in 16 parts water

Procedure:
1. Digest powdered catechu with 60 parts of water for several hours.
2. Filter the mixture to obtain a clear...
Materials Required:
• Powdered catechu: 4 parts
• Water: 60 parts
• Bichromate of potassa: 1 part in 16 parts water

Procedure:
1. Digest powdered catechu with 60 parts of water for several hours.
2. Filter the mixture to obtain a clear liquid.
3. Add sufficient of a solution of bichromate of potassa (1 part in 16 parts water) to achieve the desired color and consistency.
4. The resulting mixture is brown ink suitable for writing or artistic purposes.
powdered catechu, bichromate of potassa, brown ink preparation
Preparing Gold Ink Using Mosaic Gold and Gum Arabic
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Mosaic gold: 2 parts
• Gum Arabic: 1 part
• Water

Procedure:
1. Rub mosaic gold and gum Arabic together with water until the mixture is reduced to a proper condition.
2. The resulting mixture can be used as **gold...
Materials Required:
• Mosaic gold: 2 parts
• Gum Arabic: 1 part
• Water

Procedure:
1. Rub mosaic gold and gum Arabic together with water until the mixture is reduced to a proper condition.
2. The resulting mixture can be used as gold ink for writing or artistic purposes.
mosaic gold, gum arabic, gold ink preparation
Preparing Silver Ink Using Silver Foil and Sulphate of Potassa
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Silver foil
• Sulphate of potassa
• Mortar
• Mucilage (gum Arabic and water mixture)

Procedure:
1. Triturate equal parts of silver foil and sulphate of potassa in a mortar until reduced to a fine powder.
2. Wash out...
Materials Required:
• Silver foil
• Sulphate of potassa
• Mortar
• Mucilage (gum Arabic and water mixture)

Procedure:
1. Triturate equal parts of silver foil and sulphate of potassa in a mortar until reduced to a fine powder.
2. Wash out the salt from the mixture.
3. Mix the residue with a mucilage made by combining equal parts of gum Arabic and water.
4. The resulting mixture can be used as silver ink.
silver foil, sulphate of potassa, silver ink preparation, mucilage
Preparing Yellow Ink Using Gamboge, Alum, and Gum Arabic
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Gamboge: 1 part (or 1 1/2 parts)
• Alum: 1/2 part
• Gum Arabic: 1 part
• Acetic acid: 1 part
• Water: 24 parts

Procedure:
1. Macerate gamboge, alum, and gum arabic in acetic acid.
2. Add water to the mixture until it...
Materials Required:
• Gamboge: 1 part (or 1 1/2 parts)
• Alum: 1/2 part
• Gum Arabic: 1 part
• Acetic acid: 1 part
• Water: 24 parts

Procedure:
1. Macerate gamboge, alum, and gum arabic in acetic acid.
2. Add water to the mixture until it reaches a total volume of 24 parts.
3. The resulting solution is yellow ink, suitable for writing or artistic purposes.
