Entomology (2) -

Identify Insects' Feet Structures
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

Examine the various forms of insects' feet as illustrated in the accompanying diagram.

• Look for adhesive disks or suckers, which are used by some insects to cling to surfaces.
• Note the differences in structure between...
Procedure:

Examine the various forms of insects' feet as illustrated in the accompanying diagram.

• Look for adhesive disks or suckers, which are used by some insects to cling to surfaces.
• Note the differences in structure between species, such as:
- Insects with flat, broad feet (used for walking on smooth surfaces)
- Insects with hooked or clawed feet (used for grasping or climbing)
• Use this knowledge to identify insect species based on their foot morphology.
insect anatomy,feet structure,identification
Identify Various Forms of Antennae
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

Examine the various forms of antennae as illustrated in the accompanying diagram.

• Look for differences in shape and structure, such as:
- Filiform: Thread-like antennae (common in many insects)
- Clavate: Club-shaped...
Procedure:

Examine the various forms of antennae as illustrated in the accompanying diagram.

• Look for differences in shape and structure, such as:
- Filiform: Thread-like antennae (common in many insects)
- Clavate: Club-shaped or thickened at the end (found in beetles)
- Bidentate: Forked or two-pronged (seen in some flies)
• Note any specialized structures, such as sensory organs or receptors.
• Use this information to help identify insect species based on their antennae morphology.
insect anatomy,antennae structure,identification

Distillation Processes (2) -

Lavender Water Preparation
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Lavender Water

1. Pour 30 gallons of the best wine spirit into a copper still placed in a hot-water bath over a clear but steady fire.
2. Add 6 lbs. of large, fresh lavender flowers, having removed all stalks and green...
Procedure for Lavender Water

1. Pour 30 gallons of the best wine spirit into a copper still placed in a hot-water bath over a clear but steady fire.
2. Add 6 lbs. of large, fresh lavender flowers, having removed all stalks and green leaves to avoid a woody or faint smell.
3. Do not add any water to the still.
4. Close all junctures well and let the spirits and flowers stand in a state of digestion for 24 hours.
5. After 24 hours, begin distilling with a gentle fire until 25–26 gallons are collected.
6. Immediately pour the distilled liquid into a copper vessel designated for keeping; avoid using wooden vessels or cans as they may absorb and lose valuable oils and spirits.
7. Once distillation is complete, extinguish the fire and allow the remaining spirits and flowers to remain in the still until the next day.
8. After 4–5 days, add 10 oz. of true English oil of lavender to the distilled liquid.
9. Mix the entire contents well by drawing out 1 or 2 gallons and returning them to the vessel, repeating this process 10–12 times.
10. Seal the vessel tightly and leave it undisturbed for at least one month.
lavender water, lavender flowers, wine spirit, copper still, hot-water bath, distillation, oil of lavender, storage
Second Distillation Procedure
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Second Distillation of Spirits

1. Return the spirits drawn off into the still and add 10 or 12 gallons of water.
2. Prepare the following ingredients by bruising and mixing them in an iron mortar until they are about the size...
Procedure for Second Distillation of Spirits

1. Return the spirits drawn off into the still and add 10 or 12 gallons of water.
2. Prepare the following ingredients by bruising and mixing them in an iron mortar until they are about the size of small peas:
- 14 oz. of nutmegs
- 4 oz. of gloves
- 12 oz. of cinnamon bark
- 8 oz. of pimento
- 40 oz. of cassia-lignum
3. If any dust is produced, sift it from the ingredients before use.
4. Prepare the following additional ingredients by breaking and bruising them with minimal dust:
- 40 oz. of storax
- 40 oz. of gum Benjamin
- 44 oz. of labdanum
- 40 venellios
5. Place all the dust from these ingredients, along with the previously prepared ones, into a coarse muslin bag.
6. Hang the muslin bag in the still so that the liquor can extract all its virtues during distillation.
7. Allow the mixture to remain in the cold liquor for 48 hours, ensuring the still is properly sealed and luted as before.
8. After 48 hours, light a fire and begin distilling slowly until 26 gallons are collected.
9. Mix all the different runnings together in a copper vessel designated exclusively for this purpose.

Additional Ingredients to Add Post-Distillation
10. In a copper vessel that can hold 40 gallons, add:
- 10 oz. of spirit of musk
- 10 oz. of spirit of ambergris
- 1/2 oz. of true oil of lavender
- 1/2 oz. of essence of bergamot
- 1/2 oz. of oil of rhodium
11. Add the following liquids:
- 6 gallons of orange-flower water
- 8 gallons of recently made rose-water
12. Stir the mixture well together.
13. Add a quart of milk that has stood overnight with the cream removed, and mix thoroughly.
14. Seal the vessel tightly until it is ready for use.

Storage and Handling
15. The jar should have a lock-cock soldered into it, placed 2 inches from the bottom to allow impurities like milk to settle at the bottom.
16. If made in spring during fair weather, the mixture will be fully fined within a month if not disturbed.
17. When ready for use, draw off the liquid by checking with a glass to ensure that milk and impurities have settled at the bottom.
18. Transfer the mixture into clean, well-seasoned stone or glass bottles, or another copper jar.
19. Avoid drawing off the mixture during rainy or cloudy weather, as this may cause the milk to rise.
20. In warm weather, keep it cool; in winter, keep it as warm as possible.
21. If distilled in winter, warm the jars to ensure the honey water is fined within 5–6 months.
22. This honey-water can be stored for up to 30 years.

Uses of Second Distillation Ingredients
23. The ingredients from the second distillation are more valuable than those from the first and should be dried with extra care.
24. They are useful in making gross powders, sweet bags, and brown perfumed balls when made into a fine powder.
25. These can also be used to make pastils that burn or spirit of Benjamin with fresh ingredients.
second distillation, spirits, nutmegs, cinnamon bark, pimento, cassia-lignum, storax, gum Benjamin, labdanum, venellios, lavender oil, rose water, orange flower water, honey-water storage, distillation procedure

Fermentation (2) -

Prepare Vinegar (Impure Dilute Acetic Acid)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Any substance capable of fermentation or containing alcohol
• Water

Procedure:
1. Use any weak alcoholic liquor, such as wine, cider, beer, sugar and water, whiskey and water, or juice of plants and vegetables.
2. For the...
Ingredients:
• Any substance capable of fermentation or containing alcohol
• Water

Procedure:
1. Use any weak alcoholic liquor, such as wine, cider, beer, sugar and water, whiskey and water, or juice of plants and vegetables.
2. For the slow method, use a wash with 5 to 10% alcohol content; stronger liquors will ferment more slowly.
3. Maintain a temperature between 74° to 86°F for optimal fermentation.
4. Use sour beer, wine, or cider instead of strong liquors for better results.
5. Avoid adding sugar, honey, or other sweeteners as they may cause the vinegar to spoil.
6. The best ferment is old vinegar in a cask that has previously held good vinegar.
7. Alternatively, use bread soaked in brewer's yeast, sour dough, or wheat and rye flour soaked in cream of tartar and vinegar, but these are less reliable and may cause the vinegar to spoil more easily.
vinegar, fermentation, natural remedies
Slow Method of Making Vinegar
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Weak alcoholic wash (5 to 10% alcohol content)
• Old cask or fermenting vessel
• Ferment (old vinegar, bread soaked in brewer's yeast, sour dough, etc.)

Procedure:
1. Prepare a weak alcoholic wash with an alcohol content...
Ingredients:
• Weak alcoholic wash (5 to 10% alcohol content)
• Old cask or fermenting vessel
• Ferment (old vinegar, bread soaked in brewer's yeast, sour dough, etc.)

Procedure:
1. Prepare a weak alcoholic wash with an alcohol content between 5 to 10%, as measured by taste.
2. Place the wash into a cask that is painted black for better heat absorption during cool weather.
3. Leave the bung out and cover the bung-hole with a piece of slate.
4. Allow the mixture to ferment for approximately 4 weeks at temperatures between 74° to 86°F.
5. The acetification process is complete when the lower portion of the liquid begins to rise, indicating successful conversion to vinegar.
6. Lower temperatures will slow down the fermentation process.
vinegar, fermentation, natural remedies

Perfumery (2) -

Obtaining Essential Oils Through Distillation and Enfleurage
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:

1. Essential or volatile oils are obtained by distillation.
2. For delicate perfumes, macerate the flowers in warm oil or cold lard (enfleurage).
3. Extract the oil from the grease using alcohol.
4. Alternatively, macerate the flowers or...
Steps:

1. Essential or volatile oils are obtained by distillation.
2. For delicate perfumes, macerate the flowers in warm oil or cold lard (enfleurage).
3. Extract the oil from the grease using alcohol.
4. Alternatively, macerate the flowers or other odorous bodies in alcohol to obtain the essential oils.
essential oils,distillation,enfleurage,perfume making
Preparing J. Maria Farina Cologne
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:

1. Dissolve 4 oz. of benzoin in alcohol.
2. Add 8 oz. of essence of lavender, 4 oz. of essence of rosemary, and 325 qts. of alcohol at 75° to the solution.
3. Successively add 21 oz. of neroli, 21 oz. of petit grain, 21 oz. of cedrat, 2...
Steps:

1. Dissolve 4 oz. of benzoin in alcohol.
2. Add 8 oz. of essence of lavender, 4 oz. of essence of rosemary, and 325 qts. of alcohol at 75° to the solution.
3. Successively add 21 oz. of neroli, 21 oz. of petit grain, 21 oz. of cedrat, 2 1/2 lbs. of Portugal essence, 2 1/2 lbs. of lemon essence, and 2 1/2 lbs. of alcoholic extract of geranium.
4. Shake the mixture several times to ensure thorough blending.
5. Allow the solution to rest for 14 days before bottling.
j. maria farina cologne,perfume preparation,cologne making

Oral Hygiene (2) -

Make Perfume for Mouth Washes
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Asarum Canadense: 1 oz.
• Orris root: 1 oz.
• Strong alcohol: 1 pt.

Procedure:
1. Prepare a tincture using the asarum canadense and orris root in strong alcohol.
2. Add the following ingredients to the tincture:
- Tincture...
Ingredients:
• Asarum Canadense: 1 oz.
• Orris root: 1 oz.
• Strong alcohol: 1 pt.

Procedure:

2. Add the following ingredients to the tincture:
- Tincture of musk: 1 dr.
- Essence of millefleurs: 1/2 dr.
- Essence of patchouli: 20 drops.
mouth wash, perfume, asarum canadense, orris root, tincture, musk, millefleurs, patchouli

• Perfume as above (from the perfume for mouth washes procedure): 4 fl. oz.

1. Dissolve the white castile soap in the alcohol.
2. Add the honey and the...

• White castile soap: 2 drs.
• Alcohol: 3 fl. oz.
• Honey: 1 oz.
• Perfume as above (from the perfume for mouth washes procedure): 4 fl. oz.

2. Add the honey and the previously prepared perfume to the mixture.
3. Combine all ingredients thoroughly.
mouth wash, superior, castile soap, alcohol, honey, perfume

Painting Techniques (2) -

Paint Fire-Places and Hearths Using Molasse Stone
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Molasse stone (from Saura village, Savoy)
• Lime
• Chalk
• Gypsum
• Blue extract of indigo or charcoal black

Procedure:
1. The Genevese use a type of stone called molasse for constructing fire-places and stoves in the...
Materials Required:
• Molasse stone (from Saura village, Savoy)
• Lime
• Chalk
• Gypsum
• Blue extract of indigo or charcoal black

Procedure:
1. The Genevese use a type of stone called molasse for constructing fire-places and stoves in the German style.
2. This grayish-blue stone is sourced from Saura, a village near Geneva in Savoy.
3. Its color is similar to that achieved by common whitewashing with lime, chalk, or gypsum.
4. To correct the dullness of this tint, add a small amount of blue extract of indigo or charcoal black.
molasse stone, fire-places, hearths, lime, chalk, gypsum, indigo extract, charcoal black
Paint in Fresco on Fresh Plaster or Mortar
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Water-colors
• Fresh plaster or mortar (not dry)

Procedure:
1. Fresco painting is performed using water-colors on fresh plaster or a wall laid with mortar that has not yet dried.
2. This method allows the paint to...
Materials Required:
• Water-colors
• Fresh plaster or mortar (not dry)

Procedure:
1. Fresco painting is performed using water-colors on fresh plaster or a wall laid with mortar that has not yet dried.
2. This method allows the paint to incorporate with the mortar and dry along with it, resulting in a very durable finish.
3. The ancient technique involved painting on stucco, as noted by Vitruvius, who emphasized the importance of careful plastering for beauty and longevity.
4. Modern painters often prefer a plaster made from lime and sand over traditional methods.
fresco painting, water-colors, fresh plaster, mortar, stucco, vitruvius

Color Preservation (2) -

Preparing Ultramarine from Stone
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Preparing Ultramarine:

1. Material Preparation:
- Separate the most apparent parts of ultramarine from the stone.
- Reduce them to the size of a pea.
2. Heating Process:
- Bring the material to a red heat in a...
Procedure for Preparing Ultramarine:

1. Material Preparation:
- Separate the most apparent parts of ultramarine from the stone.
- Reduce them to the size of a pea.
2. Heating Process:
- Bring the material to a red heat in a crucible.
- Throw the heated material into the strongest distilled vinegar.
3. Grinding and Reduction:
- Grind the material with vinegar until it is reduced to an impalpable powder.
4. Mixing Ingredients:
- Take equal quantities of wax, red colophonium, and lapis lazuli (1/2 oz. each).
- Melt the wax and colophonium in a proper vessel.
5. Combining Materials:
- Add the powdered ultramarine to the melted mixture.
6. Cooling Process:
- Pour the mass into cold water and let it rest for eight days.
7. Kneading and Extraction:
- Use two glass vessels filled with hot water (as hot as the hand can bear).
- Knead the mass in the water to extract the purest part of ultramarine.
8. Separation Process:
- Remove the resinous mass into another vessel and continue kneading to separate the remainder.
9. Refinement Steps:
- If the latter portion appears paler, let it rest for 4 days to facilitate precipitation.
- Extract the ultramarine by decantation and wash it in fair water.
10. Quality Separation:
- This process yields four qualities of ultramarine.
- The first separation gives the finest quality; subsequent separations yield progressively less vibrant powder.
11. Key Insight:
- Kinckel emphasizes that immersion in vinegar is essential for dividing and dissolving zeolitic and earthy particles soluble in that acid.
ultramarine,preparation,color extraction,ancient techniques
Extracting and Preparing Ancient Colors for Longevity
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Extracting and Preserving Colors as Used by the Ancients:

1. Material Selection: Use six primary colors: white, black, blue, red, yellow, and green.
2. Application Technique:
- Begin by covering the canvas entirely...
Procedure for Extracting and Preserving Colors as Used by the Ancients:

1. Material Selection: Use six primary colors: white, black, blue, red, yellow, and green.
2. Application Technique:
- Begin by covering the canvas entirely with white.
- Trace the design in black, leaving out the lights of the ground color.
3. Color Mixing:
- Mix colors with a gummy substance for adhesion.
- Apply each color separately without blending or mixture.
4. Preservation Method:
- The ancient method ensured that painted surfaces retained their vibrancy even after centuries of exposure to the open air.
5. Historical Evidence:
- Ancient paintings at Dendaras, Egypt, have remained vivid for over 2000 years without any covering or protection.
6. Ancient vs Modern Knowledge:
- The ancients had some colors unknown to modern times but lacked knowledge of more recently discovered ones.
7. Modern Deficiencies:
- Some modern paintings, such as those by Sir Joshua Reynolds, have shown premature fading due to defects in the base or preparation methods.
8. Conclusion:
- The ancient techniques for preparing and preserving colors were superior in terms of longevity and vibrancy.
ancient colors,preservation,ultramarine,color preparation,longevity

Varnish Preparation (2) -

Preparation of Resinous Drying Oil
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Preparing Resinous Drying Oil:

1. Use 10 lbs. of drying nut-oil (for external use) or 10 lbs. of drying linseed oil (for internal use).
2. Add 3 lbs. of resin and 6 oz. of turpentine.
3. Dissolve the resin in the...
Procedure for Preparing Resinous Drying Oil:

1. Use 10 lbs. of drying nut-oil (for external use) or 10 lbs. of drying linseed oil (for internal use).
2. Add 3 lbs. of resin and 6 oz. of turpentine.
3. Dissolve the resin in the oil using gentle heat.
4. Once dissolved, incorporate it with the oil.
5. Add the turpentine to the mixture.
6. Allow the varnish to rest; this will cause portions of resin and other impurities to deposit.
7. Preserve the varnish in wide-mouthed bottles.
8. Use the varnish while fresh, as it may lose some of its resin over time if left too long.
9. If the oil becomes too thick, dilute it with a small amount of essence (for sun-sheltered use) or oil of poppies.
resinous drying oil, nut-oil, linseed oil, resin, turpentine, varnish preparation
Preparing Colors for Varnish and Lacquer Applications
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Preparing Colors in Varnish and Lacquer:

1. Grind Colors Thoroughly:
- Ensure that all colors used in varnish are well ground and mixed.
2. Warm Foils Before Application:
- For dragon's blood in seed-lac varnish or...
Procedure for Preparing Colors in Varnish and Lacquer:

1. Grind Colors Thoroughly:
- Ensure that all colors used in varnish are well ground and mixed.
2. Warm Foils Before Application:
- For dragon's blood in seed-lac varnish or lacquer, warm the foils before laying them out.
3. Apply with a Broad Soft Brush:
- Use a broad, soft brush to apply the mixture from one end of the foil to the other.
4. Avoid Repeated Strokes:
- Do not cross or reapply over any part unless the first coat is dry.
5. Apply Additional Coats if Needed:
- If the color does not appear strong enough, apply a second coat after the first has dried.

Notes:
• This method ensures even and strong color application on foils for varnish or lacquer use.
varnish preparation,foils,warm foils,broad brush,application technique

Lubricants (2) -

Prepare Anti-attrition Lubricant
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• 1 cwt plumbago
• 4 cwt hog's lard or other grease

Procedure:
1. Thoroughly mix the plumbago with the hog's lard or other grease.
2. Apply the mixture to surfaces such as axles, spindles, or any part where friction occurs.
3....

2. Apply the mixture to surfaces such as axles, spindles, or any part where friction occurs.
3. Ensure a sufficient quantity is used to prevent wear and tear in engines or machines.

1. Apply gentle heat to the mixture until the India-rubber is nearly dissolved.
2. This compound is used as a lubricant, particularly in machinery...

Wood Preservation (2) -

Make Varnish for Wood Resistant to Boiling Water
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Ingredients:
- 1 1/2 lbs. of linseed-oil.
- 5 oz. of litharge (red lead).
- 3 oz. of pulverized minium (red lead oxide).
- 1 lb. of yellow amber.
- 2 oz. of linseed oil (for melting amber).
2. Steps:
-...
Procedure:

1. Ingredients:
- 1 1/2 lbs. of linseed-oil.
- 5 oz. of litharge (red lead).
- 3 oz. of pulverized minium (red lead oxide).
- 1 lb. of yellow amber.
- 2 oz. of linseed oil (for melting amber).
2. Steps:
- Boil the linseed-oil in a red copper vessel (not tinned) over a strong fire.
- Suspend a small linen bag containing litharge and minium above the oil, ensuring it does not touch the bottom of the vessel.
- Continue boiling until the oil turns a deep brown color.
- Replace the bag with one containing a clove of garlic, repeating this process 7–8 times or adding all cloves at once.
3. Prepare amber:
- Mix 1 lb. of finely pulverized yellow amber with 2 oz. of linseed oil and melt it over a strong fire.
4. Combine ingredients:
- Pour the melted amber into the boiling linseed-oil, stirring thoroughly.
- Boil for 2–3 minutes, then let the mixture settle.
5. Storage:
- Decant the varnish when cold and store in well-corked bottles.
6. Application:
- Polish the wood first, then apply a color (e.g., soot mixed with turpentine) for walnut-wood.
- Apply the varnish using a fine sponge, repeating 4 coats, ensuring each coat is fully dry before applying the next.
linseed oil,varnish,boiling water resistance,litharge,minium,yellow amber
Prepare Varnish for Pales and Coarse Woodwork
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Tar
• Spanish-brown (in proportion to the tar, without making it too thick)

Procedure:
1. Grind the tar with Spanish-brown until a paint or varnish consistency is achieved.
2. Apply the mixture to pales or other wood surfaces...
Ingredients:
• Tar
• Spanish-brown (in proportion to the tar, without making it too thick)

Procedure:
1. Grind the tar with Spanish-brown until a paint or varnish consistency is achieved.
2. Apply the mixture to pales or other wood surfaces as soon as possible.
3. The varnish hardens quickly when left to dry.
4. Use a hard brush or house-painter's tool for application.
5. Keep the work free from dust and insects until the varnish is thoroughly dry.
6. If a glossy brown color is not desired, mix in white lead, whiting, or ivory black to achieve a grayish-brown shade.
7. Boiled coal-tar can also be used for similar purposes.
varnish,woodwork,pales,tar

Furniture Care (2) -

Another Method for Furniture Paste
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Beeswax: 4 oz
• Oil of turpentine: 1 pint
• Alkanet-root: 1 oz
• Powdered resin: 1/4 oz
Procedure:
1. Scrape 4 oz of beeswax into a basin.
2. In a glazed pipkin, add 1 pint of oil of turpentine and 1 oz of alkanet-root.
3....
Ingredients:
• Beeswax: 4 oz
• Oil of turpentine: 1 pint
• Alkanet-root: 1 oz
• Powdered resin: 1/4 oz
Procedure:
1. Scrape 4 oz of beeswax into a basin.
2. In a glazed pipkin, add 1 pint of oil of turpentine and 1 oz of alkanet-root.
3. Cover the pipkin and place it over a slow fire, ensuring it does not boil over or catch fire.
4. When the liquid turns deep red, add enough to the wax to moisten it through, along with powdered resin.
5. Cover and let stand for 6 hours before use.
another furniture paste method, beeswax, turpentine oil, alkanet root, resin
Make Furniture Oil
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Linseed-oil: sufficient quantity
• Alkanet-root: as much as the oil will cover
Procedure:
1. Place linseed-oil and alkanet-root into a glazed pipkin.
2. Boil gently until the mixture turns a strong red color.
3. Allow to cool...
Ingredients:
• Linseed-oil: sufficient quantity
• Alkanet-root: as much as the oil will cover
Procedure:
1. Place linseed-oil and alkanet-root into a glazed pipkin.
2. Boil gently until the mixture turns a strong red color.
3. Allow to cool before use.
furniture oil, linseed oil, alkanet root, red color

Art Preservation (2) -

Prepare Wash to Preserve Drawings with Black Lead Pencil
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Use either:
- A thin wash of isinglass, or
- Skimmed milk (free from cream) for fixing black lead or hard black chalk on drawings.
2. For skimmed milk method:
- Lay the drawing flat on the surface of the milk.
...
Procedure:

1. Use either:
- A thin wash of isinglass, or
- Skimmed milk (free from cream) for fixing black lead or hard black chalk on drawings.
2. For skimmed milk method:
- Lay the drawing flat on the surface of the milk.
- Lift it by one corner until it drains and dries completely.
3. Avoid using milk with cream, as it may grease the paper.
isinglass,skimmed milk,preserving drawings,black lead pencil
Varnish Drawings and Card Work with Parchment Size
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Ingredients:
- Clear parchment cuttings.
2. Steps:
- Boil the parchment cuttings in water in a glazed pipkin until they produce a clear size (a type of glue).
- Strain the solution and set it aside for use.
3....
Procedure:

1. Ingredients:
- Clear parchment cuttings.
2. Steps:
- Boil the parchment cuttings in water in a glazed pipkin until they produce a clear size (a type of glue).
- Strain the solution and set it aside for use.
3. Application:
- Apply two coats of the size to the work, using a brush and passing it quickly over the surface without disturbing the colors.
parchment size,varnish,drawing preservation

Stain Removal (2) -

Remove Marking Ink
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Use cyanide of potassium: Wet the area with a solution of cyanide of potassium to remove marking ink.

2. Wash thoroughly: After applying the solution, wash the affected area with water.

3. Handle with care: Exercise caution when...
1. Use cyanide of potassium: Wet the area with a solution of cyanide of potassium to remove marking ink.

2. Wash thoroughly: After applying the solution, wash the affected area with water.

3. Handle with care: Exercise caution when using cyanide of potassium, as it is a violent poison and must be handled safely.
marking ink removal, cyanide of potassium, poison handling
Remove Ink Spots
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Act immediately: As soon as the ink spot appears, wet the affected area with juice of sorrel or lemon, vinegar, or use a weak solution of oxalic acid.

2. Use hard white soap: Apply the best hard white soap to assist in removing the stain.
ink removal, sorrel juice, lemon juice, vinegar, oxalic acid

Metallurgy Tinning (2) -

Tin Copper and Brass
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Tin Copper and Brass:

• Boil 6 lbs of cream of tartar, 4 galls of water, and 8 lbs of grain-tin or tin shavings until the mixture is well combined.
• Add the object to be tinned into the boiling solution and continue boiling...
To Tin Copper and Brass:

• Boil 6 lbs of cream of tartar, 4 galls of water, and 8 lbs of grain-tin or tin shavings until the mixture is well combined.
• Add the object to be tinned into the boiling solution and continue boiling until the tin precipitates in its metallic form.
tinning,copper,brass,cream of tartar
Tin Iron or Copper-plate
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Tin Iron or Copper-plate:

For tinning iron:

• First steep the iron in acidic materials such as sour whey or distillers' wash.
• Scour it clean and dip it into melted tin after rubbing it with a solution of sal ammoniac.
• Cover...
To Tin Iron or Copper-plate:

For tinning iron:

• First steep the iron in acidic materials such as sour whey or distillers' wash.
• Scour it clean and dip it into melted tin after rubbing it with a solution of sal ammoniac.
• Cover the surface of the tin with a coat of fat to prevent calcining.

For tinning copper vessels:

• Clean the copper vessel thoroughly.
• Add a sufficient quantity of tin mixed with sal ammoniac into the vessel and bring it to fusion.
• Move the copper vessel about during the process.
• A small amount of resin may be added if desired.
• The sal ammoniac prevents the copper from scaling and ensures that the tin adheres wherever it touches.
tinning,iron,copper,sal ammoniac

China Glazing (2) -

Chinese Mode of Glazing China
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials:
• Petunse (finest pieces)
• Borax
• Quicklime
• Dried furze
• Water

Procedure:
1. Pound and wash over the petunse to extract the finest part of the powder, which forms a liquid mass called the oil of petunse.
2. **Mix an...
Materials:
• Petunse (finest pieces)
• Borax
• Quicklime
• Dried furze
• Water

Procedure:
1. Pound and wash over the petunse to extract the finest part of the powder, which forms a liquid mass called the oil of petunse.
2. Mix an equal weight of borax with the oil of petunse.
3. Slake quicklime and form layers of it with dried furze.
4. Set on fire and burn until the first layer is reduced to ashes.
5. Collect the ashes and lime, then form new layers with fresh furze, repeating this process 5 or 6 times.
6. Put the ashes and lime into a vessel with water, adding borax in the proportion of 1 lb. to 1 cwt. of the ashes.
7. Wash over the finer part of the mixture and pour off all fluid from the dregs, keeping them together with the solid part after washing.
8. Mix this composition of lime, ashes, and salts with an equal quantity of the oil of petunse and borax to form the glazing matter.

Alternative Material:
Instead of petunse, quartz used in Saxon manufacture may be employed for forming a similar glazing by treating it in the same manner. This method is said to be used for the glazing of Dresden China.
chinese glazing, petunse, borax, quicklime, furze
English Glazing for China
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials:
• 20 lbs. of finest white sand or calcined flints
• 18 lbs. of red-lead
• 10 lbs. of pearlash
• 4 lbs. of common salt (decrepitated)

Procedure:
1. Levigate the sand or calcined flints and red-lead well together.
2. **Mix...
Materials:
• 20 lbs. of finest white sand or calcined flints
• 18 lbs. of red-lead
• 10 lbs. of pearlash
• 4 lbs. of common salt (decrepitated)

Procedure:
1. Levigate the sand or calcined flints and red-lead well together.
2. Mix thoroughly with the pearlash and common salt.
3. Fuse the compound in the manner directed for the treatment of glass until it is perfectly vitrified.
4. Separate the fragments of the pot carefully from the mixture.
5. Reduce to an impalpable powder using a flat agate or porphyry mortar.
6. Temper with water to achieve the proper consistency for painting or glazing.
english glazing, china, white sand, red-lead, pearlash

Chemical Analysis (2) -

Test for Adulterated Wheat Flour or Bread with Chalk
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To determine if wheat flour or bread is adulterated with chalk:

1. Method 1: Mix a small amount of sulphuric acid with the flour to be tested.
- If chalk or whiting is present, an effervescence will occur due to carbon dioxide release...
To determine if wheat flour or bread is adulterated with chalk:

1. Method 1: Mix a small amount of sulphuric acid with the flour to be tested.
- If chalk or whiting is present, an effervescence will occur due to carbon dioxide release from the chalk.
- No effervescence indicates pure flour.
2. Method 2: Pour boiling water over slices of bread, then add sulphuric acid to the water.
- If chalk is present in the bread, an effervescence will occur; otherwise, no reaction will take place.
chalk detection, sulphuric acid, flour testing
Test for Hard or Soft Water
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To determine if water is hard or soft:

1. Add a few drops of soap solution in alcohol to a glassful of the water.
2. If the water is pure, it will remain clear and limpid.
3. If the water is hard, white flakes will form due to the reaction...
To determine if water is hard or soft:

1. Add a few drops of soap solution in alcohol to a glassful of the water.
2. If the water is pure, it will remain clear and limpid.
3. If the water is hard, white flakes will form due to the reaction between the soap and calcium/magnesium ions.
hard water test, soap solution, chemical analysis

Mushroom Id (2) -

Identify and Distinguish Green-Spored Lepiota Mushrooms from Edible White Mushrooms
Standard Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Examine the cap and stalk characteristics:
- Green-spored lepiotas have a cream or tan scaly cap and a large ring on the stalk.
- The gills are white to cream-colored and turn dingy green with age.
- The thick, white...
Procedure:

1. Examine the cap and stalk characteristics:
- Green-spored lepiotas have a cream or tan scaly cap and a large ring on the stalk.
- The gills are white to cream-colored and turn dingy green with age.
- The thick, white stalk enlarges toward the base and darkens when handled.

2. Check for spore print:
- The spore print of green-spored lepiotas is green.

3. Observe growth pattern:
- Green-spored lepiotas often grow in fairy rings on suburban lawns and cattle pastures.
- They can appear as single individuals or in large, circular clusters.

Warning: Green-spored lepiotas cause violent gastrointestinal upset, including vomiting and diarrhea severe enough to require hospitalization. Do not consume these mushrooms.
green spored lepiota, poisonous mushroom, foraging safety, mushroom identification, edible mushrooms
Identify and Distinguish Jack-O’-Lantern Mushrooms from Chanterelles
Reference Source: MushroomGuide
Procedure:

1. Examine the cap color and shape:
- Jack-o’-lantern mushrooms have a bright orange to yellowish-orange cap that becomes flat to funnel-shaped with a sunken center.
- Chanterelle mushrooms typically have a lighter, more...
Procedure:

1. Examine the cap color and shape:
- Jack-o’-lantern mushrooms have a bright orange to yellowish-orange cap that becomes flat to funnel-shaped with a sunken center.
- Chanterelle mushrooms typically have a lighter, more yellowish cap that is convex but not as deeply funneled.

2. Cut the mushroom length-wise down the middle:
- The inner tissue of jack-o’-lanterns will be orange.
- The inner tissue of chanterelles will be white.

3. Inspect the gills:
- Jack-o’-lantern mushrooms have narrow, sharp-edged gills that descend the stalk.
- Chanterelle mushrooms have folds and ridges on their pore surface rather than true gills.

4. Observe growth habit:
- Jack-o’-lanterns grow in large clusters on wood, often appearing to be growing on top of each other.
- Chanterelles grow as scattered individuals on the ground.

Warning: Jack-o’-lantern mushrooms can cause mild to severe gastrointestinal upset and should not be consumed.
jack o' lantern, chanterelle, mushroom identification, foraging safety, edible mushrooms

Deceased Handling (2) -

Collecting and Burying the Dead
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
The proper and dignified management of mortal remains is the duty of the authorities (judicial, police, health, community, military, etc.), who are solely responsible for identifying remains and returning them to their relatives. The primary concern...
The proper and dignified management of mortal remains is the duty of the authorities (judicial, police, health, community, military, etc.), who are solely responsible for identifying remains and returning them to their relatives. The primary concern of the families is to find out what happened to their missing loved ones and to recover their remains as quickly as possible.

In truly exceptional circumstances, when the authorities do not or cannot fulfill their duty, you might be asked to help to collect and bury human remains. In such cases, please refer to the ICRC’s _Manual on operational best practices regarding the management of human remains and information on the dead by non-specialists_ (November 2004), available from your ICRC delegation or downloadable from www.icrc.org.
handling deceased, burial procedures, ICRC guidelines
Key Requirements for Managing Human Remains
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Regardless of circumstances, non-specialists called upon in exceptional situations to help manage human remains, as you might be, should always obtain all necessary authorizations as well as the consent of the families, and if needed, of...
- Regardless of circumstances, non-specialists called upon in exceptional situations to help manage human remains, as you might be, should always obtain all necessary authorizations as well as the consent of the families, and if needed, of community leaders and religious authorities.

• Regardless of your good intentions, failure to do so may result in criminal liability and unnecessary security risks for you and all others involved and for the organizations they represent.

• You should always have a sympathetic and caring attitude towards the bereaved families.
human remains, authorization, family consent, ICRC guidelines

Assessing Home Cures (2) -

Assessing the Effectiveness of Home Remedies: Rule 3 - Use of Animal or Human Waste
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
### Assessing the Effectiveness of Home Remedies: Rule 3 - Use of Animal or Human Waste

Rule: Remedies that use animal or human waste do no good and can cause dangerous infections. Never use them.

• Example 1: Putting human feces...
### Assessing the Effectiveness of Home Remedies: Rule 3 - Use of Animal or Human Waste

Rule: Remedies that use animal or human waste do no good and can cause dangerous infections. Never use them.

• Example 1: Putting human feces around the eye to cure blurred vision does not work and may lead to infection.
• Example 2: Smearing cow dung on the head to fight ringworm can cause tetanus or other serious infections.
• Other Examples: Using rabbit droppings on burns, holding cow dung in the hand during seizures, or making tea from human, pig, or any animal feces is not only ineffective but dangerous.
• Important Note: Never put feces on the navel of a newborn baby. This can cause tetanus.
animal waste,human waste,ringworm,tetanus
Assessing the Effectiveness of Home Remedies: Rule 4 - Similarity Between Remedy and Illness
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
### Assessing the Effectiveness of Home Remedies: Rule 4 - Similarity Between Remedy and Illness

Rule: The more a remedy resembles the sickness it is said to cure, the more likely its benefits come only from the power of belief.

• **Example...
### Assessing the Effectiveness of Home Remedies: Rule 4 - Similarity Between Remedy and Illness

Rule: The more a remedy resembles the sickness it is said to cure, the more likely its benefits come only from the power of belief.

• Example 1: For a nosebleed, using *yesca* (a bright red mushroom) may be believed to help but has no real curative value.
• Example 2: Putting powdered rattlesnake’s rattle in the ear for deafness is ineffective and based on belief rather than science.
• Example 3: Drinking tea made from a dog’s tail after a dog bite is not effective and may delay proper treatment.
• Example 4: Tying a scorpion against the stung finger or using snake fangs around a baby’s neck for teething has no curative value.
• Example 5: Making tea from kapok bark to 'bring out' the rash of measles is also ineffective.
• Conclusion: These remedies may have some psychological benefit, but they should not be used as substitutes for proper medical care.
belief-based cures,nosebleed,deafness,dog bite,measles

Digestive Health (2) -

Use Willow to Treat Diarrhea
Standard Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Procedure:

1. Harvest the charcoal from Willow roots.
2. Measure out half a cup of Willow charcoal.
3. Dissolve the charcoal in water (approximately one cup of water).
4. Administer the resulting solution orally to treat diarrhea.

Notes:
-...
Procedure:

1. Harvest the charcoal from Willow roots.
2. Measure out half a cup of Willow charcoal.
3. Dissolve the charcoal in water (approximately one cup of water).
4. Administer the resulting solution orally to treat diarrhea.

Notes:
• This method is suitable for treating mild to moderate cases of diarrhea.
• Willow is commonly found near wet soils, riverbanks, sandbars, and silt flats.
willow, diarrhea treatment, natural remedies, digestive health
Use Ripe Papaya for Digestive Aid
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Select ripe papayas, which are rich in vitamins and aid digestion.
2. Offer them to weak or elderly individuals who experience upset stomachs after eating meat, chicken, or eggs.
3. The enzymes in papaya help break down these...
Procedure:

1. Select ripe papayas, which are rich in vitamins and aid digestion.
2. Offer them to weak or elderly individuals who experience upset stomachs after eating meat, chicken, or eggs.
3. The enzymes in papaya help break down these foods, making them easier to digest.

Notes:
• This is a natural remedy for improving digestion in vulnerable populations.
• It should not replace medical treatment for chronic digestive issues.
papaya,digestion,natural remedy,elderly,weak individuals

3. Apply the mashed root directly to the affected tooth or gum area.
4. Leave it in place for several minutes until the pain subsides.

Insect Repellent (2) -

Use Yarrow for Insect Repellent
Reference Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Procedure:

1. Harvest a handful of fresh Yarrow flowers and leaves.
2. Crush the harvested Yarrow thoroughly to release its oils.
3. Rub the crushed mixture onto any exposed skin to repel insects.
4. Reapply as needed, especially after sweating...
Procedure:

1. Harvest a handful of fresh Yarrow flowers and leaves.
2. Crush the harvested Yarrow thoroughly to release its oils.
3. Rub the crushed mixture onto any exposed skin to repel insects.
4. Reapply as needed, especially after sweating or swimming.

Notes:
• Do not confuse Yarrow with Poison Hemlock.
• This method is suitable for temporary insect repellent in survival situations.
yarrow, insect repellent, natural remedies, survival tips
Use Nicotine as an Insecticide Spray for Vegetables
Reference Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Prepare the nicotine solution: Extract nicotine from tobacco (wild or cultivated) and dilute it in water to create a spray.
2. Apply the spray: Use the solution to spray vegetables to prevent insect...
Procedure:

1. Prepare the nicotine solution: Extract nicotine from tobacco (wild or cultivated) and dilute it in water to create a spray.
2. Apply the spray: Use the solution to spray vegetables to prevent insect infestations.

Important Warnings:
• Nicotine is toxic to humans and animals. Handle with care and avoid contact with skin, eyes, or inhalation.
• The concentration of nicotine in tobacco varies significantly; use caution to avoid creating a highly concentrated solution that could be harmful.
• This method should only be used as a last resort when other insecticides are unavailable.
nicotine, insecticide, vegetable protection, natural pest control

Fish Study (2) -

How to Preserve and Identify Fish Specimens
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Objective: Learn how to preserve and identify small or rare fish specimens for further study.

Materials Needed:
• Salt
• Formalin
• Spirits (e.g., alcohol)
• Sketching tools (pencil, paper)
• Envelopes or containers for sending...
Objective: Learn how to preserve and identify small or rare fish specimens for further study.

Materials Needed:
• Salt
• Formalin
• Spirits (e.g., alcohol)
• Sketching tools (pencil, paper)
• Envelopes or containers for sending specimens

Procedure:
1. If a fish cannot be identified in the field, sketch it and take detailed notes on its color, size, shape, and any distinguishing features.
2. For small or rare fish that are difficult to identify, preserve them using one of the following methods:
- Salt: Place the specimen in a container with salt to prevent decay.
- Formalin: Use formalin (a preservative solution) to keep the specimen intact for long-term study.
- Spirits: Submerge the fish in alcohol or another type of spirit to preserve it.
3. Package the preserved specimens carefully and send them to an appropriate institution for identification:
- The zoological department of your local state university
- The state fish commission
- The Bureau of Fisheries, Washington, D.C.
- The United States National Museum, Washington, D.C.
4. Include sketches and notes with the specimens to aid in identification.

Notes: This method is especially useful for identifying rare or small fish that are hard to observe directly.
preserving fish specimens, identifying fish, formalin preservation, sending specimens for study
How to Study Fish in Their Natural Habitat
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Objective: Learn how to observe and study fish in their natural environment.

Materials Needed:
• A wooden pail (water bucket)
• Window glass (to replace the bottom of the pail)
• Rowboat (optional, for more advanced observation)
-...
Objective: Learn how to observe and study fish in their natural environment.

Materials Needed:
• A wooden pail (water bucket)
• Window glass (to replace the bottom of the pail)
• Rowboat (optional, for more advanced observation)
• Glass-bottom box (for advanced observation)
• Hood (to make the observer invisible to fish)

Procedure:
1. Choose a location where you can observe fish: from a bank, wharf, boat, or by wading.
2. Use a water bucket made by replacing its bottom with window glass to view the bottom and fish more clearly.
3. For advanced observation, set up a glass-bottom box at the bow of a rowboat. Cover it with a hood so that you are invisible to the fish being studied.
4. Record your observations in detail, noting behaviors such as spawning, feeding, swimming patterns, and other habits.
5. Use patience, skill, and keen observation to document findings about different species of fish encountered.

Notes: This method is ideal for studying fish that are shy or difficult to observe directly.
studying fish, water bucket, glass bottom box, observation techniques, fish behavior

Plant Usage (1) -

Uses of Madder Plant in Dyeing and Medicine
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Dyeing Applications:
- Madder is primarily used for dyeing fabrics.
- It produces a dark dull red color when used with water, and a deep bright red when used with rectified spirits.
- It imparts a durable red dye to...
Procedure:

1. Dyeing Applications:
- Madder is primarily used for dyeing fabrics.
- It produces a dark dull red color when used with water, and a deep bright red when used with rectified spirits.
- It imparts a durable red dye to woolen cloth that has been prepared with alum and tartar.

2. Combination Dyeing:
- Madder can be combined with Brazil-wood for enhanced color.
- It is also used in conjunction with more expensive red dyes like cochineal to create demiscarlets and demi-crimsons.

3. Medical Use:
- Madder-root may be used as an emmenagogue (a substance that promotes menstrual flow).

4. Animal Feed Application:
- When given to animals with their food, madder can tint their bones red.
- For example:
- Young pigeons will have rose-colored bones in 24 hours and deep scarlet bones in three days.
- Adult animals require about a fortnight for similar results.

Notes:
• Madder is the cheapest of all red drugs that provide durable color, making it ideal for ordinary fabric dyeing.
madder plant,dyeing,woolen cloth,medical use,animal feed,bone tinting

Beekeeping (1) -

Managing Bee Swarming Through Artificial Methods
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Artificial Swarming Procedure:

1. Timing and Conditions:
- Choose a time when drones are present in the hive, indicating that the colony is ready for division.
- Ensure that honey is abundant in the fields and that nights are...
Artificial Swarming Procedure:

1. Timing and Conditions:
- Choose a time when drones are present in the hive, indicating that the colony is ready for division.
- Ensure that honey is abundant in the fields and that nights are warm.

2. Preparation of Hives:
- Have a new hive at hand that matches the size and pattern of your existing hives.
- Select four existing hives and take two frames from each to place into the new hive.
- Replace these removed frames in the original hives with empty frames.

3. Relocation Process:
- Move a hive that has not been disturbed to a new location, at least a rod (approximately 16.5 feet) away from its original position.
- Place the newly assembled hive where the moved hive previously stood.

4. Execution During Bee Activity:
- Perform this process on a fine day when many bees are absent in the fields.
- Bees returning to their old location will find it strange and will begin rearing a new queen, as the hive contains stores, young bees, and eggs for queen development.

5. Repeat Process:
- This procedure can be repeated every two weeks until you achieve the desired increase in colony numbers.

6. Effects on Original Hives:
- The hives from which frames are taken, as well as those that have been moved, will lose a number of bees but will recover and become stronger over time.
- This method prevents natural swarming and allows for controlled division of colonies.

Notes:
• Artificial swarming simplifies beekeeping by eliminating the need to watch for unpredictable natural swarms.
• It enables the beekeeper to choose the timing for dividing colonies at their convenience.
• Over-dividing can weaken hives, so it is best to aim for a moderate increase in colony numbers each year.
• A strong hive with one queen will produce more bees than weaker hives with multiple queens.
artificial swarming, beekeeping, hive division, colony increase, honey production, swarm prevention

Charcoal Production (1) -

Make Charcoal for Medical or Gunpowder Use
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Making Charcoal:

1. Select coppice wood cut every 16 years.
2. Make fagots (bundles of wood) and arrange them into a large conical pile.
3. Cover the pile with clods of earth, leaving circular rows of holes from top to...
Procedure for Making Charcoal:

1. Select coppice wood cut every 16 years.
2. Make fagots (bundles of wood) and arrange them into a large conical pile.
3. Cover the pile with clods of earth, leaving circular rows of holes from top to bottom.
4. Kindle the wood, allowing it to burn until it turns red.
5. As the wood becomes red, close the holes regularly to stop further combustion.
6. Once all holes are closed, leave the pile to cool completely.
7. The resulting black skeleton of the wood is the charcoal, which burns without smoke or flame and produces no soot (except for some fine ash particles that may adhere to chimneys).

Medical Charcoal:
• Use soft woods like willow, heated in iron retorts until no volatile matter is released.

Small-Batch Charcoal:
• Bury wood under sand in a covered crucible and expose it to fire.
make charcoal,charcoal production,charcoal for medical use,charcoal for gunpowder

Adulteration Detection (1) -

Detect Adulterations in Spirits and Wines
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Detection of Adulterations:

1. Alcohol Content Determination:
- Use a hydrometer to determine the quantity of alcohol present.
- If the liquor is significantly below proof but still possesses intoxicating qualities, suspect the...
Detection of Adulterations:

1. Alcohol Content Determination:
- Use a hydrometer to determine the quantity of alcohol present.
- If the liquor is significantly below proof but still possesses intoxicating qualities, suspect the presence of coculus indicus (a type of plant used in some adulterations).

2. Burning Sensation Test:
- If swallowing the liquor produces a burning sensation at the back of the throat, it may be adulterated with pepper or similar substances.

3. Flavoring Ethers Detection:
- Flavoring ethers can be separated by distillation; however, as some exist in true wines and spirits, this method cannot conclusively determine if a liquor is factitious.

4. Evaporation Test:
- Carefully evaporate the liquor to dryness in a porcelain capsule.
- The extract will contain most of the adulterations, which can often be detected by taste and smell.

5. Nitrates Detection:
- If nitrate of baryta gives a precipitate with any spirit, suspect the presence of artificially added acid (this is not true for wines).

6. Fusel Oil Detection:
- Place some fused chloride of calcium in small pieces into a glass.
- Pour over it the suspected liquor and cover with a glass plate.
- Let it stand aside for a short time; if fusel oil is present, its smell will manifest itself immediately.
- The smaller the quantity of fusel oil, the longer it must be left before examination.
alcohol,detection,fusel,oil,test,spirit,adulteration

Spirit Measurement (1) -

Determine the Strength of Spirits Using a Hydrometer
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Determine the Strength of Spirits:

1. Use a hydrometer, which consists of a cylinder with a weighted bulb below to ensure it floats upright and a graduated stem.
2. Ensure the hydrometer is at 60° Fahr. If not, bring the spirit to that...
To Determine the Strength of Spirits:

1. Use a hydrometer, which consists of a cylinder with a weighted bulb below to ensure it floats upright and a graduated stem.
2. Ensure the hydrometer is at 60° Fahr. If not, bring the spirit to that temperature or use a table from chemical works to adjust for temperature differences.
3. Avoid wetting the stem of the hydrometer, as this can give a higher percentage than the actual strength.
4. The Tralles' hydrometer is graduated to indicate the percentage by volume of absolute alcohol in any mixture of alcohol and water.
5. To convert volume percentage into weight percentage: multiply the number of degrees on Tralles' scale by 0.794, then divide by the specific gravity of the liquid under test.
6. If a hydrometer is not available, determine the specific gravity of the liquid using other methods and refer to tables in chemical works for alcohol percentage determination.
hydrometer,specific gravity,alcohol strength,determine spirit strength

Balsams (1) -

Extract True Riga Balsam
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Obtained from the shoots of the Aphernousti pine, *pinus cembra*, previously bruised and macerated for a month in water. It is pellucid, very liquid, whitish, and has the smell and taste of oil of juniper.
riga balsam,pinus cembra,maceration,juniper

Oil Purity Testing (1) -

Detection of Adulterations in Volatile Oils
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
Resinous and Fatty Matters:
• These are left behind when the oil is evaporated.
• They leave a greasy stain on paper that does not disappear with gentle heat.
• Insoluble in alcohol.
• Left behind when mixed with water and...
Procedure:
Resinous and Fatty Matters:
• These are left behind when the oil is evaporated.
• They leave a greasy stain on paper that does not disappear with gentle heat.
• Insoluble in alcohol.
• Left behind when mixed with water and distilled.

Cheaper Volatile Oils:
• Detected by smell, taste, and specific gravity.
• Oil of turpentine (common adulterant) is undissolved when treated with 4 times its volume of alcohol of a specific gravity of 0.84.
• Oil of geranium in oil of rose can be detected using sulphuric acid, which develops an unpleasant odor if present.

Alcohol:
• Use fused chloride of calcium in a test tube.
• Add the oil to be examined and heat gently to 212° Fahr (approximately 100°C).
• If much alcohol is present, the chloride of calcium will dissolve.
• If only a small quantity is present, the fragments will form a pasty mass at the bottom of the tube.
adulteration,detection,oil testing,resin,fatty matters,alcohol,sulphuric acid

Flower Preservation (1) -

Preservation of Flowers for Distillation
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Rub 3 lb. of rose leaves with 1 lb. of common salt for 2 to 3 minutes.
2. The friction from the salt will bruise the flowers, forming a paste.
3. Place this paste into an earthen jar or water-tight barrel.
4. Repeat the process...
Procedure:
1. Rub 3 lb. of rose leaves with 1 lb. of common salt for 2 to 3 minutes.
2. The friction from the salt will bruise the flowers, forming a paste.
3. Place this paste into an earthen jar or water-tight barrel.
4. Repeat the process until the vessel is filled, ensuring all roses are equally salted.
5. Seal the vessel and store it in a cool place until needed for distillation.

Distillation Process:
1. When ready to use, add twice the weight of water to the preserved paste (by weight).
2. Proceed with distillation as usual to obtain essential water.
3. This method produces greater quantities of both oil and water compared to using unsalted leaves.
4. The preserved paste retains its flavor and strength for several years.
flower preservation,distillation,salt,rose leaves,essential water

Specialty Oils (1) -

Prepare Oil of Brick for Lapidaries
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Saturate fragments of brick with oil.
2. Distill the saturated bricks at a red heat.

Details:
• This method is used by lapidaries to prepare oil of brick, which may have specific applications in their craft.
oil of brick,lapidary oil,brick distillation

Oil Preservation (1) -

Preventing Fats and Oils from Becoming Rancid Using Slippery-elm Bark
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:

1. Heat the oil or melted fat for a few minutes with powdered slippery-elm bark, in the proportion of 1 dr. of the powder to 1 lb. of fat.
2. The bark will shrink and gradually subsides after heating.
3. Pour off the fat after the bark...
Steps:

1. Heat the oil or melted fat for a few minutes with powdered slippery-elm bark, in the proportion of 1 dr. of the powder to 1 lb. of fat.
2. The bark will shrink and gradually subsides after heating.
3. Pour off the fat after the bark has settled.
4. The treated fat will communicate an odor like that of the hickory-nut.
5. Butter treated this way has been kept unchanged for a year.
slippery-elm bark,preventing rancidity,oil preservation

Alcohol Purification (1) -

Preparing Deodorizing Alcohol Using Soap and Distillation
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Steps:

1. Use spirit of wine, brandy, or alcohol distilled over soap to remove empyreumatic odors and tastes entirely.
2. At about 215°, the soap retains neither alcohol nor wood-spirit.
3. During a second distillation, water vapor carries off...
Steps:

1. Use spirit of wine, brandy, or alcohol distilled over soap to remove empyreumatic odors and tastes entirely.
2. At about 215°, the soap retains neither alcohol nor wood-spirit.
3. During a second distillation, water vapor carries off the empyreumatic oil remaining in combination with the soap, leaving a soap free from empyreuma and fit for reuse.
4. The concentration of the alcohol increases when using soap because it retains water and alcoholic vapors, making the product richer.
5. Use 33 lbs. of soap for 100 galls. of empyreumatic brandy.
6. Ensure the soap contains no potassa; use a hard or soda soap that is completely free from any excess of fat acids or fluids to avoid rancidity and impurity.
7. Adding a little soda during the first distillation may improve results.
8. Hard soda-soaps, as exempt as possible from fluid fat acids, remove empyreumatic odor effectively.
deodorizing alcohol,soap distillation,empyreumatic oil removal

Personal Care (1) -

Remove Light Color Stains from Hands
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
1. Wash the hands in a solution of soap and water with dissolved pearlash.
2. Alternatively, wash the hands using a paste made from chloride of lime (also known as bleaching powder).
hand,stain,pearlash,chloride of lime

Painting Supplies (1) -

Prepare Painter's Cream
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
### Ingredients:
• Very clear nut-oil: 3 oz
• Mastic in tears, pulverized: 1/2 oz
• Sal saturni (acetate of lead), in powder: 1/3 oz

### Procedure:
1. Dissolve the mastic in oil over a gentle fire.
2. Pour the mixture into a marble mortar with...
### Ingredients:
• Very clear nut-oil: 3 oz
• Mastic in tears, pulverized: 1/2 oz
• Sal saturni (acetate of lead), in powder: 1/3 oz

### Procedure:
1. Dissolve the mastic in oil over a gentle fire.
2. Pour the mixture into a marble mortar with the pounded salt of lead.
3. Stir it with a wooden pestle and add water in small quantities until the mixture assumes the appearance and consistency of cream, and refuses to admit more water.

### Notes:
• This preparation preserves the freshness of painted colors during long intervals between work periods.
• It can be removed when resuming work.
painter's cream, preservation of paint, nut oil mixture

Leather Restoration (1) -

Restore Blackness of Old Leather Chairs and Shoes
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. Ingredients:
- Yolks from 2 newly-laid eggs.
- White of 1 egg.
- A tea-lump of loaf sugar (about 1 tablespoon).
- Geneva (brandy or similar spirit).
- Ivory black (a type of pigment).
2. Steps:
-...
Procedure:

1. Ingredients:
- Yolks from 2 newly-laid eggs.
- White of 1 egg.
- A tea-lump of loaf sugar (about 1 tablespoon).
- Geneva (brandy or similar spirit).
- Ivory black (a type of pigment).
2. Steps:
- Beat the yolks and white of the egg together until well mixed.
- Shake the mixture in a glass vessel to make it resemble thick oil.
- Dissolve 1 tablespoon of loaf sugar in a small amount of geneva.
- Mix this with the beaten eggs, then thicken with ivory black, working it up thoroughly with a stick.
3. Application:
- Apply the mixture to leather as you would apply shoe blacking.
- Polish with a soft, clean brush until completely dry and shiny.
4. Drying:
- Allow the treated leather to harden for at least one day.
5. For shoes:
- Repeat the process, but also:
- Shake the white of eggs in a large glass phial until it becomes oil-like.
- Brush this over the inner edges of the shoes and let dry completely to prevent soiling from leather.
6. Repeat as needed for best results.
leather restoration,blackness,egg yolk,geneva,ivory black

Surface Treatment (1) -

Apply Pearly Lustre to Objects Using Reinsch's Method
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To produce the iridescence of mother-of-pearl on stone, glass, metal, resin, paper, silk, leather etc., follow these steps:

1. Prepare the varnish mixture:
- 2 parts solution of copal
- 2 parts solution of sandarac
- 4 parts solution...
To produce the iridescence of mother-of-pearl on stone, glass, metal, resin, paper, silk, leather etc., follow these steps:

1. Prepare the varnish mixture:
- 2 parts solution of copal
- 2 parts solution of sandarac
- 4 parts solution of Damara resin (equal parts of resin and absolute alcohol)
- Half their volume of oil of bergamot or rosemary

2. Mix the ingredients together.

3. Evaporate the mixture to the thickness of castor-oil.

4. Apply the varnish:
- Draw the mixture, using a feather or brush, over the surface of some water that has about 6% pure solution of lime added and kept at approximately 72°F (22°C).
- This will form a beautiful iridescent pellicle.

5. Apply the film to the objects which are to be rendered iridescent.

6. Ensure the vessel containing the water is as large or larger than the objects being treated.

7. Dry the objects in the air after application.
pearly lustre, mother-of-pearl, iridescence, copal, sandarac, Damara resin, bergamot oil, rosemary oil, lime solution

Household Crafts (1) -

Prepare Common Bottle Wax (Method 1)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Ingredients:
• Dark resin: 18 oz.
• Shellac: 1 oz.
• Beeswax: 1 oz.

Steps:
1. Mix together the dark resin, shellac, and beeswax thoroughly.
2. Color the mixture with red-lead, Venetian red, or lampblack as desired.
bottle wax, resin, shellac, beeswax, coloring agents

Chemical Landscapes (1) -

Creating Chemical Landscapes Using Sympathetic Inks
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Indian-ink
• Sympathetic inks:
- Green: Chloride of nickel
- Blue: Pure chloride or acetate of cobalt
- Yellow: Chloride of copper
- Brown: Bromide of copper
• Paper
• Heat source

Procedure:
1....
Materials Required:
• Indian-ink
• Sympathetic inks:
- Green: Chloride of nickel
- Blue: Pure chloride or acetate of cobalt
- Yellow: Chloride of copper
- Brown: Bromide of copper
• Paper
• Heat source

Procedure:
1. Draw a picture on paper using Indian-ink and sympathetic inks.
2. The image typically represents a winter scene, with parts of the drawing invisible until heated.
3. When gently heated, the following changes occur:
- Sky turns blue
- Leaves turn green
- Flowers and fruit become visible
4. If the paper is overheated, it may not fade again.
chemical landscapes, sympathetic inks, winter scene, chloride of nickel, cobalt, copper, bromide

Imitation Stones (1) -

Imitating Stones Using Painted Sockets
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Imitating Stones with Painted Sockets:

1. Prepare the Socket and Stone:
- Heat both the socket and the counterfeit stone.
2. Mix Colors with Turpentine and Mastic:
- Temper the colors (e.g., amethyst, topaz,...
Procedure for Imitating Stones with Painted Sockets:

1. Prepare the Socket and Stone:
- Heat both the socket and the counterfeit stone.
2. Mix Colors with Turpentine and Mastic:
- Temper the colors (e.g., amethyst, topaz, vinegar-garnet, eagle-marine) with turpentine and mastic.
3. Paint the Socket:
- Apply the mixture to the socket using a brush.
4. Set the Stone Immediately:
- Place the heated stone into the painted socket and close it before the mixture cools or hardens.
5. Use Orange Lake for Vinegar-Garnet Effect:
- For a bright vinegar-garnet color, use orange lake as described in the text.
6. Grinding Colors for Oil-Based Applications:
- Grind colors used in oil thoroughly with turpentine.
7. Tempering with Oils:
- Temper with old nut or poppy-oil; alternatively, use strong fat oil diluted with spirit of turpentine if time allows for drying.
8. Polishing:
- The mixture will gain a fine polish on its own when properly prepared.

Notes:
• This method is suitable for imitating stones such as amethyst, topaz, vinegar-garnet, and eagle-marine.
• Ensure the socket is closed immediately after placing the stone to avoid cooling of the mixture.
imitate stones,painted sockets,turpentine,mastic,orange lake,vinegar-garnet

Metallurgy Coloring (1) -

Heighten the Color of Red Gold
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Heighten the Color of Red Gold:

• Take 4 oz of melted yellow wax.
• Add 1 1/2 oz of red ochre in fine powder.
• Add 1 1/2 oz of verdigris, which must be calcined until it no longer produces fumes.
• Add 1/2 oz of calcined...
To Heighten the Color of Red Gold:

• Take 4 oz of melted yellow wax.
• Add 1 1/2 oz of red ochre in fine powder.
• Add 1 1/2 oz of verdigris, which must be calcined until it no longer produces fumes.
• Add 1/2 oz of calcined borax.
• Mix all ingredients well together.
• It is essential to calcine the verdigris beforehand; otherwise, the heat applied during burning may cause vinegar to concentrate and corrode the surface, resulting in a speckled appearance.
red gold,color enhancement,verdigris,borax

Metallurgy Separation (1) -

Separate Gold from Gilt, Copper and Silver
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
To Separate Gold from Gilt, Copper and Silver:

• Apply a solution of borax in water to the gilt surface using a fine brush.
• Sprinkle some fine powdered sulphur over the treated area.
• Heat the piece until it becomes red-hot, then...
To Separate Gold from Gilt, Copper and Silver:

• Apply a solution of borax in water to the gilt surface using a fine brush.
• Sprinkle some fine powdered sulphur over the treated area.
• Heat the piece until it becomes red-hot, then quench it in water.
• The gold can be easily wiped off with a scratch-brush and recovered by cupellation.

For separating gold from silver:

• Spread a paste made of powdered sal ammoniac mixed with aquafortis over the surface of the silver.
• Heat until the mixture smokes and is nearly dry.
• Separate the gold by rubbing it with a scratch-brush.
gold separation,gilt,copper,silver,borax

Enamel Application (1) -

Apply Colored Enamel by Printing
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials Required:
• Glue-bat
• Copper plate
• Oil or color
• Prepared paper, vellum, leather
• Engravings, etchings, stamps, casts, devices
• Waters, oils, varnishes, glutinous matters

Procedure:
1. Take a glue-bat of full size for the...
Materials Required:
• Glue-bat
• Copper plate
• Oil or color
• Prepared paper, vellum, leather
• Engravings, etchings, stamps, casts, devices
• Waters, oils, varnishes, glutinous matters

Procedure:
1. Take a glue-bat of full size for the subject.
2. Charge the copper plate with oil or color.
3. Take the impression with the bat from the plate and transfer it to the glass.
4. If the impression is not strong enough, shake some dry color on it which will adhere to the moist color.
5. Use any engraving, etching, stamp, cast, or device charged with oil or color and transfer it onto the glass using prepared paper, vellum, leather, or other suitable material.
6. Charge engravings, etchings, stamps, casts, or devices with waters, oils, varnishes, or glutinous matters as necessary for printing in general.
7. Use these materials alone or mixed with the colors; when used alone, apply the color in powder.
apply enamel, printing, glue-bat, copper plate, prepared paper

Pottery Varnish (1) -

Prepare Varnish for Pottery Ware (Lead-Free)
Reference Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Materials:
• 1 oz. of fire-stone and glass
• 2 drs. of salt
• 1/2 oz. of pipeclay
• 1 1/2 oz. of borax

Procedure:
1. Melt the mixture of fire-stone and glass, salt, pipeclay, and borax.
2. Keep in fusion for 14 minutes.
3....
Materials:
• 1 oz. of fire-stone and glass
• 2 drs. of salt
• 1/2 oz. of pipeclay
• 1 1/2 oz. of borax

Procedure:
1. Melt the mixture of fire-stone and glass, salt, pipeclay, and borax.
2. Keep in fusion for 14 minutes.
3. Varnish the pots with this mixture after they have been in the fire.
4. Put them again in the fire for about 18 hours.
lead-free varnish, pottery ware, fire-stone, borax
